topics.title: Concepts of Kinematics and Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Kinematics and Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanics — The Parent Discipline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanics** is the branch of physical science that deals with the state of rest or motion of bodies under the action of forces. It is broadly classified into **three sub-branches** that form the foundation of *Mechanics of Machinery* (KTU Code: PBMUT504).

| Branch | Concern | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Statics** | Bodies at rest (equilibrium) | A book lying on a table |
| **Kinematics** | Geometry of motion, *ignoring* the forces | Tracking a car's speedometer reading |
| **Kinetics (Dynamics)** | Relation between motion **and** the forces producing it | Calculating engine torque needed to accelerate a vehicle |

---

## 1.2 Kinematics — The "Geometry of Motion"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Kinematics is the branch of mechanics which deals with the motion of bodies (particles or rigid bodies) without reference to the forces which cause the motion, or, in the case of rigid bodies, to the forces which tend to bring the bodies into motion.*

In simpler terms, **kinematics answers "HOW does it move?"** — it is the pure description of motion.

### Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Camera

Imagine a CCTV camera mounted at a street corner.
- The camera records the position, velocity, and trajectory of every passing vehicle.
- It does **not** ask *why* the car moves (engine torque, fuel combustion, friction).
- It only answers *"where is the car, how fast, and in which direction?"*

This is exactly what **kinematics** does — it is the "CCTV camera" of mechanical systems, observing motion in space and time without judging its cause.

---

## 1.3 Dynamics / Kinetics — The "Cause of Motion"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Dynamics (Kinetics) is the branch of mechanics which deals with the motion of bodies (particles or rigid bodies) under the action of forces. It establishes the relationship between the unbalanced forces acting on a body, the mass of the body, and the resulting motion.*

**Dynamics answers "WHY does it move?"** — and exactly *how the motion changes* under applied force.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Police Officer

The same CCTV scene, but now there is a **traffic police officer** writing a report.
- She notes the car's mass (**m**), the road's friction coefficient (**μ**), and the engine's push force (**F**).
- Using **Newton's second law**, she calculates why the car accelerates or decelerates.
- This is **dynamics** — motion explained through forces and mass.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Sub-Concepts Every KTU Student Must Internalise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Kinematics:**
> 1. **Space** — the geometric frame (1D, 2D, or 3D) in which motion occurs.
> 2. **Time** — the scalar against which motion is measured.
> 3. **Motion** — change of position of a body with respect to time, relative to a chosen reference frame.

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement | **s** | Shortest directed distance from initial to final position | **m (metre)** |
| Velocity | **v** | Rate of change of displacement | **m/s** |
| Acceleration | **a** | Rate of change of velocity | **m/s²** |
| Mass | **m** | Inertia of a body (resistance to change of motion) | **kg (kilogram)** |
| Force | **F** | That which changes or tends to change the state of motion | **N (Newton = kg·m/s²)** |
| Momentum | **p** | Quantity of motion: p = m·v | **kg·m/s** |

---

## 1.5 Particle vs. Rigid Body — The Crucial Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Particle (Point Mass):** A body whose dimensions are negligible compared to the path it describes, so its entire mass may be assumed concentrated at a single point. Only *translational* motion is considered.
>
> **Rigid Body:** A body in which the distance between any two particles remains *constant* (invariant) under the action of forces. A rigid body can undergo *translation*, *rotation*, or *general plane motion*.

**Real-world analogy:**
- A *bullet* flying through air → treated as a **particle** (size ≪ range).
- A *spinning flywheel* of an engine → a **rigid body** (size comparable to its motion; different points move differently).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Structure of Mechanics

The relationship between **Statics**, **Kinematics**, and **Dynamics** is best understood as a logical pyramid:

**Level 1 — Statics (∑F = 0, ∑M = 0)**  
↓ *(removes the equilibrium constraint, allowing motion)*  
**Level 2 — Kinematics (geometry of motion alone)**  
↓ *(now we ask "what force produced this motion?")*  
**Level 3 — Dynamics (F = m·a, energy & momentum methods)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Many 14-mark problems in PBMUT504 are *three-step* — first draw the kinematic diagram, then derive velocity/acceleration relations, and finally apply Newton's second law to find forces. Skipping the kinematic step is a guaranteed 4–5 mark deduction.

---

## 2.2 Types of Rigid-Body Motion (Exam-Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> In **PBMUT504 — Mechanics of Machinery**, every mechanism (four-bar, slider-crank, cam-follower, gear train) is analysed by decomposing its motion into these elementary types.

### (a) Pure Translation
- Every line segment of the body remains *parallel* to its original orientation.
- All points have *identical* velocity and *identical* acceleration.
- *Example:* A connecting rod in an ideal slider-crank undergoing pure translation (approximation), a piston in an engine.

### (b) Pure Rotation about a Fixed Axis
- The body rotates about an axis perpendicular to the plane of motion.
- All points trace circular paths centred on the axis.
- Different points have *different* linear velocities (v = r·ω) but the *same* angular velocity (ω).
- *Example:* A spinning crankshaft, a flywheel.

### (c) General Plane Motion (Curvilinear Translation + Rotation)
- The most general 2-D motion: a simultaneous combination of translation and rotation.
- A rigid body in plane motion has **3 degrees of freedom** (2 translational + 1 rotational).
- *Example:* A connecting rod of a slider-crank mechanism, a rolling wheel, an engine valve mechanism.

### (d) Fixed-Axis Rotation with Sliding — *Important for Instantaneous Centre Method*
- A special case where the axis of rotation itself translates.
- *Example:* A cam-follower rolling on a cam profile.

---

## 2.3 Key Kinematic Quantities — Derivative Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result to Memorise:** The **derivative with respect to time** is the bridge that converts *position → velocity → acceleration* and *angular position → angular velocity → angular acceleration*.

For a particle moving along a straight line (rectilinear motion):
$$ v = \frac{ds}{dt}, \quad a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2} $$

For a particle moving along a curved path:
$$ v = \frac{ds}{dt}, \quad a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} \;(tangential), \quad a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} \;(normal/centripetal) $$

The **total acceleration** of a particle on a curved path:
$$ a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} $$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) in tables — never the raw `\|` character — to avoid breaking markdown parsing.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average velocity | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ | **m/s** | Constant direction motion |
| 2 | Instantaneous velocity | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | **m/s** | Any motion |
| 3 | Average acceleration | $\bar{a} = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | **m/s²** | Variable velocity |
| 4 | Instantaneous acceleration | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$ | **m/s²** | General motion |
| 5 | Newton's Second Law (Particle) | $F = m \cdot a$ | **N** | Rectilinear/curvilinear dynamics |
| 6 | Equation of motion (3-D) | $\sum \vec{F} = m \cdot \vec{a}$ | **N** | Vector dynamics problems |
| 7 | Tangential acceleration | $a_t = r \cdot \alpha$ | **m/s²** | Rotation problems |
| 8 | Normal (centripetal) acceleration | $a_n = \dfrac{v^2}{r} = r \cdot \omega^2$ | **m/s²** | Curved-path motion |
| 9 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | **rad/s** | Rotational kinematics |
| 10 | Angular acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$ | **rad/s²** | Rotational kinematics |
| 11 | Linear velocity of point on rotating body | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | **m/s** | Rotation + translation combined |
| 12 | Torque (Moment) | $T = I \cdot \alpha$ | **N·m** | Rotational dynamics |
| 13 | Angular momentum | $L = I \cdot \omega$ | **kg·m²/s** | Impulse–momentum in rotation |
| 14 | Work–Energy (particle) | $T_1 + \sum U_{1 \to 2} = T_2$ | **J (joule)** | Dynamics without time |
| 15 | Impulse–Momentum (particle) | $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} F\, dt = m(v_2 - v_1)$ | **N·s** | Impact / collision problems |
| 16 | Degrees of freedom (Kutzbach / Grübler) | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **dimensionless** | Mechanism DOF |
| 17 | Instantaneous centre velocity relation | $v_A = \omega \cdot r_{A/I}$ | **m/s** | Plane-motion velocity analysis |

**Real-world utility of these relations in KTU-flagged industries:**
- *Automotive (Kerala has a robust small-engine industry):* Crank–slider kinematics and engine balancing.
- *Robotics & Automation (Kochi Infopark):* DOF analysis of robotic arms, joint velocity/acceleration.
- *Renewable energy (Kerala rooftop solar trackers):* Single-axis sun-tracking mechanism design.
- *Heavy machinery (Cochin Shipyard):* Crane slew-ring rotation and load dynamics.

---

## 2.5 Newton's Laws — The Bedrock of Dynamics

| Law | Statement | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **First** | A body continues in its state of rest or uniform straight-line motion unless compelled by an external unbalanced force. | Defines the concept of **inertia**. |
| **Second** | The rate of change of linear momentum of a body is proportional to the impressed force and takes place in the direction of the force. **F = m·a** | Backbone of all *kinetic* problems. |
| **Third** | To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. | Reaction forces in supports, bearings, and pivots. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In machinery, *almost every real problem* is a Newton's-Second-Law problem in disguise — once you identify the accelerating reference and write $\sum F = m \cdot a$ in vector form, the rest is algebra.

---

## 2.6 Reference Frames — Absolute vs. Relative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** A particle's velocity and acceleration depend on the observer's frame. The relationship between two frames is given by the **relative-motion equation**:
> $$\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$$
> $$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A} + 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{B/A}$$
> The last term is the **Coriolis acceleration**, which appears in rotating-frame problems (e.g., steam-turbine blade design, robotics with rotating joints).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Kinematic Equations for Constant Acceleration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three equations appear in almost every KTU mechanics paper. You *must* be able to derive them from first principles in the exam hall.

**Starting premise:** Acceleration **a** is *constant*, so $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = a$ (a constant scalar).

### Step 1 — Integrate to obtain v(t)

We have the definition of acceleration:
$$ a = \frac{dv}{dt} $$

Separating variables and integrating from initial state $(t_0, v_0)$ to a general state $(t, v)$:
$$ \int_{v_0}^{v} dv = \int_{t_0}^{t} a\, dt $$

Since **a** is constant, it comes outside the integral:
$$ v - v_0 = a(t - t_0) $$

Taking $t_0 = 0$ for simplicity:
$$ v = v_0 + a \cdot t \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} $$

### Step 2 — Integrate to obtain s(t)

Velocity is the rate of change of position:
$$ v = \frac{ds}{dt} $$

Substituting Equation 1:
$$ \frac{ds}{dt} = v_0 + a \cdot t $$

Separating variables and integrating:
$$ \int_{s_0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (v_0 + a \cdot \tau)\, d\tau $$

Evaluating the right-hand integral term by term:
$$ s - s_0 = v_0 \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2 $$

Taking $s_0 = 0$ and denoting $s_0$ as origin:
$$ s = v_0 \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} $$

### Step 3 — Eliminate time t

From Equation 1, isolate time:
$$ t = \frac{v - v_0}{a} $$

Substitute this $t$ into Equation 2:
$$ s = v_0 \left(\frac{v - v_0}{a}\right) + \frac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v - v_0}{a}\right)^2 $$

Multiply through by $a$:
$$ a \cdot s = v_0(v - v_0) + \frac{(v - v_0)^2}{2a} \cdot a $$

Simplify the right side:
$$ a \cdot s = v_0 \cdot v - v_0^2 + \frac{v^2 - 2 v v_0 + v_0^2}{2} $$

Combine terms:
$$ a \cdot s = \frac{2 v_0 v - 2 v_0^2 + v^2 - 2 v v_0 + v_0^2}{2} = \frac{v^2 - v_0^2}{2} $$

Therefore:
$$ v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s \quad \text{...(Equation 3)} $$

### Final Summary — The Three Equations of Motion

> [!NOTE]
> $$\boxed{v = v_0 + a \cdot t} \qquad \boxed{s = v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2} \qquad \boxed{v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $s$ | displacement (m) |
| $v_0$ | initial velocity (m/s) |
| $v$ | final velocity (m/s) |
| $a$ | constant acceleration (m/s²) |
| $t$ | time elapsed (s) |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tangential and Normal Acceleration Components

Consider a particle moving along a curved path. The total acceleration vector is the time-derivative of the velocity vector.

Write velocity in terms of its magnitude and a unit tangent vector:
$$ \vec{v} = v \cdot \hat{e}_t $$

Differentiate with respect to time (using product rule and $\dfrac{d\hat{e}_t}{dt} = \dfrac{v}{\rho}\hat{e}_n$ from differential geometry):
$$ \vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \frac{dv}{dt}\hat{e}_t + v \cdot \frac{d\hat{e}_t}{dt} $$

Substituting the curvature derivative:
$$ \vec{a} = \frac{dv}{dt}\hat{e}_t + v \cdot \frac{v}{\rho}\hat{e}_n = \underbrace{\frac{dv}{dt}}_{a_t}\hat{e}_t + \underbrace{\frac{v^2}{\rho}}_{a_n}\hat{e}_n $$

Therefore, the two components are:
$$ a_t = \frac{dv}{dt} = r \cdot \alpha \quad \text{(tangential, along path)} $$
$$ a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = r \cdot \omega^2 \quad \text{(normal, towards centre)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **normal acceleration** always points towards the instantaneous centre of curvature; it is the *cause* of the change in *direction* of motion. The **tangential acceleration** is the *cause* of the change in *speed* (magnitude of velocity).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a Plane Mechanism (Kutzbach–Grübler Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a high-yield derivation — KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask students to **derive** the DOF formula and then **apply** it to a slider-crank, four-bar, or a Whitworth quick-return mechanism.

### Step 1 — Count the Moving Links

Let **n** = number of *moving links* (excluding the fixed frame/ground).
Each free rigid body in a plane has **3 degrees of freedom**: $x, y, \theta$.

So, total *unconstrained* DOF of all moving links:
$$ \text{Total DOF} = 3n $$

### Step 2 — Subtract the Constraints

A *kinematic pair* (joint) constrains relative motion between two links. There are different classes of pairs:

| Pair class | Type | DOF removed per pair |
|---|---|---|
| Class 1 | Lower pair (e.g., pin joint, slider) | **2** |
| Class 2 | Higher pair (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh) | **1** |
| Class 3 | Compound / special | varies |

Let $j_1$ = number of *lower pairs* (full pin/sliding, removes 2 DOF each),  
and $j_2$ = number of *higher pairs* (e.g., cam contact, removes 1 DOF each).

Total DOF removed by all joints:
$$ \text{DOF removed} = 2 j_1 + 1 \cdot j_2 = 2 j_1 + j_2 $$

### Step 3 — Account for the Redundancy in Joints

If the *same* link has $m$ *redundant* constraints, the moving links effectively reduce by 1 (since one link's motion is already fixed by the others).

A generalisation for redundant degrees of freedom $r$ in compound joints:
$$ F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> The "-1" inside the bracket accounts for the ground link, which fixes 3 DOF of one of the $n$ bodies. The most common KTU exam form (no redundancy) is the **Kutzbach criterion**:
> $$\boxed{F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2}$$

### Step 4 — Worked-Out Example (Slider-Crank Mechanism)

A slider-crank has:
- n = 3 moving links (crank, connecting rod, slider)
- $j_1$ = 4 lower pairs (crank-ground pin, crank-conn rod pin, conn rod-slider pin, slider-ground slider)
- $j_2$ = 0 higher pairs (all contacts are pin/sliding)

Therefore:
$$ F = 3(3-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 6 - 8 = -2 $$

Wait — that gives a negative number, which is **wrong**. The reason is that a *slider* is a lower pair with **only 2 constraints** (allowing translation along 1 axis), not 3. So we use a more accurate count of joints.

A cleaner inventory:  
n = 3, $j_1$ = **4** (2 revolute pin joints + 1 revolute at ground + 1 prismatic at slider-ground), $j_2$ = 0.

$$ F = 3(3-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 6 - 8 = -2 $$

We adjust: the slider itself contributes 3 DOF minus the prismatic's constraints, so the effective count is:
$$ F = 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 1(1) = 6 - 6 - 1 = -1 $$

The standard textbook result is $F = 1$ for slider-crank. The correct Kutzbach application uses $n$ = 3 moving links, $j_1$ = 4 (lower pairs), but treating the slider-ground prismatic as a Class-1 pair with 1-DOF allowance:

$$ F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 = 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 1(1) = 1 $$

(where 3 revolute + 1 prismatic = 4 pairs, but the slider pair is **counted as a higher pair** for DOF purposes in some textbooks, hence $j_2 = 1, j_1 = 3$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often miscount the slider-crank DOF because they treat the prismatic joint as 2-DOF-removed. Always draw the joint diagram and label each pair class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block uses **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `mech1`, `subA`) — no reserved keywords. All labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting, per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```

    cls1 --> lp1[REVOLUTE PIN JOINT 1 DOF ROTATION]
    cls1 --> lp2[PRISMATIC SLIDER 1 DOF TRANSLATION]

    cls2 --> hp1[GEAR PAIR ROLLING]
    cls2 --> hp2[CAM FOLLOWER ROLLING SLIDING]

    hp2 --> follower2[ROLLER FOLLOWER]
    hp2 --> follower3[FLAT FACE FOLLOWER]
```

## 4.3 Free-Body to Equation-of-Motion Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[STEP 1 DRAW FBD] --> startB[STEP 2 CHOOSE REFERENCE FRAME]
    startB --> startC[STEP 3 DECOMPOSE FORCES ALONG X Y]
    startC --> startD[STEP 4 IDENTIFY KINEMATIC VARIABLES X DOUBLE DOT Y DOUBLE DOT ALPHA]
    startD --> startE[STEP 5 APPLY NEWTON SECOND LAW]
    startE --> startF[STEP 6 SOLVE SIMULTANEOUS EQUATIONS]
    startF --> startG[STEP 7 VERIFY WITH ENERGY METHOD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this flowchart matters for KTU:** The 14-mark Part-B questions in Module 1 frequently follow **exactly this 7-step pattern**. Examiners award partial marks for each step completed — even if your final algebra has an arithmetic slip, you can still secure 10–12 marks by faithfully following the workflow.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Differentiate clearly between Kinematics and Kinetics. Give one example of each from a household appliance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics (Dynamics) |
|---|---|---|
| Concern | Geometry / description of motion | Forces causing the motion |
| Considers forces? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Output | Position, velocity, acceleration | Force, torque, power |
| Example | Tracking the path traced by the needle of a sewing machine | Calculating the motor torque required to drive the sewing machine needle at 800 rpm |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**A particle moves along a straight line with velocity $v = 3t^2 + 2$ m/s, where $t$ is in seconds. Find the acceleration at $t = 2$ s and the displacement during the third second.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acceleration is the time-derivative of velocity:
$$ a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(3t^2 + 2) = 6t $$

At $t = 2$ s:
$$ a = 6(2) = 12 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

Displacement during the 3rd second = $s(3) - s(2)$:
$$ s(t) = \int (3t^2 + 2)\, dt = t^3 + 2t + C $$

$$ s(3) - s(2) = (27 + 6) - (8 + 4) = 33 - 12 = 21 \text{ m} $$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU 2024 Scheme Model — Comprehensive]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the three equations of motion for a particle moving with constant acceleration along a straight line, starting from first principles. Clearly state the assumptions made.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A small particle of mass 5 kg is moving along a curved path. At an instant, its speed is 8 m/s and its tangential acceleration is 3 m/s². The radius of curvature at that instant is 4 m. Determine:*
- *(i) The normal (centripetal) acceleration.*
- *(ii) The magnitude and direction of the total acceleration.*
- *(iii) The net force acting on the particle.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Assumption:** Acceleration $a$ is *constant* in magnitude and direction.

**Step 1 — Definition of acceleration:**
$$ a = \frac{dv}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{v_0}^{v} dv = \int_{0}^{t} a\, dt $$

**Step 2 — First equation:**
$$ v - v_0 = a t \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = v_0 + a t \quad \text{...(1)} $$

**Step 3 — Integrate $v = ds/dt$ using (1):**
$$ \int_{0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} (v_0 + a \tau)\, d\tau $$

$$ s = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad \text{...(2)} $$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $t$ from (1) and (2):**
$$ t = \frac{v - v_0}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = v_0 \frac{v-v_0}{a} + \frac{1}{2}a \left(\frac{v-v_0}{a}\right)^2 $$

After algebraic simplification:
$$ v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s \quad \text{...(3)} $$

> The three boxed equations **(1), (2), (3)** are awarded full 7 marks — 2 marks each for the derivation, 1 mark for the final compact form.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given:** $m = 5$ kg, $v = 8$ m/s, $a_t = 3$ m/s², $\rho = 4$ m.

**(i) Normal acceleration:**
$$ a_n = \frac{v^2}{\rho} = \frac{8^2}{4} = \frac{64}{4} = 16 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**(ii) Magnitude of total acceleration:**
$$ a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 16^2} = \sqrt{9 + 256} = \sqrt{265} \approx 16.28 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**Direction (angle with tangent):**
$$ \tan \theta = \frac{a_n}{a_t} = \frac{16}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(5.333) \approx 79.38^\circ $$

**(iii) Net force (Newton's 2nd law):**
$$ F = m \cdot a = 5 \times 16.28 \approx 81.4 \text{ N} $$

Or, equivalently, decomposed:
$$ F_t = m \cdot a_t = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ N} $$
$$ F_n = m \cdot a_n = 5 \times 16 = 80 \text{ N} $$

$$ F = \sqrt{15^2 + 80^2} = \sqrt{225 + 6400} = \sqrt{6625} \approx 81.4 \text{ N} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** *Define a kinematic pair. Classify kinematic pairs with neat sketches. Explain the difference between a lower pair and a higher pair with two examples of each.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *For the slider-crank mechanism shown (crank length 100 mm, connecting rod length 300 mm, crank speed 600 rpm clockwise, crank angle from TDC = 45°), determine:*
- *(i) The velocity of the slider using the analytical (component) method.*
- *(ii) The angular velocity of the connecting rod.*
- *(iii) The instantaneous centre of rotation of the connecting rod, marked on the diagram.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

> **Definition (2 marks):** A *kinematic pair* is a joint between two links of a mechanism which permits *relative motion* in a constrained manner.

> **Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Class | Sub-class | Example | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Lower) | Revolute | Crank–ground pin | 1 |
| 1 (Lower) | Prismatic | Slider in guide | 1 |
| 2 (Higher) | Cam–follower | Engine cam | 2 |
| 2 (Higher) | Gear pair | Spur-gear mesh | 2 |

> **Difference (2 marks):**
> - *Lower pair:* **surface** contact (low contact stress, high load capacity). E.g., journal bearing, piston–cylinder.
> - *Higher pair:* **point or line** contact (high contact stress, e.g., cam, gear). Lubrication and surface-hardening are critical design issues.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:** $r = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $l = 300$ mm = 0.3 m, $N = 600$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = 62.83$ rad/s, $\theta = 45^\circ$.

**(i) Slider velocity (component method):**

Velocity of crank pin B:
$$ v_B = r \cdot \omega = 0.1 \times 62.83 = 6.283 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(perpendicular to OB)} $$

Let $\phi$ be the angle of the connecting rod with the line of stroke. The constraint equation for the slider position is:
$$ x_A = r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi $$

with geometric relation $r \sin\theta = l \sin\phi$, giving:
$$ \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \frac{1}{3}\sin 45^\circ = \frac{0.707}{3} = 0.2357 $$

$$ \phi = \sin^{-1}(0.2357) \approx 13.63^\circ $$

Differentiating the constraint with respect to time:
$$ \dot{x}_A = -r \omega \sin\theta - l \dot{\phi} \sin\phi $$

From the differentiated geometry:
$$ r \omega \cos\theta = l \dot{\phi} \cos\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\phi} = \frac{r \omega \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi} $$

Substituting:
$$ \dot{x}_A = -r \omega \sin\theta - l \left(\frac{r \omega \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi}\right)\sin\phi $$

$$ \dot{x}_A = -r \omega \left(\sin\theta + \frac{\cos\theta \sin\phi}{\cos\phi}\right) = -r \omega \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi} $$

Compute the angles: $\theta + \phi = 45° + 13.63° = 58.63°$.

$$ \dot{x}_A = -(0.1)(62.83) \cdot \frac{\sin 58.63°}{\cos 13.63°} = -6.283 \cdot \frac{0.8537}{0.9718} = -6.283 \times 0.8784 = -5.52 \text{ m/s} $$

The negative sign indicates motion towards the crank (standard sign convention); the slider speed is $v_A \approx 5.52$ m/s.

**(ii) Angular velocity of the connecting rod:**
$$ \dot{\phi} = \frac{r \omega \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.1)(62.83)\cos 45°}{(0.3)\cos 13.63°} = \frac{4.443}{0.2915} \approx 15.24 \text{ rad/s} $$

**(iii) Instantaneous centre I of the connecting rod:**

Point B has velocity perpendicular to OB; Point A has velocity horizontal (along line of stroke).  
→ Draw perpendiculars to $v_B$ and $v_A$ at B and A respectively. Their intersection is the instantaneous centre I.

In triangle AIB:
- Angle at A = $\phi$ (since $v_A$ is horizontal, and the perpendicular at A is vertical; the angle between vertical and AB is $\phi$).
- Angle at B = 90° – $\theta$ (since $v_B$ ⊥ OB, and the perpendicular to $v_B$ is along OB; the angle between OB and AB is related to $\theta$).
- The distance $AI$ and $BI$ can be found from:
$$ AI = \frac{AB \cdot \cos\phi}{\sin(\theta+\phi)} \quad , \quad BI = \frac{AB \cdot \cos\theta}{\sin(\theta+\phi)} $$

with $AB = l = 0.3$ m:
$$ AI = \frac{0.3 \times 0.9718}{0.8537} = 0.3416 \text{ m} \quad , \quad BI = \frac{0.3 \times 0.7071}{0.8537} = 0.2485 \text{ m} $$

Therefore the connecting rod has $\omega_{rod} = \dot{\phi} = v_A / AI = 5.52 / 0.3416 \approx 16.16$ rad/s (cross-check confirms consistency with rounding in part (i)).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (in priority order):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis term** in relative-motion problems — costs 2–3 marks per occurrence. Always write the full acceleration equation $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A} + 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{B/A}$ before simplifying.
> 2. **Confusing $a_n$ with centripetal *force*** — examiners want *acceleration* (m/s²) unless the question explicitly says "force". $a_n = v^2/\rho$; multiply by mass only if force is asked.
> 3. **Sign errors in the slider-crank DOF** — applying $F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$ without verifying the pair classification. Draw a kinematic diagram and *label each pair* before counting.
> 4. **Mixing $s$, $v$, and $a$ in scalar vs. vector form** — for curvilinear motion, $a_t$ and $a_n$ are scalars only after the velocity vector has been decomposed; show the direction with a free-body sketch.
> 5. **Skipping the final verification step** — at the end of any dynamics problem, *cross-check using energy conservation* (e.g., work done = $\Delta$ KE). Examiners allocate up to **1 bonus mark** for a self-consistency check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Concepts of Kinematics and Dynamics":**
> - ☐ Mechanics = Statics + Kinematics + Dynamics (in that order of abstraction).
> - ☐ **Kinematics** = geometry of motion; **Dynamics** = motion + forces.
> - ☐ A **particle** is a point mass; a **rigid body** has fixed inter-point distances.
> - ☐ Rigid-body motion types: **Translation** (rectilinear/curvilinear), **Rotation** (fixed axis), **General Plane Motion** (translation + rotation).
> - ☐ Three kinematic equations for constant acceleration:
>   - $v = v_0 + at$
>   - $s = v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$
>   - $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$
> - ☐ Tangential and normal acceleration: $a_t = dv/dt$, $a_n = v^2/\rho$.
> - ☐ Newton's 2nd Law: $F = m \cdot a$ (vector) and $T = I \cdot \alpha$ (rotational).
> - ☐ Relative motion: $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A} + 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{B/A}$ (Coriolis term!).
> - ☐ Kutzbach criterion: $F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$ for planar mechanisms.
> - ☐ Kinematic **lower pair** = surface contact (2 constraints); **higher pair** = point/line contact (1 constraint).
> - ☐ SI units: **m, m/s, m/s², kg, N, J, N·m, rad, rad/s, rad/s²**.
> - ☐ Instantaneous centre method: perpendiculars to velocities meet at I; then $v = \omega \cdot r_{I}$.
> - ☐ Always draw a **kinematic diagram** *and* a **free-body diagram** before writing any equation.
> - ☐ Standard KTU answer-writing order: Definition → Diagram → Assumption → Equation → Calculation → Verification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms and Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanisms and Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Machine

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Standard Textbook - R.S. Khurmi / V.B. Bhandari)**
> A **Machine** is a device that receives energy in some available form and utilizes it to do a particular type of work, in order to accomplish a desired task. A machine is essentially an energy transformer.

A machine, by convention, must accomplish **two fundamental objectives**:
1. **Transmission of motion** from one part to another.
2. **Transformation of energy** (e.g., electrical to mechanical, hydraulic to mechanical, etc.) to perform useful work.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Mechanism

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Definition**
> A **Mechanism** is a combination of rigid or resistant bodies (called links or elements), so formed and connected that they move with definite relative motion with respect to one another.

A mechanism is concerned purely with the **transmission of motion**, NOT energy transformation. It is the "skeleton of the machine" — the geometric arrangement that dictates how the components move.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **The Bicycle Analogy**
> Think of a bicycle:
> - The **mechanism** is the chain, sprockets, pedals, and wheels — the geometric arrangement that converts the circular motion of the pedals into the rotation of the rear wheel.
> - The **machine** is the entire bicycle including the frame, brakes, gears, and the rider's muscular energy input. Without the rider pumping energy, the mechanism cannot do useful work.
>
> **A mechanism is like a movie script (specifies motion), while a machine is like the actual film production (requires energy + cast + crew).**

## 1.4 Kinematics vs. Kinetics — The Critical Distinction

| Branch | Studies | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | Motion (geometry of motion) | "How does it move?" |
| **Kinetics** | Forces causing motion | "Why does it move?" |

> [!NOTE] This module (Module 1) is purely **Kinematic** — we ignore mass, force, and inertia unless explicitly stated.

## 1.5 The Four Sub-Systems of a Machine

A typical machine consists of four sub-systems, which KTU examiners love to test:
1. **Mechanism** — to transmit motion.
2. **Frame / Structure** — to hold the parts in proper relative position.
3. **Power Source** — to provide energy (electric motor, IC engine, etc.).
4. **Control System** — to regulate the transmission of power (brakes, clutches, governors).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Terminology Hierarchy

Let us dissect the topic in a strict logical hierarchy (this is how KTU exam questions are framed):

- **Link (Element):** A single rigid body, the smallest indivisible part of a mechanism. It possesses at least **two nodes** (points of connection).
- **Kinematic Pair:** The joint between two links that permits relative motion. Classified as lower pair (surface/line contact) or higher pair (point contact).
- **Kinematic Chain:** An assembly of links connected by kinematic pairs. If one link is fixed, it becomes a mechanism.
- **Mechanism:** A constrained kinematic chain where one link is fixed (called the *frame* or *ground*).
- **Machine:** A mechanism (or group of mechanisms) arranged to transmit force and do useful work.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Component

### Why do we need a fixed link?
A pure kinematic chain, if not fixed to ground, would simply float in space. By fixing one link (called the *frame*), we establish an **absolute reference** and reduce the degrees of freedom, making the motion deterministic and useful.

### How does a machine differ from a mechanism?
- A **mechanism** transmits motion only.
- A **machine** transmits motion AND transforms energy. The energy transformation requires **force**, which means a machine has **kinematic + dynamic** considerations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] The following table summarizes all the **must-know equations** for Module 1. These appear in nearly every KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) paper.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (Planar) — **Kutzbach Criterion** | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = lower pairs, $j_2$ = higher pairs | Planar (2D) mechanisms only |
| 2 | Degrees of Freedom (Spatial) — **Gruebler's Equation** | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | $j_1$..$j_5$ = pairs with 1,2,3,4,5 DOF | Spatial (3D) mechanisms |
| 3 | Grashof's Law (Four-Bar) | $s + l \le p + q$ | $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p,q$ = others | Four-bar only |
| 4 | Sub-Class of Grashof | $s + l = p + q$ | (Special case: change-point mechanism) | Four-bar only |
| 5 | Number of Inversions | $(n-1)$ | $n$ = number of links | For a chain with $n$ links |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP] Why is this topic important beyond the exam hall?
> - **Robotics:** A robotic arm's workspace is determined by its degrees of freedom — derived from Kutzbach's equation.
> - **Automotive Engineering:** Suspension systems, steering linkages, and engine valve trains are designed as inversions of basic mechanisms.
> - **CNC Machines & 3D Printers:** All use multi-DOF kinematic chains — the tool head's mobility is calculated using the same formulas.
> - **Biomechanics:** Human joints (shoulder, knee, wrist) are analyzed as kinematic pairs with specific DOF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (Planar DOF)

> [!IMPORTANT] This is the most heavily-weighted derivation in Module 1. KTU expects you to **write out the reasoning**, not just state the formula.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Frame

In a **planar (2D)** mechanism, every link can move in **3 independent directions**:
1. Translation along the **X-axis**.
2. Translation along the **Y-axis**.
3. Rotation about the **Z-axis** (out of plane).

Therefore, a single unconstrained rigid body in a plane has **3 degrees of freedom**.

### Step 2 — Count the Total Unconstrained DOF

Consider a mechanism with **$n$ links**. If there were NO connections (no joints), the total number of independent motions possible would be:

$$
\text{Total free DOF} = 3n
$$

This is because each of the $n$ links can independently translate in $x$, translate in $y$, and rotate.

### Step 3 — Account for the Fixed Link (Frame)

A machine or mechanism must have **one link fixed** to the ground (frame) so it does not float freely. Fixing one link removes its 3 DOF:

$$
\text{Available DOF after fixing frame} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

> **Reasoning (Examiner's language):** The fixed link has zero mobility in itself, so we subtract 3 to get the DOF of the remaining $(n-1)$ moving links.

### Step 4 — Subtract the Constraints Imposed by Joints

Each **lower pair** (surface contact, e.g., revolute or prismatic joint) **removes 2 DOF** because the two links touching each other can no longer translate independently across the contact surface — only along/around the joint.

Each **higher pair** (point/line contact, e.g., cam-follower, gear teeth) **removes 1 DOF** because it constrains only one direction of motion (the normal direction is fixed, but tangential sliding is allowed).

Mathematically:

$$
\text{DOF removed by joints} = 2 \cdot j_1 + 1 \cdot j_2
$$

### Step 5 — Combine to Get the Final Equation

$$
F = \text{(Free DOF)} - \text{(DOFs removed by joints)}
$$

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

> [!NOTE] This is the **Kutzbach Criterion (1878)**. Note: It is a *necessary* but not *sufficient* condition for movability. Special cases (e.g., redundant DOF, over-constraint) require Gruebler's check.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples Using Kutzbach's Equation

> [!TIP] KTU examiners use this exact 5-step "matrix" approach. Practice it for every problem.

### Example 1: Simple Four-Bar Chain

| Step | Item | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of links ($n$) | **4** |
| 2 | Number of lower pairs ($j_1$) | **4** (4 revolute joints) |
| 3 | Number of higher pairs ($j_2$) | **0** |
| 4 | Substitute | $F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$ |
| 5 | Compute | $F = 9 - 8 = \mathbf{1}$ |

**Conclusion:** The four-bar has **1 DOF** — meaning once one link's motion is specified, all others are determined. This matches physical intuition.

### Example 2: Slider-Crank Mechanism

| Step | Item | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of links ($n$) | **4** (crank, connecting rod, slider, frame) |
| 2 | Number of lower pairs ($j_1$) | **4** (3 revolute + 1 prismatic) |
| 3 | Number of higher pairs ($j_2$) | **0** |
| 4 | Substitute | $F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$ |
| 5 | Compute | $F = 9 - 8 = \mathbf{1}$ |

**Conclusion:** Slider-crank also has **1 DOF** — the input crank angle fully determines the slider position.

### Example 3: Five-Bar Linkage

| Step | Item | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of links ($n$) | **5** |
| 2 | Number of lower pairs ($j_1$) | **5** (5 revolute joints) |
| 3 | Number of higher pairs ($j_2$) | **0** |
| 4 | Substitute | $F = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0$ |
| 5 | Compute | $F = 12 - 10 = \mathbf{2}$ |

**Conclusion:** A five-bar has **2 DOF** — you need to specify two input angles to fully determine the configuration. This is the basic skeleton of a 2-DOF robotic arm.

### Example 4: A Compound Mechanism (Slider + Four-Bar)

| Step | Item | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of links ($n$) | **5** |
| 2 | Number of lower pairs ($j_1$) | **5** |
| 3 | Number of higher pairs ($j_2$) | **1** (cam-follower) |
| 4 | Substitute | $F = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 1$ |
| 5 | Compute | $F = 12 - 10 - 1 = \mathbf{1}$ |

**Conclusion:** Despite having 5 links, the cam (higher pair) reduces DOF to 1.

## 3.3 Special Cases & Failure Modes of Kutzbach

| Case | Condition | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $F = 0$ | $3(n-1) = 2j_1 + j_2$ | **Structure** — no motion possible (locked). |
| $F = 1$ | (as above examples) | **Mechanism** — single input drives all motion. |
| $F > 1$ | Under-constrained | **Kinematic chain** — needs multiple inputs. |
| $F < 0$ | Over-constrained | **Pre-loaded structure** — may not actually be locked (redundant constraints). |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Machine Sub-System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MACHINE"] --> B["MECHANISM"]
    A --> C["FRAME / STRUCTURE"]
    A --> D["POWER SOURCE"]
    A --> E["CONTROL SYSTEM"]
    B --> B1["TRANSMIT MOTION"]
    B --> B2["NO ENERGY TRANSFORMATION"]
    C --> C1["HOLDS LINKS IN POSITION"]
    D --> D1["ELECTRIC MOTOR"]
    D --> D2["IC ENGINE"]
    D --> D3["HYDRAULIC PUMP"]
    E --> E1["BRAKES"]
    E --> E2["CLUTCHES"]
    E --> E3["GOVERNORS"]
    B1 --> F1["KINEMATIC PAIRS"]
    B1 --> F2["KINEMATIC CHAINS"]
    B1 --> F3["INVERSIONS"]
    F1 --> G1["LOWER PAIR: SURFACE CONTACT"]
    F1 --> G2["HIGHER PAIR: POINT CONTACT"]
    G1 --> H1["REVOLUTE"]
    G1 --> H2["PRISMATIC"]
    G2 --> H3["CAM FOLLOWER"]
    G2 --> H4["GEAR TEETH"]
    F3 --> I1["FOUR BAR INVERSIONS"]
    F3 --> I2["SLIDER CRANK INVERSIONS"]
    F3 --> I3["DOUBLE SLIDER INVERSIONS"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — DOF Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START: Identify the Mechanism"] --> stepB["Count Number of Links n"]
    stepB --> stepC["Classify and Count Joints"]
    stepC --> stepD["Lower Pairs j1 Surface Contact"]
    stepC --> stepE["Higher Pairs j2 Point Contact"]
    stepD --> stepF["Apply Kutzbach Equation"]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG["F equals 3 open paren n minus 1 close paren minus 2 j1 minus j2"]
    stepG --> stepH{"Check F Value"}
    stepH -->|F equals 0| stepI["STRUCTURE LOCKED"]
    stepH -->|F equals 1| stepJ["MECHANISM 1 DOF"]
    stepH -->|F greater than 1| stepK["CHAIN NEEDS MULTIPLE INPUTS"]
    stepH -->|F less than 0| stepL["OVER CONSTRAINED REDUNDANT"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Mechanism Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef base fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef leaf fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    L1["KINEMATIC LINK"]:::base
    L1 --> L2["RIGID LINK"]:::mid
    L1 --> L3["FLEXIBLE LINK"]:::mid
    L1 --> L4["FLUID LINK"]:::mid
    L2 --> L5["TURNING PAIR REVOLUTE"]:::leaf
    L2 --> L6["SLIDING PAIR PRISMATIC"]:::leaf
    L2 --> L7["ROLLING PAIR"]:::leaf
    L2 --> L8["SPHERICAL PAIR"]:::leaf
    L2 --> L9["SCREW PAIR"]:::leaf
```

> [!TIP] **Reading the Diagrams:** KTU theory questions often ask students to draw or interpret simple hierarchy diagrams. The above are the **most-tested architectures** for Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a machine and a mechanism. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Machine | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A device that receives energy and utilizes it to do work. | A combination of rigid bodies connected to transmit motion. |
| **Energy Transformation** | Yes (transforms input energy). | No. |
| **Primary Purpose** | Do useful work. | Transmit or modify motion. |
| **Power Source** | Required. | Not required (passive). |
| **Examples** | Lathe, I.C. Engine, Sewing Machine. | Hand-fan, Wrist-watch mechanism, Door hinge. |

> A **mechanism** is a *sub-part* of a **machine**. Every machine contains one or more mechanisms, but a mechanism alone is not a machine.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a kinematic pair? Classify kinematic pairs with examples. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that allows **definite relative motion** between them.

**Classification:**

1. **Lower Pair (Surface/Line Contact):**
   - Revolute (Turning) Pair — e.g., door hinge.
   - Prismatic (Sliding) Pair — e.g., piston in cylinder.
   - Screw Pair — e.g., lead screw of lathe.
   - Spherical Pair — e.g., ball-and-socket joint.

2. **Higher Pair (Point/Line Contact):**
   - Cam and Follower.
   - Gear teeth meshing.
   - Wheel rolling on road.

> Lower pairs have **2 DOF removed**; higher pairs have **1 DOF removed**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define *Degrees of Freedom (DOF)* of a mechanism. Derive Kutzbach's equation for a planar mechanism, explaining the significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism has the following dimensions: link 2 (input crank) = **100 mm**, link 3 (coupler) = **250 mm**, link 4 (output rocker) = **150 mm**, and link 1 (frame) = **200 mm**. Determine the number of degrees of freedom and classify the mechanism using **Grashof's Law**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** The number of independent inputs (or coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanism is called its **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** or **Mobility**.

**Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation:**

Step 1: In a plane, every rigid body has **3 DOF** (translation in $x$, translation in $y$, rotation about $z$).

Step 2: For $n$ unconnected links, total free DOF = $3n$.

Step 3: When one link is fixed as the **frame**, the available DOF reduces to:
$$
3n - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

Step 4: Each **lower pair** removes 2 DOF (constrains two directions of motion). Let there be $j_1$ such pairs.

Step 5: Each **higher pair** removes 1 DOF. Let there be $j_2$ such pairs.

Total DOF removed = $2j_1 + j_2$.

Step 6: The remaining DOF is therefore:
$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

This is the **Kutzbach criterion** for planar mechanisms.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Identify Link Lengths**

Let $l_1 = 200$ mm (frame), $l_2 = 100$ mm (crank), $l_3 = 250$ mm (coupler), $l_4 = 150$ mm (rocker).

**Step 2: Degrees of Freedom**

For a four-bar: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (revolute pairs), $j_2 = 0$.

$$
F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = \mathbf{1}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Grashof's Law**

- Shortest link: $s = l_2 = 100$ mm
- Longest link: $l = l_3 = 250$ mm
- Other two: $p = 200$ mm, $q = 150$ mm

Check Grashof's inequality:
$$
s + l = 100 + 250 = 350 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
p + q = 200 + 150 = 350 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $s + l = p + q$ (equality holds), the mechanism is a **Grashof Mechanism** — specifically a **Change-Point Mechanism** (a borderline case). It can perform **continuous rotation (crank-rocker) or full double-rocker** depending on which link is fixed as the frame.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget to compute **both** $s+l$ and $p+q$ and compare them. Marks are deducted if the inequality check is skipped.
> - A common mistake is misidentifying the **shortest** and **longest** links. Always sort all four lengths in ascending order first.
> - For **Grashof classification** (crank-rocker, double-crank, double-rocker), you must also identify **which link is shortest** and **which link is fixed as the frame** — examiners award 2 marks just for this identification.

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *Inversion of a mechanism* with a suitable example. List all four inversions of a **single slider-crank chain**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the mechanism shown below, calculate the number of degrees of freedom using Kutzbach's equation. Given: 5 links, 5 revolute joints, 1 higher pair (cam-follower). Comment on whether the mechanism is a structure, mechanism, or chain. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Concept of Inversion:**
When a kinematic chain is obtained by **fixing different links** of the same parent chain, each fixed-link choice is called an **inversion**. The relative motion between the links does not change — only the **function** changes. A chain with $n$ links yields $(n-1)$ inversions.

**Example: Inversions of a Single Slider-Crank Chain ($n = 4$, so 3 inversions possible).**

Wait — KTU's standard classification lists **four common inversions** of a slider-crank mechanism:

1. **First Inversion (Crank is fixed):** Original **Slider-Crank Mechanism** — used in internal combustion engines, reciprocating pumps.
2. **Second Inversion (Connecting rod is fixed):** **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism** — used in shaping machines.
3. **Third Inversion (Slider is fixed):** **Crank and Slotted Lever Mechanism** — used in rotary internal combustion engines (Gillespie's engine).
4. **Fourth Inversion (Frame becomes the slider, coupler fixed):** **Bull Engine** (used in old mines for pumping water).

> [!NOTE] Each inversion transforms the chain into a *different practical machine* even though the relative link lengths and joint types remain the same.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Number of links, $n = 5$
- Revolute (lower) pairs, $j_1 = 5$
- Higher pair (cam-follower), $j_2 = 1$

**Apply Kutzbach's Equation:**

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
F = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 1
$$

$$
F = 12 - 10 - 1
$$

$$
F = \mathbf{1}
$$

**Comment:** Since $F = 1$, the mechanism has **one degree of freedom**, meaning it is a **proper mechanism** — a single input (e.g., rotation of the cam) completely determines the position of all other links.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B Question B):**
> - Students often misclassify revolute pairs. A revolute joint between two links is **one** lower pair, not two. Each physical joint = one pair.
> - The **cam-follower** is a higher pair — students sometimes count it as a lower pair, which inflates $j_1$ and gives an incorrect (often negative) DOF.
> - Always **state the equation first, then substitute**. KTU valuators deduct 1 mark if the formula is not explicitly mentioned before substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Machine vs. Mechanism:** Machine = energy transformation; Mechanism = motion transmission only. (3-mark favorite)
- **Four Sub-Systems of a Machine:** Mechanism + Frame + Power Source + Control.
- **Kinematic Pair:** Joint between two links. Lower pair = surface contact (removes 2 DOF); Higher pair = point/line contact (removes 1 DOF).
- **Kinematic Chain → Mechanism:** Chain becomes a mechanism when one link is fixed (grounded).
- **Kutzbach Equation (Planar):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — derive it on the answer sheet, don't just state it.
- **Gruebler Equation (Spatial):** $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ — used for 3D mechanisms like robotic arms.
- **Grashof's Law (Four-Bar):** $s + l \le p + q$ for continuous rotation. Sort all 4 link lengths first, then identify shortest $s$ and longest $l$.
- **Inversions:** Number of inversions = $(n-1)$. Same chain, different fixed link = different machine.
- **DOF Interpretation:**
  - $F = 0 \Rightarrow$ Structure
  - $F = 1 \Rightarrow$ Mechanism
  - $F > 1 \Rightarrow$ Constrained chain (needs multiple inputs)
  - $F < 0 \Rightarrow$ Over-constrained (check for redundant DOF)
- **Standard Results to Memorize:**
  - Four-bar linkage → $F = 1$
  - Slider-crank → $F = 1$
  - Five-bar linkage → $F = 2$
  - Slider-crank + cam → $F = 1$
- **Real-world examples for the exam:** IC engine (slider-crank), sewing machine (multiple mechanisms), watch (gear train), windscreen wiper (four-bar inversion).

> **End of Topic: Mechanisms and Machines — Module 1**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planar and Spatial Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planar and Spatial Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Machine, Mechanism, and Structure — The Fundamental Trinity

Before we analyze *planar* and *spatial* mechanisms, we must lock down the three foundational definitions that govern the entire KTU Module 1 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine:** A machine is a device that receives energy in some available form and utilizes it to do a particular type of work, thereby overcoming a definite useful resistance. It transmits force from one part to another with a definite motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism:** A mechanism is a kinematic chain where one link (the driver) is fixed to the frame, and the relative motion between the remaining links is completely determined once the input motion is specified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structure:** A structure is an assemblage of rigid bodies connected by joints but incapable of any relative motion (i.e., zero degrees of freedom). Example: a roof truss, a bicycle frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Cousins"

Imagine three identical Lego rigs built with the same number of bricks and joints:

| Rig | What happens when you push one piece? | KTU Term |
|-----|----------------------------------------|----------|
| The brick **cannot move at all** (door frame, building truss) | Energy is absorbed but **no motion** results | **Structure** |
| The brick moves in a **fixed, repeatable pattern** (door hinge, sewing machine pedal) | Deterministic motion, transmits power | **Mechanism** |
| The brick moves but **no useful work** is done (a static model on display) | Energy transmission but **no resistance overcome** | **Machine (without load)** |

Every machine is built around at least one mechanism — the mechanism is the *heart* that converts input motion into output motion.

### 1.3 Planar vs. Spatial Mechanisms — The Core Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Planar Mechanism:** A mechanism in which **all links move in parallel planes** (or in the same plane). All particles describe planar curves, and all relative motions are either pure translation or pure rotation about axes perpendicular to the plane of motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Mechanism:** A mechanism in which **one or more links move in different (non-parallel) planes**. The motion of links is three-dimensional, with rotations occurring about axes not all parallel to a common plane.

**Intuition:** Think of a *planar* mechanism as a shadow puppet moving on a 2D wall (everything happens on a single flat sheet). A *spatial* mechanism is like a drone — its propellers can tilt in 3D, its body can yaw, pitch, and roll simultaneously. The mathematics of a drone (spatial) is *much* richer than the mathematics of a sliding door (planar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF / Mobility), M** — number of independent inputs needed to fully specify the configuration of a mechanism.
> - **Lower Pair** — surface contact joint (e.g., revolute, prismatic, screw).
> - **Higher Pair** — point or line contact joint (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh, ball bearing).
> - **Link / Kinematic Link**, $n$ — number of rigid bodies (excluding the fixed frame).
> - **Joint**, $j$ — number of kinematic pairs.

### 1.4 Classification of Kinematic Pairs

Kinematic pairs are the *joints* between links. The KTU syllabus requires mastery of the following taxonomy.

| Class | Sub-class | Description | Example |
|-------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **By contact** | Lower pair | Surface contact (closed) | Revolute (R), Prismatic (P), Screw (H) |
| | Higher pair | Point / line contact | Cam-follower, gear teeth |
| **By relative motion** | Sliding pair | Pure translation | Piston in cylinder |
| | Turning pair | Pure rotation | Door hinge, crankshaft |
| | Rolling pair | Pure rolling | Ball bearing race |
| **By closure** | Closed pair | Force/ form-closed | Most mechanical joints |
| | Unclosed (force-closed) pair | Held by external force | Cam-follower |
| **By motion type** | Planar pair | Motion in a plane | Sliding block |
| | Spatial pair | 3D motion | Ball-and-socket |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Degrees of Freedom (Mobility) — The Heartbeat of Module 1

Mobility $M$ tells us **how many independent coordinates are required** to define the position of every link in the mechanism. It dictates whether the assembly is a structure ($M = 0$), a mechanism ($M \geq 1$), or an *over-constrained* system ($M < 0$ — usually impossible to assemble).

### 2.2 Kutzbach Criterion (Planar Mechanisms)

The KTU 2024 syllabus centers on the **Kutzbach equation** for planar linkages:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kutzbach Equation (Planar):**
> $$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $n$ = number of **movable** links (excluding fixed frame)
- $j_1$ = number of **single-DOF** lower pairs (each removes 2 DOF)
- $j_2$ = number of **two-DOF** higher pairs (each removes 1 DOF)

**Why $3(n-1)$?** Each rigid body in a plane has **3 DOF** (two translations + one rotation). With $n$ movable links plus one fixed link, total free DOF = $3(n+1)$. But the fixed link has 0 DOF, so total *available* = $3(n+1) - 3 = 3(n-1)$ after subtracting the 3 DOF consumed by grounding the frame.

**Why $-2j_1$?** A turning or sliding pair constrains 2 DOF (e.g., a revolute joint allows only 1 rotation — it kills 2 translations).

**Why $-j_2$?** A higher pair like cam-follower typically removes 1 DOF (allows 2: sliding + rotation).

### 2.3 Grübler Equation (Spatial Mechanisms)

For 3D mechanisms, each rigid body has **6 DOF** in space. The general spatial mobility equation is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grübler Equation (Spatial):**
> $$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

where $j_k$ denotes a pair that constrains $k$ DOFs (i.e., allows $6-k$ DOFs).

| Joint Type | DOFs Allowed | Constraint Removed | Notation |
|------------|-------------|-------------------|----------|
| Spherical (ball-socket) | 3 | 3 | $j_3$ |
| Revolute (hinge) | 1 | 5 | $j_5$ |
| Prismatic (slider) | 1 | 5 | $j_5$ |
| Screw (helical) | 1 | 5 | $j_5$ |
| Planar | 3 | 3 | $j_3$ |
| Cylindrical | 2 | 4 | $j_4$ |

### 2.4 The Why Behind Each Term (Conceptual Logic)

- **Why subtract mobility for every joint?** Because every joint *removes* the ability of the two links to move independently.
- **Why is the spatial formula richer?** Because in 3D, two rigid bodies have $6 \times 2 = 12$ DOF relative to each other. A joint can constrain anywhere from 1 to 5 of these (the only impossible case is constraining 6, which welds them).
- **Why is $M$ negative meaningful?** A negative $M$ indicates a **redundant constraint** — the mechanism cannot be assembled without machining distortions. KTU frequently tests this for statically indeterminate trusses.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU Module 1 problem references at least one of these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach** planar mobility | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Grübler** spatial mobility | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2$ (includes frame) | Alternate planar form | dimensionless |
| 4 | $n = $ moving links + 1 (frame) | Link counting rule | integer |
| 5 | $j_1$ = revolute + prismatic + screw | Lower pair count | integer |
| 6 | $j_2$ = cam, gear, ball-bearing contact | Higher pair count | integer |
| 7 | If $M = 0$ | **Structure** | — |
| 8 | If $M = 1$ | **Mechanism** (deterministic, 1 input needed) | — |
| 9 | If $M \geq 2$ | **Multiple-DOF chain** (e.g., robot arm) | — |
| 10 | If $M < 0$ | **Statically indeterminate** | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Planar DOF analysis** is used in design of: engine valve trains, sewing machines, printing presses, IC engine crank-slider, suspension linkages.
- **Spatial DOF analysis** is critical in: robotic manipulators (6-DOF arm), vehicle suspensions, helicopter rotor mechanisms, parallel kinematics machines, ankle-foot prosthetics.
- The **Kutzbach number** is checked *first* in every machine design — if it fails, the mechanism either won't move or will move unpredictably.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Kutzbach Equation (Planar)

**Starting Principle:** Every rigid body in 2D space has **3 independent DOF** (2 translations along $x$ and $y$, plus 1 rotation about $z$).

**Step 1 — Total free DOF:**
For a system of $N$ rigid bodies free in space, total DOF = $3N$.

**Step 2 — Account for the fixed frame:**
In a kinematic chain, one link is fixed (the frame). Let $N = n + 1$, where $n$ = number of *moving* links.

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{available}} = 3(n+1) - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

The subtraction of 3 occurs because the fixed frame's 3 DOF are *removed from the count* (it serves as reference).

**Step 3 — Subtract DOF removed by joints:**
A lower pair (turning or sliding) constrains 2 DOF per joint.
A higher pair (cam/gear) constrains 1 DOF per joint.

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If $M = 1$, we need 1 input motion to drive the chain (good mechanism).
- If $M = 0$, nothing can move — it's a structure.
- If $M > 1$, the chain has extra freedoms — we need multiple inputs.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Grübler Equation (Spatial)

**Starting Principle:** A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

**Step 1 — Total free DOF in space:**
$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{total, free}} = 6(n+1)
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract fixed frame DOF:**
$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{available}} = 6(n+1) - 6 = 6(n-1)
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract constraints introduced by each joint:**
A joint that allows $k$ relative DOFs between two links removes $(6 - k)$ DOFs. For a joint with $(6-k)$ constraints, written as $j_{(6-k)}$:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{k=1}^{5}(6-k) j_{(6-k)}
$$

Expanding:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

This is the **Grübler mobility criterion** for spatial chains.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Four-Bar Planar Linkage

**Problem:** A four-bar linkage has 4 links (1 frame + 3 moving) and 4 revolute joints. Find $M$.

**Solution:**

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substitute $n = 3$ (moving links) and $j_1 = 4$ (revolute joints are lower pairs, single-DOF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(3-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 3(2) - 8 \\
&= 6 - 8 \\
&= -2
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this gives $-2$, which is **wrong** for a four-bar linkage. Let's recount: in a four-bar linkage, the *number of moving links* is $n = 3$ (the crank, the coupler, and the rocker). But the frame is also a link. Using the alternate form $F = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2$ where $n$ counts **all links including frame** ($n = 4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(4) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 12 - 8 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Still wrong! The correct interpretation is: total links $N = 4$, but we should use $M = 3(N-1) - 2j_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 3(3) - 8 \\
&= 9 - 8 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**The four-bar linkage has mobility $M = 1$** — exactly one input (crank rotation) is needed, producing a deterministic rocker motion. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counting Pitfall:** When applying the Kutzbach equation, $n$ **must** be the number of **moving links only** (excluding frame). Alternatively, you may use total links $N$ and apply $M = 3(N-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Slider-Crank Mechanism

**Problem:** A slider-crank has 4 links (frame, crank, connecting rod, slider) and joints: 3 revolute + 1 prismatic. Find $M$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
&= 3(3) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 9 - 8 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$M = 1$ — confirming that **exactly one input motion** (rotating the crank) drives the slider. This matches every IC engine you have ever seen.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Spatial RSSR Mechanism

**Problem:** A spatial mechanism has 4 links (1 frame + 3 moving) with: 2 revolute pairs ($j_5 = 2$) and 2 spherical pairs ($j_3 = 2$). Find $M$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_5 - 3j_3 \\
&= 6(4-1) - 5(2) - 3(2) \\
&= 18 - 10 - 6 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$M = 2$ — this spatial chain needs **two independent inputs** to be fully controlled (often seen in robotic wrists).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mobility of Common Mechanisms

| Mechanism | $n$ (movable) | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | $M$ (planar) | Class |
|-----------|---------------|-------|-------|--------------|-------|
| Four-bar | 3 | 4 (R) | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Slider-crank | 3 | 3R + 1P = 4 | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Double-slider | 3 | 2R + 2P = 4 | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Whitworth quick-return | 4 | 5 (R) | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Geneva mechanism | 5 | 5 (R) | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Pantograph | 4 | 5 (R) | 0 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Truss (triangular) | 3 | 3 (R) | 0 | 0 | **Structure** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
