## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pattern Compliance:** All questions below strictly follow KTU 2024 ESE scheme — Part A (3 marks, ~50 words), Part B (14 marks, two sub-parts of 7 marks each, internal choice).

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define kinematic inversion. Why is it important in the design of mechanisms?**

**Model Answer:**
Kinematic inversion is the method of obtaining different mechanisms by fixing different links of a kinematic chain successively. It is important because the same abstract chain, when inverted, yields multiple useful mechanisms (e.g., beam engine, Whitworth QRRM) for varied industrial applications like shaping machines, pumps, and locomotives — maximizing design economy.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for application insight.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Name the inversion of a four-bar chain in which the *coupler link* is fixed. Give one practical application.**

**Model Answer:**
When the *coupler link* (middle link) is fixed, the four-bar chain becomes the **Coupling Rod of a Locomotive**. It is used to transmit motion from the driving crank to the driven crank of locomotive wheels while accommodating the parallel offset between the wheel axles.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **inversions of a four-bar kinematic chain**. Mention the practical application of each inversion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
A four-bar chain has four links connected by four revolute pairs. Since the chain has `F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1` DOF, fixing any one link gives a useful 1-DOF mechanism.

**Step 2 — List all four inversions (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

| Inversion | Fixed Link | Practical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Beam Engine (Crank & Lever) | Input crank | Pumps, workshop machines |
| 2. Coupling Rod of Locomotive | Coupler (middle link) | Steam locomotive drive |
| 3. Whitworth Quick-Return | Output crank | Shapers and planers |
| 4. Crank & Slotted-Lever | Slider (or oscillating link) | Shapers, milling machines |

**Step 3 — Detailed sketch and motion explanation (2 marks):**
For each inversion, draw the schematic: pivot locations (A, B, C, D), the crank, coupler, follower, and ground. For the Crank-and-Slotted-Lever, show the slot that allows the crank pin to slide while the lever pivots.

---

**(b)** A **Crank and Slotted Lever mechanism** has crank radius `r = 100 mm` and slotted-lever distance from pivot to crank centre `L = 350 mm`. Determine the **quick-return ratio (QRR)** and the **angles of cutting and return strokes**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (3 marks):**
$$
\sin\alpha = \frac{r}{L+r} = \frac{100}{350+100} = \frac{100}{450} = 0.2222
$$
$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.2222) = 12.84^\circ
$$
$$
\sin\beta = \frac{r}{L-r} = \frac{100}{350-100} = \frac{100}{250} = 0.4000
$$
$$
\beta = \sin^{-1}(0.4000) = 23.58^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Cutting and return angles (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Cutting angle } = 2\beta = 2 \times 23.58^\circ = 47.16^\circ
$$
$$
\text{Return angle } = 2\alpha = 2 \times 12.84^\circ = 25.68^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Quick-Return Ratio (2 marks):**
$$
\text{QRR} = \frac{\text{Cutting angle}}{\text{Return angle}} = \frac{47.16^\circ}{25.68^\circ} = \mathbf{1.836}
$$

The mechanism spends 1.836 times longer on the cutting stroke than on the return — a typical QRR for shapers.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (Alternative)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Elliptical Trammel** as an inversion of a **double slider-crank chain**. Show mathematically that the tracing point traces an **ellipse**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Describe the inversion (2 marks):**
In a double slider-crank chain, fixing the link with the two slider guides (perpendicular x and y axes) yields the **Elliptical Trammel**. A rigid bar of length `L` connects two sliders, one on each axis. A point `P` at distance `a` from one slider and `b` from the other acts as the tracer.

**Step 2 — Sketch description (1 mark):**
- x-axis slider at distance `a` from point `P`
- y-axis slider at distance `b` from point `P`
- `a + b = L`
- Crank pin `A` at origin makes angle $\theta$ with x-axis

**Step 3 — Derivation (4 marks):**
From geometry of the rigid bar inclined at angle $\theta$ with the x-axis:
$$
x = a\cos\theta, \quad y = b\sin\theta
$$
$$
\cos\theta = \frac{x}{a}, \quad \sin\theta = \frac{y}{b}
$$
Applying Pythagorean identity:
$$
\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1
$$
$$
\boxed{\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1}
$$

This is the canonical ellipse equation, confirming point `P` traces an ellipse with semi-major axis `a` and semi-minor axis `b`. For `a = b`, the trace degenerates into a circle.

---

**(b)** A **Scotch Yoke mechanism** has crank radius `r = 50 mm` and rotates at `N = 240 rpm`. Calculate the **maximum velocity** and **maximum acceleration** of the slider. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of crank (1 mark):**
$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 25.13 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Slider displacement equation (1 mark):**
$$
x = r\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity derivation (2 marks):**
$$
v = -r\omega\sin\theta
$$
Maximum velocity occurs at $\sin\theta = \pm 1$, i.e., $\theta = 90°$ or $270°$:
$$
v_{max} = r\omega = 0.050 \times 25.13 = \mathbf{1.257 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration derivation (2 marks):**
$$
a = -r\omega^2\cos\theta
$$
Maximum acceleration occurs at $\cos\theta = \pm 1$, i.e., $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$:
$$
a_{max} = r\omega^2 = 0.050 \times (25.13)^2 = 0.050 \times 631.65 = \mathbf{31.58 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**
Both velocity and acceleration follow pure sinusoidal patterns, characteristic of simple harmonic motion — making the Scotch yoke ideal for force pumps where uniform pressure delivery is essential.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Inversion Identification (2 marks lost per wrong answer):** Many students confuse the **Whitworth QRRM** (input crank fixed, output crank rotates) with the **Coupling Rod of Locomotive** (coupler fixed). Always *physically trace which link is bolted to the ground* before naming the inversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Sign Convention in QRR Formula:** The quick-return ratio is **always greater than 1** for any genuine QRRM. If you obtain a value < 1, you have swapped cutting and return angles. Re-read the problem statement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Skipping the "Why Inverse" Explanation:** KTU examiners award **1 mark for stating the practical use**. A common 14-mark question asks *"Explain the inversion with its application"* — students who skip the application lose 1–2 marks without realising.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — DOF Verification:** Always *verify* $F = 1$ in the parent chain before declaring it a mechanism. KTU frequently asks: *"Is the inverted chain a mechanism? Justify."* A chain with $F = 0$ is a *structure*, not a mechanism, even if it has fixed links.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Oldham's Coupling Misconception:** Students often think Oldham's coupling introduces *variable* velocity ratio. It doesn't — both shafts rotate at the **same constant angular velocity**; only the lateral offset between the shafts is accommodated by the middle floating disc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Kinematic Inversion = Fixing successive links of a kinematic chain**
- [x] **Four-bar chain gives 4 inversions** (Beam Engine, Locomotive Coupler, Whitworth QRRM, Crank-and-Slotted-Lever)
- [x] **Single slider-crank gives 4 useful inversions** (Reciprocating Engine, Rotary IC Engine, Oscillating Cylinder, Bull Engine)
- [x] **Double slider-crank gives 3 useful inversions** (Elliptical Trammel, Scotch Yoke, Oldham's Coupling)
- [x] **Gruebler's Formula must always yield F = 1** for a mechanism
- [x] **Whitworth QRR** formula: $\text{QRR} = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$
- [x] **Crank-Slotted-Lever QRR** derived from $\sin\alpha = \frac{r}{L+r}$, $\sin\beta = \frac{r}{L-r}$
- [x] **Locomotive Coupler velocity ratio** is **NOT constant**: $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{\sin\beta}{\sin\alpha}$
- [x] **Scotch Yoke produces SHM**: $x = r\cos\theta$, $v_{max} = r\omega$, $a_{max} = r\omega^2$
- [x] **Elliptical Trammel trace**: $\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$
- [x] **Oldham's Coupling** has constant $\omega_1 = \omega_2$ despite parallel offset
- [x] **Quick-Return Ratio is always > 1** — if you get < 1, swap cutting and return angles
- [x] **Always state the practical application** for full marks on KTU 14-mark questions
- [x] **Draw neat sketches** with labelled pivot points (A, B, C, D) — examiners award **1–2 marks** purely for a labelled diagram

---

topics.title: Four-Bar Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Four-Bar Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Four-Bar Chain** (also called a **Four-Bar Linkage** or **Quadric Crank Chain**) is a fundamental planar kinematic chain consisting of **four rigid links** connected in a closed loop by **four revolute (turning/pin) joints**. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, the four-bar chain forms the foundational building block for almost every planar mechanism studied in the kinematics of machinery.

The four links are conventionally named as follows:
1. **Fixed Link / Frame (d)**: The link that is stationary with respect to the ground (also called the *ground link* or *fixed pivot*).
2. **Input Link / Crank (a)**: The link that receives motion from the driver (prime mover).
3. **Coupler / Connecting Rod (b)**: The floating link that connects the input and output links.
4. **Output Link / Follower (c)**: The link that transmits the resultant motion to the machine tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Check:** A chain becomes a *mechanism* when one of its links is fixed. When the **shortest link (d)** is fixed in a Grashof chain, the resulting mechanism is called a **Crank-Crank (Double-Crank) Mechanism**. When the link **adjacent to the shortest** is fixed, it becomes a **Crank-Rocker Mechanism**. When the link **opposite to the shortest** is fixed, it becomes a **Double-Rocker (Drag-Link)** mechanism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

**Think of a bicycle pedaling system on a flat road.** Your leg pushes the pedal (input crank) in a complete circle. That motion is transferred through the chain (coupler) to the rear wheel sprocket (output). The bicycle's main triangular frame acts as the *ground link* — stationary with respect to the pedaling action. Four links, four hinges, one closed loop — that is essentially a four-bar chain in action.

Another beautiful real-world example is the **windshield wiper** of a car. The wiper arm does not rotate a full 360°; it oscillates back and forth. This is achieved using a four-bar chain where the shortest link is fixed and the link opposite to it is constrained to oscillate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "chain"?** A *kinematic chain* is an assemblage of links and joints that does not require a fixed link to define its motion. A *mechanism* is obtained by fixing one link of a kinematic chain. So strictly speaking, a "four-bar chain" is a kinematic chain with 4 bars. When we fix one bar, we get a *four-bar mechanism*. KTU exam questions often use these terms interchangeably — but for full marks, always clarify which link is fixed in your answer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a planar four-bar chain using **Gruebler's Equation**:

$$\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where $n$ = number of links, $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints), $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints). For a four-bar chain: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$ → **DOF = 1**. This is highlighted in **bold** as the standard metric for the chain.

- **Standard metric units** for all link lengths: **millimeters (mm)** in design drawings, **meters (m)** in theoretical derivations.
- **Linkage Ratio (LR)**: Often taken in the range **1 ≤ LR ≤ 5** for good mechanical design.
- **Transmission Angle (μ)**: The angle between the coupler and the follower, ideally between **40° and 90°** for efficient force transmission.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Four-Bar Chain "Thinks"

To understand the four-bar chain logically, imagine you are given **four sticks** of different lengths and **four pins**. You pin them end-to-end in a closed loop on a table. Now pin one stick to the table (this becomes your *frame*). If you wiggle the structure:

- The input link rotates about its pivot on the frame.
- Through the coupler, this motion is transmitted to the output link.
- Depending on the *relative lengths* of the four links, the output may rotate fully, oscillate, or rock back and forth.

The mathematical magic that governs *what kind of motion* the output undergoes is encapsulated in **Grashof's Law** (covered in Section 3 derivation).

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Inversions (KTU Board Favorite)

When we fix a *different* link of the same four-bar chain each time, we obtain different mechanisms. KTU frequently asks: *"List the inversions of a four-bar chain and give one practical application of each."* Master this:

| Inversion Name | Link Fixed | Output Motion | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Beam Engine (Crank & Lever)** | Smallest link (crank) | Lever (follower) oscillates | Old stationary steam engines; bull engines used in Kerala coir industry |
| **Coupling Rod (Double-Crank)** | Coupler (connecting rod) | Both end links rotate fully | Parallel-rod coupling used in locomotive wheels |
| **Whitworth Quick-Return** | Smallest link (crank) | Slotted lever oscillates | Shaping machines, slotting machines |

> [!TIP]
> **Inversion Trick:** Remember the rule — *fixing a link changes the relative motion of all other links but never changes the relative lengths between them*. Hence, Grashof's classification remains the same; only the *type of output* changes.

## 2.3 Grashof's Classification — The Heart of the Four-Bar Chain

**Franz Grashof** (1871) established the criterion that determines whether continuous rotation, oscillation, or a special "change-point" behavior occurs in a four-bar chain. For a four-bar chain with link lengths $S$ (shortest), $L$ (longest), $P$ and $Q$ (the other two):

**Grashof's Inequality (for at least one link to rotate fully):**

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

The behavior then depends on **which link is fixed**:

| Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism | Type of Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Shortest link (S)** | Crank-Crank / Double-Crank | Both end links rotate fully (e.g., coupling rods of locomotives) |
| **Link adjacent to S** | Crank-Rocker | One link (S) rotates; the other end oscillates (e.g., windshield wiper, sewing machine needle drive) |
| **Link opposite to S** | Double-Rocker (Drag-Link) | Both end links oscillate; can change to crank-rocker mid-motion (change-point mechanism) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Grashof Case:** When $S + L > P + Q$, **no link can rotate fully**. All mechanisms become *double-rockers* (or *change-point* mechanisms if equality holds in a special way). This is a high-yield KTU question topic.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Gruebler's equation for planar mechanisms | $DOF \geq 1$ for useful mechanism |
| 2 | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Grashof's inequality (necessary for full rotation) | Equality = change-point; strict = Grashof; violation = Non-Grashof |
| 3 | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2cd}\right)$ | Angle of output link (Freudenstein's configuration) | Used in displacement analysis |
| 4 | $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2}{2bc}\right)$ | Transmission angle (force transmission quality) | Ideal: $40° \leq \mu \leq 90°$ |
| 5 | $LR = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ | Linkage Ratio | $1 \leq LR \leq 5$ (good design) |
| 6 | $v_A = \omega_2 \cdot a$ | Velocity of crank tip (input link) | $a$ = crank length, $\omega_2$ = input angular velocity |
| 7 | $\dfrac{v_B}{v_A} = \dfrac{\omega_4}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{a \sin\theta_3}{c \sin\theta_4}$ | Velocity ratio (Klein's construction result) | Useful in IC engine connecting rod analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing **transmission angle** in the exam, do not use the symbol $\mu$ alone. KTU evaluators prefer the full phrase *"transmission angle between coupler and follower"* to fetch full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Four-bar chains are the **silent workhorses of mechanical engineering**. From the **rocker arms in motorcycle valve trains** (Hero Honda, Royal Enfield — popular in Kerala), to the **suspension systems of bicycles** (BSA, Firefox), to **surgical robots** in medical engineering — the four-bar mechanism is everywhere. The design principle is the same: convert one type of motion (rotary) into another (oscillatory, intermittent, or quick-return) reliably and with minimum wear.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degrees of Freedom using Gruebler's Equation

**Statement:** Show that a four-bar chain has **1 degree of freedom (DOF = 1)**.

**Given:**
- Number of links, $n = 4$
- Number of lower pairs (1 DOF revolute joints), $j_1 = 4$
- Number of higher pairs, $j_2 = 0$

**Step 1 — Write Gruebler's equation for a planar mechanism:**

$$DOF = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values for the four-bar chain:**

$$DOF = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify inside the parentheses first:**

$$DOF = 3 \times 3 - 8$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication and subtraction:**

$$DOF = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Result:** $\boxed{DOF = 1}$. This means a **single input** (e.g., one rotating crank) is sufficient to produce a **deterministic, fully-defined output** motion. This is why the four-bar chain is the simplest *useful* planar mechanism.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grashof's Law (Geometric Proof)

**Statement:** For a four-bar chain to have at least one link capable of **continuous full rotation**, prove that the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links must be **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two links.

**Given:** Four links of lengths $a, b, c, d$ arranged in a closed loop. Let $S$ be the shortest and $L$ the longest; $P$ and $Q$ the remaining two links.

**Step 1 — Consider the four-bar loop with vertices at $A, B, C, D$.** When one link (say $AD = d$) is the fixed frame, the other three form a moving triangle. For the moving link opposite the frame to rotate fully, the *opposite* pivot must be able to reach all positions around the fixed pivot.

**Step 2 — Apply the triangle inequality.** For any triangle with sides $a, b, c$ to close:

$$a + b \geq c$$

For the four-bar mechanism to assemble, when one link is stretched out maximally (collinear with frame), the diagonal must equal the sum of the two adjacent links. When folded back, the diagonal equals the difference.

**Step 3 — Maximum and minimum reach of the moving pivot.**

Let the fixed frame be of length $d$. The moving pivot opposite to it (call it $B$) traces a circle of radius $a$ (crank) around fixed pivot $A$. For $B$ to be able to *cross over* the line $AD$ extended, the distance from $B$ to $D$ must be able to reach $0$ — i.e., the sum of the two links reaching $B$ (which are $a$ and $b$) must exceed or equal the path $D$ can cover.

**Step 4 — Write the assembly condition for both extreme positions:**

- **Folded position (closest):** $BD_{\min} = \vert b - a \vert$
- **Extended position (farthest):** $BD_{\max} = b + a$

**Step 5 — For continuous rotation, the moving pivot $B$ must be able to encircle pivot $D$.** This requires the *longest* possible distance to $D$ to be at least the *frame length*, AND the *shortest* distance to be less than the frame length:

$$b + a \geq d \quad \text{and} \quad \vert b - a \vert \leq d$$

**Step 6 — Combining all four links symmetrically,** the only link that is "special" is the shortest and longest. The condition for **at least one full rotation** reduces to:

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

where $S$ = shortest link, $L$ = longest link, $P$ and $Q$ = the other two.

**Step 7 — Case analysis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } S + L < P + Q &: \text{ Strict Grashof — full rotation possible.} \\
\text{If } S + L = P + Q &: \text{ Change-point — the mechanism can flip between Grashof and Non-Grashof behavior at one singular position.} \\
\text{If } S + L > P + Q &: \text{ Non-Grashof — no link can complete full rotation; only oscillation is possible.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{S + L \leq P + Q}$ is the necessary and sufficient condition (Grashof's Law).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission Angle (Quality of Force Transmission)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the transmission angle $\mu$ between the coupler and the follower in a four-bar chain.

**Given:** Four-bar chain with links $a$ (input), $b$ (coupler), $c$ (output/follower), $d$ (frame). $\mu$ is the angle between link $b$ and link $c$ at the joint connecting them.

**Step 1 — Apply the law of cosines to the triangle formed by $a, b, c, d$.**

In the loop $ABCD$, applying cosine rule at the joint between coupler and follower:

$$a^2 = b^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2cd\cos\theta_2 - 2bc\cos\mu$$

where $\theta_2$ is the input crank angle. Rearranging for $\cos\mu$:

$$2bc\cos\mu = b^2 + c^2 + d^2 - a^2 - 2cd\cos\theta_2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the transmission angle:**

$$\cos\mu = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2}{2bc} + \frac{d\cos\theta_2}{b}$$

**Step 3 — For a *quick estimation* of the *worst-case* transmission angle** (used in KTU design problems), consider the extreme positions of the crank where the mechanism is folded or extended. In these positions, $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = 180°$, simplifying the expression to:

$$\mu_{\min} = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^2 + c^2 - (a+d)^2}{2bc}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Acceptable design range:** For good force transmission, the transmission angle should be **close to 90°**. The empirically accepted KTU/standard textbook range is:

$$40° \leq \mu \leq 90°$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2}{2bc}\right)}$ is the transmission angle, and it should be designed to lie between **40°** and **90°** for efficient operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Booster:** Always mention **why** we need $\mu \geq 40°$. If $\mu$ is too small, the cosine approaches 1, and the force component along the coupler becomes very small, leading to *self-locking* and *jamming* of the mechanism. This is a frequent 2-mark follow-up question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Grashof vs. Non-Grashof

| Property | Grashof Chain | Non-Grashof Chain |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical condition | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | $S + L > P + Q$ |
| Number of full-rotation links | At least 1 (depending on fixed link) | Zero (no full rotation possible) |
| Output motion type | Rotary or oscillatory (selectable) | Always oscillatory |
| Best application | Crank-rocker, double-crank mechanisms | Rocker-rocker mechanisms, walking robot legs |
| Transmission angle | Usually favorable | Often poor near dead-center positions |
| KTU question weightage | **High (8-12 marks in ESE)** | Medium (3-5 marks) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard Four-Bar Chain — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a generic four-bar chain, showing how the input motion propagates through the kinematic loop to the output. (Note: Mermaid is used here as a *block architecture map* rather than a physical drawing, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback rule.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fixed Frame Link d]:::frameLink
    B[Input Link a - Crank]:::crankLink
    C[Coupler Link b - Connecting Rod]:::couplerLink
    D[Output Link c - Follower Rocker]:::rockerLink
    P1((Revolute Joint A)):::jointNode
    P2((Revolute Joint B)):::jointNode
    P3((Revolute Joint C)):::jointNode
    P4((Revolute Joint D - Fixed Pivot)):::jointNode
    SRC[Prime Mover / Driver Torque]:::driver
    OUT[Output Motion - Oscillatory or Rotary]:::output

    SRC -->|rotates| B
    B ---|pin joint| P1
    B ---|pin joint| P2
    P2 ---|rigid bar| C
    C ---|pin joint| P3
    P3 ---|rigid bar| D
    D ---|pin joint| P4
    P1 ---|rigid bar| A
    P4 ---|grounded| A
    D --> OUT

    classDef frameLink fill:#222831,stroke:#00ADB5,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef crankLink fill:#FF5722,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef couplerLink fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef rockerLink fill:#3F51B5,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef jointNode fill:#FFC107,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef driver fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef output fill:#00BCD4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Fixed Frame (d)** is shown in dark teal — it stays stationary.
- The **Crank (a)** receives rotational input from the prime mover.
- The **Coupler (b)** floats in space, transferring motion without being fixed to the ground.
- The **Rocker (c)** produces the output motion (oscillatory in this configuration).
- **Revolute joints** (yellow circles) at the four vertices allow pure rotation between adjacent links.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Grashof's Classification Logic

This flowchart captures the decision logic a designer follows when classifying a four-bar chain based on link lengths.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Four Link Lengths Given: a, b, c, d]):::startNode
    IDENTIFY[Identify S, L, P, Q: Sort links in ascending order]:::stepNode
    CHECK{S + L versus P + Q ?}:::decisionNode

    SGR[S + L less than P + Q - STRICT GRASHOF]:::grashofNode
    EGR[S + L equals P + Q - CHANGE POINT]:::changeNode
    NGR[S + L greater than P + Q - NON GRASHOF]:::nongrashofNode

    FIXS[FIX Shortest Link: Double Crank Mechanism]:::crankcrank
    FIXADJ[FIX Link Adjacent to Shortest: Crank Rocker Mechanism]:::crankrocker
    FIXOPP[FIX Link Opposite Shortest: Double Rocker Drag Link]:::doublerocker

    NONGRFIX[FIX any link: ALL mechanisms become Double Rocker]:::nongrashoffix

    CHANGEFIX[FIX Shortest: Double Crank with Singular Toggle Position]:::changefix

    RESULT([Output Mechanism Type Determined]):::resultNode

    START --> IDENTIFY
    IDENTIFY --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|Strictly less| SGR
    CHECK -->|Equal| EGR
    CHECK -->|Strictly greater| NGR

    SGR --> FIXS
    SGR --> FIXADJ
    SGR --> FIXOPP
    FIXS --> RESULT
    FIXADJ --> RESULT
    FIXOPP --> RESULT

    EGR --> CHANGEFIX
    CHANGEFIX --> RESULT

    NGR --> NONGRFIX
    NONGRFIX --> RESULT

    classDef startNode fill:#673AB7,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef stepNode fill:#009688,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FF9800,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef grashofNode fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef changeNode fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef nongrashofNode fill:#F44336,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef crankcrank fill:#03A9F4,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef crankrocker fill:#3F51B5,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef doublerocker fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef nongrashoffix fill:#795548,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef changefix fill:#CDDC39,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resultNode fill:#00BCD4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Inversions Mapping — Functional Equivalence Across Applications

```mermaid
graph LR
    FOURBAR[Base Four-Bar Chain with 4 links a, b, c, d]:::base

    INV1[Inversion 1: Fix a - Crank Crank]:::inv1
    INV2[Inversion 2: Fix b - Coupling Rod]:::inv2
    INV3[Inversion 3: Fix c - Double Rocker]:::inv3
    INV4[Inversion 4: Fix d - Crank Rocker]:::inv4

    APP1[App: Parallel Rod Drive Locomotive Wheels]:::app1
    APP2[App: Beam Engine of Old Steam Pump]:::app2
    APP3[App: Walking Robot Leg Mechanism]:::app3
    APP4[App: Windshield Wiper Sewing Machine Drive]:::app4

    FOURBAR --> INV1
    FOURBAR --> INV2
    FOURBAR --> INV3
    FOURBAR --> INV4

    INV1 --> APP1
    INV2 --> APP2
    INV3 --> APP3
    INV4 --> APP4

    classDef base fill:#1A237E,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef inv1 fill:#0277BD,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef inv2 fill:#00695C,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef inv3 fill:#558B2F,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef inv4 fill:#BF360C,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef app1 fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef app2 fill:#6A1B9A,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef app3 fill:#AD1457,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef app4 fill:#00838F,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Key Insight from Diagram 4.3:** Notice that fixing *different* links of the *same* four-bar chain gives rise to *four distinct mechanisms* with vastly different industrial applications. This is the genius of inversion as a design philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a kinematic chain. How is a four-bar chain classified as a mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *kinematic chain* is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints such that each link has a definite relative motion with respect to the others. A four-bar chain is a kinematic chain with 4 links and 4 revolute pairs. **It is classified as a mechanism (specifically a 1-DOF mechanism) when one of its four links is fixed to the ground**, and the resulting motion of the other three links becomes deterministic and useful for performing work. By Kuebler's/Gruebler's equation, $DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1$, confirming it is a 1-DOF mechanism.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**State Grashof's law. What happens when the shortest and longest links are collinear?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Grashof's Law* states that for a four-bar chain, **at least one link can rotate continuously (complete a full 360°) if and only if the sum of the lengths of the shortest (S) and longest (L) links is less than or equal to the sum of the lengths of the other two links (P and Q)**, i.e., $S + L \leq P + Q$. When the shortest and longest links become collinear, the four-bar chain reaches a *toggle* or *dead-center position*. In this configuration, the mechanism experiences **singular motion** — the input link's effort produces zero useful motion at the output, and the mechanism can change its behavior from Grashof to Non-Grashof. This is called a **change-point mechanism** and occurs when $S + L = P + Q$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) under the 2024 scheme mandate an **internal choice** in Part B for Module 1 questions. Students attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are designed for 14 marks with sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** A four-bar chain ABCD has links of lengths AB = 100 mm, BC = 250 mm, CD = 200 mm, and DA = 300 mm. Determine whether this chain satisfies Grashof's condition. If yes, classify all possible mechanisms obtained by fixing each link one at a time. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the same four-bar chain as in part (a), if link AB is fixed, identify the resulting mechanism and sketch the extreme positions of link CD. Calculate the transmission angle at the position where AB and BC are collinear. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Sort the four link lengths in ascending order.**

Links: $a = AB = 100$ mm, $b = BC = 250$ mm, $c = CD = 200$ mm, $d = DA = 300$ mm.

Sorted: $S = 100$ mm, $P = 200$ mm, $Q = 250$ mm, $L = 300$ mm.

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's inequality:**

$$S + L = 100 + 300 = 400 \text{ mm}$$

$$P + Q = 200 + 250 = 450 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$S + L = 400 < 450 = P + Q$$

Hence, the chain **satisfies Grashof's condition strictly**. It is a *Grashof chain* with at least one link capable of full rotation.

**Step 4 — Classify by fixing each link:**

- **Fix AB (100 mm) — shortest link** → **Crank-Crank (Double-Crank) mechanism** — both CD and BC can rotate fully.
- **Fix BC (250 mm) — adjacent to shortest** → **Crank-Rocker mechanism** — AB rotates fully; CD oscillates.
- **Fix CD (200 mm) — adjacent to shortest** → **Crank-Rocker mechanism** — AB rotates fully; BC oscillates.
- **Fix DA (300 mm) — opposite to shortest** → **Double-Rocker (Drag-Link) mechanism** — both AB and CD oscillate.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the mechanism.** With AB (100 mm) fixed, the shortest link is fixed. Hence, by the table in Section 2.3, this is a **Double-Crank (Crank-Crank) mechanism**. Both BC and CD can complete full 360° rotation.

**Step 2 — Extreme positions of link CD.** With AB fixed at 100 mm and BC = 250 mm rotating around B, the pivot C traces a circle of radius 250 mm around B. Link CD = 200 mm connects C to D (fixed pivot on AB extended line). The extreme positions of D and CD are determined by the collinear positions of AB and BC:

- **Position 1 (BC folded over AB):** $B$ and $C$ are on the same side; CD extends to the right of C.
- **Position 2 (BC extended away from AB):** $C$ is at maximum distance from $D$; CD points in the opposite direction.

**Step 3 — Calculate the transmission angle at the AB-BC collinear position.**

In the four-bar loop, the transmission angle $\mu$ is the angle between the coupler BC and the follower CD at joint C. Applying the law of cosines to the triangle formed by $A$, $C$, and $D$ when $AB$ and $BC$ are collinear:

The effective distance $AC$ becomes either $AB + BC = 350$ mm (extended) or $\vert BC - AB \vert = 150$ mm (folded).

**Case 1 — Extended (AC = 350 mm):**

$$\cos\mu = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - AC^2}{2bc} = \frac{250^2 + 200^2 - 350^2}{2 \times 250 \times 200}$$

$$\cos\mu = \frac{62500 + 40000 - 122500}{100000} = \frac{-20000}{100000} = -0.2$$

$$\mu = \cos^{-1}(-0.2) \approx 101.5°$$

**Case 2 — Folded (AC = 150 mm):**

$$\cos\mu = \frac{250^2 + 200^2 - 150^2}{100000} = \frac{62500 + 40000 - 22500}{100000} = \frac{80000}{100000} = 0.8$$

$$\mu = \cos^{-1}(0.8) \approx 36.87°$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The transmission angle varies between **36.87° and 101.5°** during the cycle. Since the minimum value of 36.87° is **slightly below 40°**, the design is **borderline acceptable**; for a robust mechanism, the shortest link AB should be slightly increased or DA slightly decreased.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the various inversions of a four-bar chain. List any two practical applications for each inversion. (7 marks)

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism has links of lengths 50 mm, 100 mm, 150 mm, and 200 mm. Identify the type of mechanism obtained when: (i) the 50 mm link is fixed; (ii) the 100 mm link is fixed; (iii) the 200 mm link is fixed. Justify your answer using Grashof's law. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition of Inversion:** When a kinematic chain is modified by fixing different links successively, each resulting mechanism is called an *inversion* of the original chain. The relative lengths of the links do not change, but the type of output motion changes.

**Inversion 1 — Beam Engine (Crank and Lever Mechanism):**
- **Fixed link:** Smallest link (crank, $a$).
- **Output:** A lever (follower) oscillates about a fixed pivot.
- **Application 1:** Old horizontal stationary steam engines used in Kerala's coir and rice mills.
- **Application 2:** Water pumping engines for agricultural irrigation.

**Inversion 2 — Coupling Rod (Double-Crank Mechanism):**
- **Fixed link:** The coupler or connecting rod.
- **Output:** Both end links rotate fully at equal or proportional speeds.
- **Application 1:** Locomotive drive coupling rods (used in Indian Railways).
- **Application 2:** Parallel shaft synchronization in industrial machinery.

**Inversion 3 — Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism:**
- **Fixed link:** Smallest link.
- **Output:** A slotted lever oscillates, producing quick-return motion suitable for shaping machines.
- **Application 1:** Shaping machine tool in mechanical workshops.
- **Application 2:** Slotting machines for cutting keyways.

**Inversion 4 — Crank-Rocker (Oscillating Cylinder Mechanism):**
- **Fixed link:** Link adjacent to the shortest.
- **Output:** One link rotates fully; the other oscillates.
- **Application 1:** Windshield wiper of automobiles.
- **Application 2:** Sewing machine needle drive mechanism.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Sort the four link lengths.**

Links: 50 mm, 100 mm, 150 mm, 200 mm.

$$S = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad Q = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Check Grashof's condition:**

$$S + L = 50 + 200 = 250 \text{ mm}$$
$$P + Q = 100 + 150 = 250 \text{ mm}$$

$$S + L = P + Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Change-Point Mechanism}$$

**Step 3 — Case-by-case identification:**

**(i) When the 50 mm link is fixed (S is fixed):**
- Since $S + L = P + Q$ exactly, the mechanism is at a **change-point**. Theoretically, both end links (100 mm and 200 mm) can rotate fully, but the mechanism will experience a **toggle position** where the motion can lock. In practice, this is treated as a **Double-Crank (Crank-Crank) mechanism** with a singular toggle.

**(ii) When the 100 mm link is fixed (P, adjacent to S):**
- The shortest link is **not** fixed, but the link *adjacent* to it is fixed. With equality condition met, this is a **Crank-Rocker mechanism** at the change-point — the 50 mm link can theoretically rotate, but the 200 mm link will oscillate. Near the toggle position, the mechanism may switch behavior.

**(iii) When the 200 mm link is fixed (L, opposite to S):**
- The longest link is fixed. This is a **Double-Rocker (Drag-Link) mechanism** at the change-point. Both the 50 mm and 100 mm links will oscillate, and at the exact toggle position, the mechanism may undergo a sudden flip in motion direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "change-point" with "non-Grashof":** Students often classify a change-point mechanism as Non-Grashof. This is **wrong** — it is a *boundary case* of Grashof. Full marks are awarded only when the student mentions "boundary case" or "change-point."
> 2. **Forgetting to sort the links:** Many students apply Grashof's law without sorting $S$ and $L$ first. This leads to wrong classification. Always sort in ascending order.
> 3. **Mixing up Crank-Crank with Crank-Rocker:** Remember — *Crank-Crank* arises only when the **shortest** link is fixed in a Grashof chain. *Crank-Rocker* arises when the link **adjacent** to the shortest is fixed.
> 4. **Skipping the transmission angle discussion:** Even if the question doesn't explicitly ask, mentioning the transmission angle range (40°–90°) in your answer can earn you 1-2 grace marks.
> 5. **Using wrong units:** Always keep link lengths in **mm** for design, **m** for theoretical derivations. Mixing units is a guaranteed 0.5 mark cut.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four-Bar Chain** = 4 links, 4 revolute joints, 1 DOF (by Gruebler's equation).
- **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ is *necessary* and *sufficient* for at least one link to rotate fully.
- **Three Inversions (Kerala Board High-Yield):** Beam Engine, Coupling Rod, Whitworth Quick-Return. Always state the *fixed link* and *one real-world application* for each.
- **Fixing the shortest link** → **Double-Crank (Crank-Crank)**.
- **Fixing a link adjacent to the shortest** → **Crank-Rocker**.
- **Fixing the link opposite to the shortest** → **Double-Rocker (Drag-Link)**.
- **Non-Grashof chains** ($S + L > P + Q$) → No link can rotate fully; output is always oscillatory.
- **Change-point chains** ($S + L = P + Q$) → Boundary case; behavior is sensitive to manufacturing tolerances.
- **Transmission Angle $\mu$** should lie between **40° and 90°** for good force transmission.
- **Linkage Ratio (LR)** should be between 1 and 5 for practical, compact designs.
- **Freudenstein's Equation** is the algebraic tool to compute the output angle from the input angle — remember the formula: $K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$, where $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are constants based on link ratios.
- **Always sort links** into S, P, Q, L before applying Grashof's law — this is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks on this topic.
- **Mention Grashof's law in words AND formula** in every answer — the KTU evaluator allocates 1 separate mark for the verbal statement.
- **Use clean diagrams** (sketches with labeled lengths $a, b, c, d$) — a well-labeled diagram fetches 1-2 grace marks even if the algebra has minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slider Crank Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Slider Crank Mechanisms # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slider–Crank Mechanism (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **slider–crank mechanism** is a kinematic linkage obtained by the **inversion of a four-bar chain** in which one of the turning pairs is replaced by a **sliding (prismatic) pair**. It comprises four links: a fixed link (frame), a **crank** (rotating link), a **connecting rod (coupler)**, and a **slider (piston)**. The slider reciprocates linearly along a fixed straight-line path while the crank rotates continuously. **Key terminology used in KTU exam papers:** | Term | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | Crank | **OA** | Rotates fully (360°) about fixed pivot O | | Connecting rod | **AB** | Length *l*, oscillates + translates | | Slider / Piston | **B** | Pure reciprocation along line of stroke | | Crank length | **r** | Distance OA | | Crank angle | **θ** | Angle OA makes with line of stroke (from TDC) | | Connecting rod angle | **φ** | Angle AB makes with line of stroke | | Stroke length | **2r** | Distance travelled by slider (TDC → BDC) | | Offset | **e** | Perpendicular distance between crank centre and slider path | | Length ratio | **n = l/r** | Reciprocating ratio (typically 3 to 4) | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Bicycle Pedal Analogy 🚲** > Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle. Your foot pushing the pedal is the **crank pin (A)** rotating in a circle. Your leg is the **connecting rod (AB)**, hinged at the hip (slider pivot) — but here, instead of the leg pumping, imagine the *pedal axle moves up and down* through a vertical slot on the frame. That vertical motion is the **slider (B)**. The pedal axle traces a perfect circle, but the *effective* point at the frame connection oscillates up and down — exactly what a piston does inside an engine cylinder. > > The **connecting rod transfers force AND motion** from the rotating crank to the reciprocating slider, *converting rotation into oscillation* (or vice versa in pumps and compressors). ## 1.3 Real-World Applications - **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** — petrol/diesel car engines - **Reciprocating Compressors & Pumps** — water pumps, air compressors - **Sewing Machines, Printing Presses, Shapers, Slotter machines** - **Crankshaft–piston assembly of all two-stroke and four-stroke engines** - **Whitworth quick-return and crank-and-slotted-lever mechanisms** (used in shaping machines) > [!NOTE] > The **slider–crank** is *the* most industrially important mechanism in the world — without it, no car, no compressor, no shaping machine could operate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Slider–Crank Mechanisms The slider–crank chain is obtained by **three inversions** of the basic four-bar chain, giving rise to three distinct kinematic configurations: | Inversion | Configuration | Fixed Link | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1st | **In-line Slider–Crank** | Frame (slider path through crank centre) | IC engine, reciprocating pump | | 2nd | **Offset Slider–Crank** | Frame (slider path offset by *e*) | Offset cylinder engines | | 3rd | **Rotating slider / Crank & Slotted Lever** | Coupler link | Whitworth, Shaper machines | ## 2.2 Geometric Setup (Inline Slider–Crank, e = 0) Consider fixed pivot **O** (crank centre), crank **OA = r**, connecting rod **AB = l**, slider **B** reciprocates on the line of stroke through **O**. At any instant, let: - θ = crank angle from TDC (top dead centre) - φ = connecting rod angle from line of stroke **Triangle OAB analysis using Sine Rule:** $$\frac{OA}{\sin B} = \frac{AB}{\sin O} = \frac{OB}{\sin A}$$ $$\frac{r}{\sin \phi} = \frac{l}{\sin \theta} = \frac{x}{\sin(\theta + \phi)}$$ From the first equality: **sin φ = (r/l) sin θ** — the *fundamental constraint equation*. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the *master reference* for any KTU 2024 problem on slider–crank. Memorise the constraint equation and the three approximate kinematic expressions. ### A. Exact Equations (inline, e = 0) | Quantity | Expression | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Displacement x (slider position from O)** | $x = r \cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}$ | Exact, includes square root | | **Displacement y (from TDC)** | $y = r(1-\cos\theta) + l(1-\cos\phi)$ | $y_{max} = 2r$ at θ = 180° | | **Velocity of slider** | $v = -r\omega \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi}$ | Angular velocity of crank = ω | | **Acceleration of slider** | $a = -r\omega^2 \left[\dfrac{\cos(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi} + \dfrac{r}{l}\cdot\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\cos^3\phi}\right]$ | Exact expression | ### B. Approximate Equations (using l/r = n ≫ 1) Expanding $\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ via binomial: $$l\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{r^2}{l^2}\sin^2\theta} \;\approx\; l\left(1 - \tfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l^2}\right) = l - \tfrac{r^2}{4l}(1-\cos 2\theta)$$ | Approximate Equation | Final Form | |---|---| | **Displacement y** | $y \approx r(1-\cos\theta) + \dfrac{r}{4n}(1-\cos 2\theta)$ | | **Velocity v** | $v \approx r\omega \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | | **Acceleration a** | $a \approx r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | ### C. Offset Slider–Crank (e ≠ 0) | Quantity | Expression | |---|---| | **Slider displacement (exact)** | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - (e - r\sin\theta)^2}$ | | **Connecting rod angle** | $\sin\phi = \dfrac{e - r\sin\theta}{l}$ | | **Stroke length** | Still $2r$ (independent of offset *e*) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **stroke length is ALWAYS 2r**, regardless of the offset *e*. The offset only shifts the *mid-stroke position* of the slider and creates **asymmetry** in forward and return strokes. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **IC Engine design:** Acceleration equation is critical for computing *inertia force* $F_i = ma$ and balancing the reciprocating mass. - **Quick Return Mechanisms (QRM):** Used in shaping machines, slotting machines, and capstans to save time on the idle (return) stroke. Two classical QRMs: 1. **Crank & Slotted Lever (CSL) mechanism** — Whitworth QRM 2. **Whitworth Quick-Return mechanism** (double crank + slotted lever) - **Mechanical Advantage:** In slider–crank, the toggle position (θ + φ = 90°) gives a theoretically *infinite* mechanical advantage — basis of toggle-jack, rock crushers, knuckle-joint presses. > [!TIP] > **KTU Hot Topic (Dec 2022, June 2023):** A common 14-mark problem asks to derive the *velocity and acceleration of the slider* using the sine-rule approach and to draw the relevant crank–connecting rod geometry. The approximate equations are *expected to be quoted* in the model answer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Exact Displacement of the Slider **Given:** Crank radius *r*, connecting rod length *l*, crank angle *θ* from TDC, slider at point B, crank centre O. **Step 1 — Geometry Setup.** Place O at the origin. The line of stroke lies along the x-axis. Crank pin A is at: $$A = (r\cos\theta,\ r\sin\theta)$$ **Step 2 — Position of slider B.** Since B lies on the x-axis: $B = (x, 0)$. The distance AB = *l*: $$(x - r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2 = l^2$$ **Step 3 — Expand the squared term.** $$x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta = l^2$$ Using $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$: $$x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2 = l^2$$ **Step 4 — Solve the quadratic in x.** $$x^2 - 2r\cos\theta \cdot x + (r^2 - l^2) = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula (taking positive root for the slider in front of the crank): $$x = \frac{2r\cos\theta + \sqrt{4r^2\cos^2\theta - 4(r^2 - l^2)}}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify under the square root.** $$4r^2\cos^2\theta - 4r^2 + 4l^2 = 4(l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}$$ **Step 6 — Displacement from TDC.** The slider is at maximum distance $(r + l)$ from O at TDC (θ = 0). Displacement *y* from TDC: $$y = (r + l) - x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + l - \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$ $$y = r(1-\cos\theta) + l\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{r^2}{l^2}\sin^2\theta}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** At θ = 0° → y = 0 (slider at TDC). At θ = 180° → y = 2r (slider at BDC). ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Approximate Displacement Using Binomial Expansion For a long connecting rod ($l \gg r$), let $n = l/r$. Then: $$\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} = l\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{\sin^2\theta}{n^2}}$$ Apply binomial expansion $(1 - x)^{1/2} \approx 1 - \tfrac{x}{2} - \tfrac{x^2}{8} - \cdots$ keeping first two terms: $$\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} \approx l\left(1 - \tfrac{\sin^2\theta}{2n^2}\right) = l - \tfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l}$$ Use $\sin^2\theta = \tfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$: $$\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} \approx l - \tfrac{r^2}{4l}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$ Substitute into the displacement from TDC: $$y \approx r(1-\cos\theta) + l - l + \tfrac{r^2}{4l}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$ $$\boxed{y \approx r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r}{4n}(1 - \cos 2\theta)}$$ > [!NOTE] > This **approximate form** is the one KTU expects you to use in numerical problems because it allows simple differentiation for velocity and acceleration. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity of the Slider (Exact) Differentiate $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ with respect to time: $$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\sin\theta \cdot \dot\theta + \frac{1}{2\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} \cdot (-2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta) \cdot \dot\theta$$ Let $\dot\theta = \omega$. Then: $$v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}$$ Factor $-r\omega\sin\theta$: $$v = -r\omega\sin\theta\left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$$ Now use the constraint $\sin\phi = (r/l)\sin\theta$, which gives $\cos\phi = \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}/l$. The bracket becomes: $$1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi} = \frac{l\cos\phi + r\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi}$$ Combine with $-r\omega\sin\theta$: $$v = -r\omega\sin\theta \cdot \frac{l\cos\phi + r\cos\theta}{l\cos\phi}$$ Using sine addition formula: $r\sin\theta\cos\phi + r\cos\theta\sin\phi = r\sin(\theta + \phi)$. But $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$, so: $$r\cos\theta\sin\phi + l\cos\phi\sin\theta = ?$$ Recognise: $r\sin(\theta+\phi) = r\sin\theta\cos\phi + r\cos\theta\sin\phi$. With $r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$: $$= l\sin\phi\cos\phi + r\cos\theta\sin\phi = \sin\phi(l\cos\phi + r\cos\theta)$$ So: $$v = \frac{-r\omega \cdot \sin\phi(l\cos\phi + r\cos\theta)}{l\cos\phi\sin\theta} \cdot \sin\theta$$ But $\sin\theta/\sin\phi = l/r$ (from constraint), so $\sin\theta = (l/r)\sin\phi$. Then: $$\boxed{v = -r\omega \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The magnitude $|v| = r\omega \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$. The sign tells us the *direction* of motion (positive = away from crank centre, negative = towards it). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Acceleration of the Slider (Approximate) Differentiate the approximate velocity $v = r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ with respect to time: $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r\omega\left(\cos\theta \cdot \dot\theta + \frac{1}{2n}\cos 2\theta \cdot 2\dot\theta\right)$$ With $\dot\theta = \omega$: $$\boxed{a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$ where $n = l/r$. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU university exams for the inertia force calculation: $F_i = m \cdot a = m \cdot r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$. ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mechanical Advantage in Toggle Position For the inline slider–crank, the *instantaneous mechanical advantage* (IMA) between force on slider $F_B$ and tangential force on crank $F_T$: $$\text{IMA} = \frac{F_B}{F_T} = \frac{l\cos\phi}{r\sin(\theta+\phi)}$$ **Toggle position:** $\theta + \phi = 90°$ ⟹ $\sin(\theta+\phi) = 1$. In this configuration, the connecting rod and crank are **perpendicular**, and the slider is momentarily stationary. The IMA becomes: $$\text{IMA}_{\text{toggle}} = \frac{l\cos\phi}{r} = \frac{l}{r}\cos\phi$$ When the connecting rod is *also* nearly aligned with the slider (φ → 0), $\cos\phi \to 1$, and: $$\text{IMA}_{\max} = \frac{l}{r} = n$$ > [!NOTE] > For *n* = 4, IMA = 4, meaning the slider force is 4× the tangential crank force. This is the operating principle of toggle-jacks, knuckle-joint presses, and rock crushers. ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Mechanism Type | Stroke | Acceleration Profile | Quick-Return Ratio | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Inline slider-crank (e = 0) | 2r, symmetric | Symmetric, $a = r\omega^2(\cos\theta + \cos 2\theta/n)$ | **1 : 1** | IC engine, pump | | Offset slider-crank (e ≠ 0) | 2r, but asymmetric | Asymmetric; different a during forward/return | Slightly > 1 : 1 | Offset cylinder engine | | Crank & Slotted Lever | Variable, ≤ 2r | Highly asymmetric | Up to 2 : 1 | Shaper, slotter | | Whitworth QRM | Variable | Double reversal, asymmetric | Up to 3 : 1 | High-speed shaper | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Slider–Crank Mechanism > The following Mermaid diagram maps the *interactions* between the four kinematic elements, the inputs, the outputs, and the energy transformation — the schematic of a slider–crank's logical flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fixed Frame
Link 1] -- Hinge at O --> B[Crank OA
Length r
Rotates 360 deg] B -- Pin joint A --> C[Connecting Rod AB
Length l
Oscillates] C -- Pin joint B --> D[Slider B
Translates along
line of stroke] A -. Provides .-> D A -- Permits rotation --> B A -- Permits translation --> D P[Prime Mover
Torque T at O] -->|Input| B B -->|Rotational motion| C C -->|Converted reciprocation| D D -->|Useful output F_B| W[Work Output
e.g. Pump piston
Engine cylinder] B -. v = r sin theta omega .-> V[Velocity at A] D -. v = r omega sin theta plus phi over cos phi .-> VEL[Slider velocity] D -. a = r omega squared cos theta plus cos 2 theta by n .-> ACC[Slider acceleration] ``` ## 4.2 Velocity–Acceleration Profile Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT ["INPUT"] I1[Crank rotates at omega rad per s] I2[Crank angle theta from TDC] end subgraph CRANK ["CRANK OA, length r"] C1[Crank pin A position:
r cos theta, r sin theta] end subgraph CONROD ["CONNECTING ROD AB, length l"] R1[Constraint:
sin phi = r sin theta by l] R2[Angle phi of conrod] end subgraph SLIDER ["SLIDER B"] S1[Displacement x = r cos theta + sqrt l squared minus r squared sin squared theta] S2[Velocity v = r omega sin theta plus phi by cos phi] S3[Acceleration a = r omega squared cos theta plus cos 2 theta by n] end subgraph OUTPUT ["OUTPUT"] O1[Linear reciprocation of piston] O2[Inertia force F = m times a] end I1 --> C1 I2 --> C1 C1 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> S1 C1 --> S2 R2 --> S2 C1 --> S3 R2 --> S3 S1 --> O1 S2 --> O1 S3 --> O2 ``` ## 4.3 Kinematic Inversion Map (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB FB[Four Bar Chain
All four pairs revolute] FB --> INV1[Inversion 1:
Fix one of the short links] FB --> INV2[Inversion 2:
Fix the coupler] FB --> INV3[Inversion 3:
Fix the longest link] INV1 --> SCM1[Slider Crank Mechanism
Inline e = 0] INV1 --> SCM2[Slider Crank Mechanism
Offset e not equal to 0] INV2 --> DBL[Double Crank Mechanism
Drag link] INV3 --> CRL[Crank and Slotted Lever
Whitworth type] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this diagram as a hierarchical tree:** The four-bar chain is the *parent* mechanism. By holding (fixing) each of its four links in turn, we obtain four kinematic inversions. The slider-crank is one such inversion — fixing the *shortest* link produces the inline or offset slider-crank (most common in IC engines). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *Define a slider-crank mechanism. Sketch an inline slider-crank mechanism and label the crank, connecting rod, slider, and line of stroke.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A slider–crank mechanism is a kinematic chain obtained by the **inversion of a four-bar chain** in which one of the four turning pairs is replaced by a sliding (prismatic) pair. It converts rotary motion of the crank into reciprocating (oscillating) motion of the slider, or vice versa. > > **Sketch (1 mark):** A horizontal line of stroke, crank OA of length *r* pivoted at O, connecting rod AB of length *l*, and slider B moving along the line of stroke. > > **Labels (1 mark):** Crank (OA), Connecting rod (AB), Slider (B), Frame (containing O and the line of stroke). Crank angle θ is measured from the TDC direction; connecting rod angle φ is measured from the line of stroke to AB. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *State the fundamental constraint equation of the slider-crank mechanism and write the approximate expressions for displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the slider.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Constraint equation (1 mark):** From the sine rule in triangle OAB, > $$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$ > > **Approximate displacement from TDC (1 mark):** > $$y \approx r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r}{4n}(1 - \cos 2\theta), \quad n = \frac{l}{r}$$ > > **Approximate velocity and acceleration (1 mark):** > $$v \approx r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right), \quad a \approx r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the *exact* equation $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ but then forget to **define *y* as displacement from TDC**, not from the crank centre. The KTU 2024 rubric *explicitly* awards 1 mark for the correct reference (TDC vs crank centre). **Always clarify your reference.** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) — Slider Kinematics with Numerical Evaluation **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024]** > In an inline slider-crank mechanism, the crank length $r = 150$ mm and the connecting rod length $l = 600$ mm. The crank rotates at a constant angular velocity of $240$ rpm in the clockwise direction. > > **(a)** Derive the expressions for the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** of the slider using the approximate kinematic analysis. (7 marks) > > **(b)** For a crank angle $\theta = 45°$ from the inner dead centre (TDC), compute the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** of the slider. (7 marks) --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Setup and constraint (1 mark):** Let O be the crank centre, A the crank pin, B the slider. Crank length OA = *r* = 150 mm, connecting rod length AB = *l* = 600 mm. Let θ be the crank angle from TDC, φ the angle of the connecting rod with the line of stroke. By the sine rule in ΔOAB: $$\frac{r}{\sin\phi} = \frac{l}{\sin\theta} \implies \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{4}$$ **Step 2 — Exact displacement (1 mark):** The distance of slider B from O along the line of stroke is: $$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$ Displacement from TDC (where TDC is at $x = r + l$): $$y = (r+l) - x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + l - \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$ **Step 3 — Binomial approximation (1 mark):** With $l \gg r$, expand the square root: $$\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} = l\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}} \approx l - \tfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l}$$ Substitute and use $\sin^2\theta = \tfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$: $$\boxed{y \approx r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r}{4n}(1 - \cos 2\theta), \quad n = \frac{l}{r} = 4}$$ **Step 4 — Velocity (2 marks):** Differentiate with respect to time using $\dot\theta = \omega$: $$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = r\omega\sin\theta + \frac{r\omega}{2n}\sin 2\theta$$ $$\boxed{v \approx r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Acceleration (2 marks):** Differentiate again with respect to time: $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r\omega^2\cos\theta + \frac{r\omega^2}{n}\cos 2\theta$$ $$\boxed{a \approx r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Substitution logic:** The first term ($\cos\theta$) is the *primary* simple harmonic component, while the second term ($\cos 2\theta / n$) is the *secondary* harmonic caused by the finite length of the connecting rod. As $n \to \infty$ (infinitely long rod), the slider undergoes **pure SHM** — the idealised textbook model. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks) **Given:** - $r = 150$ mm = 0.15 m - $l = 600$ mm - $N = 240$ rpm ⟹ $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.133$ rad/s - $n = l/r = 600/150 = 4$ - $\theta = 45°$ from TDC **Compute displacement y (2 marks):** $$y = r(1 - \cos 45°) + \frac{r}{4n}(1 - \cos 90°)$$ $$y = 150(1 - 0.7071) + \frac{150}{16}(1 - 0)$$ $$y = 150 \times 0.2929 + 9.375 \times 1$$ $$y = 43.935 + 9.375 = \boxed{53.31 \text{ mm from TDC}}$$ **Compute velocity v (2 marks):** $$v = r\omega\left(\sin 45° + \frac{\sin 90°}{2 \times 4}\right)$$ $$v = 0.15 \times 25.133 \times \left(0.7071 + \frac{1}{8}\right)$$ $$v = 3.770 \times (0.7071 + 0.1250)$$ $$v = 3.770 \times 0.8321 = \boxed{3.137 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Compute acceleration a (3 marks):** $$a = r\omega^2\left(\cos 45° + \frac{\cos 90°}{4}\right)$$ $$\omega^2 = (25.133)^2 = 631.65 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$a = 0.15 \times 631.65 \times \left(0.7071 + 0\right)$$ $$a = 94.748 \times 0.7071 = \boxed{66.99 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (directed towards crank centre)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Step-marking:** The KTU 2024 scheme awards 1 mark for the correct unit (mm, m/s, m/s²) and 1 mark for the correct *sign convention* in acceleration. Many students lose marks by writing only magnitudes and forgetting that **acceleration is negative (towards crank centre) at TDC and positive at BDC**. > - **Common slip:** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s. The factor $2\pi/60$ is essential — students who write ω = 240 lose 2 marks. > - **Another common error:** Using $\sin 90° = 0$ or $\cos 90° = 1$ incorrectly. The 2θ values MUST be computed correctly. --- ### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Offset Slider-Crank **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the **offset slider-crank mechanism** with a neat sketch. Show that the **stroke length remains 2r** independent of the offset. (7 marks) > > **(b)** A Whitworth quick-return mechanism has a crank of length 50 mm rotating at 200 rpm. The slotted lever is pivoted at a distance of 200 mm from the crank centre. Determine the **time ratio of cutting to return stroke**. (7 marks) --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Definition + sketch (2 marks):** In an offset slider–crank, the line of stroke of the slider **does not pass through the crank centre O**; instead, it is offset by a perpendicular distance *e*. The slider is connected to the crank pin A through a connecting rod of length *l*. **Step 2 — Geometry (2 marks):** From the geometry of the offset mechanism, the perpendicular distance of crank pin A from the line of stroke is $(e - r\sin\theta)$. The distance of slider B from the crank centre O along its line of motion is: $$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - (e - r\sin\theta)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Stroke length derivation (3 marks):** At the **outer dead centre (ODC)**, $\theta = 0°$: $$x_{ODC} = r + \sqrt{l^2 - e^2}$$ At the **inner dead centre (IDC)**, $\theta = 180°$: $$x_{IDC} = -r + \sqrt{l^2 - e^2}$$ **Stroke length:** $$\text{Stroke} = x_{ODC} - x_{IDC} = (r + \sqrt{l^2 - e^2}) - (-r + \sqrt{l^2 - e^2}) = 2r$$ This is independent of *e*, proving the **stroke is always 2r**, regardless of offset. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Although stroke is independent of *e*, the offset introduces **asymmetry** in time spent on forward vs return strokes. The mechanism is therefore a primitive quick-return device, used historically in some shaper designs. --- #### Part (b) — Whitworth Quick-Return — Model Solution (7 marks) **Step 1 — Geometry setup (2 marks):** The Whitworth QRM consists of: - A **crank** OA of radius *r* = 50 mm rotating at N = 200 rpm - A **slotted lever** pivoted at fixed point P, at distance $L = 200$ mm from O - A **sliding block** at the crank pin A, sliding in the slot of the lever **Step 2 — Determining the cutting and return angles (3 marks):** The slotted lever oscillates as the crank rotates. The lever is vertical (extreme positions) when the crank pin A is at the points where OA is perpendicular to the line PB. The **half-angle of lever oscillation** $\beta$ is found from: $$\sin\beta = \frac{r}{L} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25$$ $$\beta = \sin^{-1}(0.25) = 14.48°$$ The **cutting stroke** corresponds to the crank sweeping through $2\alpha_c$ where $\alpha_c = 180° - 2\beta$: $$\alpha_c = 180° - 2(14.48°) = 151.04°$$ **Return stroke** angle: $$\alpha_r = 180° + 2\beta = 180° + 28.96° = 208.96°$$ **Step 3 — Time ratio (2 marks):** At constant crank speed, the time ratio equals the angle ratio: $$\boxed{\text{Time ratio} = \frac{T_c}{T_r} = \frac{\alpha_c}{\alpha_r} = \frac{151.04°}{208.96°} = 0.723 \approx 1 : 1.38}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Whitworth Problems):** > - The "lever oscillation angle" 2β is *not* the same as the "crank angle for cutting." For Whitworth, the cutting stroke corresponds to the crank moving through the angle $(180° - 2\beta)$ on **each side** of the horizontal — total = $180° - 2\beta$ only if the lever starts vertical; otherwise it's $180° + 2\beta$. **Read the problem wording carefully.** > - **Never confuse** Whitworth (drive through slotted lever) with Crank-and-Slotted-Lever (CSL) where the slot is on the *lever* and the crank pin drives the slider. The Whitworth uses the *crank itself* as the driver and the slot is on a *separate* pivoted lever. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorise Before Exam)** - ✅ Slider–crank = 4-bar chain inversion; replaces one revolute pair with a prismatic pair. - ✅ **Fundamental constraint:** $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta$ (Sine rule in ΔOAB). - ✅ **Exact slider position:** $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$. - ✅ **Approximate displacement from TDC:** $y \approx r(1-\cos\theta) + \dfrac{r}{4n}(1-\cos 2\theta)$. - ✅ **Approximate velocity:** $v \approx r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$. - ✅ **Approximate acceleration:** $a \approx r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$. - ✅ $n = l/r$ is the *reciprocating ratio* — typical values 3 to 4 for IC engines. - ✅ **Stroke length** = 2r — *always*, irrespective of offset *e*. - ✅ **Inline slider-crank:** $e = 0$, symmetric motion. **Offset:** $e \neq 0$, asymmetric motion. - ✅ **Toggle position** ($\theta + \phi = 90°$) gives maximum mechanical advantage = $l/r$. - ✅ **Conversion of units:** $N$ (rpm) → $\omega$ (rad/s) using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. - ✅ **Whitworth QRM** has time ratio $\dfrac{180° - 2\beta}{180° + 2\beta}$ where $\sin\beta = r/L$. - ✅ **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)** = Time of return ÷ Time of cutting = $\dfrac{180° + 2\beta}{180° - 2\beta}$ for Whitworth. - ✅ The **primary harmonic** $\cos\theta$ dominates; **secondary harmonic** $\cos 2\theta / n$ becomes negligible as $n \to \infty$ (i.e., infinitely long rod → pure SHM). - ✅ Inertia force on slider: $F_i = m \cdot a = m \cdot r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — this is the basis of *engine balancing* in higher semesters. > **Final Note:** In the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), examiners emphasise *derivation skills* and *clarity of free-body/kinematic diagrams*. Always **draw the geometry**, **label all symbols**, **state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming long connecting rod, $l \gg r$"), and **show units in the final answer** to secure full marks.

topics.title: Double Slider Crank Mechanisms and Their Inversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double Slider Crank Mechanisms and Their Inversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Double Slider Crank Chain** (also called a *Double Slider Chain*) is a kinematic chain consisting of **two turning pairs and two sliding pairs**, formed by **four links**. In its fundamental form, it comprises a fixed frame, two sliders (pistons/blocks), and a connecting link that joins the two sliders through a swiveling pin. When the chain is operated with the appropriate link held stationary, it yields three classical inversions: the **Elliptical Trammel**, the **Scotch Yoke**, and **Oldham's Coupling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The double slider crank chain is treated as a foundational 4-bar chain with 2 prismatic and 2 revolute pairs (after inversion). It has **2 degrees of freedom** when the standard link is fixed, making it a constrained 2-DOF mechanism.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine two straight rulers fixed perpendicular to each other (like the X and Y axes on a graph paper). Now take a third rod whose two ends have rings that slide freely along these two perpendicular rulers. As you move this rod around, the rings slide along the rulers, but the rod always stays within the rectangular boundary. The curve traced by any point on this rod will be an **ellipse** — this is exactly how an **Elliptical Trammel** works!

- **Scotch Yoke** is like a wheel pushing a straight rod back and forth through a horizontal slot. The wheel rotates in a circle, but the rod only feels the horizontal projection of that circle — giving a perfect **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**.
- **Oldham's Coupling** is like two parallel pencils (shafts) that need to spin in sync but cannot be in the exact same axis. Imagine a card with two perpendicular slots sliding over two pegs — one on each pencil. When one pencil turns, the card follows, dragging the other pencil along. Even if the pencils are slightly offset, the rotation is faithfully transferred at the **same angular velocity** (1:1 ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The double slider crank chain in its generic form has **K = 4 links** and **j = 4 lower pairs** (2 turning + 2 sliding). Using Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms: **DOF = 3(n−1) − 2j₁ − j₂ = 3(4−1) − 2(4) − 0 = 1**, but when operated with a fixed reference, the effective input-output relationship is governed by geometry.

### Three Classical Inversions at a Glance
| Inversion | Fixed Link | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Elliptical Trammel | Connecting link | Traces an ellipse / copies elliptical profiles |
| Scotch Yoke | One slider | Converts rotation into SHM (sinusoidal reciprocation) |
| Oldham's Coupling | Middle flange/link | Transmits rotation between two parallel, offset shafts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Elliptical Trammel

#### Construction & Operation
- The **connecting link (middle link)** is fixed.
- The two sliders move along **two perpendicular straight guides** fixed to the ground.
- The connecting link has pivots at both ends engaging the two sliders.
- A **tracing point** is fixed on the middle link; as the sliders move, the tracing point describes an **ellipse**.

#### Mathematical Foundation
Let the perpendicular guides be the X and Y axes. The two pivots on the connecting link are at distances **2a** (along the link) and **2b** (perpendicular offset of the trace point from the line joining pivots). The locus of the tracing point is:

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$$

where:
- $a$ = semi-major axis
- $b$ = semi-minor axis

> [!NOTE]
> When the trace point lies on the line joining the two pivots, $b = 0$ and the locus degenerates into a **straight line** of length $2a$ — this special case is the basis of **Watt's Straight-Line Approximation** (not the trammel itself, but a related concept sometimes asked in KTU vivas).

### 2.2 The Scotch Yoke

#### Construction & Operation
- One of the **sliders is fixed** to the ground frame.
- A **crank pin** attached to the rotating slider (or an external crank) engages a slot in the reciprocating slider.
- As the crank rotates with constant angular velocity $\omega$, the reciprocating slider executes **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**.

#### Kinematic Equations
Let $r$ be the crank radius, $\theta = \omega t$ the crank angle, and $y$ the displacement of the reciprocating slider from the mean position.

$$
y = r \cos(\theta) = r \cos(\omega t)
$$

$$
v = \frac{dy}{dt} = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega^2 \cos(\omega t)
$$

> [!TIP]
> The scotch yoke is a **kinematic benchmark** for SHM. It is used as a reference in vibration analysis because it produces mathematically perfect sinusoidal motion, free from higher harmonics that contaminate piston motion in real slider-crank engines.

### 2.3 Oldham's Coupling

#### Construction & Operation
- Two **parallel shafts** with axes separated by a small distance $d$ are coupled without intersecting.
- Each shaft carries a **disc with a diametrical slot**.
- A **central flange (middle disc)** has **two perpendicular pins** that engage the slots in the two end discs.
- The flange rotates and is the moving link; the end discs are the sliders (rotational+translational combined motion).

#### Kinematic Behavior
- The **velocity ratio between the two shafts is exactly 1:1** (synchronous rotation).
- The maximum sliding velocity in the slots occurs at the mid-position and equals $\omega \cdot d$, where $d$ is the offset between the shaft axes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Mechanism | Key Equation | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elliptical Trammel | $x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1$ | $a, b$ = semi-axes | Copying elliptical profiles, oval lathe attachments |
| 2 | Elliptical Trammel | $P_x = 2a\cos\theta$, $P_y = 2b\sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = link angle | Parametric form for computer plotting |
| 3 | Scotch Yoke | $y = r\cos(\omega t)$ | $r$ = crank radius, $\omega$ = angular velocity | SHM generator, vibration test rigs |
| 4 | Scotch Yoke | $v_{\max} = r\omega$ | — | Peak velocity in reciprocating systems |
| 5 | Scotch Yoke | $a_{\max} = r\omega^2$ | — | Peak acceleration (inertia force) |
| 6 | Oldham's Coupling | $\omega_1 = \omega_2$ | Shaft angular velocities | Parallel shaft coupling |
| 7 | Oldham's Coupling | $v_{\text{slot,max}} = \omega d$ | $d$ = axis offset | Slip velocity in slot pins |
| 8 | DOF (Kutzbach) | $D = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j$ = joints | Quick DOF check for any planar chain |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Elliptical Trammel** is used in **ellipse-tracing instruments**, **oval-lathe turning attachments**, and as a teaching tool in kinematics laboratories.
- **Scotch Yoke** appears in **vibration exciters**, **shaker tables**, and historically in **gas compressor valves** where clean SHM is required.
- **Oldham's Coupling** is used wherever two parallel shafts must rotate at identical speeds with **angular misalignment tolerance** — e.g., **lead screws of machine tools**, **driveshafts in confined spaces**, and **instrumentation drives**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ellipse Equation for an Elliptical Trammel

**Setup:** Let the two perpendicular guides be the X-axis and Y-axis, intersecting at O (origin). The connecting link AB has two pivots: A slides on the Y-axis and B slides on the X-axis. The trace point P is located on the link at distances such that $AP = 2b$ (along the perpendicular from AB to P) and the full link length projection needs careful handling.

Let the **length of the connecting link** be $L$ (the distance AB), and let a point P on the link be at distance $a$ from A and $b$ from B (so that $a + b = L$, but P need not lie on AB itself — P is at perpendicular distance from line AB).

**Step 1:** Let A be on the Y-axis with coordinates $(0, y)$ and B be on the X-axis with coordinates $(x, 0)$. Let P be a fixed point on the link with known distance ratios.

Let $\theta$ be the angle that the link AB makes with the X-axis. Then:
- A has coordinates $(0, L\sin\theta)$
- B has coordinates $(L\cos\theta, 0)$

**Step 2:** Take a point P on the link such that $AP = a$ (along the link direction) and the link is at angle $\theta$. The position of P along the link from A is:
- $P_x = a\cos\theta$
- $P_y = (L - a)\sin\theta$... 

Wait — let me redefine cleanly. Let P be on the line AB itself such that $AP = a$ and $PB = b$ (with $a + b = L$). Then position of P:
- $P_x = a\cos\theta$
- $P_y = b\sin\theta$

**Step 3:** Square and add the parametric equations.

$$
x = a\cos\theta
$$

$$
y = b\sin\theta
$$
