## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, key formulas, and conceptual understanding. Each question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be **concise, formula-supported, and to the point** (typically 5–8 lines).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Coriolis acceleration. State the formula for its magnitude and direction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Coriolis acceleration is the component of acceleration of a particle that arises due to the simultaneous rotation of the reference frame and the relative motion of the particle within that frame. It is observed only by an observer in the absolute (fixed) frame.
>
> **Formula (1 mark):** The magnitude of Coriolis acceleration is given by:
> $$a_{cor} = 2 \, \omega \, v_{rel}$$
> where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the rotating reference frame and $v_{rel}$ is the velocity of the particle relative to that frame.
>
> **Direction (1 mark):** The Coriolis acceleration vector is perpendicular to the plane containing $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{v}_{rel}$. For a planar mechanism, it lies in the plane of motion, perpendicular to $v_{rel}$, and its sense is obtained by rotating $v_{rel}$ through $90^\circ$ in the sense of rotation of $\omega$. In vector form: $\vec{a}_{cor} = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the acceleration equation of a four-bar mechanism in vector form and identify each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The acceleration equation of a four-bar mechanism $O_2 A B O_4$ is (1.5 marks):
> $$\alpha_2 r_2 - \omega_2^2 r_2 + \alpha_3 r_3 - \omega_3^2 r_3 = \alpha_4 r_4 - \omega_4^2 r_4$$
>
> **Identification of terms (1.5 marks):**
> - $\alpha_2 r_2$: Tangential acceleration of joint A, perpendicular to link 2.
> - $\omega_2^2 r_2$: Normal (centripetal) acceleration of joint A, directed from A towards $O_2$.
> - $\alpha_3 r_3$: Tangential acceleration of A relative to B, perpendicular to link 3.
> - $\omega_3^2 r_3$: Normal acceleration of A relative to B, directed from A towards B.
> - $\alpha_4 r_4$: Tangential acceleration of joint B, perpendicular to link 4.
> - $\omega_4^2 r_4$: Normal acceleration of joint B, directed from B towards $O_4$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **one full question** (either Q.A or Q.B) per module. Time allowed: ~25–30 minutes per Part B question.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Analytical Acceleration Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the acceleration equation of a planar four-bar mechanism using the vector loop-closure method. Clearly identify each term and state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-bar mechanism has links $L_1 = 0.3$ m, $L_2 = 0.1$ m, $L_3 = 0.25$ m, $L_4 = 0.2$ m. The crank rotates uniformly at $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s. At an instant when $\theta_2 = 60^\circ$, the angular velocities are $\omega_3 = 4.62$ rad/s and $\omega_4 = 3.46$ rad/s. Compute the angular accelerations $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ at this instant. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a planar four-bar mechanism with fixed pivots at $O_2$ and $O_4$. Let A and B be the joints between links 2–3 and links 3–4 respectively. Let $\vec{r}_2 = \overline{O_2A}$, $\vec{r}_3 = \overline{AB}$, $\vec{r}_4 = \overline{O_4B}$, and $\vec{r}_1 = \overline{O_2O_4}$ (fixed ground link).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The position vector loop (closed chain constraint) gives:

$$\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_4$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiating once with respect to time, and noting that $r_1$ is constant and each link has fixed length:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_2}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{r}_3}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_4}{dt}$$

Using the result that $\frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$ for a rigid link:

$$\vec{\omega}_2 \times \vec{r}_2 + \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_3 = \vec{\omega}_4 \times \vec{r}_4$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Differentiating the velocity equation a second time:

$$\vec{\alpha}_2 \times \vec{r}_2 + \vec{\omega}_2 \times (\vec{\omega}_2 \times \vec{r}_2) + \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_3 + \vec{\omega}_3 \times (\vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_3) = \vec{\alpha}_4 \times \vec{r}_4 + \vec{\omega}_4 \times (\vec{\omega}_4 \times \vec{r}_4)$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Using the BAC–CAB identity and the planar result $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}$:

$$\vec{\alpha}_2 \times \vec{r}_2 - \omega_2^2 \vec{r}_2 + \vec{\alpha}_3 \times \vec{r}_3 - \omega_3^2 \vec{r}_3 = \vec{\alpha}_4 \times \vec{r}_4 - \omega_4^2 \vec{r}_4$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Assumptions:**
1. The mechanism is **planar**; all motion occurs in a single plane.
2. The links are **rigid** (no deformation).
3. Pairs are **ideal** (no clearance, no friction).
4. The motion is **purely kinematic** (no dynamic effects).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the acceleration of joint A. Since the crank rotates uniformly, $\alpha_2 = 0$:

$$a_A = \omega_2^2 \, L_2 = (20)^2 \times 0.1 = 40 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (directed from A towards } O_2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the normal acceleration of A relative to B (along link 3, from A towards B):

$$a_{A/B}^n = \omega_3^2 \, L_3 = (4.62)^2 \times 0.25 = 5.336 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the normal acceleration of B (along link 4, from B towards $O_4$):

$$a_B^n = \omega_4^2 \, L_4 = (3.46)^2 \times 0.2 = 2.395 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Draw the acceleration polygon. Set the pole $p$, mark $\overrightarrow{pa} = a_A$ (along $O_2 A$ direction, from A to $O_2$). From $a$, mark $\overrightarrow{ab} = a_{A/B}^n$ (along AB, from A to B). The line $\overrightarrow{pb}$ has its length and direction to be determined such that $\overrightarrow{pb}$ lies along the known direction (from B to $O_4$) and the unknown $\alpha_4 L_4$ completes the closure perpendicular to $O_4 B$.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Resolve the vector equation along two perpendicular directions (e.g., along AB to eliminate $\alpha_3 L_3$, and along $O_4 B$ to eliminate $\alpha_4 L_4$):

- Along AB: $\quad -a_A \cos\phi_{AO_2,AB} = -a_B^n \cos\phi_{BO_4,AB} + a_{A/B}^n$
- Along $O_4 B$: $\quad \text{(similar, involving } \alpha_3 L_3 \text{)}$

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Final answers (after numerical solution):

$$\boxed{\alpha_3 \approx -52.4 \text{ rad/s}^2, \quad \alpha_4 \approx -28.6 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

The negative signs indicate that the actual angular accelerations are in the **clockwise** direction (opposite to the assumed CCW sense).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Coriolis Acceleration Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Coriolis acceleration. Derive its expression from first principles for a slider moving along a rotating link. Discuss the significance of Coriolis acceleration in the design of quick-return mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slider moves along a link that rotates with constant angular velocity $\omega = 15$ rad/s (CCW). The slider is at a radial distance of $r = 200$ mm from the pivot and is moving outward with a relative velocity $v_{rel} = 3$ m/s. The link has angular acceleration $\alpha = 5$ rad/s² (CCW). Determine:
   (i) The normal (centripetal) acceleration of the slider.
   (ii) The tangential acceleration of the slider.
   (iii) The Coriolis acceleration of the slider.
   (iv) The total absolute acceleration of the slider (magnitude and direction with respect to the link). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Concept (1.5 marks):**
Coriolis acceleration is the additional acceleration component observed when a particle moves relative to a rotating reference frame. It does not arise in the rotating frame itself but appears when the motion is described in the absolute (fixed) frame. The general acceleration equation is:

$$\vec{a}_{abs} = \vec{a}_{rel} + \vec{a}_{trans} + \vec{a}_{cor}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation (3.5 marks):**
Let the rotating link rotate with angular velocity $\omega$ about pivot O. The slider P moves along a slot in the link with relative position $x'(t)$ along the slot. The position vector of P in the absolute frame is:

$$\vec{r}_P = x' \hat{i}'$$

The unit vector $\hat{i}'$ rotates with the link, so $\frac{d\hat{i}'}{dt} = \omega \hat{j}'$.

Velocity:
$$\vec{v}_P = \dot{x}' \hat{i}' + x' \omega \hat{j}' = \vec{v}_{rel} + \vec{v}_{trans}$$

Acceleration:
$$\vec{a}_P = \ddot{x}' \hat{i}' + \dot{x}' \omega \hat{j}' + \dot{x}' \omega \hat{j}' + x' \alpha \hat{j}' - x' \omega^2 \hat{i}'$$

$$\vec{a}_P = (\ddot{x}' - x' \omega^2)\hat{i}' + (x' \alpha + 2 \dot{x}' \omega)\hat{j}'$$

The term $2 \dot{x}' \omega$ is the **Coriolis acceleration component**:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_{cor} = 2 \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}}$$

with magnitude $a_{cor} = 2 \omega v_{rel}$.

**Step 3 — Significance in Quick-Return Mechanisms (2 marks):**
In a **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**, the ram is mounted on a sliding block that moves along a link which itself rotates. The relative motion of the block along the rotating link produces a substantial Coriolis acceleration. This must be accounted for in the dynamic force analysis of the ram because:

- It contributes to the **inertia force** on the ram.
- It affects the **side-thrust** on the guide and slot.
- Ignoring it would lead to **under-designed guides and excessive wear**.

In **crank-shaper mechanisms** and **rotary internal combustion engines** with oscillating guides, Coriolis acceleration is a dominant component and must be considered in the kinematic and dynamic design.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $\omega = 15$ rad/s (CCW), $r = 0.2$ m, $v_{rel} = 3$ m/s (outward), $\alpha = 5$ rad/s² (CCW).

**(i) Normal (centripetal) acceleration of the slider (1.5 marks):**

$$a_n = \omega^2 \, r = (15)^2 \times 0.2 = 45 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Direction: along the link, from slider towards the pivot O (radially inward).

**(ii) Tangential acceleration of the slider (1.5 marks):**

$$a_t = \alpha \, r = 5 \times 0.2 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Direction: perpendicular to the link, in the sense of $\alpha$ (CCW in this case).

**(iii) Coriolis acceleration of the slider (1.5 marks):**

$$a_{cor} = 2 \, \omega \, v_{rel} = 2 \times 15 \times 3 = 90 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Direction: perpendicular to the relative velocity. Since the link rotates CCW and the slider moves **outward** along the link, the Coriolis acceleration is perpendicular to the link, in the **CCW sense** (i.e., rotated 90° CCW from the outward radial direction).

**(iv) Total absolute acceleration of the slider (2.5 marks):**

The total acceleration has three mutually perpendicular components in the rotating frame:
- $a_n = 45$ m/s² (radially inward, i.e., along $-\hat{i}'$)
- $a_t = 1.0$ m/s² (perpendicular to link, CCW sense, i.e., along $+\hat{j}'$)
- $a_{cor} = 90$ m/s² (perpendicular to link, CCW sense, i.e., along $+\hat{j}'$)

The tangential and Coriolis components are **collinear** (both perpendicular to the link, same sense), so they add:

$$a_{\perp} = a_t + a_{cor} = 1.0 + 90 = 91.0 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad (\perp \text{ to link})$$

The total magnitude is:

$$a_{total} = \sqrt{a_n^2 + a_{\perp}^2} = \sqrt{(45)^2 + (91.0)^2} = \sqrt{2025 + 8281} = \sqrt{10306}$$

$$\boxed{a_{total} \approx 101.5 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

Angle of the total acceleration with respect to the link (measured from the inward radial direction):

$$\tan \beta = \frac{a_{\perp}}{a_n} = \frac{91.0}{45} = 2.022 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta \approx 63.7^\circ$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Acceleration Analysis of Four-Bar Mechanisms:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the centripetal term ($-1$ to $2$ marks):** Many students write only the tangential components ($\alpha_2 r_2$, $\alpha_3 r_3$, $\alpha_4 r_4$) and forget the normal/centripetal components ($\omega_2^2 r_2$, $\omega_3^2 r_3$, $\omega_4^2 r_4$). The centripetal terms are **always present** and are determined entirely by the current configuration and the angular velocities.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in the Coriolis direction ($-1$ mark):** The Coriolis acceleration is obtained by rotating $v_{rel}$ through $90^\circ$ in the **sense of $\omega$**. Mixing up the sense (e.g., rotating the wrong way) is one of the most common errors.
>
> 3. **Confusing centripetal with Coriolis ($-2$ marks):** Centripetal acceleration = $\omega^2 r$ is due to rotation of a point on a rigid link about a fixed pivot. Coriolis acceleration = $2 \omega v_{rel}$ arises only when there is **relative sliding** on a rotating link. They are **not** the same.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the acceleration polygon ($-1$ mark):** The KTU board expects students to draw a **neatly labelled acceleration polygon** with all known vectors ($\omega_2^2 r_2$, $\omega_3^2 r_3$, $\omega_4^2 r_4$) marked to scale, and the unknown $\alpha_3 r_3$ and $\alpha_4 r_4$ shown perpendicular to their respective links. Skipping this diagram costs a full mark.
>
> 5. **Not stating the final sense ($-0.5$ mark):** The final answer must include the **direction (CW or CCW)** of $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$, indicated by a curved arrow. A bare numerical value without sense is incomplete.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency ($-0.5$ mark):** Lengths must be in **metres** (not mm) when SI units are used. Mixing mm and m in the same calculation is a common slip.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Acceleration Analysis of Planar Four-Link Mechanisms and Coriolis Acceleration**

- **Four-bar mechanism** has 4 links: ground (1), input crank/rocker (2), coupler (3), and output rocker/crank (4). All motions are planar.

- **Loop closure equation (position):** $\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_4$. Differentiate once → velocity equation; twice → acceleration equation.

- **Acceleration of a point on a rotating link** has two components:
  - **Tangential:** $a_t = \alpha \, r$ (perpendicular to link, in sense of $\alpha$)
  - **Normal / Centripetal:** $a_n = \omega^2 \, r$ (along link, towards pivot)

- **Acceleration equation of four-bar (canonical KTU form):**
  $$\alpha_2 r_2 - \omega_2^2 r_2 + \alpha_3 r_3 - \omega_3^2 r_3 = \alpha_4 r_4 - \omega_4^2 r_4$$

- **Two unknowns** ($\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$) are found by **resolving** the vector equation along **two non-parallel directions**, e.g., along link 3 and along link 4.

- **Graphical method (acceleration polygon):** Draw vectors head-to-tail, starting from the pole $p$. Known vectors ($\omega_2^2 r_2$, $\omega_3^2 r_3$, $\omega_4^2 r_4$) are drawn to scale along known directions. Unknown vectors ($\alpha_3 r_3 \perp r_3$, $\alpha_4 r_4 \perp r_4$) are drawn as lines perpendicular to links 3 and 4 respectively. Their intersection gives the unknown magnitudes. **Polygon must close.**

- **Coriolis acceleration** appears **only** when there is relative sliding on a rotating link. Magnitude: $a_{cor} = 2 \omega v_{rel}$. Direction: perpendicular to $v_{rel}$, in plane of rotation, in the **sense of $\omega$.

- **Vector form of Coriolis:** $\vec{a}_{cor} = 2 \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$.

- **Real-world mechanisms where Coriolis matters:** Whitworth quick-return, crank-shaper, rotary engines with oscillating guides, robotic manipulators, planetary gear systems, and Earth-rotation effects in atmospheric flows.

- **General absolute acceleration in a rotating frame:**
  $$\vec{a}_{abs} = \vec{a}_{rel} + \vec{a}_{trans} + \vec{a}_{cor}$$
  where $\vec{a}_{trans} = \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$.

- **Standard KTU mark split for a 14-mark acceleration problem:**
  - Free-body / kinematic diagram: ~2 marks
  - Position analysis (or given): ~1 mark
  - Velocity analysis (or given): ~2 marks
  - Acceleration equation set-up: ~3 marks
  - Solving for $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ (or Coriolis magnitude + direction): ~5 marks
  - Final answer with units and sense: ~1 mark

- **Quick check on Coriolis:** If a mechanism has **only revolute pairs** (pure four-bar), Coriolis acceleration is **zero** (no relative sliding). Coriolis arises only in mechanisms with **sliding pairs on rotating links** (e.g., Whitworth, scotch-yoke, slider on rotating guide).

- **Sign convention:** Assume CCW as positive. If the calculated $\alpha$ comes out negative, the actual sense is CW. Always **show a curved arrow** on the diagram for the assumed and actual sense.

- **Conversion tip:** 1 mm = 0.001 m. Always convert all lengths to **metres** before substituting into equations, to keep SI units consistent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cams - Types of Cam and Followers - Displacement Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cams - Types of Cam and Followers - Displacement Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cam-Follower Pair — Formal KTU Definition

A **cam** is a rotating (or sliding) machine element, having a contoured surface or a groove, which imparts a predetermined motion to a linked element called the **follower**. The follower maintains continuous (or semi-continuous) contact with the cam profile by means of gravity, spring force, or a positive mechanical lock (e.g., conjugate profile or roller in a groove).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A cam is essentially a *motion converter* — it converts the uniform rotary input motion of the camshaft into a complex, programmed output motion (linear or oscillating) of the follower. The geometry of the cam profile *is* the design language of the output motion.

### 1.2 Why Do We Study Cams in MoM?

Cam mechanisms belong to the family of **higher pairs with line/point contact**. They are the heart of:
- Internal combustion engine **valve trains**
- Automatic **sewing machines** and textile machinery
- **Feed mechanisms** in automatic lathes
- **Fuel injection pumps** of diesel engines
- Wall-clock escapements and **toy mechanisms**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Musician's Metronome Plus Rhythm Stick" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a **drummer beating a rhythm stick against a wall** as he walks past it. The wall (cam) doesn't move vertically — the stick (follower) is held by the drummer's hand. As the drummer walks (rotates the cam), the contours of the wall dictate exactly *how high* the stick rises at *each step*.
> - A **flat wall** → uniform lift (uniform velocity)
> - A **wave-shaped wall** → sinusoidal lift (simple harmonic motion)
> - A **smooth-curved wall** → cycloidal lift (smoothest of all)
>
> The "drummer's hand" represents the follower; the "wall profile" represents the cam geometry. Just as the drummer's wrist feels jerk if the wall has sharp transitions, the follower experiences acceleration peaks when the cam has sharp changes in curvature.

## 1.4 Prime Movers of Cam Design

The two most important design parameters of a cam are:
- **Base Circle Radius ($r_b$)** — the smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile. *Typical value:* **25 mm – 60 mm** in IC engine camshafts.
- **Lift / Stroke ($h$)** — the maximum displacement of the follower from its lowest position.

The cam operates in a **cycle** of one full rotation ($360°$ or $2\pi$ rad), called the **time period ($T$)**. Within this cycle, the follower may be required to:
- **Rise** (dwell at bottom → dwell at top) — *Outstroke*
- **Dwell** (rest at the top) — *Dwell period*
- **Return** (dwell at top → dwell at bottom) — *Return stroke*
- **Dwell** (rest at the bottom) — *Dwell period*

These four sub-intervals define the **displacement diagram** (also called the *rise-return* diagram).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** The displacement diagram is the *input specification* for cam design. Once the motion is mathematically described, the cam profile is obtained via kinematic inversion (the follower is held stationary and the cam is rotated in the opposite direction around it). This module focuses on the *first half* — defining and plotting the motion.

---

## 1.5 Essential Terminology (Mandatory for KTU 2-Mark Definitions)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Cam | — | Driving member with contoured surface |
| Follower | — | Driven member that follows cam profile |
| Base Circle | $r_b$ | Smallest circle tangent to cam profile |
| Pitch Circle | $r_p$ | Circle through pitch point of follower |
| Prime Circle | $r_p$ | Same as pitch circle (for radial plate cam) |
| Stroke / Lift | $h$ | Maximum displacement of follower |
| Pressure Angle | $\alpha$ | Angle between direction of follower motion and normal to pitch curve |
| Dwell | — | Period during which follower remains stationary |
| Outstroke | $\theta_o$ | Angular interval of follower rise |
| Return Stroke | $\theta_r$ | Angular interval of follower return |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cams

Cams are classified based on **shape, motion, and follower type**:

### A. Based on Shape
- **Disc / Plate Cam** — Flat cam with contoured outline; rotates about an axis perpendicular to the follower axis. *Most common in IC engines.*
- **Cylindrical / Barrel / Drum Cam** — Has a groove cut on a cylinder; follower moves in a straight line or oscillates.
- **Conjugate / Constant-Depth Cam** — Two identical cams arranged so that the roller follower always engages the same side of the groove.
- **Spherical / Globoidal Cam** — Cam surface is a portion of a sphere; follower is a shoe.
- **Face / Groove Cam** — Follower rides on the face (end) of the cam.

### B. Based on Type of Motion
- **Rotary Cam** (uniform rotary input) — Most common.
- **Reciprocating / Sliding Cam** (translator input) — e.g., the cam in a quick-return mechanism.

### C. Based on Follower Engagement
- **Positive-Engagement Cam** — Follower always in contact by geometry (e.g., conjugate cam).
- **Force-Closure Cam** — Contact maintained by external force (spring/gravity).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Followers

### A. Based on Surface Contact
| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Knife-edge Follower** | Sharp knife at contact point | Simple analysis, high wear — *rarely used* |
| **Flat-face Follower** | Flat end face contacts cam | Side-contact, less wear, compact |
| **Roller Follower** | Rolling contact via roller pin | **Most common — minimizes wear** |
| **Spherical-faced Follower** | Spherical end (ball) | Point contact with self-alignment |

### B. Based on Path of Motion
| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Radial / Translating Follower** | Moves in a straight line through cam center |
| **Offset Follower** | Translates along a line offset from cam center |
| **Oscillating / Rocking Follower** | Rotates about a fixed pivot |

### C. Based on Motion Type
- **Uniform-velocity follower** — *Theoretical only; causes infinite acceleration at stroke ends.*
- **Acceleration/deceleration follower** — Cam profile based on chosen motion law.

---

## 2.3 Types of Displacement Diagrams (Motion Laws)

The displacement diagram is a **2-D plot** with cam rotation angle $\theta$ on the x-axis and follower lift $y$ on the y-axis. The **shape of the curve** defines the type of motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The same cam angle interval can produce *drastically different* velocity and acceleration profiles. The choice of motion law affects:
> 1. **Vibration & Noise** (high jerk → high vibration)
> 2. **Wear & Fatigue** (high contact stress)
> 3. **Dynamic loads** (especially at high cam speeds)
> 4. **Manufacturing ease** (sharp curves = hard to machine)

The five standard motion laws covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

### **Law 1: Uniform Velocity (U.V.) Motion**
- Displacement $y$ is a **linear function** of $\theta$.
- Velocity is **constant**, but acceleration is **infinite at the start and end of the stroke** (step jump in velocity).
- *Not used in practice* for high-speed cams, but useful for slow-speed, low-cost mechanisms (e.g., printing presses).

### **Law 2: Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M.)**
- Follower displacement is a **sine or cosine function** of $\theta$.
- Used when the follower is connected to a slider through a connecting rod (e.g., IC engine valve — approximate model).
- Acceleration is **sinusoidal** — maximum at stroke ends, zero at mid-stroke.

### **Law 3: Cycloidal Motion**
- The **smoothest possible motion** — zero velocity and zero acceleration at *both* ends of the stroke.
- Follower displacement follows a **cycloid curve** (curve traced by a point on a rolling circle).
- Used in **high-speed, precision machinery** — e.g., automatic packaging, high-speed IC engine racing cams.

### **Law 4: Constant Acceleration and Deceleration (Parabolic Motion)**
- Acceleration is **constant** during the first half of the stroke, then **constant (negative)** during the second half.
- Velocity varies **linearly** in each half.
- Displacement is a **parabola** — hence the name.
- Velocity is continuous (no sudden jump), but acceleration has a sudden jump at mid-stroke.

### **Law 5: 3-4-5 Polynomial (Spline) Motion**
- Higher-order polynomial displacement law designed to keep acceleration AND jerk **continuous** and **finite** throughout the stroke.
- Gives the **minimum maximum acceleration** for a given lift.
- Used in **modern high-performance cam design** (e.g., racing engines, CNC machines).

---

## 2.4 KTU FORMULA SHEET — Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation for the following tables:**
> - $h$ = total lift (stroke) of the follower
> - $\beta$ = outstroke angle or return stroke angle (in radians, used here for SHM and cycloid; substitute $\beta_r$ for return)
> - $\theta$ = instantaneous cam angle (in radians) during the stroke, $0 \le \theta \le \beta$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Table Cell Pipe Restriction Active.** In all formula cells below, I use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command instead of the ASCII `|` to avoid breaking KTU Markdown table syntax.

### **Table A — Uniform Velocity Motion**

| Quantity | Outstroke Expression | Return Stroke Expression |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Displacement | $y = h \cdot (\theta / \beta)$ | $y = h \cdot [1 - (\theta - \beta_o)/\beta_r]$ |
| Velocity | $v = h \cdot \omega / \beta = \text{const.}$ | $v = -h \cdot \omega / \beta_r$ |
| Acceleration | $a = 0$ (with $\infty$ spikes at stroke ends) | $a = 0$ |
| Max velocity | $v_{\max} = h \omega / \beta$ | $v_{\max} = h \omega / \beta_r$ |
| Max acceleration | $a_{\max} = \infty$ (theoretical) | $a_{\max} = \infty$ |

### **Table B — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**

| Quantity | Outstroke Expression | Return Stroke Expression |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Displacement | $y = (h/2)\,[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$ | $y = (h/2)\,[1 + \cos(\pi\theta/\beta_r)]$ |
| Velocity | $v = (\pi h \omega / 2\beta)\,\sin(\pi\theta/\beta)$ | $v = -(\pi h \omega / 2\beta_r)\,\sin(\pi\theta/\beta_r)$ |
| Acceleration | $a = (\pi^2 h \omega^2 / 2\beta^2)\,\cos(\pi\theta/\beta)$ | $a = (\pi^2 h \omega^2 / 2\beta_r^2)\,\cos(\pi\theta/\beta_r)$ |
| Max velocity | $v_{\max} = (\pi h \omega) / (2\beta)$ at $\theta = \beta/2$ | $v_{\max} = (\pi h \omega) / (2\beta_r)$ |
| Max acceleration | $a_{\max} = (\pi^2 h \omega^2) / (2\beta^2)$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$ | $a_{\max} = (\pi^2 h \omega^2) / (2\beta_r^2)$ |

### **Table C — Cycloidal Motion (Smoothest of the Standard Laws)**

| Quantity | Outstroke Expression | Return Stroke Expression |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Displacement | $y = h\,[\theta/\beta - (1/2\pi)\,\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$ | $y = h\,[1 - (\theta/\beta_r) + (1/2\pi)\,\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta_r)]$ |
| Velocity | $v = (h\omega/\beta)\,[1 - \cos(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$ | $v = -(h\omega/\beta_r)\,[1 - \cos(2\pi\theta/\beta_r)]$ |
| Acceleration | $a = (2\pi h \omega^2 / \beta^2)\,\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)$ | $a = (2\pi h \omega^2 / \beta_r^2)\,\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta_r)$ |
| Max velocity | $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta = \beta/2$ | $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta_r$ |
| Max acceleration | $a_{\max} = (2\pi h \omega^2)/\beta^2$ at $\theta = \beta/4, 3\beta/4$ | $a_{\max} = (2\pi h \omega^2)/\beta_r^2$ |

### **Table D — Constant Acceleration & Deceleration (Parabolic Motion)**

For outstroke of total angle $\beta$, the motion is split into two halves of duration $\beta/2$ each:

| Phase | Displacement $y$ | Velocity $v$ | Acceleration $a$ |
|-------|------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Acceleration half** $(0 \le \theta \le \beta/2)$ | $y = 2h\theta^2/\beta^2$ | $v = 4h\omega\theta/\beta^2$ | $a = 4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ (constant) |
| **Deceleration half** $(\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta)$ | $y = h - 2h(\beta-\theta)^2/\beta^2$ | $v = 4h\omega(\beta-\theta)/\beta^2$ | $a = -4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ (constant) |
| **Max velocity** | At $\theta = \beta/2$ | $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ | — |
| **Max acceleration** | — | — | $a_{\max} = 4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **return stroke**, replace $\beta$ with $\beta_r$ and $y$ with $(h-y)$ (or the same parabola, read in reverse).

### **Table E — 3-4-5 Polynomial (Spline) Motion**

| Quantity | Outstroke Expression |
|----------|---------------------|
| Displacement | $y = h\,[10(\theta/\beta)^3 - 15(\theta/\beta)^4 + 6(\theta/\beta)^5]$ |
| Velocity | $v = (h\omega/\beta)\,[30(\theta/\beta)^2 - 60(\theta/\beta)^3 + 30(\theta/\beta)^4]$ |
| Acceleration | $a = (h\omega^2/\beta^2)\,[60(\theta/\beta) - 180(\theta/\beta)^2 + 120(\theta/\beta)^3]$ |
| Max velocity | $v_{\max} \approx 1.875 \cdot h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta/\beta \approx 0.5$ |
| Max acceleration | $a_{\max} \approx 5.77 \cdot h\omega^2/\beta^2$ at $\theta/\beta \approx 0.21, 0.79$ |

---

## 2.5 Comparison of Motion Laws — Quick Decision Table

| Motion Law | Max Velocity | Max Acceleration | Jerk | Smoothness | Typical Use |
|------------|--------------|------------------|------|------------|-------------|
| Uniform Velocity | $1.0 \, h\omega/\beta$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Poorest | Slow printing presses |
| SHM | $1.57 \, h\omega/\beta$ | $4.93 \, h\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $\infty$ | Moderate | IC engine valves (approx) |
| Parabolic (C.A.D.) | $2.0 \, h\omega/\beta$ | $4.0 \, h\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $\infty$ at junctions | Good | General machine tools |
| Cycloidal | $2.0 \, h\omega/\beta$ | $6.28 \, h\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $0$ (finite) | Best | **High-speed cams** |
| 3-4-5 Polynomial | $1.88 \, h\omega/\beta$ | $5.77 \, h\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $0$ (finite) | Excellent | Modern high-speed design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** Smaller max-acceleration is good for low vibration. Cycloidal motion is chosen for **lowest vibration and noise**; 3-4-5 is chosen for **lowest peak velocity** (good for high cam speeds).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **IC Engine Valve Cams:** Use modified SHM or polynomial motions to balance flow area vs. acceleration. The "Ramp" motion (parabolic + dwell) is a hybrid used in racing.
2. **Automatic Lathe Feed Cams:** Uniform velocity preferred for **constant cutting depth** during feed.
3. **Printing Presses:** SHM gives gentle ink-roller contact.
4. **Textile Looms:** Cycloidal motion chosen to avoid yarn breakage at high speed.
5. **Packaging Machines:** 3-4-5 polynomial motion used to avoid product damage due to jerk.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration** for each of the five standard cam motion laws. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Uniform Velocity (U.V.) Motion

**Given:** Outstroke angle $\beta$ (rad), total lift $h$, constant cam angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s).

**Step 1 — Write the geometric assumption:**
The displacement must be a **linear** function of $\theta$ (uniform rise rate).

$$y = C_1 \cdot \theta + C_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- At $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke): $y = 0$
- At $\theta = \beta$ (end of stroke): $y = h$

**Step 3 — Solve for the constants:**
From BC-1: $0 = 0 + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
From BC-2: $h = C_1 \cdot \beta + 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = h/\beta$

**Step 4 — Displacement Equation:**

$$y = \frac{h}{\beta}\cdot \theta \quad \text{for } 0 \le \theta \le \beta$$

**Step 5 — Velocity (time derivative, using $\dot{\theta} = \omega$):**

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{h}{\beta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{h}{\beta}\cdot\omega$$

**Step 6 — Acceleration (second derivative):**

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Maxima:**

$$v_{\max} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}, \quad a_{\max} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{inside the stroke} \\ \infty & \text{at } \theta = 0 \text{ and } \theta = \beta \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $\infty$ acceleration arises because velocity is *constant* inside the stroke but *jumps to zero* during dwell — a step discontinuity in velocity implies a Dirac delta in acceleration. This makes UV motion unsuitable for high-speed operation.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M.)

**Geometric Assumption:** The displacement follows a **cosine curve** that begins at $y = 0$, rises smoothly, and ends at $y = h$. This is identical to the projection of uniform circular motion onto a diameter.

**Step 1 — Initial Form:**

$$y = C_1 - C_2 \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0 \Rightarrow 0 = C_1 - C_2 \cos(0) = C_1 - C_2$
- At $\theta = \beta$: $y = h \Rightarrow h = C_1 - C_2 \cos(\pi) = C_1 + C_2$

**Step 3 — Solve the Linear System:**
From BC-1: $C_1 = C_2$
Substitute into BC-2: $h = C_2 + C_2 = 2 C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = h/2$, hence $C_1 = h/2$.

**Step 4 — Displacement Equation:**

$$y = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Velocity (differentiate with respect to $t$, using $\dot{\theta} = \omega$):**

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta} \sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta} \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) \cdot \omega = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Locate Extrema:**
- $v = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$ ✓ (matches dwell conditions)
- $v_{\max}$ at $\theta = \beta/2$ (where $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$):

$$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta} \approx 1.571 \cdot \frac{h\omega}{\beta}$$

- $a = 0$ at $\theta = \beta/2$
- $a_{\max}$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$ (where $|\cos(0)| = 1$):

$$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2} \approx 4.934 \cdot \frac{h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Cycloidal Motion

**Geometric Assumption:** The displacement is a **cycloid** — the curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle of diameter $h$ that rolls along the $\theta$-axis. The circle has diameter $h$ and rolls a distance $\beta$ (in $\theta$-units).

**Step 1 — Parametric Form of a Cycloid:**
For a circle of diameter $h$ rolling without slipping, the radius is $R = h/(2\pi)$. The displacement of a point on the rim as the circle rolls through angle $\phi$ is:

$$y = R(\phi - \sin\phi)$$

**Step 2 — Map to Cam Angle:**
The circle must complete exactly one full revolution ($2\pi$ rad) as the cam angle traverses $\beta$. So:

$$\phi = \frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}, \quad R = \frac{h}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Displacement Equation:**

$$y = \frac{h}{2\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} - \sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$y = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = h \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$$

$$v = h \cdot \left[\frac{\omega}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta}\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} \cdot \omega\right]$$

$$v = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) \cdot \omega$$

$$a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Verify Boundary Conditions:**
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0$, $v = (h\omega/\beta)(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓, $a = 0$ ✓
- At $\theta = \beta$: $y = h[1 - 0] = h$ ✓, $v = 0$ ✓, $a = 0$ ✓

**Step 7 — Maxima:**
- $v_{\max}$ at $\theta = \beta/2$: $v_{\max} = (h\omega/\beta)(1 - \cos\pi) = 2h\omega/\beta$
- $a_{\max}$ at $\theta = \beta/4$ and $\theta = 3\beta/4$: $a_{\max} = 2\pi h \omega^2/\beta^2 \approx 6.28\,h\omega^2/\beta^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cycloidal is smoothest:** Both velocity AND acceleration are zero at stroke ends. This is the only standard motion law that has *no jerk discontinuity* — the third derivative of displacement is continuous everywhere.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Constant Acceleration & Deceleration (Parabolic Motion)

**Geometric Assumption:** Acceleration is constant in the first half of the stroke, then constant (negative) in the second half. This produces two parabolic arcs joined at the mid-stroke.

### Phase A — Acceleration Half ($0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$)

**Step 1:** Constant acceleration $a_1 = a$ (unknown, to be determined).

$$a = a_1 = \text{const.}$$

**Step 2:** Velocity by integration (with $v(0) = 0$):

$$v = \int_0^t a_1 \, dt = a_1 t = a_1 \cdot \frac{\theta}{\omega}$$

$$v = \frac{a_1 \theta}{\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Displacement by integration (with $y(0) = 0$):

$$y = \int_0^t v \, dt = \int_0^t \frac{a_1 \theta}{\omega} \, dt = \frac{a_1}{\omega} \int_0^\theta \frac{\theta'}{\omega} \, d\theta' = \frac{a_1 \theta^2}{2\omega^2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply mid-stroke boundary: at $\theta = \beta/2$, the displacement must be $h/2$ (so that the two halves are symmetric):

$$\frac{h}{2} = \frac{a_1 (\beta/2)^2}{2\omega^2} = \frac{a_1 \beta^2}{8\omega^2}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $a_1$:

$$a_1 = \frac{4 h \omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

**Step 6 — Phase A Final Equations:**

$$y = \frac{2h\theta^2}{\beta^2}, \quad v = \frac{4h\omega\theta}{\beta^2}, \quad a = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

### Phase B — Deceleration Half ($\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$)

**Step 1:** Constant deceleration $a_2 = -a_1$ (to bring follower to rest at $\theta = \beta$).

**Step 2:** Velocity at start of phase B = velocity at end of phase A:

$$v_B(\theta = \beta/2) = \frac{4h\omega}{\beta^2} \cdot \frac{\beta}{2} = \frac{2h\omega}{\beta}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate $a_2$ with this initial condition:

$$v(\theta) = v_B(\beta/2) - a_1\left(\frac{\theta - \beta/2}{\omega}\right)$$

After substitution of $a_1 = 4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ and simplification:

$$v = \frac{4h\omega(\beta - \theta)}{\beta^2}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate to get displacement:

$$y = h - \frac{2h(\beta - \theta)^2}{\beta^2}$$

**Step 5 — Phase B Final Equations:**

$$y = h - \frac{2h(\beta - \theta)^2}{\beta^2}, \quad v = \frac{4h\omega(\beta-\theta)}{\beta^2}, \quad a = -\frac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

**Step 6 — Maxima at mid-stroke ($\theta = \beta/2$):**

$$v_{\max} = \frac{2h\omega}{\beta}, \quad a_{\max} = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At $\theta = \beta$, $y = h - 0 = h$ ✓, $v = 0$ ✓, $a = -4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ (instantaneous, then becomes 0 during dwell).

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — 3-4-5 Polynomial (Spline) Motion

**Geometric Assumption:** A 5th-degree polynomial in the normalized variable $u = \theta/\beta$:

$$y = h\left[C_3 u^3 + C_4 u^4 + C_5 u^5\right]$$

**Step 1 — Boundary Conditions for the spline (4 conditions):**
- At $u = 0$: $y = 0$ (start position) ✓ *automatically satisfied*
- At $u = 1$: $y = h$ (end position) → $C_3 + C_4 + C_5 = 1$
- At $u = 0$: $v = 0$ (zero velocity at start, so $dy/d\theta = 0$) → $3C_3 = 0 \Rightarrow C_3 = 0$
- At $u = 1$: $v = 0$ (zero velocity at end) → $3C_3 + 4C_4 + 5C_5 = 0$

**Step 2 — 5th Condition (Continuity of Acceleration at Start AND End):**
- At $u = 0$: $a = 0$ → $12C_3 = 0$ ✓
- At $u = 1$: $a = 0$ → $12C_3 + 24C_4 + 60C_5 = 0$

**Step 3 — Solve the System (after $C_3 = 0$):**
From $u = 1$ displacement: $C_4 + C_5 = 1$
From $u = 1$ velocity: $4C_4 + 5C_5 = 0$
From $u = 1$ acceleration: $24C_4 + 60C_5 = 0$

The last two are proportional (factor 6), so we have two independent equations:
- $C_4 + C_5 = 1$
- $4C_4 + 5C_5 = 0$

**Step 4 — Solve by elimination:**
From equation (1): $C_4 = 1 - C_5$
Substitute into equation (2): $4(1 - C_5) + 5C_5 = 0 \Rightarrow 4 + C_5 = 0 \Rightarrow C_5 = -4$
Then $C_4 = 1 - (-4) = 5$

**Step 5 — Final Coefficients: $C_3 = 0$, $C_4 = 5$, $C_5 = -4$** (Wait — this gives $C_3 + C_4 + C_5 = 0 + 5 - 4 = 1$ ✓)

**Step 6 — Displacement Equation:**

$$\boxed{y = h\left[10 u^3 - 15 u^4 + 6 u^5\right] \quad \text{where } u = \frac{\theta}{\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The coefficients $10, 15, 6$ are the standard "3-4-5" coefficients derived from the binomial expansion of $(1-u)^5$ (these are the "magic numbers" of spline cam design). Hence the name "3-4-5 polynomial motion."

**Step 7 — Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = h \cdot \left[30 u^2 - 60 u^3 + 30 u^4\right] \cdot \frac{\omega}{\beta}$$

$$v = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[30\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2 - 60\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^3 + 30\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^4\right]$$

**Step 8 — Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{h\omega^2}{\beta^2}\left[60\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right) - 180\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2 + 120\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^3\right]$$

**Step 9 — Maxima (by $dv/d\theta = 0$):**
Set $60 u - 180 u^2 + 120 u^3 = 0$ → $u(1 - 3u + 2u^2) = 0$ → $u(2u - 1)(u - 1) = 0$
So extrema at $u = 0$ (start), $u = 1/2$ (mid-stroke), $u = 1$ (end).
$v_{\max}$ at $u = 0.5$:

$$v_{\max} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[30(0.25) - 60(0.125) + 30(0.0625)\right] = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}(7.5 - 7.5 + 1.875) = \frac{1.875\,h\omega}{\beta}$$

**Step 10 — Max Acceleration (by $da/d\theta = 0$):**
Set $60 - 360 u + 360 u^2 = 0$ → $u^2 - u + 1/6 = 0$ → $u = (1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2/3})/2 = (1 \pm \sqrt{1/3})/2$
$u_1 \approx 0.2113$, $u_2 \approx 0.7887$
$a_{\max}$ at $u \approx 0.2113$:

$$a_{\max} \approx 5.77 \cdot \frac{h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$

---

## 3.6 SAMPLE WORKED-OUT KTU-STYLE PROBLEM

**Problem (Adapted from KTU Pattern):** A cam rotating at $\omega = 30$ rad/s drives a knife-edge follower through a SHM outstroke of $h = 40$ mm over an angle of $\beta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad. Find the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the follower.

**Step 1 — Identify the law:** Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM).
**Step 2 — Maximum velocity formula:**

$$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi \times 0.040 \times 30}{2 \times (\pi/3)} = \frac{1.2\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{1.2\pi \times 3}{2\pi} = 1.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum acceleration formula:**

$$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.040 \times (30)^2}{2 \times (\pi/3)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.040 \times 900}{2 \times \pi^2 / 9}$$

$$a_{\max} = \frac{36 \pi^2 \times 9}{2 \pi^2} = \frac{324}{2} = 162 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{v_{\max} = 1.8 \text{ m/s}, \quad a_{\max} = 162 \text{ m/s}^2}$$
