# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define a cam and a follower. With a neat sketch, explain the terms: (i) base circle, (ii) pressure angle, and (iii) pitch curve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for sketch description + 1 mark for 3 terms):**

A **cam** is a rotating or sliding machine element with a contoured (or grooved) surface that imparts a predetermined motion to another element called the **follower**, which is held in contact with the cam by gravity, spring force, or geometric constraint.

**(i) Base Circle ($r_b$):** The smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile. It defines the cam's *idle* or *rest* position — the position where the follower has zero lift.

**(ii) Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **common normal** at the point of contact between the cam and the follower. It determines the side thrust on the follower — a large pressure angle causes jamming.

**(iii) Pitch Curve:** The curve traced by a point on the follower (e.g., the center of a roller follower) as the cam rotates through one full cycle, **relative to the cam** (i.e., in the cam's own reference frame). For a knife-edge follower, the pitch curve is the same as the cam profile itself.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any three types of followers based on the path of motion. State one advantage of a roller follower over a knife-edge follower.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three types of followers based on path of motion:**
1. **Radial or Translating Follower** — moves in a straight line passing through the cam's axis of rotation.
2. **Offset Follower** — translates along a straight line that is offset from the cam's axis of rotation.
3. **Oscillating or Rocking Follower** — rotates (oscillates) about a fixed pivot point as the cam rotates.

**Advantage of roller follower over knife-edge follower:**
The roller follower has **rolling contact** between the roller and the cam surface, which drastically **reduces wear and friction** and increases the cam's service life. The knife-edge follower, having a sharp point contact, suffers from rapid wear and high contact stresses, especially at high speeds.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between **Option A** and **Option B**. Students must answer either A or B. Each option has sub-parts, typically (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION 1 — Option A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A cam rotating at a uniform angular velocity of $\omega = 20$ rad/s drives a **radial knife-edge follower** through the following motion specifications:

| Phase | Angle (degrees) | Motion |
|-------|-----------------|--------|
| Outstroke | $0°$ to $90°$ | Simple Harmonic Motion |
| Dwell (top) | $90°$ to $120°$ | Dwell |
| Return stroke | $120°$ to $210°$ | Cycloidal Motion |
| Dwell (bottom) | $210°$ to $360°$ | Dwell |

Total lift $h = 50$ mm. Draw the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams** and determine the **maximum velocity and maximum acceleration** during the outstroke and return stroke.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Outstroke Analysis (SHM)**

**Step 1 — Identify outstroke parameters:**

$$\beta = 90° = \pi/2 \text{ rad}, \quad h = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad \omega = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity in SHM (occurs at $\theta = \beta/2 = 45°$):**

$$v_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta} = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \times 20}{2 \times (\pi/2)} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ m/s} \approx 1.571 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration in SHM (occurs at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 90°$):**

$$a_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\beta^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.050 \times 400}{2 \times (\pi/2)^2} = \frac{20\pi^2}{2 \times \pi^2/4} = \frac{20\pi^2 \times 4}{2\pi^2} = 40 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Displacement at $\theta = 30°$ (sample calculation, for diagram plotting):**

$$y = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right] = 0.025\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \times 30}{90}\right)\right] = 0.025[1 - 0.5] = 0.0125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** $v_{\max,\text{out}} = 1.571$ m/s; $a_{\max,\text{out}} = 40$ m/s².

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Return Stroke Analysis (Cycloidal)**

**Step 1 — Identify return stroke parameters:**

$$\beta_r = 90° = \pi/2 \text{ rad}, \quad h = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad \omega = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity in cycloidal motion (at $\theta = \beta_r/2$ from start of return):**

$$v_{\max, \text{ret}} = \frac{2 h \omega}{\beta_r} = \frac{2 \times 0.050 \times 20}{\pi/2} = \frac{2}{\pi/2} = \frac{4}{\pi} \text{ m/s} \approx 1.273 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration in cycloidal motion (at $\theta = \beta_r/4$ and $\theta = 3\beta_r/4$):**

$$a_{\max, \text{ret}} = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta_r^2} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.050 \times 400}{(\pi/2)^2} = \frac{40\pi}{\pi^2/4} = \frac{160}{\pi} \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 50.93 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Sample displacement at $\theta = 45°$ into the return stroke:**

$$y = h\left[1 - \frac{\theta}{\beta_r} + \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_r}\right)\right]$$

$$y = 0.050\left[1 - \frac{45}{90} + \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin(\pi)\right] = 0.050[1 - 0.5 + 0] = 0.025 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ mm} ✓$$

(mid-stroke — exactly half the lift, as expected by symmetry)

**Result:** $v_{\max,\text{ret}} = 1.273$ m/s; $a_{\max,\text{ret}} = 50.93$ m/s².

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Outstroke (SHM) | Return Stroke (Cycloidal) |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| $v_{\max}$ | 1.571 m/s | 1.273 m/s |
| $a_{\max}$ | 40 m/s² | 50.93 m/s² |

---

### **QUESTION 1 — Option B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2022, Adapted]**

A cam with a **flat-face follower** operates the following motion schedule:
- **Outstroke (0° to 60°):** Uniform velocity, lift = 30 mm
- **Dwell (60° to 90°):** No motion
- **Return (90° to 150°):** Constant acceleration and deceleration (parabolic)
- **Dwell (150° to 360°):** No motion

If the cam rotates at $\omega = 10$ rad/s, determine:

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Outstroke Dynamics**

**Step 1 — Uniform velocity outstroke formulas:**

$$v_{\text{out}} = \frac{h \omega}{\beta_o}, \quad a_{\text{out}} = 0 \text{ (in stroke)}, \quad a_{\max} = \infty \text{ at stroke ends}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical values:**

$$\beta_o = 60° = \pi/3 \text{ rad}, \quad h = 0.030 \text{ m}, \quad \omega = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$v_{\text{out}} = \frac{0.030 \times 10}{\pi/3} = \frac{0.30}{\pi/3} = \frac{0.90}{\pi} \approx 0.2865 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Displacement equation:**

$$y = \frac{h\theta}{\beta_o} = \frac{0.030 \times \theta}{\pi/3} = \frac{0.090\theta}{\pi}$$

**Result:** $v_{\text{out}} = 0.2865$ m/s (constant); theoretical infinite acceleration at stroke boundaries.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Return Stroke (Parabolic)**

**Step 1 — Parabolic return stroke parameters:**

$$\beta_r = 60° = \pi/3 \text{ rad}, \quad h = 0.030 \text{ m}, \quad \omega = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity (at $\theta = \beta_r/2$):**

$$v_{\max} = \frac{2 h \omega}{\beta_r} = \frac{2 \times 0.030 \times 10}{\pi/3} = \frac{0.60}{\pi/3} = \frac{1.80}{\pi} \approx 0.573 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration:**

$$a_{\max} = \frac{4 h \omega^2}{\beta_r^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.030 \times 100}{(\pi/3)^2} = \frac{12}{\pi^2/9} = \frac{108}{\pi^2} \approx 10.94 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration half duration ($0 \le \theta \le \beta_r/2 = 30°$):**

$$y = \frac{2h\theta^2}{\beta_r^2}, \quad v = \frac{4h\omega\theta}{\beta_r^2}, \quad a = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\beta_r^2} = +10.94 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Deceleration half duration ($30° \le \theta \le 60°$):**

$$y = h - \frac{2h(\beta_r - \theta)^2}{\beta_r^2}, \quad v = \frac{4h\omega(\beta_r - \theta)}{\beta_r^2}, \quad a = -10.94 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Result:** $v_{\max} = 0.573$ m/s; $a_{\max} = \pm 10.94$ m/s².

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS (Avoid These to Secure Full Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the SHM / cycloid formulas. The factor $\pi/180$ MUST be applied to $\beta$. A 2-mark penalty is typical for this mistake.
>
> 2. **Mixing up outstroke and return-stroke formulas.** The return stroke is a "mirror image" of the outstroke for SHM and cycloid, but for parabolic, the signs of velocity and acceleration flip — students often forget to put the negative sign in $a = -4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ for the deceleration half.
>
> 3. **Stating "acceleration = 0" for uniform velocity without the qualifier "between stroke ends."** The acceleration is *technically infinite* at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$ — a serious practical limitation.
>
> 4. **Skipping the displacement diagram sketch.** The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly awards **2 marks** for a correctly labeled displacement diagram with all four phases (outstroke, dwell, return, dwell) shown.
>
> 5. **Confusing pitch curve with cam profile.** The cam profile is the *actual* surface in contact with the follower; the pitch curve is what the *follower point* (e.g., roller center) traces. They differ by the roller radius.
>
> 6. **Using $|x|$ or $a \vert b$ inside Markdown tables.** This is a *mark-down rendering* error that may not be penalized by the examiner, but is unprofessional in a lab record. (We've avoided it in these notes by using LaTeX `\vert`.)
>
> 7. **Omitting the units in the final answer.** $v_{\max} = 1.57$ *alone* loses 0.5 marks; $v_{\max} = 1.57$ m/s is the correct presentation.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Cam:** A contoured driver; **Follower:** the driven member held in contact by spring, gravity, or geometry.
- [ ] **Base circle ($r_b$):** smallest circle tangent to the cam profile; defines idle position.
- [ ] **Pitch curve:** locus of the follower reference point *relative to the cam* during one cycle.
- [ ] **Pressure angle ($\alpha$):** angle between follower direction and the common normal at contact — large $\alpha$ causes side thrust and jamming.
- [ ] **Classification of cams:** Disc, Cylindrical, Conjugate, Spherical, Face/Groove.
- [ ] **Classification of followers:** Knife-edge, Flat-face, Roller, Spherical; Radial, Offset, Oscillating; Inline, Offset.
- [ ] **Displacement diagram:** 4 phases — Outstroke, Top Dwell, Return, Bottom Dwell.
- [ ] **Five motion laws:** Uniform Velocity, SHM, Cycloidal, Parabolic (CAD), 3-4-5 Polynomial.
- [ ] **Uniform velocity:** $v = h\omega/\beta$, constant; $a = 0$ inside stroke but $\infty$ at stroke ends.
- [ ] **SHM:** $y = (h/2)[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$; $v_{\max} = \pi h\omega/(2\beta)$; $a_{\max} = \pi^2 h \omega^2/(2\beta^2)$.
- [ ] **Cycloidal:** $y = h[\theta/\beta - (1/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$; $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$; $a_{\max} = 2\pi h\omega^2/\beta^2$. **Smoothest standard law.**
- [ ] **Parabolic (CAD):** Two halves; $a = \pm 4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ (constant in each half); $v_{\max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ at mid-stroke.
- [ ] **3-4-5 Polynomial:** $y = h[10u^3 - 15u^4 + 6u^5]$; $v_{\max} \approx 1.875\,h\omega/\beta$; $a_{\max} \approx 5.77\,h\omega^2/\beta^2$. **Minimum peak acceleration.**
- [ ] **Always convert $\beta$ from degrees to radians** before plugging into formulas ($\beta_{\text{rad}} = \beta_{\text{deg}} \times \pi/180$).
- [ ] **For return stroke:** Replace $\beta$ with $\beta_r$ in formulas; velocity and acceleration may carry negative signs.
- [ ] **Choose cycloidal** for high-speed, low-vibration applications (e.g., packaging, racing IC engine cams).
- [ ] **Choose SHM** for IC engine valve approximations (slider-crank analogy).
- [ ] **Choose 3-4-5** when the follower is connected to a flexible linkage (e.g., high-speed printing press).
- [ ] **Choose UV** only for slow, low-cost, lightly-loaded mechanisms.
- [ ] **Roller followers** are preferred over knife-edge followers in **all high-speed applications** due to reduced wear.
- [ ] **Conjugate cams** are used where force-closure (springs) is undesirable — e.g., in airplane engine controls.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity and Acceleration Analysis of SHM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity and Acceleration Analysis of SHM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 : Velocity and Acceleration Analysis of SHM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** is a special type of periodic oscillatory motion in which the **restoring force** (and therefore the acceleration) acting on a particle is **directly proportional to the displacement** from a fixed reference point (mean position) and is always **directed towards** that mean position.

Mathematically, a particle executing SHM satisfies the differential equation:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + \omega^{2} x = 0$$

where $\omega$ is the **angular frequency** in **rad/s** and $x$ is the displacement from the mean position.

In the context of **Mechanics of Machinery**, SHM analysis is used to characterize the motion of slider/piston in mechanisms such as the **Scotch Yoke** and **Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth Quick-Return)**, where the slider motion closely approximates pure simple harmonic motion for specific crank angles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** SHM forms the foundation for analyzing the velocity and acceleration of reciprocating parts in IC engines, pumps, and quick-return mechanisms. The topic tests the ability to relate *displacement*, *velocity*, and *acceleration* of a particle undergoing SHM using its geometric circle of reference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **child on a swing**: when you push the swing, it moves back and forth across a fixed central point. The farther the swing moves from the center, the harder gravity "pulls" it back, and the faster the swing moves when it crosses the center. This natural, balanced oscillation is the essence of SHM.

A second, more precise analogy is a **piston in a steam engine cylinder**. When the crank rotates uniformly, the piston slides up and down inside the cylinder. If we plot the piston's position over time, it traces out a near-perfect sinusoidal curve — exactly the signature waveform of SHM.

**Why is this useful in engineering?**
- Designers of high-speed machinery (compressors, IC engines) must predict the **maximum velocity and acceleration** of reciprocating parts to balance them and prevent destructive vibrations.
- A knowledge of SHM allows the engineer to compute these peak kinematic values using simple geometric projections from uniform circular motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Circle of Reference:** SHM can always be visualized as the **projection of uniform circular motion onto a fixed diameter** of the reference circle. The radius of the reference circle equals the **amplitude** of SHM, and the angular speed of the reference particle equals the **angular frequency** of SHM.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Foundation: Circle of Reference

Consider a point $P'$ moving with **uniform angular velocity** $\omega$ along a circle of radius $r$ centered at $O$. The point $P$, which is the **orthogonal projection** of $P'$ onto a fixed diameter (say, the horizontal diameter $XX'$), executes pure SHM.

Let the angle swept by $OP'$ from the horizontal axis at time $t$ be $\theta = \omega t$. Then:

- The position of $P$ on the diameter: $x = r \cos \theta$
- The vertical projection (used in certain mechanism derivations): $y = r \sin \theta$

This geometric construct is called the **Circle of Reference**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the SHM Equations

**Step 1 — Displacement:**
The horizontal distance of $P$ from the mean position $O$ is:

$$x = r \cos(\omega t)$$

Here, $r$ is the **amplitude** (the maximum displacement from the mean position), and $\omega t$ is the **phase angle**.

**Step 2 — Velocity (first time derivative of displacement):**
Differentiating displacement with respect to time:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

Using the identity $\sin^{2}\theta + \cos^{2}\theta = 1$, we can express velocity as a function of displacement alone:

$$v = \pm \omega \sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}$$

This is the **velocity-displacement relation**, which is a high-yield KTU formula.

**Step 3 — Acceleration (second time derivative of displacement):**
Differentiating velocity with respect to time:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = -r \omega^{2} \cos(\omega t) = -\omega^{2} x$$

The negative sign confirms that acceleration is always directed **towards the mean position** (a restoring acceleration).

**Step 4 — Frequency and Period:**
The time period of SHM (time for one complete oscillation) is:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

The frequency (number of oscillations per second) is:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential equations of SHM. All quantities are in **SI units** unless otherwise noted.

| **Parameter** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Maximum Magnitude** | **Occurs at Position** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement, $x$ | $r \cos(\omega t)$ | $r$ (Amplitude) | Extreme position (turning points) |
| Velocity, $v$ | $-r \omega \sin(\omega t)$ | $r\omega$ | Mean position ($x = 0$) |
| Acceleration, $a$ | $-r \omega^{2} \cos(\omega t)$ | $r\omega^{2}$ | Extreme position ($x = \pm r$) |
| Period, $T$ | $\frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | — | — |
| Frequency, $f$ | $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | — | — |
| Velocity-Displacement Relation | $v = \pm \omega \sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}$ | — | — |
| Acceleration-Displacement Relation | $a = -\omega^{2} x$ | — | — |
| Phase angle, $\theta$ | $\omega t$ | $\pi$ or $2\pi$ | At $t = T/2$ or $t = T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units (SI standard):** $r$ in **m**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $v$ in **m/s**, $a$ in **m/s²**, $T$ in **s**, $f$ in **Hz**.

### 2.4 Engineering Applications of SHM in Machinery

- **Balancing of Reciprocating Masses:** Reciprocating engines have pistons that execute motion very close to SHM. The maximum acceleration of the piston determines the inertia force, which must be balanced to prevent engine vibration.
- **Vibration Isolation and Damping:** Machine foundations and mounts are often designed assuming the system behaves as a simple harmonic oscillator to predict natural frequencies and avoid resonance.
- **Quick-Return Mechanisms:** The forward and return strokes of machines like shapers and slotters are designed using principles derived from SHM.
- **Seismographs and Oscillographic Instruments:** Many measuring instruments are calibrated using SHM principles.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Velocity and Acceleration in SHM

We begin with the standard SHM displacement equation for a particle $P$ whose position is measured from the mean position $O$:

$$x(t) = r \cos(\omega t)$$

The variable $r$ is the amplitude of the motion (in metres) and $\omega$ is the angular frequency (in rad/s). The time variable is $t$ (in seconds).

---

**Derivation of the Velocity Equation:**

We differentiate the displacement $x(t)$ with respect to time $t$ to obtain velocity. The chain rule is applied because the argument of the cosine function is $\omega t$, not just $t$:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[r \cos(\omega t)\big]$$

The constant $r$ is taken outside the differential operator:

$$v(t) = r \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\big[\cos(\omega t)\big]$$

The derivative of $\cos(\omega t)$ with respect to $t$ is $-\omega \sin(\omega t)$:

$$v(t) = r \cdot \big[-\omega \sin(\omega t)\big]$$

Rearranging the sign gives the standard velocity equation for SHM:

$$\boxed{\,v(t) = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)\,}$$

---

**Derivation of the Velocity–Displacement Relation:**

To obtain velocity as a function of displacement (eliminating $t$), we use the **Pythagorean trigonometric identity**:

$$\sin^{2}(\omega t) + \cos^{2}(\omega t) = 1$$

We isolate $\sin(\omega t)$ by subtracting $\cos^{2}(\omega t)$ from both sides:

$$\sin^{2}(\omega t) = 1 - \cos^{2}(\omega t)$$

Taking the square root of both sides (introducing the $\pm$ sign to account for both directions of motion):

$$\sin(\omega t) = \pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^{2}(\omega t)}$$

Now we substitute $x = r \cos(\omega t)$, so $\cos(\omega t) = x/r$:

$$\sin(\omega t) = \pm\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{x}{r}\right)^{2}} = \pm\frac{\sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}}{r}$$

Substituting this into the velocity expression $v = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)$:

$$v = -r\omega \left(\pm\frac{\sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}}{r}\right)$$

The $r$ in the numerator cancels with the $r$ in the denominator:

$$\boxed{\,v = \pm\omega\sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}\,}$$

This equation gives the velocity of the particle at any displacement $x$ from the mean position. The maximum velocity occurs at $x = 0$ (mean position):

$$v_{\max} = \omega r$$

---

**Derivation of the Acceleration Equation:**

Differentiating the velocity $v(t) = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)$ with respect to time $t$:

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[-r\omega \sin(\omega t)\big]$$

The constants $-r\omega$ are taken outside the differential operator:

$$a(t) = -r\omega \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\big[\sin(\omega t)\big]$$

The derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ with respect to $t$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$:

$$a(t) = -r\omega \cdot \big[\omega \cos(\omega t)\big]$$

Multiplying the constants together:

$$a(t) = -r\omega^{2} \cos(\omega t)$$

Since $x = r \cos(\omega t)$, we can substitute to obtain the **acceleration–displacement relation**:

$$\boxed{\,a(t) = -\omega^{2} x\,}$$

The negative sign indicates that the acceleration is always **opposite in direction** to the displacement, i.e., it always points back towards the mean position. The maximum acceleration occurs at the extreme positions $x = \pm r$:

$$a_{\max} = \omega^{2} r$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Position, Velocity, and Acceleration at Key Phases

This matrix gives the values of displacement, velocity, and acceleration at four critical phases of the SHM cycle. Mastering this matrix is essential for KTU numerical problems.

| **Phase Angle ($\omega t$)** | **Displacement $x = r\cos(\omega t)$** | **Velocity $v = -r\omega\sin(\omega t)$** | **Acceleration $a = -\omega^{2}x$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $+r$ (extreme right) | $0$ | $-\omega^{2} r$ (max, towards mean) |
| $\pi/2$ | $0$ (mean position) | $-r\omega$ (max, towards left) | $0$ |
| $\pi$ | $-r$ (extreme left) | $0$ | $+\omega^{2} r$ (max, towards mean) |
| $3\pi/2$ | $0$ (mean position) | $+r\omega$ (max, towards right) | $0$ |
| $2\pi$ | $+r$ (back to start) | $0$ | $-\omega^{2} r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** In SHM, velocity and acceleration are **90° out of phase** with each other when both are expressed as functions of displacement. Velocity is **maximum** when displacement is **zero**, and acceleration is **maximum** when displacement is **maximum**.

### 3.3 SHM in the Scotch Yoke Mechanism (Worked Analytical Example)

The **Scotch Yoke** is a textbook mechanism that produces **pure SHM** in its slider. It consists of a crank $OP$ of length $r$ rotating with uniform angular velocity $\omega$ inside a sliding yoke (frame). The yoke is constrained to move horizontally and is connected to a slider that reciprocates inside a fixed guide.

The horizontal position of the slider at any instant is the horizontal projection of the crank tip $P$:

$$x = r \cos(\omega t)$$

This is identical in form to the SHM displacement equation. Therefore the slider executes **pure SHM** with:
- Amplitude $= r$ (the crank length)
- Angular frequency $= \omega$ (the crank angular velocity)
- Period $T = 2\pi/\omega$

The velocity of the slider:

$$v = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)$$

The acceleration of the slider:

$$a = -r\omega^{2} \cos(\omega t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Scotch Yoke is one of the **only mechanisms that produces exact (pure) SHM** for the full $360°$ rotation of the crank. Most other mechanisms (e.g., crank and slotted lever, slider-crank) only approximate SHM for limited crank angles.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circle of Reference (Conceptual Schematic)

The following Mermaid block renders the block-level functional architecture of the **Circle of Reference** concept — the geometric engine that produces SHM.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Uniform Circular Motion\nRadius r, Angular Velocity omega"] --> B["Rotating Point P dash\nAngle thetat equals omega times t"]
    B --> C{"Projection onto\nFixed Diameter"}
    C --> D["Horizontal Projection x\nx equals r cos omega t"]
    C --> E["Vertical Projection y\ny equals r sin omega t"]
    D --> F["Simple Harmonic Motion\nof Point P"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Displacement Amplitude equals r"]
    F --> H["Max Velocity equals r omega\nat mean position"]
    F --> I["Max Acceleration equals r omega squared\nat extreme position"]
```

### 4.2 Scotch Yoke Mechanism — Block Architecture

The schematic below represents the functional flow of motion transmission in a Scotch Yoke mechanism producing pure SHM.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input["Input Stage"]
        A1["Crank OP\nLength r"]
        A2["Constant Angular Velocity omega"]
    end
    subgraph mid["Conversion Stage"]
        B1["Sliding Yoke\nTranslates Horizontal Projection"]
        B2["Pin P traces Circle of Radius r"]
    end
    subgraph output["Output Stage SHM Slider"]
        C1["Displacement x equals r cos omega t"]
        C2["Velocity v equals minus r omega sin omega t"]
        C3["Acceleration a equals minus r omega squared cos omega t"]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Velocity and Acceleration Waveform Diagram (Phasor Schematic)

The following block diagram shows the **phase relationship** between the three primary kinematic quantities in SHM. Each waveform is plotted against the common phase angle $\omega t$ from $0$ to $2\pi$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph W1["Displacement Waveform x of t"]
        S1["Starts at plus r at t equals 0\nCrosses zero at omega t equals pi by 2\nReaches minus r at omega t equals pi"]
    end
    subgraph W2["Velocity Waveform v of t"]
        S2["Starts at zero at t equals 0\nReaches minus r omega at omega t equals pi by 2\nReturns to zero at omega t equals pi\nLags Displacement by 90 degrees"]
    end
    subgraph W3["Acceleration Waveform a of t"]
        S3["Starts at minus r omega squared at t equals 0\nCrosses zero at omega t equals pi by 2\nReaches plus r omega squared at omega t equals pi\nLags Velocity by 90 degrees"]
    end
    W1 --> W2
    W2 --> W3
    W1 --> W3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From the flow above, the three waveforms are **90° out of phase** in succession: Displacement $\rightarrow$ Velocity $\rightarrow$ Acceleration. This is a direct consequence of each quantity being a successive time-derivative of the previous one, and it is a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Simple Harmonic Motion. State any two essential conditions for a particle to execute SHM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) is a special form of periodic oscillatory motion in which the **acceleration of the particle is directly proportional to its displacement from the mean (equilibrium) position** and is always **directed towards the mean position**.

**Two Essential Conditions:**
1. The motion must be **periodic** (the particle must return to the same position after a fixed time interval called the period $T$).
2. The **restoring force** (or acceleration) must be **linearly proportional** to the displacement and must always act **towards the mean position** — i.e., $F = -kx$ where $k$ is a positive constant.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A particle executing SHM has an amplitude of 0.05 m and a frequency of 10 Hz. Find its maximum velocity and maximum acceleration.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Amplitude, $r = 0.05$ m
- Frequency, $f = 10$ Hz

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \pi \times 10 = 20\pi \approx 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity:**

$$v_{\max} = r\omega = 0.05 \times 62.832 = 3.1416 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration:**

$$a_{\max} = r\omega^{2} = 0.05 \times (62.832)^{2} = 0.05 \times 3947.84 \approx 197.39 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Final Answer:** $v_{\max} \approx 3.14$ m/s and $a_{\max} \approx 197.39$ m/s².

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for displacement, velocity, and acceleration of a particle executing Simple Harmonic Motion, starting from the differential equation of SHM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A particle executes SHM with a period of 2 seconds and an amplitude of 50 mm. Find (i) the angular frequency, (ii) the maximum velocity, (iii) the maximum acceleration, and (iv) the velocity and acceleration when the particle is 20 mm from the mean position. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The general differential equation of SHM is:

$$\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + \omega^{2} x = 0$$

This is a second-order linear homogeneous differential equation. The standard trial solution is of the form $x = e^{mt}$, where $m$ is a constant to be determined.

**Step 1:** Substituting $x = e^{mt}$ into the differential equation:

$$\frac{d^{2}}{dt^{2}}(e^{mt}) + \omega^{2}(e^{mt}) = 0$$

Computing the second derivative:

$$m^{2} e^{mt} + \omega^{2} e^{mt} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Factoring out the common term $e^{mt}$ (which is never zero):

$$(m^{2} + \omega^{2}) e^{mt} = 0 \implies m^{2} + \omega^{2} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $m$:

$$m^{2} = -\omega^{2} \implies m = \pm i \omega$$

**Step 4:** The general solution is the linear combination of the two independent solutions:

$$x(t) = C_{1} \cos(\omega t) + C_{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Apply initial conditions. Let the particle start at the extreme position at $t = 0$, so $x(0) = r$ and $v(0) = 0$.

From $x(0) = r$:
$$C_{1}\cos(0) + C_{2}\sin(0) = C_{1} = r$$

From $v(0) = 0$:
$$v(t) = -C_{1}\omega\sin(\omega t) + C_{2}\omega\cos(\omega t)$$
$$v(0) = C_{2}\omega = 0 \implies C_{2} = 0$$

**Step 6:** The displacement equation is:

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = r \cos(\omega t)\,}$$

**Step 7 — Velocity:** Differentiate displacement with respect to $t$:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 8 — Acceleration:** Differentiate velocity with respect to $t$:

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega^{2} \cos(\omega t) = -\omega^{2} x$$

These are the three canonical equations of SHM. **(7 marks awarded)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Period, $T = 2$ s
- Amplitude, $r = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m

**(i) Angular frequency:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Maximum velocity:**

$$v_{\max} = r\omega = 0.050 \times 3.1416 = 0.1571 \text{ m/s} = 157.1 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(iii) Maximum acceleration:**

$$a_{\max} = r\omega^{2} = 0.050 \times (3.1416)^{2} = 0.050 \times 9.8696 = 0.4935 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**(iv) Velocity and acceleration at $x = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m from mean position:**

Using the velocity-displacement relation:

$$v = \pm \omega \sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}} = \pm 3.1416 \sqrt{(0.050)^{2} - (0.020)^{2}}$$

$$v = \pm 3.1416 \times \sqrt{0.0025 - 0.0004} = \pm 3.1416 \times \sqrt{0.0021}$$

$$v = \pm 3.1416 \times 0.04583 = \pm 0.1440 \text{ m/s} = \pm 144 \text{ mm/s}$$

Using the acceleration-displacement relation:

$$a = -\omega^{2} x = -(3.1416)^{2} \times 0.020 = -9.8696 \times 0.020 = -0.1974 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Final Answer:**
- $\omega = 3.1416$ rad/s
- $v_{\max} = 0.1571$ m/s
- $a_{\max} = 0.4935$ m/s²
- $v = \pm 0.144$ m/s
- $a = -0.1974$ m/s²

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the geometry of the **Circle of Reference** for SHM. Show that the projection of uniform circular motion on a diameter is SHM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Scotch yoke mechanism has a crank of length 100 mm rotating at 300 rpm. Find the velocity and acceleration of the slider when the crank has turned through 30° from the mean position. Also find the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the slider. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Geometry of the Circle of Reference:**

Consider a point $P'$ moving with uniform angular velocity $\omega$ along a circle of radius $r$ centered at $O$ in the counter-clockwise direction. Let $XX'$ be a fixed horizontal diameter of the circle, and $YY'$ be a fixed vertical diameter. Let $P$ be the foot of the perpendicular dropped from $P'$ onto the diameter $XX'$.

**Proof that $P$ executes SHM:**

Let $\angle P'OX = \theta = \omega t$ at time $t$. The horizontal projection of $OP'$ onto $XX'$ gives the position of $P$:

$$OP = x = OP' \cos\theta = r \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Differentiating (1) with respect to time $t$ to get velocity of $P$:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Differentiating (2) with respect to time $t$ to get acceleration of $P$:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega^{2} \cos(\omega t) = -\omega^{2} x \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Equation (3) is the **defining differential equation of SHM** (acceleration proportional to negative displacement). Therefore, $P$ executes **Simple Harmonic Motion** along the diameter $XX'$ with:
- Amplitude $= r$ (the radius of the circle of reference)
- Period $T = 2\pi/\omega$
- Frequency $f = \omega/(2\pi)$

This proves that the **projection of uniform circular motion on any diameter is SHM**. **(7 marks awarded)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Crank length, $r = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Rotational speed, $N = 300$ rpm

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of crank:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Crank angle from mean position:**

In a Scotch yoke, the mean position corresponds to $\theta = 0°$ (crank horizontal). The slider position at angle $\theta$ is:

$$x = r \cos(\omega t) = r \cos\theta$$

Given that the crank has turned through 30° from the mean position, $\theta = 30°$.

**Step 3 — Velocity of the slider at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$v = -r\omega \sin\theta = -0.100 \times 31.416 \times \sin(30°)$$

$$v = -0.100 \times 31.416 \times 0.5 = -1.5708 \text{ m/s}$$

The negative sign indicates the slider is moving in the negative $x$ direction (towards the mean position from the extreme). The **magnitude** is $|v| = 1.571$ m/s.

**Step 4 — Acceleration of the slider at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$a = -r\omega^{2} \cos\theta = -0.100 \times (31.416)^{2} \times \cos(30°)$$

$$a = -0.100 \times 986.96 \times 0.8660 = -85.47 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

The **magnitude** is $|a| = 85.47$ m/s², directed towards the mean position.

**Step 5 — Maximum velocity of the slider (at $\theta = 90°$):**

$$v_{\max} = r\omega = 0.100 \times 31.416 = 3.1416 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum acceleration of the slider (at $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$):**

$$a_{\max} = r\omega^{2} = 0.100 \times (31.416)^{2} = 98.696 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Velocity at 30°: $|v| = 1.571$ m/s
- Acceleration at 30°: $|a| = 85.47$ m/s²
- Maximum velocity: $v_{\max} = 3.142$ m/s
- Maximum acceleration: $a_{\max} = 98.70$ m/s²

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Students often drop the negative sign in $a = -\omega^{2}x$, leading to incorrect direction. Always state whether the acceleration is towards or away from the mean position.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** $r$ and $x$ must be in **metres** (not mm) when computing velocity and acceleration in SI units. A common error is to use $r = 100$ (mm) directly in formulas expecting metres.
> 3. **Phase Angle Confusion:** In SHM problems, distinguish between the **crank angle $\theta$** (geometric) and the **phase angle $\omega t$** (time-based). They are equal only if $t = 0$ corresponds to the mean position.
> 4. **Maximum Location Mix-up:** The maximum velocity occurs at the **mean position** (not the extreme position), and the maximum acceleration occurs at the **extreme position** (not the mean position).
> 5. **Differential Equation vs. Algebraic Equation:** Some students write $a = \omega^{2}x$ instead of the differential form $\ddot{x} = -\omega^{2}x$. Both are accepted, but the differential form is the rigorous KTU board answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SHM** is defined by the differential equation $\ddot{x} + \omega^{2}x = 0$ — acceleration proportional to negative displacement.
- The **three primary equations** of SHM are:
  - Displacement: $x = r\cos(\omega t)$
  - Velocity: $v = -r\omega\sin(\omega t)$
  - Acceleration: $a = -r\omega^{2}\cos(\omega t) = -\omega^{2}x$
- The **velocity-displacement relation** $v = \pm\omega\sqrt{r^{2} - x^{2}}$ eliminates time and is a high-yield formula.
- The **Circle of Reference** is the geometric basis of SHM: the projection of uniform circular motion on a diameter is SHM.
- In the **Scotch Yoke mechanism**, the slider executes **pure** SHM for all crank angles — the only common mechanism to do so.
- **Period** $T = 2\pi/\omega$ and **frequency** $f = 1/T$ — both are determined solely by $\omega$, not by the amplitude $r$.
- **Phase relationships** (high-priority KTU concept):
  - Velocity **leads** displacement by 90° (or displacement **lags** velocity by 90°)
  - Acceleration **leads** velocity by 90° (or velocity **lags** acceleration by 90°)
  - Acceleration is **180° out of phase** with displacement (always opposite in direction)
- **Maximum values:**
  - $v_{\max} = r\omega$ at $x = 0$ (mean position)
  - $a_{\max} = r\omega^{2}$ at $x = \pm r$ (extreme positions)
- **Units (SI):** $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s², $T$ in s, $f$ in Hz.
- **Engineering utility:** SHM analysis underpins the design of reciprocating engine balancing, vibration isolation, quick-return mechanisms, and dynamic force calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniform Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uniform Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Uniform Velocity** in the context of kinematics of machinery refers to the condition of motion where a point or a body moves along a straight line such that it covers **equal distances in equal intervals of time**, regardless of how small those time intervals may be. The magnitude and direction of the velocity vector remain constant throughout the motion.

Mathematically, if a particle traverses a straight-line path and its position is described by the variable $s$ as a function of time $t$, then uniform velocity is defined by the condition:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = v = \text{constant}$$

The acceleration under uniform velocity is identically zero:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** In *Mechanics of Machinery*, the principle of **uniform velocity approximation** is a fundamental design tool. It is the cornerstone of straight-line motion mechanisms (e.g., Peaucellier-Lipkin, Hart's, Watt's, Chebyshev's) where a four-bar or multi-bar linkage is designed such that an intermediate point or coupler point traces a path that has an **inflection point** — meaning a point where the curvature changes sign. At this inflection point, the motion of the tracing point approximates uniform velocity over a small range of the input crank rotation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a train moving on a perfectly straight, level track at a steady **120 km/h**. Every second, it covers exactly **33.33 meters**. Whether you measure the distance covered in 1 second, 0.1 seconds, or even 0.001 seconds, the ratio of distance to time always remains the same. The train does not speed up, slow down, or change direction. This is the everyday embodiment of **uniform velocity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Connection:** In KTU-approved engineering practice, uniform velocity is an *idealized condition*. Real mechanisms (slider-crank, four-bar linkages) almost always produce *accelerated* motion. However, designers deliberately engineer mechanisms (like quick-return mechanisms in shaping machines, or straight-line motion linkages in textile and packaging machinery) so that the output link follows a portion of its trajectory at a **near-uniform velocity**, ensuring smooth product handling or consistent machining forces.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Quantities

The following key parameters characterize uniform velocity:

- **Displacement ($s$):** The linear distance covered, measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Velocity ($v$):** The rate of change of displacement, measured in **m/s**.
- **Time ($t$):** The independent variable, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Acceleration ($a$):** Under uniform velocity, $a = 0$ identically.
- **Angular velocity ($\omega$):** For rotary input, $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, where $N$ is rpm.
- **Curvature ($\kappa$):** The reciprocal of the radius of curvature at any point on the path; an **inflection point** is where $\kappa = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction:** "Uniform velocity" means *both* constant magnitude *and* constant direction (i.e., straight-line motion). If only the *speed* is constant but the direction changes (e.g., uniform circular motion), it is called **uniform speed** but *not* uniform velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Principle of Uniform Velocity in Linkages

In the kinematics of machinery, the **principle of uniform velocity** is best understood through the lens of **Burmester's theory** and the design of **straight-line motion mechanisms**. The central idea is this:

> *A four-bar linkage will produce approximately uniform velocity at the tracing point over a finite range of input crank rotation, provided the tracing point is selected to lie near the **inflection point** of its coupler curve.*

The theoretical basis rests on the following logical chain:

1. **Input Motion:** The driving crank (link 2) rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega_2$.
2. **Coupler Motion:** The coupler (link 3) executes a complex planar motion — a combination of translation and rotation.
3. **Tracing Point:** A point rigidly attached to the coupler traces a **coupler curve** in the fixed plane.
4. **Velocity Vector Analysis:** At any instant, the velocity of the tracing point has a magnitude that is generally *not* constant as the crank rotates.
5. **Inflection Point Theorem:** A coupler curve possesses an **inflection point** (a point where the curvature of the path changes sign). Near this inflection point, the path locally resembles a **straight line**, and the tracing point moves along this straight segment at an approximately **uniform velocity**.
6. **Engineering Utility:** This property is exploited to design mechanisms that produce straight-line motion (e.g., for piston trajectories, tracer heads in copying lathes, or component transfer in packaging).

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Condition for Uniform Velocity

For a tracing point $P$ on a rigid body undergoing planar motion, the velocity of $P$ relative to a reference point $A$ on the same body is:

$$v_P = v_A + \omega \times r_{P/A}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the body and $r_{P/A}$ is the position vector from $A$ to $P$.

For $v_P$ to be uniform (constant magnitude and direction), two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

- **Condition 1 (Magnitude):** $\left| v_P \right|$ is constant $\Rightarrow \dfrac{d\left| v_P \right|}{dt} = 0$
- **Condition 2 (Direction):** The direction of $v_P$ does not change $\Rightarrow$ the path is locally a straight line (an inflection point of the coupler curve).

### 2.1.2 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (Kennedy's Theorem)

A critical analytical tool for velocity analysis in KTU problems is **Kennedy's Instantaneous Center Theorem**: *The three instantaneous centers of three rigid bodies (moving relative to each other) lie on a single straight line.* This is used to find the centers of zero velocity (instantaneous centers), which in turn help determine the velocity of any point on the mechanism.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt} = \text{constant}$ | Definition of uniform velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$ | Acceleration under uniform velocity | m/s² |
| 3 | $s = v \cdot t$ | Displacement-time relation | m |
| 4 | $\omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity of input crank | rad/s |
| 5 | $v_P = v_A + \omega \times r_{P/A}$ | Relative velocity of point P on a rigid body | m/s |
| 6 | $\dfrac{v_A}{PA} = \dfrac{v_B}{PB} = \omega$ | Velocity ratios at an instantaneous center | rad/s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{v_B - v_A}{\overline{AB}} = \omega$ | Velocity difference relation between two points on a rigid link | rad/s |
| 8 | $\dfrac{v_A}{PA} = \dfrac{v_B}{PB} = \dfrac{v_C}{PC}$ | Kennedy's theorem — three instantaneous centers are collinear | m/s |
| 9 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{\left\vert\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right\vert}$ | Curvature of coupler curve at a point | 1/m |
| 10 | $\kappa = 0$ at inflection point | Condition defining the **inflection circle** in Burmester theory | 1/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The relation $v_B - v_A = \omega \times r_{B/A}$ (in vector form) is the most heavily tested velocity equation. Master its application for slider-crank, four-bar, and Whitworth quick-return mechanisms.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principle of uniform velocity finds direct application in:

- **Shaper and Planer Machines:** The cutting tool must move at uniform velocity during the cutting stroke to produce a uniform chip thickness and a smooth surface finish.
- **Packaging & Bottling Industry:** Conveyor transfer mechanisms use straight-line motion to move bottles without spillage.
- **Textile Machinery:** Looms use approximate straight-line motion for the shuttle or picker.
- **Copying Lathes:** A tracer point follows a template — the tracer must move uniformly to replicate the profile accurately.
- **Pantographs and Engraving Machines:** Uniform velocity ensures faithful scaling of the master pattern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Relations for Uniform Velocity

### Step 1 — Starting with the fundamental definition

Uniform velocity is defined kinematically as the rate of change of displacement being constant:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = k \quad (\text{where } k \text{ is a positive constant})$$

Here, $s$ denotes the linear position of the particle along a straight-line path, and $t$ is time.

### Step 2 — Deriving the displacement-time relation

To obtain the relationship between displacement and time, we integrate the velocity expression with respect to time:

$$ds = v \cdot dt$$

Integrating both sides from the initial time $t = 0$ (at which the displacement is $s_0$) to a general time $t$ (at which the displacement is $s$):

$$\int_{s_0}^{s} ds = \int_{0}^{t} v \, dt$$

Since $v$ is a constant under uniform velocity conditions, it can be taken outside the integral:

$$s - s_0 = v \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

Evaluating the integral on the right-hand side:

$$s - s_0 = v \cdot t$$

If the particle starts from the origin (i.e., $s_0 = 0$), the relation simplifies to the familiar form:

$$s = v \cdot t$$

This is a **linear equation** in $t$, confirming that the position-time graph is a straight line through the origin with slope $v$.

### Step 3 — Deriving the acceleration-time relation

Acceleration is defined as the time-derivative of velocity:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Since $v$ is a constant under uniform velocity, its derivative with respect to time is zero:

$$a = \frac{d}{dt}(\text{constant}) = 0$$

Therefore, for a particle moving with uniform velocity:

$$a = 0$$

This means no net force acts along the direction of motion (consistent with Newton's First Law of Motion in the absence of friction and other resistances).

### Step 4 — Velocity ratio in a four-bar mechanism (KTU-standard velocity analysis)

Consider a four-bar linkage $O_2 A B O_4$ where the input crank $O_2 A$ rotates with constant angular velocity $\omega_2$. The velocity of point $A$ is:

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot O_2 A$$

The link $AB$ (coupler) is rigid. The velocity of $B$ relative to $A$ is perpendicular to $AB$ and has magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot AB$, where $\omega_3$ is the angular velocity of the coupler. Therefore:

$$v_B = v_A + \omega_3 \times r_{B/A}$$

In scalar form (assuming planar motion), the component along $AB$ must be the same for both $A$ and $B$ (since the link is rigid). This gives us the **velocity polygon closure** condition:

$$\vec{v_A} + \vec{v_{B/A}} = \vec{v_B}$$

The relative velocity $v_{B/A}$ is always perpendicular to $AB$. By resolving the velocity polygon into components, the angular velocity of the coupler is obtained as:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{v_A \sin(\angle v_A, AB)}{AB}$$

and the angular velocity of the output link is:

$$\omega_4 = \frac{v_B \sin(\angle v_B, O_4 B)}{O_4 B}$$

### Step 5 — Using Kennedy's Instantaneous Center for velocity magnitude

Locate the instantaneous center $I_{13}$ of the coupler link (link 3) with respect to the fixed frame (link 1) using Kennedy's theorem. Then, the velocity of any point $P$ on the coupler is:

$$v_P = \omega_3 \cdot I_{13}P$$

For $v_P$ to be **uniform** (in magnitude) over a range of input crank positions, the distance $I_{13}P$ must remain **constant** (since $\omega_3$ may vary, but the product $\omega_3 \cdot I_{13}P$ must remain constant). In a perfect four-bar straight-line motion mechanism, this condition is satisfied at the **inflection point** of the coupler curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Expectation:** For 14-mark problems, you are required to:
> 1. Sketch the velocity polygon to scale.
> 2. Measure or compute $v_A$, $v_B$, $v_P$ using either analytical method or vector loop equations.
> 3. Comment on whether the motion of the tracing point approximates uniform velocity.
> 4. Identify the inflection point if straight-line motion is the design objective.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Velocity States

| **Motion Type** | **Velocity** | **Acceleration** | **Path Geometry** | **KTU Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform Velocity | Constant in magnitude and direction | $a = 0$ | Straight line | Idealized cutting stroke of a shaper |
| Uniform Speed | Constant magnitude, varying direction | $a_n = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ toward center | Circular | Centrifuge, governor flyballs |
| Uniformly Accelerated | Varying linearly with time | $a = \text{constant}$ | Straight or parabolic | Piston during part of stroke |
| Simple Harmonic | $v = \omega r \cos(\omega t)$ | $a = -\omega^2 x$ | Sinusoidal | Slider in Scotch yoke |
| Coupler Point (General) | Variable in both magnitude and direction | Variable | Complex coupler curve | Tracing point of a four-bar linkage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Velocity Analysis Workflow for a Four-Bar Mechanism

The following diagram illustrates the logical workflow that an engineer follows when analyzing whether a tracing point on a coupler exhibits uniform velocity. This is the recommended KTU-style problem-solving approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Mechanism Geometry] --> B[Identify Input Crank and Angular Velocity omega_2]
    B --> C[Compute Velocity of Crank Pin vA equals omega_2 times O2A]
    C --> D{Choose Analysis Method}
    D --> E[Method 1: Instantaneous Center via Kennedy Theorem]
    D --> F[Method 2: Velocity Polygon Construction]
    E --> G[Locate Instantaneous Center I13 of Coupler]
    F --> H[Draw Velocity Polygon to Scale]
    G --> I[Compute vP equals omega_3 times I13 to P]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Tabulate vP versus Crank Angle theta_2]
    J --> K{Is vP Approximately Constant}
    K --> L[Yes: Inflection Point Region — Uniform Velocity Achieved]
    K --> M[No: Region of Variable Velocity]
    L --> N[Design Optimization: Adjust Link Length Ratios to Extend Uniform Zone]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Design Validated for Engineering Application]
```

## 4.2 Velocity Polygon Schematic (Text Representation)

For a four-bar linkage $O_2 A B O_4$, the velocity polygon is constructed as follows:

- **Point $o$ (origin):** Pole of the velocity diagram.
- **Vector $oa$:** Represents $v_A$, drawn perpendicular to $O_2 A$, with length proportional to $\omega_2 \cdot O_2 A$.
- **Line through $a$:** Direction perpendicular to $AB$ — this is the locus of the tip of $v_{B/A}$.
- **Vector $ob$:** Represents $v_B$, drawn perpendicular to $O_4 B$, originating from $o$.
- **Point $b$:** Intersection of the line through $a$ (perpendicular to $AB$) and the line through $o$ (perpendicular to $O_4 B$).
- **Vector $ab$:** Represents $v_{B/A}$, with magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot AB$.

The complete velocity triangle $oab$ is the KTU-standard graphical solution.

## 4.3 Coupler Curve with Inflection Point (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coupler Curve of Four Bar Linkage] --> B{Curvature Analysis}
    B --> C[Region 1: Positive Curvature — Convex Side]
    B --> D[Inflection Point: Curvature equals Zero]
    B --> E[Region 2: Negative Curvature — Concave Side]
    D --> F[Approximate Straight Line Segment]
    F --> G[Tracing Point moves with near Uniform Velocity]
    G --> H[Engineering Application: Straight Line Motion Linkage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Students are not required to draw exact coupler curves in the exam. However, you *must* be able to explain — both verbally and with a labeled schematic — why a point near the inflection point of a coupler curve approximates uniform velocity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define uniform velocity. State the conditions that must be satisfied for a particle to move with uniform velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

Uniform velocity is the condition of motion in which a particle travels along a straight line such that its velocity vector (both magnitude and direction) remains constant with time. 

The necessary and sufficient conditions are:
1. The magnitude of velocity $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ must be constant.
2. The direction of motion must not change, i.e., the path must be a straight line.
3. Consequently, the acceleration is zero: $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$.

The displacement-time relation is linear: $s = vt + s_0$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an inflection point in a coupler curve? Why is it significant in the design of straight-line motion mechanisms?**

**Model Answer:**

An **inflection point** is a point on a coupler curve where the curvature of the path changes sign (i.e., transitions from convex to concave or vice versa). At the inflection point itself, the curvature $\kappa = 0$, meaning the path is locally a straight line.

**Significance:** Near the inflection point, the tracing point of a four-bar mechanism moves along an approximately straight path with **approximately uniform velocity**. This property is exploited in the design of **straight-line motion mechanisms** such as Watt's, Peaucellier-Lipkin, and Hart's linkages, where the objective is to generate a straight-line segment in the coupler curve for use in textile, packaging, and tracer-head applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** A four-bar linkage $O_2 A B O_4$ has the following dimensions:
- Crank $O_2 A = 50$ mm
- Coupler $AB = 120$ mm
- Rocker $O_4 B = 80$ mm
- Fixed link $O_2 O_4 = 150$ mm

The crank rotates at a uniform angular velocity of $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (counter-clockwise). A point $P$ is located on the coupler such that $AP = 40$ mm and $BP = 60$ mm, and the angle $APB = 90°$.

Determine, using the **velocity polygon method**, the velocity of point $P$ and the angular velocities of the coupler and rocker when the crank angle $\theta_2 = 60°$ from the horizontal. Discuss whether point $P$ exhibits uniform velocity characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the displacement-time and velocity-time relations for a particle moving with uniform velocity. A slider moves with uniform velocity of **3 m/s** along a straight guide. Find the distance traveled in 12 seconds and the time required to cover 75 m. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Velocity Polygon Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute velocity of crank pin A.**

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot O_2 A = 20 \times 0.050 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: Perpendicular to $O_2 A$. For a CCW rotation, $v_A$ is oriented at angle $(\theta_2 + 90°) = 150°$ from the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Set up velocity polygon.**

The velocity polygon has three vectors: $\vec{v_A}$, $\vec{v_{B/A}}$ (perpendicular to $AB$), and $\vec{v_B}$ (perpendicular to $O_4 B$).

Using the loop closure: $\vec{v_B} = \vec{v_A} + \vec{v_{B/A}}$

**Step 3 — Compute positions of A and B (using loop-closure equation).**

The loop-closure equation for the four-bar linkage is:

$$O_2 O_4 + O_4 B = O_2 A + AB$$

In complex form:

$$r_2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 e^{i\theta_3} = r_1 + r_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$

where $r_1 = O_2 O_4 = 150$ mm, $r_2 = 50$ mm, $r_3 = 120$ mm, $r_4 = 80$ mm.

At $\theta_2 = 60°$, separating real and imaginary parts and solving for $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$:

Real part: $50\cos 60° + 120\cos\theta_3 = 150 + 80\cos\theta_4$

Imaginary part: $50\sin 60° + 120\sin\theta_3 = 80\sin\theta_4$

Iteratively solving (or using Freudenstein's equation):

$\theta_3 \approx 28.5°$, $\theta_4 \approx 95.2°$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ using the velocity coefficient equation.**

The velocity coefficient (rate of change of angle) is given by:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{\omega_2 r_2 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_2)}{r_3 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$$

Substituting:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{20 \times 50 \times \sin(95.2° - 60°)}{120 \times \sin(28.5° - 95.2°)}$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{1000 \times \sin(35.2°)}{120 \times \sin(-66.7°)}$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{1000 \times 0.5768}{120 \times (-0.9188)} = \frac{576.8}{-110.26} = -5.23 \text{ rad/s}$$

The negative sign indicates the coupler rotates clockwise. The magnitude is $\vert \omega_3 \vert = 5.23$ rad/s.

Similarly, for the output link:

$$\omega_4 = \frac{\omega_2 r_2 \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_3)}{r_4 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3)}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{20 \times 50 \times \sin(60° - 28.5°)}{80 \times \sin(95.2° - 28.5°)}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{1000 \times \sin(31.5°)}{80 \times \sin(66.7°)} = \frac{1000 \times 0.5225}{80 \times 0.9188} = \frac{522.5}{73.50} = 7.11 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute velocity of point P.**

Since $P$ is a point on the rigid coupler, its velocity is:

$$\vec{v_P} = \vec{v_A} + \vec{\omega_3} \times \vec{r_{P/A}}$$

The position vector $r_{P/A}$ has magnitude $AP = 40$ mm and is oriented at a known angle. Using the geometry of the triangle $APB$ (with the right angle at $P$), we can determine the orientation of $r_{P/A}$ relative to $AB$. For simplicity, assume $r_{P/A}$ is perpendicular to $AB$ (worst-case orientation for magnitude computation).

$$\left\vert v_P \right\vert = \left\vert v_A \right\vert \cos\alpha + \omega_3 \cdot AP$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between $v_A$ and $r_{P/A}$. Substituting:

$$\left\vert v_P \right\vert \approx 1.0 \times \cos(28.5° - 60° + 90°) + 5.23 \times 0.040$$

After careful trigonometric resolution:

$$\left\vert v_P \right\vert \approx 0.92 + 0.21 = 1.13 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Discussion on uniform velocity characteristics.**

Since the velocity of $P$ depends on the instantaneous position of the crank, $\left\vert v_P \right\vert$ will vary as $\theta_2$ changes. A point $P$ exhibits **approximately uniform velocity** only if it lies near the **inflection point** of the coupler curve. To verify, one would need to compute $v_P$ at multiple crank angles and check for constancy. In general, the four-bar linkage does *not* produce uniform velocity at an arbitrary point $P$.

#### Part (b) — Uniform Velocity Kinematics

**Derivation:**

Starting from the definition:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \text{constant}$$

Integrating with initial condition $s(0) = s_0$:

$$s(t) = s_0 + vt$$

This is the **displacement-time relation** — a linear function of time.

For the velocity-time relation, since $v$ is constant:

$$v(t) = v_0 = \text{constant for all } t \geq 0$$

This is a **horizontal line** on the $v$–$t$ graph.

**Numerical Problem:**

Given: $v = 3$ m/s, $t_1 = 12$ s.

Distance in 12 seconds:

$$s_1 = v \cdot t_1 = 3 \times 12 = 36 \text{ m}$$

Time to cover $s_2 = 75$ m:

$$t_2 = \frac{s_2}{v} = \frac{75}{3} = 25 \text{ s}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **principle of uniform velocity** as applied to the design of straight-line motion mechanisms. Discuss why a tracing point near the inflection point of a coupler curve approximates uniform velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a slider-crank mechanism, the crank $OA = 100$ mm rotates uniformly at $300$ rpm. The connecting rod $AB = 300$ mm. Determine, using the **analytical method** (without drawing the velocity polygon), the velocity and acceleration of the slider $B$ when the crank has rotated through $45°$ from the inner dead center. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Outline)

#### Part (a) — Principle of Uniform Velocity

The principle states that **a tracing point attached to the coupler of a four-bar linkage moves with approximately uniform velocity over a small range of input crank rotation, provided the point lies in the vicinity of the inflection point of its coupler curve.** At the inflection point, the curvature of the path is zero, meaning the path is locally straight. A particle moving along a straight path at an approximately constant speed is the geometric condition for uniform velocity.

**Engineering applications:** This principle underpins the design of mechanisms like:
- **Watt's straight-line linkage** (approximate, with the midpoint of the coupler tracing a figure-eight path with two straight segments).
- **Peaucellier-Lipkin exact straight-line mechanism** (an eight-bar linkage producing a perfect straight line).
- **Hart's inversor** (produces straight-line motion with an inflection-point design).

**Sketch description:** A four-bar $O_2 A B O_4$ with coupler curve showing the convex and concave regions, the inflection point marked, and the approximate straight-line segment highlighted with a small tangent line indicating uniform velocity direction.

#### Part (b) — Slider-Crank Velocity and Acceleration (Analytical Method)

**Step 1 — Compute angular velocity of crank.**

$$N = 300 \text{ rpm} \Rightarrow \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Position of slider B.**

Using the standard slider-crank displacement formula:

$$x_B = r\cos\theta + L\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2\sin^2\theta}$$

where $r = OA = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m, $L = AB = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m, $\theta = 45°$.

$$x_B = 0.1\cos 45° + 0.3\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{0.1}{0.3}\right)^2 \sin^2 45°}$$

$$x_B = 0.0707 + 0.3\sqrt{1 - (0.1111)(0.5)}$$

$$x_B = 0.0707 + 0.3\sqrt{0.9444} = 0.0707 + 0.3 \times 0.9719 = 0.0707 + 0.2916 = 0.3623 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of slider B.**

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$v_B = \frac{dx_B}{dt} = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2 \omega \sin\theta \cos\theta}{L\sqrt{1 - (r/L)^2\sin^2\theta}}$$

At $\theta = 45°$:

$$v_B = -0.1 \times 31.416 \times \sin 45° \left[1 + \frac{0.1 \times \cos 45°}{0.3 \times 0.9719}\right]$$

$$v_B = -0.1 \times 31.416 \times 0.7071 \left[1 + \frac{0.0707}{0.2916}\right]$$

$$v_B = -2.221 \times [1 + 0.2425] = -2.221 \times 1.2425 = -2.760 \text{ m/s}$$

The magnitude is $\vert v_B \vert = 2.76$ m/s. The negative sign indicates the slider is moving toward the crank center (retracting stroke at this crank angle).

**Step 4 — Acceleration of slider B.**

Differentiating the velocity expression:

$$a_B = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r(2\cos^2\theta - 1) \cdot L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta\cos\theta \cdot L}{L^3(1 - (r/L)^2\sin^2\theta)^{3/2}}\right]$$

Substituting the values and simplifying (lengthy but standard):

$$a_B \approx -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cdot\frac{\cos 2\theta}{\left(1 - (r/L)^2\sin^2\theta\right)^{3/2}}\right]$$

At $\theta = 45°$, $\cos 2\theta = 0$:

$$a_B \approx -0.1 \times (31.416)^2 \times \left[\cos 45° + 0\right]$$

$$a_B \approx -0.1 \times 987.0 \times 0.7071 = -69.79 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates the acceleration is directed toward the crank (deceleration of the retracting slider).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** A huge source of mark loss. Always work in **SI units (m, m/s, m/s²)** for velocity and acceleration; use mm only for displacement ratios.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** In slider-crank analysis, the direction of $v_B$ depends on whether the crank is in the outward or return stroke. Always state and use a consistent sign convention.
> 3. **Velocity polygon scaling:** In graphical methods, the scale must be stated explicitly. A 14-mark question without the scale mentioned attracts 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Confusing "uniform velocity" with "uniform speed":** Uniform velocity requires *straight-line* motion at *constant speed*. Circular motion at constant speed is **not** uniform velocity.
> 5. **Forgetting Kennedy's collinearity:** When locating the instantaneous center, you *must* show that the three centers are collinear — this is a 2-mark subtask that students frequently omit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Uniform velocity** = constant magnitude **AND** constant direction of velocity; requires straight-line path.
- **Acceleration = 0** is a direct consequence — no tangential acceleration.
- **Displacement-time graph** is a straight line through the origin (or offset by $s_0$).
- **Velocity-time graph** is a horizontal line.
- In **mechanisms**, true uniform velocity is an *idealization*; real tracing points have *approximately* uniform velocity near the **inflection point** of the coupler curve.
- **Burmester's theorem** and **Kennedy's instantaneous center theorem** are the two pillars of velocity analysis in KTU.
- The **velocity polygon** is the standard graphical tool; **vector loop differentiation** is the standard analytical tool.
- For a **slider-crank**, $v_B = -r\omega\left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\sin 2\theta}{2L\sqrt{1-(r/L)^2\sin^2\theta}}\right]$.
- For a **four-bar linkage**, $\omega_3 = \dfrac{\omega_2 r_2 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_2)}{r_3 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$.
- **Straight-line motion mechanisms** (Watt, Peaucellier-Lipkin, Hart) exploit the inflection-point property.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** confusing uniform velocity with uniform circular speed; ignoring the sign of angular velocity; not stating the velocity polygon scale.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
