<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mechanics of Machinery** (PBMUT504), **Uniform Acceleration** refers to the motion of a particle or a rigid body link in which the **acceleration remains constant in both magnitude and direction** with respect to time. The term "uniform" indicates that the rate of change of velocity is steady — neither increasing nor decreasing.

Mathematically, if $a$ is the acceleration of a particle at any instant $t$:

$$\frac{d^{2}s}{dt^{2}} \;=\; a \;=\; \text{constant}$$

where:
- $s$ = linear displacement of the particle
- $a$ = uniform (constant) linear acceleration, measured in **m/s²**
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Uniform Acceleration forms the bedrock for deriving motion cycles of follower displacement diagrams (e.g., rise, dwell, return with constant acceleration). It is the **simplest non-trivial motion profile** and is frequently tested in ESE questions linked to cam design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a car at a red traffic signal. The moment the light turns green, the driver slowly but steadily presses the accelerator pedal such that the speedometer needle climbs at a **constant rate** — say 5 km/h every second. You are experiencing **uniform acceleration**.

| Real-World Situation | Analogy Mapping |
|---|---|
| Car gaining 5 km/h every second | Velocity increases linearly with time |
| Cruise control on a highway | Acceleration ≈ 0 (special case of uniform acc.) |
| Free-falling object (ignoring air resistance) | Gravity gives uniform $a = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Uniform acceleration means the object speeds up (or slows down) by the **same amount every second**. If a bike accelerates uniformly at $2 \; m/s^2$, then after every second its velocity grows by exactly $2 \; m/s$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

- **Initial Velocity ($u$)**: Velocity of the particle at $t = 0$. Unit: **m/s**.
- **Final Velocity ($v$)**: Velocity of the particle at time $t$. Unit: **m/s**.
- **Acceleration ($a$)**: Rate of change of velocity. Unit: **m/s²**.
- **Displacement ($s$)**: Net distance covered in time $t$. Unit: **m**.
- **Jerk ($j$)**: Rate of change of acceleration. Under uniform acceleration, $j = 0$.
- **Standard Gravity (g)**: $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (often rounded to $9.81 \; m/s^2$ in KTU problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Under uniform acceleration, the motion is governed by a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation**. The reasoning unfolds in three logical stages:

**Stage 1 — Velocity Relationship**
Acceleration is defined as the derivative of velocity with respect to time:

$$a \;=\; \frac{dv}{dt} \;=\; \text{constant}$$

Integrating both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} a \; dt$$

This yields the **first kinematic equation** of uniformly accelerated motion.

**Stage 2 — Displacement Relationship**
Velocity is defined as the derivative of displacement with respect to time:

$$v \;=\; \frac{ds}{dt}$$

Substituting $v = u + a t$ and integrating again with respect to $t$ yields the **second kinematic equation**.

**Stage 3 — Velocity-Displacement Relationship**
Eliminating $t$ between the first two equations yields the **third kinematic equation**, which is independent of time.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity–Time | $v \;=\; u + a t$ | $v, u, a, t$ | Time is given or required |
| 2 | Displacement–Time | $s \;=\; u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$ | $s, u, a, t$ | Distance and time are involved |
| 3 | Velocity–Displacement | $v^{2} \;=\; u^{2} + 2 a s$ | $v, u, a, s$ | Time is unknown |
| 4 | Average Velocity | $s \;=\; \left(\dfrac{u + v}{2}\right) t$ | $s, u, v, t$ | Finding distance using mean speed |
| 5 | Distance in $n^{th}$ second | $s_{n} \;=\; u + \dfrac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$ | $s_n, u, a, n$ | Distance covered between $(n-1)$ and $n$ seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Distances measured in the direction of motion: **POSITIVE**
> - Distances measured against the direction of motion: **NEGATIVE**
> - For **deceleration**, write $a$ as a **negative value** (e.g., $a = -3 \; m/s^2$).
> - Use $\vert a \vert$ when only magnitude is given, but apply sign manually per context.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Mechanics of Machinery

- **Cam Follower Motion Profiles:** Uniform acceleration is used as a basic segment of complex cam displacement diagrams (UD, UA, SHM combinations).
- **Slider-Crank Mechanism:** Piston acceleration in a slider-crank is *not* uniform, but the **analysis methodology** mirrors uniform-acceleration calculus.
- **Braking Systems:** Designing brake deceleration profiles for lifts, escalators, and vehicles.
- **Quick-Return Mechanisms:** Determining average and maximum velocities of cutting tools.
- **Vibration-Free Machinery:** Establishing baseline kinematic behaviour before introducing higher-order effects (jerk).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Velocity–Time Equation ($v = u + at$)

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of acceleration.**

$$a \;=\; \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the velocity differential.**

$$dv \;=\; a \; dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary conditions.**
At time $t = 0$, velocity is $u$. At time $t = t$, velocity is $v$. Since $a$ is uniform (constant), we integrate.

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv \;=\; a \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals.**

$$[v]_{u}^{v} \;=\; a \, [t]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits.**

$$v - u \;=\; a (t - 0)$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$v \;=\; u + a t \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Displacement–Time Equation ($s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$)

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of velocity.**

$$v \;=\; \frac{ds}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $v$ from the previously derived equation ($v = u + at$).**

$$\frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; u + a t$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the displacement differential.**

$$ds \;=\; (u + a t) \; dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with limits.**
At $t = 0$, $s = 0$. At $t = t$, $s = s$.

$$\int_{0}^{s} ds \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} (u + a t) \; dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the right-hand integral term by term.**

$$s \;=\; u \int_{0}^{t} dt \;+\; a \int_{0}^{t} t \; dt$$

**Step 6 — Apply power rule of integration.**

$$s \;=\; u \, [t]_{0}^{t} \;+\; a \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the limits.**

$$s \;=\; u (t - 0) \;+\; a \left(\frac{t^{2} - 0}{2}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Final result.**

$$s \;=\; u t + \frac{1}{2} a t^{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity–Displacement Equation ($v^2 = u^2 + 2as$)

**Step 1 — Use the chain rule of calculus to eliminate time.**
Acceleration can be written as:

$$a \;=\; \frac{dv}{dt} \;=\; \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; v \cdot \frac{dv}{ds}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate differentials.**

$$v \, dv \;=\; a \, ds$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides.**
At $s = 0$, $v = u$. At $s = s$, $v = v$.

$$\int_{u}^{v} v \, dv \;=\; a \int_{0}^{s} ds$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the left-hand integral using $\int v \, dv = \tfrac{v^2}{2}$.**

$$\left[\frac{v^{2}}{2}\right]_{u}^{v} \;=\; a \, [s]_{0}^{s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits.**

$$\frac{v^{2}}{2} - \frac{u^{2}}{2} \;=\; a (s - 0)$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by 2.**

$$v^{2} - u^{2} \;=\; 2 a s$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$v^{2} \;=\; u^{2} + 2 a s \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Distance Travelled in the $n^{th}$ Second

**Step 1 — Compute the distance covered in $n$ seconds.**

$$s_{n} \;=\; u(n) + \frac{1}{2} a (n)^{2} \;=\; u n + \frac{1}{2} a n^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the distance covered in $(n-1)$ seconds.**

$$s_{n-1} \;=\; u(n-1) + \frac{1}{2} a (n-1)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to find the distance covered *only* during the $n^{th}$ second.**

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; s_{n} - s_{n-1}$$

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; u n + \frac{1}{2} a n^{2} - u(n-1) - \frac{1}{2} a (n-1)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Expand $(n-1)^{2} = n^{2} - 2n + 1$.**

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; u n + \frac{1}{2} a n^{2} - u n + u - \frac{1}{2} a (n^{2} - 2 n + 1)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $un$ terms and expand.**

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; u + \frac{1}{2} a n^{2} - \frac{1}{2} a n^{2} + a n - \frac{1}{2} a$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the terms.**

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; u + a n - \frac{1}{2} a$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $\tfrac{a}{2}$.**

$$s_{n}^{th} \;=\; u + \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A slider starts from rest and moves with uniform acceleration of $4 \; m/s^2$ for 6 seconds. Find:
> (a) Final velocity, (b) Distance travelled, (c) Distance covered in the 6th second.

**Given Data:**
- Initial velocity, $u = 0 \; m/s$ (starts from rest)
- Acceleration, $a = 4 \; m/s^2$
- Time, $t = 6 \; s$

**Solution (a): Final velocity**

Using $v = u + at$:

$$v \;=\; 0 + (4)(6) \;=\; 24 \; m/s$$

**Solution (b): Distance travelled**

Using $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$:

$$s \;=\; (0)(6) + \frac{1}{2}(4)(6)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(4)(36) \;=\; 72 \; m$$

**Solution (c): Distance in the 6th second**

Using $s_{n} = u + \tfrac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$ with $n = 6$:

$$s_{6} \;=\; 0 + \frac{4}{2}(2 \cdot 6 - 1) \;=\; 2 \times 11 \;=\; 22 \; m$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Uniform Acceleration Kinematic Chain

The diagram below shows how the four fundamental kinematic quantities are inter-linked under uniform acceleration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define a is constant] --> B[Integrate a = dv/dt]
    B --> C[Equation 1: v = u plus a t]
    C --> D[Substitute v into ds/dt]
    D --> E[Equation 2: s = u t plus half a t squared]
    E --> F[Eliminate t via chain rule]
    F --> G[Equation 3: v squared = u squared plus 2 a s]
    G --> H[Derive nth second distance]
    H --> I[Equation 4: s_n = u plus a by 2 times 2n minus 1]
    I --> J[Apply to cam design and slider crank analysis]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Application in Cam Follower Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input: Cam Rotation Angle theta] --> UA[Block A: Uniform Acceleration Segment]
    UA --> COMPUTEV[Compute Velocity v = u plus a t]
    UA --> COMPUTES[Compute Displacement s = u t plus half a t squared]
    UA --> COMPUTEJ[Compute Jerk j = 0 constant acceleration]
    COMPUTEV --> OUTVEL[Output: Velocity Profile]
    COMPUTES --> OUTDISP[Output: Displacement Profile]
    COMPUTEJ --> OUTJERK[Output: Jerk Profile Zero]
    OUTVEL --> APP[Application: Cam Synthesis and Quick Return Analysis]
    OUTDISP --> APP
    OUTJERK --> APP
```

## 4.3 Graphical Interpretation Table

| Quantity | Graph Type | Nature Under Uniform Acceleration |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement vs Time ($s$–$t$) | Parabolic curve | Quadratic in $t$ |
| Velocity vs Time ($v$–$t$) | Straight line | Linear in $t$, slope $= a$ |
| Acceleration vs Time ($a$–$t$) | Horizontal line | Constant value |
| Acceleration vs Displacement ($a$–$s$) | Horizontal line | Constant value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue for Students:** If you ever see a displacement-time graph that is a perfect parabola, you can immediately conclude the motion is **uniformly accelerated** (or uniformly decelerated if the parabola opens the other way).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define uniform acceleration. State any two kinematic equations for a particle moving with uniform acceleration.**

**Model Answer:**
Uniform acceleration is a type of motion in which the acceleration of the particle remains **constant in magnitude and direction** with respect to time. Two kinematic equations are:
1. $v = u + a t$
2. $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for equations — 1 mark each.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A particle starting from rest accelerates uniformly at $3 \; m/s^2$. Find the velocity and distance covered after 5 seconds.**

**Model Answer:**
Given: $u = 0$, $a = 3 \; m/s^2$, $t = 5 \; s$.

$$v = u + a t = 0 + 3(5) = 15 \; m/s$$

$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2} = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(25) = 37.5 \; m$$

*(3 marks: 1 for given data, 1 for velocity, 1 for distance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the units** in the final answer — always write $m/s$, $m/s^2$, or $m$.
> - A common mistake is writing $a = 9.8$ instead of $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ for gravitational problems.
> - For **retardation**, ensure the sign of $a$ is **negative**. The examiner deducts 1 mark if the deceleration case is treated as acceleration.
> - In cam design sub-questions, students often forget to mention that uniform acceleration gives **zero jerk** — a key phrase that fetches the full CO mark.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]`

**(a) Derive the three kinematic equations of motion for a particle moving with uniform acceleration.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The derivation is reproduced here in condensed form. Refer to **SECTION 3** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.

**Equation 1: $v = u + at$**

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} \implies dv = a \, dt \implies \int_{u}^{v} dv = a \int_{0}^{t} dt \implies v - u = at$$

$$\boxed{v = u + a t}$$

**Equation 2: $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$**

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = u + at \implies ds = (u + at) dt \implies s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^{2}$$

**Equation 3: $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$**

$$a = v \frac{dv}{ds} \implies v \, dv = a \, ds \implies \int_{u}^{v} v \, dv = a \int_{0}^{s} ds \implies v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$$

*(7 marks: 2 for each equation with proper integration steps and limits, 1 for final boxed answer.)*

---

**(b) A train moving at $36 \; km/h$ is brought to rest by applying brakes which produce a uniform retardation of $0.5 \; m/s^2$. Find: (i) the time taken to come to rest, (ii) the distance covered before stopping.** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- Initial velocity: $u = 36 \; km/h = 36 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 10 \; m/s$
- Final velocity: $v = 0 \; m/s$ (comes to rest)
- Retardation: $a = -0.5 \; m/s^2$

**(i) Time taken to come to rest:**

$$v = u + a t \implies 0 = 10 + (-0.5) t$$

$$0.5 \, t = 10 \implies t = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20 \; s$$

**(ii) Distance covered before stopping:**

Using $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$:

$$0 = (10)^{2} + 2(-0.5) s \implies 0 = 100 - s$$

$$s = 100 \; m$$

**Verification using $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$:**

$$s = 10(20) + \tfrac{1}{2}(-0.5)(20)^{2} = 200 - 100 = 100 \; m \quad \checkmark$$

*(7 marks: 2 for unit conversion, 2 for time calculation, 2 for distance, 1 for verification.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 14 Marks]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) With the help of a velocity-time graph, derive the kinematic equation $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ for uniformly accelerated motion.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a particle moving with uniform acceleration $a$. Plot $v$ on the y-axis and $t$ on the x-axis.

**Step 2:** Under uniform acceleration, the velocity-time graph is a **straight line**.

- At $t = 0$, velocity is $u$ (initial velocity) — this is the y-intercept.
- At $t = t$, velocity is $v = u + at$ — this is the terminal point.
- Slope of the line $= \dfrac{v - u}{t - 0} = a$ (uniform acceleration).

**Step 3:** The displacement $s$ covered in time $t$ equals the **area under the $v$–$t$ graph**.

The area under the line forms a **trapezium** bounded by:
- Parallel sides: $u$ and $v$
- Perpendicular distance (height): $t$

**Step 4:** Area of trapezium:

$$s = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{sum of parallel sides}) \times (\text{height})$$

$$s = \frac{1}{2}(u + v)(t)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $v = u + at$:

$$s = \frac{1}{2}(u + u + at)(t) = \frac{1}{2}(2u + at)(t)$$

$$\boxed{s = u t + \frac{1}{2} a t^{2}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for graph description, 2 for trapezium area concept, 2 for substitution, 1 for final result.)*

---

**(b) A body moving with uniform acceleration has velocities $5 \; m/s$ and $15 \; m/s$ at two points which are $50 \; m$ apart. Find the acceleration and the time taken to traverse this distance.** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- Velocity at point 1: $u = 5 \; m/s$
- Velocity at point 2: $v = 15 \; m/s$
- Distance between the points: $s = 50 \; m$

**Find:** Acceleration $a$ and time $t$.

**Step 1: Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$:**

$$(15)^{2} = (5)^{2} + 2 a (50)$$

$$225 = 25 + 100 a$$

$$200 = 100 a \implies a = 2 \; m/s^2$$

**Step 2: Using $v = u + at$ to find time:**

$$15 = 5 + 2 t$$

$$2 t = 10 \implies t = 5 \; s$$

**Verification using $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$:**

$$s = 5(5) + \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(25) = 25 + 25 = 50 \; m \quad \checkmark$$

*(7 marks: 3 for acceleration derivation, 3 for time, 1 for verification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Uniform Acceleration**

- **Definition:** Acceleration is **constant** in both magnitude and direction ($a = \text{const}$).
- **Five Master Equations:**
  1. $v = u + a t$
  2. $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
  3. $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$
  4. $s = \tfrac{(u + v)}{2} \cdot t$
  5. $s_{n} = u + \tfrac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$
- **Three Graph Signatures:** Parabolic $s$–$t$, linear $v$–$t$, horizontal $a$–$t$.
- **Jerk = 0:** Uniform acceleration has **zero jerk** — a property highly valued in cam design.
- **Sign Convention:** Deceleration always carries a **negative** sign; never omit it.
- **Unit Conversions:** Remember $1 \; km/h = \tfrac{5}{18} \; m/s$ (a frequent trap in KTU problems).
- **Real-World Relevance:** Cam followers, braking systems, quick-return mechanisms, slider-crank baseline analysis.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** Forgetting units, mixing up retardation with acceleration, skipping limits in integration steps.
- **Linked COs:** Maps to **CO2** of PBMUT504 (Kinematic Analysis of Mechanisms) under KTU 2024 OBE framework.
- **Cognitive Level:** Mostly K3 (Apply) and K4 (Analyze) per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy in KTU ESE pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycloidal Motion - Graphical Cam Profile Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cycloidal Motion - Graphical Cam Profile Synthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cycloidal Motion** is a follower displacement motion law in cam mechanisms in which the follower moves such that its displacement curve traces a segment of a **cycloid** — the locus generated by a point on the rim of a circle of radius $R$ that rolls without slipping along a straight line.

For a follower with stroke $h$ during a cam rotation angle $\beta$ (outstroke), the governing displacement equation is:

$$s = h \left[ \frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Cycloidal motion belongs to the family of **high-order polynomial / trigonometric displacement functions** used in cam design. It is preferred for high-speed mechanisms (e.g., IC engine inlet/exhaust valves, automatic stamping machines) because both **acceleration and velocity are zero at the start and end of every stroke**, eliminating impact loading.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Wheel" Picture

Imagine a small wheel of radius $R$ rolling smoothly along a flat road without slipping. Mark a single point on the rim (a "chalk dot" on the tyre). As the wheel rolls:

- At the instant the dot touches the road → the dot has **zero velocity** (instantaneous center of rotation).
- A quarter-rotation later → the dot is at the **top of the wheel** and moving **twice as fast** as the wheel's center.
- Half-rotation later → the dot touches the road again, **back to rest**.

The path traced by this dot in space is the **cycloid**. Now, if the horizontal axis of that trace represents **time (or cam angle $\theta$)** and the vertical axis represents **follower displacement $s$**, you have an *exact* picture of cycloidal follower motion.

### Real-World Analogy

Think of a **modern express elevator**: it does not jerk you off your feet when the doors close, does not slam you upward as it leaves the lobby, and decelerates so smoothly that you cannot tell exactly when it stops. That effortless start–cruise–stop profile is precisely what cycloidal motion gives a cam follower.

## 1.3 Standard Cam Terminology (Recap)

> [!NOTE] **Key Cam Geometry Terms**
> - **Base Circle:** Smallest circle drawn tangent to the cam profile. Radius = $R_b$.
> - **Prime Circle:** Circle passing through the pitch point. Radius = $R_p = R_b + r$ (for roller follower, $r$ = roller radius). For knife-edge follower, $R_p = R_b$.
> - **Pitch Curve:** Locus of the trace point (knife edge tip or roller center) as the cam rotates.
> - **Pressure Angle:** Angle between the direction of follower motion and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact.
> - **Trace Point:** Theoretical point on the follower used to generate the pitch curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Geometry

A cycloid is generated by a generating circle of radius $R$ rolling without slipping along a straight line. For cam synthesis, the generating circle's diameter equals the **stroke $h$** of the follower:

$$R = \frac{h}{2\pi}$$

When the cam rotates through angle $\beta$, the generating circle makes exactly one complete revolution. Hence the angular parameter of the cycloid $\phi$ is mapped to the cam angle as:

$$\phi = \frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

## 2.2 The Four Characteristic Functions

Let $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ be the angular velocity of the cam (in rad/s). The four time/angle derivatives of displacement are:

| Function | Symbol | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|----------|------------------|
| Displacement | $s$ | $s = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | Instantaneous follower position |
| Velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | Rate of change of position |
| Acceleration | $a$ | $a = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | Inertial load on follower |
| Jerk | $j$ | $j = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} h\omega^{3}}{\beta^{3}}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | Rate of change of acceleration (vibration driver) |

## 2.3 Critical Values (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Magnitude | Occurs at |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Maximum Velocity $v_{max}$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\theta = \beta/2$ |
| Maximum Acceleration $a_{max}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ | $\theta = \beta/4$ and $\theta = 3\beta/4$ |
| Maximum Jerk $j_{max}$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^{2} h\omega^{3}}{\beta^{3}}$ | $\theta = 0,\ \beta/2,\ \beta$ |

> [!NOTE] **Valuation Tip from KTU Examiners**
> The acceleration curve is a **pure sine wave** and the jerk is a **pure cosine wave** (no DC offset) — a property that no other standard motion (simple harmonic, constant velocity, constant acceleration) possesses in such a clean form. Examiners love asking for "maximum acceleration" because the answer follows directly from $a_{max} = 2\pi h \omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$.

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions (Auto-Satisfied)

A motion law is *useful* only if it satisfies the four end-point constraints. Cycloidal motion inherently satisfies **all four**:

| Condition | At $\theta = 0$ | At $\theta = \beta$ |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| $s$ | $0$ | $h$ |
| $v$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $a$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $j$ | Finite (not zero) | Finite (not zero) |

The only "cost" is that jerk does **not** vanish at the endpoints — this is why cycloidal motion is sometimes called a **"third-order smooth"** motion (continuous up to acceleration, but with a kink in the jerk profile).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Industry Uses Cycloidal Cams**
> - **Internal Combustion Engines** — inlet and exhaust valve cams of high-speed petrol engines (e.g., Formula 1 valve trains) often use cycloidal or modified cycloidal profiles.
> - **Automatic Looms & Packaging Machines** — where follower rest periods and smooth reversions extend product life.
> - **High-Speed Printing Presses** — ink rollers need zero-jerk reversal to avoid smearing.
> - **Aircraft Control Surfaces** — where inertial loads must remain bounded.
>
> The "trade-off" is a slightly **larger maximum acceleration** (factor of $2\pi$ vs. $\pi/2$ for SHM) but the **acceleration profile is single-sinusoidal**, making the cam profile easier to grind on a CNC profile grinder.

## 2.6 Cam Profile Synthesis — Why Graphical?

For any prescribed motion law, the **exact analytical cam profile** is generally not a standard curve. Hence the engineer synthesizes the profile by:
1. Computing follower positions at discrete cam angles from the motion equation, and
2. Plotting these positions geometrically using a **layout drawing** (compass, protractor, scale).

This is the **graphical cam profile synthesis** procedure that KTU Module 2 tests on.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cycloidal Displacement Equation

**Step 1 — Parametric Equations of a Pure Cycloid.**
Let a circle of radius $R$ roll along the $x$-axis without slipping. After rotating through angle $\phi$ (radians), a point $P$ initially at the contact point has coordinates:

$$x = R(\phi - \sin\phi) \qquad y = R(1 - \cos\phi)$$

**Step 2 — Map to the Cam Problem.**
We identify the cam rotation $\theta$ with the rolling angle $\phi$ as:

$$\phi = \frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

so that when $\theta = \beta$, the generating circle has rolled through exactly $2\pi$ rad (one full revolution).

**Step 3 — Identify the Vertical Locus with the Follower Stroke.**
We require that when $\phi = 2\pi$ (i.e., $\theta = \beta$), the follower has lifted by exactly $h$:

$$y(\phi = 2\pi) = R(1 - \cos 2\pi) = R(1 - 1) = 0 \ \text{(returns to baseline)}$$

This is correct for a *single* arch. But the *horizontal* travel of the center is:

$$x(\phi = 2\pi) = R(2\pi - 0) = 2\pi R$$

So the horizontal extent of one arch equals $2\pi R$. If we **re-interpret** the horizontal axis as the *vertical* displacement by using a *re-parameterized* cycloid where the center of the rolling circle lifts linearly, we get the cam-follower version. The standard textbook construction simply **chooses $R = h/(2\pi)$** and identifies $s = x$ of the cycloid (i.e., the displacement along the rolling axis):

$$s = R\!\left(\phi - \sin\phi\right) = \frac{h}{2\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} - \sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,s = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Equation

Differentiate $s$ with respect to time, using $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$\frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta} - \frac{h}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{2\pi}{\beta}\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = \frac{h}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$$

Therefore:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Acceleration Equation

Differentiate $v$ with respect to time:

$$\frac{dv}{d\theta} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\cdot\frac{2\pi}{\beta}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi h\omega}{\beta^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Jerk Equation

Differentiate $a$ with respect to time:

$$\frac{da}{d\theta} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\cdot\frac{2\pi}{\beta}\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = \frac{4\pi^{2} h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{3}}\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

$$j = \frac{da}{dt} = \frac{4\pi^{2} h\omega^{3}}{\beta^{3}}\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

## 3.5 Maximum Acceleration — Closed Form

Set $\dfrac{da}{d\theta} = 0$ (jerk = 0):

$$\cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = \frac{\pi}{2},\ \frac{3\pi}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta = \frac{\beta}{4},\ \frac{3\beta}{4}$$

Substituting $\theta = \beta/4$:

$$a_{max} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Worked Analytical Problem)

> **Problem:** A cam rotating at $N = 300$ rpm drives a knife-edge follower with stroke $h = 40$ mm during a rise of $\beta = 180°$. Cycloidal motion is used. Calculate the displacement, velocity, and acceleration when the cam has rotated $\theta = 30°$ from the start of rise.

**Given:** $h = 40$ mm, $\beta = 180° = \pi$ rad, $N = 300$ rpm, $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of cam:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Dimensionless ratio:**

$$\frac{\theta}{\beta} = \frac{\pi/6}{\pi} = \frac{1}{6}, \qquad \frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta} = 2\pi \times \frac{1}{6} = 60°$$

**Step 3 — Displacement:**

$$s = 40\left[\frac{1}{6} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin 60°\right] = 40\left[0.1667 - 0.1592 \times 0.8660\right]$$

$$s = 40\,[0.1667 - 0.1378] = 40 \times 0.0289 = 1.156 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{40 \times 10\pi}{\pi}[1 - \cos 60°] = 400 \times 0.5 = 200 \text{ mm/s} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{2\pi \times 40 \times (10\pi)^{2}}{(\pi)^{2}}\sin 60° = \frac{2\pi \times 40 \times 100}{\pi} \times 0.8660$$

$$a = 8000 \times 0.8660 = 6928 \text{ mm/s}^{2} = 6.928 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

> [!NOTE] **Result Summary**
> $s = 1.156$ mm, $v = 0.2$ m/s, $a = 6.928$ m/s²

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Cam Profile Synthesis Procedure (Graphical Method)

The following block diagram maps the **step-by-step graphical construction** workflow that examiners expect a student to either describe in words or sketch on a layout sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Draw base circle of radius Rb] --> B[Step2: Draw prime circle of radius Rp = Rb for knife edge or Rb + r for roller]
    B --> C[Step3: Mark angular positions of outstroke beta and return stroke alpha on prime circle]
    C --> D[Step4: Divide outstroke into N equal parts N typically 6 or 12]
    D --> E[Step5: Divide stroke h into N equal linear parts]
    E --> F[Step6: For each angle compute s_i using cycloidal equation]
    F --> G[Step7: Draw radial lines at each angular division from cam center]
    G --> H[Step8: On each radial line measure displacement s_i from prime circle outward]
    H --> I[Step9: Join the displaced points to obtain the pitch curve]
    I --> J[Step10a: For knife edge follower the pitch curve IS the cam profile]
    I --> K[Step10b: For roller follower draw rollers of radius r at each pitch point to obtain cam profile]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Standard Cam Motions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standard Cam Motion Laws] --> B[Simple Harmonic Motion SHM]
    A --> C[Constant Velocity Motion]
    A --> D[Constant Acceleration Motion]
    A --> E[Cycloidal Motion]
    A --> F[Polynomial Motion 3 4 5]
    B --> B1[Acceleration has sudden jumps at start and end infinite jerk]
    C --> C1[Velocity has sudden jumps at start and end infinite acceleration]
    D --> D1[Acceleration has sudden jumps at mid stroke infinite jerk]
    E --> E1[Acceleration is pure sine wave finite jerk everywhere]
    F --> F1[Acceleration and jerk are zero at start and end of stroke]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Function Hierarchy of Cycloidal Derivatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Displacement s theta] --> V[Velocity v theta = ds/dt]
    V --> A[Acceleration a theta = dv/dt]
    A --> J[Jerk j theta = da/dt]
    P1[s: cycloid arch] --> V1[v: shifted cosine]
    V1 --> A1[a: pure sine wave]
    A1 --> J1[j: pure cosine wave]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (For Hand-Drawn Layout)

When KTU asks you to "draw the cam profile," the **graphical layout** consists of:

1. **Concentric circles** (base and prime) drawn about the cam center $O$.
2. **Radial construction lines** marked at angular intervals of $\beta/N$ over the outstroke and return.
3. **Displacement vector arrows** of length $s_i$ drawn along each radial line, starting from the prime circle.
4. **Pitch curve** — a smooth fair curve joining the arrow tips.
5. **Cam profile** — obtained by offsetting the pitch curve inward by roller radius $r$ (or by direct transfer for knife-edge follower).

> [!NOTE] **Examiners' Visual Expectation**
> The pitch curve for a cycloidal cam is visibly **smoother** than for constant-velocity motion near the start/end, but **slightly more bulged at mid-stroke** due to the $\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)$ term.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define cycloidal motion as applied to cam followers. State any two advantages of using cycloidal motion over simple harmonic motion.**

**Model Answer:**
Cycloidal motion is a follower displacement law in which the follower moves such that its displacement $s$ follows a segment of a cycloid, governed by the equation $s = h[\theta/\beta - (1/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$.

**Two advantages over SHM:**
1. The acceleration is a **pure sinusoidal function** (no constant offset), giving a cleaner vibration spectrum.
2. The maximum acceleration, although larger in magnitude, occurs **earlier and later in the stroke** (at $\beta/4$ and $3\beta/4$), distributing inertial loading more uniformly.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the four boundary conditions satisfied by cycloidal follower motion. State the value of the maximum acceleration and the cam angle at which it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**
The four boundary conditions satisfied by cycloidal motion are:
1. $s = 0$ at $\theta = 0$
2. $s = h$ at $\theta = \beta$
3. $v = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$
4. $a = 0$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$

Maximum acceleration:

$$a_{max} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$$

occurs at $\theta = \beta/4$ and $\theta = 3\beta/4$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the expressions for displacement, velocity and acceleration of a follower moving with cycloidal motion during the outstroke. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Displacement.** From the cycloid parametric equations with $R = h/(2\pi)$ and $\phi = 2\pi\theta/\beta$:

$$s = R(\phi - \sin\phi) = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Velocity.** Differentiate $s$ w.r.t. $\theta$ and multiply by $\omega$:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration.** Differentiate $v$ w.r.t. $\theta$ and multiply by $\omega$:

$$a = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of boundary conditions.** At $\theta = 0$: $s = 0$, $v = 0$, $a = 0$. At $\theta = \beta$: $s = h$, $v = 0$, $a = 0$. All four conditions satisfied.

#### (b) A cam rotates at 240 rpm and has a rise of 30 mm during 180° of cam rotation with cycloidal motion. Determine (i) maximum velocity, (ii) maximum acceleration, and (iii) time required for the outstroke. (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N = 240$ rpm, $h = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m, $\beta = 180° = \pi$ rad.

**Step 1 — Cam angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum velocity (occurs at $\theta = \beta/2$):**

$$v_{max} = \frac{2h\omega}{\beta} = \frac{2 \times 0.03 \times 8\pi}{\pi} = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum acceleration (occurs at $\theta = \beta/4$):**

$$a_{max} = \frac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.03 \times 64\pi^{2}}{\pi^{2}} = 3.84\pi \approx 12.06 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Time for outstroke:**

$$t_{out} = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{\pi}{8\pi} = 0.125 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfall**
> A very frequent error is forgetting to convert $\beta$ from degrees to **radians** before plugging into the velocity and acceleration equations. Every time you see $\beta$ in a cycloidal formula, it must be in **radians**, NOT degrees. Loss of 2–3 marks is common if this is missed.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the graphical procedure for synthesizing the profile of a radial cam with a knife-edge follower moving with cycloidal motion. (7 Marks)

**Procedure (Step-by-step description):**

1. **Draw the base circle** of radius $R_b$ with center $O$.
2. **Draw the prime circle** of radius $R_p = R_b$ (since the follower is knife-edged, $R_p = R_b$).
3. **Mark the angular outstroke $\beta$** on the prime circle, starting from the point of first contact. The follower lifts over $\beta$, dwells over the next angle, and returns over the return angle.
4. **Divide the outstroke $\beta$** into $N$ equal angular parts (typically $N = 6$ or $12$). Mark the angular positions $1, 2, 3, \dots, N$ on the prime circle.
5. **Divide the stroke $h$** into $N$ equal linear parts.
6. **Calculate displacements** $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N$ for each angular position using:

$$s_i = h\left[\frac{\theta_i}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta_i}{\beta}\right]$$

7. **Draw radial lines** from $O$ through each marked angular position.
8. **On each radial line**, measure the corresponding displacement $s_i$ outward from the prime circle. Mark points $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N$.
9. **Join the points** $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N$ with a smooth fair curve. This is the **pitch curve**, which for a knife-edge follower is identical to the **cam profile**.

> [!NOTE] **Sketch Requirement (3 marks)**
> You must accompany the description with a neat, labelled sketch showing the base circle, prime circle, radial lines, displacement arrows, and the resulting pitch curve. A diagram without proper labels scores 0 for the sketch component.

#### (b) Compare cycloidal motion with simple harmonic motion for cam followers. (7 Marks)

| Feature | Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) | Cycloidal Motion |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------|
| Displacement equation | $s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$ | $s = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$ |
| Velocity equation | $v = \dfrac{h\omega\pi}{2\beta}\sin\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$ |
| Acceleration equation | $a = \dfrac{h\omega^{2}\pi^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}\cos\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}$ | $a = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$ |
| Acceleration at $\theta = 0$ and $\beta$ | Maximum (finite, non-zero) | Zero |
| Maximum acceleration magnitude | $\dfrac{\pi^{2}h\omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}$ (larger) | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ (smaller by factor 0.405) |
| Jerk at start/end | Infinite (discontinuous) | Finite (continuous) |
| Shape of pitch curve | Simple harmonic arch | Cycloidal arch |
| Best suited for | Low–medium speed mechanisms | High-speed mechanisms |

**Engineering conclusion:** Cycloidal motion is superior to SHM for **high-speed applications** because it eliminates infinite jerk at the endpoints, reducing noise, vibration, and follower bouncing.

> [!WARNING] **Examiners' Valuation Pitfall**
> - Do **not** write SHM as $s = h\sin(\pi\theta/\beta)$ — the constant term $h/2$ is essential. A wrong SHM equation will cascade into wrong velocity/acceleration values.
> - Always state the **unit** of maximum acceleration ($\text{m/s}^{2}$) — losing 0.5 marks for missing units is very common in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Displacement:** $s = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$ — must be in **radians** for $\beta$ and $\theta$.
> - **Velocity:** $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right]$
> - **Acceleration:** $a = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$
> - **Jerk:** $j = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}h\omega^{3}}{\beta^{3}}\cos\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}$
> - **Maximum velocity** $= \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ at $\theta = \beta/2$.
> - **Maximum acceleration** $= \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ at $\theta = \beta/4$ and $3\beta/4$.
> - **Both $v$ and $a$ are zero at the start and end of every stroke** — this is the chief reason cycloidal motion is preferred for high-speed cams.
> - **Generating circle radius** $R = h/(2\pi)$.
> - **Graphical construction** always uses the prime circle as the displacement reference, with displacement vectors drawn outward along radial lines.
> - **For a knife-edge follower**, the pitch curve coincides with the cam profile. **For a roller follower**, the cam profile is the offset of the pitch curve by the roller radius $r$.
> - **Cam rotation during outstroke** $\beta$ must be in **radians** when substituted into all formulas. Use $\beta_{\text{rad}} = \beta_{\text{deg}} \times \pi/180$.
> - **Units check:** $h$ in meters, $\omega$ in rad/s, $\beta$ in rad → $v$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s². Mixing mm with m/s² is a guaranteed mark loss.
> - **Most common mistakes:** (i) $\beta$ in degrees, (ii) forgetting to convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s), (iii) computing $\sin$ of angle in degrees when formula requires radian argument (or vice versa).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gears - Classification - Terminology of Spur Gears - Law of Gearing - Tooth Profiles - Contact Ratio - Interference - Backlash - Gear Standardization - Interchangeability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gears - Classification - Terminology of Spur Gears - Law of Gearing - Tooth Profiles - Contact Ratio - Interference - Backlash - Gear Standardization - Interchangeability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear:** A gear is a rotating machine element having **cut teeth** (or cogs) of uniform profile, which mesh with another toothed part (another gear, rack, or sprocket) to transmit torque and motion between shafts, while maintaining a definite speed ratio.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMUT504 - Mechanics of Machinery)**, gears form the most fundamental class of **positive (positive-engagement) power-transmission drives**. Unlike belt or chain drives that rely on friction or tension, gears transmit power through the **direct geometric contact** of their meshing teeth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — "Positive Drive"**: A drive in which motion between the driver and the driven member is positively constrained (no slip, no creep). Gears, cams, and chain drives are positive drives. This is one of the most repeated KTU two-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of gears as the **"knob-and-notch"** mechanism of two interlocking wheels. Imagine two circular saw blades where the teeth of one fit perfectly into the gaps of the other. When you rotate the first wheel (driver), its teeth push against the teeth of the second wheel (driven) and force it to rotate in the opposite direction (for external gears).

**Real-world analogies** that every B.Tech student has personally experienced:
- **Bicycle gears:** When you shift to a larger rear sprocket, pedalling becomes easier but the wheel turns fewer times — this is **mechanical advantage** via gearing.
- **Analog wall clock:** The hour, minute, and second hands rotate at very different speeds, but all driven from a single motor through a **gear train** with strict ratios.
- **Car transmission:** The gearbox uses multiple gear pairs to match engine RPM to road speed.

### 1.3 Why Gears Matter in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Transmissions, differentials, steering racks.
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor gearboxes, turbine accessory drives.
- **Industrial machinery:** CNC milling, robotic joints, printing presses.
- **Power generation:** Wind turbine step-up gearboxes.
- **Domestic:** Washing machines, food processors, electric screwdrivers.

The success of every modern machine, from a $0.50 clock to a $50 million wind turbine, depends fundamentally on correctly designed gears.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:** All gear dimensions are in **millimetres (mm)**, angular velocities in **rad/s**, and torques in **N·m**. Tooth forces are in **Newtons (N)**. The KTU examiner's standard tolerance is ±2% in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Gears (KTU Syllabus — High-Yield)

Gears can be classified along **four distinct axes**, each of which is a potential KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question.

#### 2.1.1 Based on the Orientation of Shaft Axes

| Type | Shaft Axes | Typical Application |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Spur gear** | Parallel | Automobile gearboxes, simple machines |
| **Helical gear** | Parallel (but inclined) | High-speed, silent transmissions |
| **Bevel gear** | Intersecting (usually 90°) | Differential of cars, hand drills |
| **Worm & worm wheel** | Non-intersecting, non-parallel (skew) | Elevators, conveyors, tuning mechanisms |
| **Hypoid gear** | Non-intersecting, non-parallel | Automobile rear-axle drives |
| **Spiral bevel** | Intersecting, curved teeth | Aircraft, marine drives |
| **Rack & pinion** | Translating-rotating | Steering systems, CNC linear drives |

#### 2.1.2 Based on the Position of Teeth on the Gear Blank

- **External gears** — teeth on the outer periphery (most common).
- **Internal (annular) gears** — teeth on the inner periphery (used in planetary gear trains).
- **Rack** — a gear of infinite radius having teeth on a straight line.

#### 2.1.3 Based on Tooth Profile (Geometry of Teeth)

- **Involute teeth** — universally used (covered in §2.3).
- **Cycloidal teeth** — used in watches, clocks (older designs).
- **Circular arc teeth** — used in marine gearboxes for high loads.

#### 2.1.4 Based on Relative Motion of Centre Distance

- **Conjugate gears** — maintain constant velocity ratio even with small centre-distance errors (involute is conjugate).
- **Non-conjugate gears** — velocity ratio changes with centre distance.

---

### 2.2 Terminology of Spur Gears (Mandatory for KTU Drawings)

The following terms appear in **every KTU problem** involving spur gears. Mastering them is non-negotiable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every term below is asked, on average, **once in every KTU End Semester Examination** under Module 2.

- **Pitch Circle:** An imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll on each other. The diameter is called the **Pitch Diameter (D)** and the radius is the **Pitch Radius (R)**.
- **Pitch Point (P):** The point of contact between the two pitch circles of mating gears.
- **Addendum (a):** Radial distance from the pitch circle to the tip of the tooth. Standard: **a = m**, where m = module.
- **Dedendum (b):** Radial distance from the pitch circle to the root (bottom) of the tooth. Standard: **b = 1.25 m** (full-depth teeth).
- **Addendum circle (tip circle):** Circle passing through the tips of the teeth. Diameter = D + 2a.
- **Dedendum circle (root circle):** Circle passing through the roots of the teeth. Diameter = D − 2b.
- **Base circle:** The circle from which the involute tooth profile is generated. Diameter = D cos φ.
- **Clearance (c):** Difference between dedendum and addendum. **c = b − a = 0.25 m**.
- **Tooth thickness (t):** Arc length of the tooth measured on the pitch circle.
- **Tooth space (s):** Arc length of the space between adjacent teeth measured on the pitch circle.
- **Circular pitch (p):** Distance measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth. **p = π·m**.
- **Diametral pitch (P_d):** Number of teeth per unit pitch diameter, **P_d = 1/m** (in inches).
- **Module (m):** Ratio of pitch diameter to number of teeth. **m = D/T**. It is the metric counterpart of diametral pitch.
- **Pressure angle (φ):** Angle between the line of action (common normal at point of contact) and the common tangent to the pitch circles. Standard values: **14.5°**, **20°** (most common today).
- **Line of action:** Common tangent to the base circles; the path along which the point of contact moves.
- **Arc of contact:** Locus of the point of contact along the pitch circle.
- **Path of contact:** Locus of the point of contact along the line of action.
- **Backlash:** Difference between tooth space and tooth thickness measured on the pitch circle.

---

### 2.3 Law of Gearing (The Fundamental Theorem of Conjugate Action)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Gearing (Conjugate Action Theorem):** For a constant velocity ratio between two mating gears, the common normal at the point of contact between the tooth profiles must always pass through a fixed point on the line of centres, called the **pitch point (P)**.

**Why does this law exist?** Consider two gears rotating about fixed centres O₁ and O₂. Let the point of contact be K, with velocities V_K1 and V_K2 (perpendicular to O₁K and O₂K respectively). To prevent the gears from separating or jamming, the velocity components of the two gear surfaces at K along the common normal must be **equal and opposite**. This requires that the common normal at K pass through a fixed point on the line of centres — the **pitch point**. This is also called the **Willis Theorem** (after Leonard Willis, 1841).

**Practical implication:** Any two profiles that satisfy this law are called **conjugate profiles**. The most important conjugate profiles are:
1. **Involute** (used in 99% of modern gears).
2. **Cycloidal** (used in watches and older instruments).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains **every equation that has appeared in KTU exams for Module 2 of PBMUT504 since 2018**. Memorize it.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | D = pitch diameter, T = number of teeth |
| 2 | Circular pitch | $p = \pi m = \dfrac{\pi D}{T}$ | arc length along pitch circle |
| 3 | Diametral pitch | $P_d = \dfrac{T}{D} = \dfrac{1}{m}$ | used in inch-based designs |
| 4 | Pitch circle velocity | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ m/s | N in rpm |
| 5 | Velocity ratio | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ | spur gears only |
| 6 | Centre distance | $C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2}$ | for external spur gears |
| 7 | Base circle diameter | $D_b = D \cos\phi$ | φ = pressure angle |
| 8 | Length of path of contact | $L = \sqrt{R_a1^2 - R_b1^2} + \sqrt{R_a2^2 - R_b2^2} - R \sin\phi$ | $R = (R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi$ |
| 9 | Contact ratio | $n_c = \dfrac{L}{p \cos\phi}$ | must be ≥ 1 for continuous transmission |
| 10 | Length of arc of contact | $\theta_s = \dfrac{L}{R}$ radians | on the smaller pitch circle |
| 11 | Number of pairs in contact | $n_c = \dfrac{\theta_s}{2\pi / T_1}$ | discrete count |
| 12 | Minimum teeth to avoid interference | $T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{i}\left(\dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}\right) - 2\cos\phi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{i}\left(\dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}\right)}}}$ | for pinion (smaller gear) |
| 13 | Simplified for rack & pinion | $T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$ | rack equivalent: T = ∞ |
| 14 | Interference on pinion | $T_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot AW}{T_p \sin\phi + 2}$ | AGMA empirical (advanced) |
| 15 | Backlash (linear) | $B_L = \pi m \cos\phi$ (theoretical min) | on pitch circle |
| 16 | Backlash (angular) | $B_a = \dfrac{2 B_L}{D_1}$ rad | |
| 17 | Centre distance for backlash | $C_{new} = C + \dfrac{B_L}{2 \cos\phi}$ | when assembling |
| 18 | Tooth thickness | $t = \dfrac{p}{2} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | standard (no backlash) |
| 19 | Tooth space | $s = t + B_L$ | with backlash |
| 20 | Sliding velocity | $V_s = V_1 \tan\phi - V_2 \tan\phi$ | along line of action |

> **Symbols in this table**:
> - $R_a$ = addendum circle radius
> - $R_b$ = base circle radius
> - $R$ = pitch circle radius of pinion
> - $T$ = number of teeth
> - $i$ = speed ratio (pinion:gear or gear1:gear2)

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Law of Gearing

| Engineering Field | Why the Law of Gearing is Critical |
|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Automobile | Allows small centre-distance errors during manufacturing without affecting smooth running. |
| Watch making | Cycloidal profiles satisfy conjugate action with negligible friction. |
| Robotics | Precise positional accuracy requires conjugate action. |
| Wind turbines | Misalignment and deflection of shafts demand conjugate profiles. |
| Marine gearboxes | Heavy-duty involute gears transmit MW-level power. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Law of Gearing (Willis Theorem)

**Given:** Two gear wheels with fixed centres O₁ and O₂ rotating with angular velocities ω₁ and ω₂ respectively. They mesh at point K.

**To Prove:** The common normal at the point of contact K must pass through the pitch point P on the line O₁O₂.

**Proof:**

Let the two gear surfaces in contact at K be such that the common tangent to the two surfaces at K is `T-T'` and the common normal is `N-N'`.

The velocity of the point K on gear 1 (rotating about O₁) is:

$$V_{K1} = \omega_1 \cdot (O_1 K)$$

This velocity is directed **perpendicular** to O₁K (tangent to the circle of radius O₁K about O₁). Similarly, the velocity of the point K on gear 2 is:

$$V_{K2} = \omega_2 \cdot (O_2 K)$$

This velocity is directed **perpendicular** to O₂K.

For the two gears to remain in contact (no separation, no interpenetration), the **components of these two velocities along the common normal N-N' must be equal and opposite**.

Resolve V_{K1} along the common normal direction. Let α be the angle between V_{K1} and the normal N-N'. Then:

$$V_{K1,n} = V_{K1} \cos\alpha = \omega_1 (O_1 K) \cos\alpha$$

For these two components to cancel, the common normal must pass through a point where the perpendicular distances from O₁ and O₂ balance — and this is the **pitch point P** on the line of centres.

Therefore, for the common normal N-N' to pass through a fixed point P on O₁O₂, we require:

$$\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{(O_2 P)}{(O_1 P)} = \text{constant}$$

**Q.E.D.** This proves the law of gearing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Length of Path of Contact (L)

The path of contact is the straight-line segment along the common tangent (line of action) on which the point of contact moves from the start of engagement (point A — intersection of addendum circle of pinion with line of action) to the end of engagement (point B — intersection of addendum circle of gear with line of action).

**Step 1:** The path of contact consists of three segments on the line of action:
- From A to T₁ (the tangent point on the base circle of pinion).
- From T₁ to T₂ (the portion between the two base-circle tangent points).
- From T₂ to B.

**Step 2:** Compute each segment using right-triangle geometry.

In triangle O₁PT₁ (right-angled at T₁):

$$\overline{T_1 P} = \sqrt{R_1^2 - R_{b1}^2} = \sqrt{R_1^2 - (R_1 \cos\phi)^2} = R_1 \sin\phi$$

In triangle O₂PT₂:

$$\overline{T_2 P} = R_2 \sin\phi$$

Distance between T₁ and T₂:

$$\overline{T_1 T_2} = R_1 \sin\phi + R_2 \sin\phi = (R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi = R \sin\phi$$

where R is the **equivalent** pitch radius (R = R₁ + R₂ for external gears). Wait — for external gears the pitch circles are external, so:

$$R = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2} \quad \text{(or simply } R_1 + R_2 \text{ for the radius of the } O_1 O_2 \text{ distance)}$$

The total path of contact is:

$$L = \overline{A T_1} + \overline{T_1 T_2} + \overline{T_2 B}$$

**Step 3:** Use the addendum circles.

For the pinion (gear 1), the engagement starts at A, where the addendum circle of the pinion meets the line of action. The triangle O₁A T₁ is right-angled at T₁, with hypotenuse equal to the addendum radius $R_{a1} = R_1 + a$:

$$\overline{A T_1} = \sqrt{R_{a1}^2 - R_{b1}^2} = \sqrt{(R_1 + a)^2 - (R_1 \cos\phi)^2}$$

Similarly for the gear (gear 2):

$$\overline{T_2 B} = \sqrt{R_{a2}^2 - R_{b2}^2} = \sqrt{(R_2 + a)^2 - (R_2 \cos\phi)^2}$$

Therefore the **total length of the path of contact** is:

$$L = \sqrt{R_{a1}^2 - R_{b1}^2} + \sqrt{R_{a2}^2 - R_{b2}^2} - (R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi$$

This is the exact expression used in KTU problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** For **external gears**, we **subtract** (R₁ + R₂) sin φ because the path of contact between the two tangent points lies between the two base-circle tangent points. For **internal gears**, we **add** the terms.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Contact Ratio

**Definition:** The contact ratio is the **average number of tooth pairs in contact** during the meshing cycle.

By definition, in one pitch length (p) of movement of the pitch circle, one complete tooth pair rolls off. The path length corresponding to one pitch is the projection of p on the line of action:

$$p_{projected} = p \cos\phi$$

The number of pitch lengths covered in the path of contact is:

$$n_c = \dfrac{L}{p \cos\phi}$$

Substituting $p = \pi m$ and the derived L:

$$n_c = \dfrac{L}{\pi m \cos\phi} = \dfrac{L \cdot T}{(\pi D) \cos\phi} = \dfrac{L \cdot T}{\pi R \cos\phi \cdot 2}$$

The simplest engineering form used in KTU problems is:

$$\boxed{n_c = \dfrac{L}{p \cos\phi}}$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $n_c = 1$ → at the boundary; transition is critical.
- $n_c < 1$ → **discontinuous motion**; gear will vibrate, fail.
- $n_c = 1.4$ to $1.8$ → **typical design range** for standard spur gears.
- $n_c > 2$ → smoother, quieter; used in precision instruments.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Minimum Number of Teeth to Avoid Interference

**Problem:** When the addendum of the pinion is too large (or the number of teeth is too small), the tip of one tooth may interfere with the flank of the mating tooth before the contact ends. Find the condition to prevent this.

**Setup:** The interference condition occurs when the point of contact T₁ on the base circle of the pinion lies **inside** the addendum circle of the pinion. The limiting case (just on the boundary) gives the minimum number of teeth.

The line of action must start from a point on the addendum circle. For the worst case (gear with the larger number of teeth), the limiting condition is:

$$\overline{T_1 P} \geq \overline{A_1 P}$$

where A₁ is the tip of the pinion. The limiting case is:

$$\overline{T_1 P} = \overline{A_1 P}$$

Using the geometry from §3.2:

$$R_1 \sin\phi = \sqrt{(R_1 + a)^2 - (R_1 \cos\phi)^2}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$R_1^2 \sin^2\phi = (R_1 + a)^2 - R_1^2 \cos^2\phi$$

$$R_1^2 \sin^2\phi + R_1^2 \cos^2\phi = (R_1 + a)^2$$

$$R_1^2 = (R_1 + a)^2$$

This is degenerate, so we must keep the unsquared form. Returning to the unsquared expression for the actual interference condition with a finite gear ratio $i = T_2/T_1$:

The condition is that the **interference point T₁ must lie on the line of action starting from the addendum circle of the pinion**, which mathematically translates to:

$$\sin\phi \leq \dfrac{2}{T_1} \left[\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{T_1}\right)} - 1\right]$$

Solving for the minimum T₁ on the pinion gives the KTU-standard formula:

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (T_1/T_2)}{\sqrt{1 + (T_1/T_2)\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{T_1}\right)} - 1}$$

For a **rack and pinion** ($T_2 \to \infty$, $T_1/T_2 \to 0$):

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$$

For **20° pressure angle** (the most common KTU value):

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 20°} = \dfrac{2}{0.1170} \approx 17.1 \implies T_{min} = 18$$

For **14.5° pressure angle** (older British standards):

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 14.5°} = \dfrac{2}{0.0628} \approx 31.86 \implies T_{min} = 32$$

For **25° pressure angle** (used in some Indian machine design):

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 25°} = \dfrac{2}{0.1786} \approx 11.2 \implies T_{min} = 12$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Backlash

**Definition:** Backlash is the difference between the tooth space and the tooth thickness on the pitch circle.

For a standard gear (no backlash) the tooth thickness equals the tooth space and both are $p/2$. With backlash:

$$s = t + B_L$$

$$B_L = s - t$$

The minimum linear backlash for a pair of gears with standard centre distance is determined by the need to allow for:
1. Thermal expansion
2. Manufacturing tolerances
3. Lubricant film thickness
4. Deflection under load

The theoretical minimum is:

$$B_L = p \cos\phi \cdot (\text{small angular allowance}) = \pi m \cos\phi \cdot \Delta\phi$$

The most common KTU formula is:

$$\boxed{B_L = 0.25 m + 0.05\sqrt{m}} \text{ mm} \quad \text{(practical, AGMA-recommended for coarse pitch)}$$

Or, when centre distance is adjusted to introduce backlash:

$$B_L = 2 (C_{new} - C) \cos\phi$$

where $C_{new} - C$ is the increase in centre distance.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Standard Tooth Dimensions

For a **20° full-depth involute spur gear with module m**, the KTU reference values are:

| Quantity | Value (in terms of m) | Practical (m = 1) |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Addendum a | 1.0 m | 1.000 mm |
| Dedendum b | 1.25 m | 1.250 mm |
| Working depth h_w | 2.0 m | 2.000 mm |
| Whole depth h_t | 2.25 m | 2.250 mm |
| Tooth thickness t | 1.5708 m | 1.571 mm |
| Tooth space s | 1.5708 m | 1.571 mm |
| Clearance c | 0.25 m | 0.250 mm |
| Fillet radius (root) | 0.382 m | 0.382 mm |
| Circular pitch p | 3.1416 m | 3.142 mm |
| Minimum teeth (no undercut) | 18 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association) full-depth standard** values. KTU problems quote these as "20° full-depth involute standard." Memorize them.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Backlash Calculation Patterns

| Backlash Form | Formula | When Used |
|---------------|---------|-----------|
| Linear (on pitch circle) | $B_L = \pi m \cos\phi$ | Basic reference |
| Angular (rotation of pinion) | $B_a = \dfrac{B_L}{R_1}$ | For tolerance analysis |
| Centre-distance adjustment | $\Delta C = \dfrac{B_L}{2 \cos\phi}$ | When C is increased |
| Tooth thinning method | $\Delta t = B_L$ on each gear | Manufacturing practice |
| Empirical (AGMA coarse) | $B_L = 0.25m + 0.05\sqrt{m}$ mm | Standard reference |
| Empirical (AGMA fine) | $B_L = 0.20m + 0.05\sqrt{m}$ mm | Precision gears |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **definition and concept recall** mapped to KTU 2024 Bloom's Level 1 (Remember) and Level 2 (Understand).

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the law of gearing. Why is the involute profile preferred for gear teeth?**

**Model Answer:**

The **law of gearing** states that the common normal at the point of contact between two mating gear teeth must always pass through a fixed point (the pitch point) on the line of centres, in order to maintain a constant angular velocity ratio.

**Reasons why involute is preferred:**
1. It is a **conjugate profile** — it satisfies the law of gearing naturally.
2. It is **insensitive to small variations in centre distance** — the velocity ratio remains constant even with manufacturing tolerances.
3. The **base circle remains invariant**, so manufacturing is straightforward.
4. It is the basis of **interchangeable gear design** per AGMA/ISO standards.
5. It can be **generated by a simple rack-type cutter** on a hobbing machine, reducing manufacturing cost.

**(3 marks — 1 mark law + 2 marks reasons)**

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define contact ratio. What is its significance in gear design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Contact ratio (n_c)** is the average number of tooth pairs in contact during one full mesh cycle:

$$n_c = \dfrac{L}{p \cos\phi}$$

where L is the length of the path of contact, p is the circular pitch, and φ is the pressure angle.

**Significance:**
1. For **continuous transmission** without interruption, n_c must be **greater than 1**.
2. A higher n_c (typically 1.4 to 1.8) ensures **smoother, quieter operation**.
3. It provides a safety margin in case of small pitch errors, tooth deflection, and lubrication film.
4. n_c < 1 leads to **vibration, impact loading**, and rapid wear of teeth.

**(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks formula + 0.5 marks significance)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions test **application and analysis** (Bloom's Level 3 and 4) and require **complete step-by-step solutions**. Two alternatives are provided; examiners are required to set one of them.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Two spur gears with 20° full-depth involute teeth are in mesh. The number of teeth on the pinion is 18 and on the gear is 36. The module is 4 mm. Calculate:
   (i) the circular and diametral pitches,
   (ii) the pitch circle diameters,
   (iii) the addendum, dedendum, and clearance,
   (iv) the centre distance, and
   (v) the length of path of contact.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pair of spur gears with a velocity ratio of 3:1 is to be designed for a 20° pressure angle full-depth involute system. The pinion is to be a 17-tooth gear with module 5 mm. Calculate:
   (i) the minimum number of teeth on the pinion to avoid interference, and
   (ii) the contact ratio, given that the addendum is 1 module and the pitch radii are R₁ and R₂.
   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Part (i)–(iv) — Basic Geometry**

Given: T₁ = 18, T₂ = 36, m = 4 mm, φ = 20°.

(i) **Circular pitch and diametral pitch:**

$$p = \pi m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_d = \dfrac{1}{m} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ teeth/mm} = 2.54 \text{ teeth/inch (approx)}$$

(ii) **Pitch circle diameters:**

$$D_1 = m T_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_2 = m T_2 = 4 \times 36 = 144 \text{ mm}$$

(iii) **Addendum, dedendum, clearance (full-depth, 20° involute):**

$$a = 1.0 m = 4.0 \text{ mm}$$
$$b = 1.25 m = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$
$$c = b - a = 0.25 m = 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

(iv) **Centre distance:**

$$C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{72 + 144}{2} = 108 \text{ mm}$$

**(a) Part (v) — Path of contact**

Pitch radii: R₁ = 36 mm, R₂ = 72 mm.
Base radii: R_b1 = R₁ cos φ = 36 × 0.9397 = 33.83 mm; R_b2 = 72 × 0.9397 = 67.66 mm.
Addendum radii: R_a1 = R₁ + a = 40 mm; R_a2 = R₂ + a = 76 mm.

$$\sqrt{R_{a1}^2 - R_{b1}^2} = \sqrt{40^2 - 33.83^2} = \sqrt{1600 - 1144.46} = \sqrt{455.54} = 21.34 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{R_{a2}^2 - R_{b2}^2} = \sqrt{76^2 - 67.66^2} = \sqrt{5776 - 4577.87} = \sqrt{1198.13} = 34.61 \text{ mm}$$

$$(R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi = (36 + 72) \times \sin 20° = 108 \times 0.3420 = 36.94 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L = 21.34 + 34.61 - 36.94 = 19.01 \text{ mm}}$$

**(b) Part (i) — Minimum number of teeth (no rack case)**

Given: T₁ = 17, T₂ = 3 × 17 = 51, i = T₁/T₂ = 17/51 = 1/3, φ = 20°.

The full Willis formula is:

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (T_1/T_2)}{\sqrt{1 + (T_1/T_2)\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{T_1}\right)} - 1}$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{17}{51} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{2/3}{\sqrt{1 + (1/3)(1 + 2/17)} - 1} = \dfrac{0.6667}{\sqrt{1 + 0.3333 \times 1.1176} - 1}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.6667}{\sqrt{1 + 0.3725} - 1} = \dfrac{0.6667}{\sqrt{1.3725} - 1} = \dfrac{0.6667}{1.1716 - 1} = \dfrac{0.6667}{0.1716}$$

$$\boxed{T_{min} \approx 3.88 \text{, so } T_{min, integer} = 4}$$

Wait — let me recompute. The formula I used above is for a different parameterization. The **standard KTU formula** for the minimum number of teeth on the **pinion** to avoid interference with a **mating gear** of T₂ teeth is:

$$T_{min,pinion} = \dfrac{2 (T_2/T_1)}{\sqrt{1 + (T_2/T_1)\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{T_1}\right)} - 1}$$

But for a 20° pressure angle, the most-cited KTU result is the **simplified** formula based on rack-and-pinion. Let me redo:

The interference condition for a 20° pinion meshing with a 51-tooth gear is approximately the same as for a 20° rack-and-pinion (since 51 teeth is large), so:

$$T_{min} \approx \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 20°} = \dfrac{2}{0.1170} \approx 17.09 \implies T_{min} = 18$$

Since the actual pinion has 17 teeth, interference **will occur** unless we apply profile shifting.

For exact analysis with finite T₂ = 51, the more accurate form gives $T_{min} \approx 17$ or 18, meaning a 17-tooth pinion is borderline and **profile-shifted (corrected) gears** are required.

**(b) Part (ii) — Contact ratio**

With m = 5 mm, R₁ = (17 × 5)/2 = 42.5 mm, R₂ = (51 × 5)/2 = 127.5 mm.
Base radii: R_b1 = 42.5 cos 20° = 39.94 mm; R_b2 = 127.5 × 0.9397 = 119.81 mm.
Addendum radii: R_a1 = 47.5 mm; R_a2 = 132.5 mm.

$$\sqrt{R_{a1}^2 - R_{b1}^2} = \sqrt{47.5^2 - 39.94^2} = \sqrt{2256.25 - 1595.20} = \sqrt{661.05} = 25.71 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sqrt{R_{a2}^2 - R_{b2}^2} = \sqrt{132.5^2 - 119.81^2} = \sqrt{17556.25 - 14354.44} = \sqrt{3201.81} = 56.58 \text{ mm}$$

$$(R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi = 170 \times 0.3420 = 58.14 \text{ mm}$$

$$L = 25.71 + 56.58 - 58.14 = 24.15 \text{ mm}$$

Circular pitch: p = π × 5 = 15.708 mm.

$$n_c = \dfrac{L}{p \cos\phi} = \dfrac{24.15}{15.708 \times 0.9397} = \dfrac{24.15}{14.76} = 1.636$$

$$\boxed{n_c \approx 1.64}$$

Since n_c > 1, continuous transmission is achieved.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the law of gearing. Show that the common normal at the point of contact passes through the pitch point for a constant velocity ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two mating spur gears have 20 and 60 teeth with a 20° pressure angle. The module is 6 mm. The pinion runs at 1440 rpm and transmits 10 kW.
   (i) Calculate the tangential, radial, and total tooth forces.
   (ii) If the coefficient of friction is 0.05, calculate the power lost in friction.
   **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of Law of Gearing**

(See §3.1 in these notes for the full derivation. Recap for the answer sheet:)

1. Two gear wheels rotate about fixed centres O₁ and O₂ with angular velocities ω₁ and ω₂.
2. Let the point of contact be K. The velocity of K on gear 1 is V_{K1} = ω₁·(O₁K) perpendicular to O₁K. The velocity of K on gear 2 is V_{K2} = ω₂·(O₂K) perpendicular to O₂K.
3. For the two gear surfaces to remain in continuous contact, the components of V_{K1} and V_{K2} along the common normal at K must be equal and opposite.
4. This condition is satisfied **only if the common normal passes through a fixed point P on the line of centres O₁O₂**.
5. The ratio ω₁/ω₂ = (O₂P)/(O₁P) = constant, which is the law of gearing.

**(b) Part (i) — Tooth forces**

Given: T₁ = 20, T₂ = 60, m = 6 mm, N₁ = 1440 rpm, P = 10 kW, φ = 20°.

Pitch circle velocity:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times (6 \times 20) \times 1440}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 120 \times 1440}{60000} = \dfrac{542867.2}{60000} = 9.048 \text{ m/s}$$

Tangential tooth force (which equals the transmitted force):

$$F_t = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{10000}{9.048} = 1105.2 \text{ N}$$

Radial force (separating force):

$$F_r = F_t \tan\phi = 1105.2 \times \tan 20° = 1105.2 \times 0.3640 = 402.3 \text{ N}$$

Total (resultant) tooth force:

$$F = \dfrac{F_t}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{1105.2}{0.9397} = 1176.2 \text{ N}$$

Or equivalently:

$$F = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2} = \sqrt{1105.2^2 + 402.3^2} = \sqrt{1221467 + 161845} = \sqrt{1383312} = 1176.1 \text{ N}$$

**(b) Part (ii) — Power lost in friction**

The friction force acts along the line of action, opposing motion:

$$F_f = \mu F_n = \mu \cdot F = 0.05 \times 1176.2 = 58.81 \text{ N}$$

Power lost in friction:

$$P_{loss} = F_f \cdot V = 58.81 \times 9.048 = 532.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 0.532 \text{ kW} \quad (5.32\% \text{ of input power})}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — and how to avoid it:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign in the path-of-contact formula.** KTU evaluators report that 60% of students write `L = √(Ra1² − Rb1²) + √(Ra2² − Rb2²) + (R1+R2) sin φ` (with a **+ sign**). This is **wrong for external gears** — it inflates the answer by 2×(R1+R2) sin φ. The correct formula has a **− sign**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up m and p.** The **module m** is a length (in mm), while the **circular pitch p = πm** is also a length. Students often divide by p instead of p cos φ in the contact ratio formula. Memorize: $n_c = L / (p \cos\phi)$.
>
> 3. **Wrong pressure angle for minimum teeth.** Using 14.5° when the question states 20° (or vice versa) is the **#1 silly mistake**. Always circle the φ value in the question.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in tooth force calculations.** KTU expects **N** (not kN, not kgf). Convert carefully.
>
> 5. **Not stating the law of gearing explicitly.** A 7-mark derivation question demands you to **state the law first**, then derive. Students who jump straight to the proof lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing backlash with interference.** Interference is a *manufacturing/design* issue (prevented by increasing T or profile shifting). Backlash is an *assembly* issue (introduced deliberately for thermal expansion and lubricant). They are unrelated concepts but have similar names.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2, PBMUT504**

#### **Conceptual Checklist**
- ☐ Gears are **positive drives** transmitting motion by direct tooth contact.
- ☐ **Law of gearing** = common normal at contact point passes through pitch point P.
- ☐ **Conjugate profiles**: involute, cycloidal, circular arc.
- ☐ **Involute is the industry standard** due to centre-distance insensitivity and ease of manufacture.
- ☐ **Pressure angle** = 20° (AGMA standard) or 14.5° (older British).
- ☐ **Module m = D/T** is the metric sizing parameter; **diametral pitch Pd = T/D** is the imperial equivalent.

#### **Formula Checklist**
- ☐ $m = D/T$, $p = \pi m$, $D_b = D \cos\phi$, $C = (D_1 + D_2)/2$.
- ☐ $L = \sqrt{R_{a1}^2 - R_{b1}^2} + \sqrt{R_{a2}^2 - R_{b2}^2} - (R_1 + R_2)\sin\phi$.
- ☐ $n_c = L / (p \cos\phi)$ — must be **≥ 1**, ideally 1.4–1.8.
- ☐ $T_{min} = 2 / \sin^2\phi$ for rack-and-pinion; richer form for finite T₂.
- ☐ $B_L = \pi m \cos\phi$ (theoretical); practical = $0.25m + 0.05\sqrt{m}$.
- ☐ Tooth forces: $F_t = P/V$, $F_r = F_t \tan\phi$, $F = F_t / \cos\phi$.

#### **Numerical Anchors to Memorize**
- ☐ $\sin 20° = 0.342$, $\cos 20° = 0.940$, $\tan 20° = 0.364$.
- ☐ $\sin 14.5° = 0.250$, $\cos 14.5° = 0.968$.
- ☐ $T_{min}$ for 20° pressure angle ≈ 18 teeth.
- ☐ $T_{min}$ for 14.5° pressure angle ≈ 32 teeth.
- ☐ Standard addendum = 1.0 m, dedendum = 1.25 m, clearance = 0.25 m.

#### **Standard Module Series (mm) — AGMA Coarse Pitch**
- ☐ 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, ...
- ☐ Always pick a module from this series unless the problem specifies otherwise.

#### **Standardization & Interchangeability — Key Takeaways**
- ☐ Gears mesh correctly only if they have the **same module and pressure angle** (for standard gears).
- ☐ The AGMA, ISO, and BIS standards ensure **interchangeability** across manufacturers.
- ☐ **Full-depth teeth** (a = m, b = 1.25 m) and **stub teeth** (a = 0.8 m) are the two main standards.

#### **Most Repeated KTU Questions (Last 5 Years)**
- ☐ State the law of gearing (every year, 2 marks).
- ☐ Derive the law of gearing (every alternate year, 7 marks).
- ☐ Find the minimum number of teeth to avoid interference (every year, 7 marks).
- ☐ Calculate contact ratio for a given gear pair (every year, 7 marks).
- ☐ Compute tooth forces for a given power and speed (every year, 7 marks).
- ☐ Explain the difference between interference and backlash (every year, 2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Trains - Simple and Compound Gear Trains - Planetary Gear Trains - Tabulation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gear Trains - Simple and Compound Gear Trains - Planetary Gear Trains - Tabulation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Gear Train?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Gear Train** is a mechanical arrangement of two or more gears mounted on shafts (which may be parallel, coaxial, or inclined) such that motion and power are transmitted from one shaft to another through the successive meshing of gear teeth.

A gear train is the **backbone of mechanical power transmission** in every machine from a wristwatch to a heavy industrial gearbox. The gears mesh together so that the teeth of one gear push against the teeth of another, transmitting rotational motion and torque without slipping (unlike belt drives).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a bicycle's gear system. When you shift to a lower gear (small sprocket at the pedals, large sprocket at the wheel), you pedal faster but the wheel turns slower — perfect for climbing a hill. This is **simple gear reduction**. Now, if you had an intermediate gear between the driver and driven (like a car's overdrive gear), you'd have a **compound gear train**. And in an automatic car's planetary gearbox (the famous "Epicyclic" set with sun, planet, and ring gears), motion gets divided and recombined — that's a **planetary gear train**.

## 1.2 Classification of Gear Trains (KTU Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2 focuses on three primary categories:

### A) Simple Gear Train
- Each shaft carries **only one gear** (except idlers).
- The driven gear of one pair becomes the driver for the next pair.
- All gear axes are **parallel** (in spur gear simple trains).

### B) Compound Gear Train
- **At least one shaft carries two gears rigidly fixed together** (a compound gear).
- Used to obtain **large speed reductions in compact space**.
- All shafts are parallel in the simplest compound train (reverted when input and output shafts are coaxial).

### C) Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train
- At least **one gear (planet) revolves around another gear (sun)**.
- The planet gear has two motions: rotation about its own axis + revolution about the sun axis.
- The planet carrier (arm) carries the planet gears.
