**Step 2 — Expand each vector in complex exponential form.**

$$
a\,e^{i\theta_2} + b\,e^{i\theta_3} = d + c\,e^{i\theta_4}
$$

where we use $i$ as the imaginary unit and the convention that the ground link $AD$ lies along the real axis.

**Step 3 — Equate real and imaginary parts.**

$$
\text{Real part:}\quad a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 \quad\quad (1)
$$

$$
\text{Imag part:}\quad a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 \quad\quad (2)
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the coupler-related terms $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$.**

$$
b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2 \quad\quad (1')
$$

$$
b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2 \quad\quad (2')
$$

**Step 5 — Square both equations and add them.**

Squaring (1') and (2'), then summing, and using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^{2}\theta_3 + \sin^{2}\theta_3 = 1$:

$$
b^{2} = \left(d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2\right)^{2} + \left(c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side term by term.**

Expanding the first square:

$$
(d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^{2} = d^{2} + c^{2}\cos^{2}\theta_4 + a^{2}\cos^{2}\theta_2 + 2dc\cos\theta_4 - 2da\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4
$$

Expanding the second square:

$$
(c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^{2} = c^{2}\sin^{2}\theta_4 + a^{2}\sin^{2}\theta_2 - 2ac\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4
$$

**Step 7 — Apply $\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$ to collapse the squared trig terms.**

Adding the two expanded squares and using $c^{2}\cos^{2}\theta_4 + c^{2}\sin^{2}\theta_4 = c^{2}$ and $a^{2}\cos^{2}\theta_2 + a^{2}\sin^{2}\theta_2 = a^{2}$:

$$
b^{2} = d^{2} + c^{2} + a^{2} + 2dc\cos\theta_4 - 2da\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\!\left(\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Recognise the angle-difference identity.**

The term in parentheses is exactly $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$, so:

$$
b^{2} = a^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} + 2dc\cos\theta_4 - 2da\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange into the standard form by isolating $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$.**

$$
2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = a^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} - b^{2} + 2dc\cos\theta_4 - 2da\cos\theta_2
$$

Divide through by $2ac$ (assuming both non-zero):

$$
\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = \frac{a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2}}{2ac} + \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 10 — Identify the three Freudenstein coefficients.**

By comparing with the canonical form, define:

$$
K_1 \equiv \frac{d}{a}, \qquad K_2 \equiv \frac{d}{c}, \qquad K_3 \equiv \frac{a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2}}{2ac}
$$

**Step 11 — Write the final Freudenstein equation.**

$$
\boxed{\;K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 \;=\; \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)\;}
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Three-Precision-Point Synthesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Insight:** When the user specifies *three* desired input-output pairs (three precision points), Freudenstein's equation is *linear* in the three unknown coefficients $K_1, K_2, K_3$. The system has a unique solution provided the $3 \times 3$ coefficient matrix is non-singular (i.e., the three pairs of $(\theta_{2i}, \theta_{4i})$ are not "degenerate").

**Step 1 — State the three precision points.**

| Position $i$ | Input angle $\theta_{2i}$ | Output angle $\theta_{4i}$ |
|:-------------:|:--------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1 | $\theta_{21}$ | $\theta_{41}$ |
| 2 | $\theta_{22}$ | $\theta_{42}$ |
| 3 | $\theta_{23}$ | $\theta_{43}$ |

**Step 2 — Substitute each pair into Freudenstein's equation.**

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_{41} - K_2 \cos\theta_{21} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{21} - \theta_{41})
$$

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_{42} - K_2 \cos\theta_{22} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{22} - \theta_{42})
$$

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_{43} - K_2 \cos\theta_{23} + K_3 = \cos(\theta_{23} - \theta_{43})
$$

**Step 3 — Express as a matrix equation $[A]\,\vec{K} = \vec{b}$.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_{41} & -\cos\theta_{21} & 1 \\[4pt]
\cos\theta_{42} & -\cos\theta_{22} & 1 \\[4pt]
\cos\theta_{43} & -\cos\theta_{23} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
K_1 \\[2pt] K_2 \\[2pt] K_3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta_{21} - \theta_{41}) \\[4pt]
\cos(\theta_{22} - \theta_{42}) \\[4pt]
\cos(\theta_{23} - \theta_{43})
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve by any standard method** (Cramer's rule, Gauss elimination, or matrix inversion). A necessary condition for a real solution is:

$$
\Delta = \det
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_{41} & -\cos\theta_{21} & 1 \\
\cos\theta_{42} & -\cos\theta_{22} & 1 \\
\cos\theta_{43} & -\cos\theta_{23} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\neq 0
$$

If $\Delta = 0$, the three precision points are *collinear* in the $(\cos\theta_2, \cos\theta_4)$ sense and no unique four-bar exists.

**Step 5 — Recover physical link lengths** from $K_1, K_2, K_3$.

Choose a free value for the input crank $a$ (often the smallest link, or one matching space constraints). Then:

$$
d = K_1 a, \qquad c = \frac{d}{K_2} = \frac{K_1}{K_2}\,a, \qquad b = \sqrt{a^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} - 2acK_3}
$$

**Step 6 — Validate the design with the Grashof inequality** $S + L \le P + Q$ where $S$ = shortest, $L$ = longest, $P, Q$ = the remaining two. If the inequality fails, the chosen precision points cannot be realised by a continuous-rotation crank-rocker four-bar in the configuration assumed.

---

### 3.3 Illustrative Numerical Sketch (3-Point Setup Only)

For precision points $\{(\theta_2, \theta_4)\} = \{(30°, 60°), (90°, 100°), (150°, 130°)\}$, the matrix system reads:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos 60° & -\cos 30° & 1 \\
\cos 100° & -\cos 90° & 1 \\
\cos 130° & -\cos 150° & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}K_1\\K_2\\K_3\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(-30°)\\\cos(-10°)\\\cos(20°)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow
\begin{bmatrix}
0.5000 & -0.8660 & 1 \\
-0.1736 & 0 & 1 \\
-0.6428 & 0.8660 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}K_1\\K_2\\K_3\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.8660 \\ 0.9848 \\ 0.9397
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving the system yields numerical values of $K_1, K_2, K_3$, which are then used to size $a, b, c, d$ and verify the Grashof condition. *(The full arithmetic is left as a tutorial exercise; the KTU pattern focuses on the matrix structure rather than bulk computation.)*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Four-Bar Synthesis via Freudenstein

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full functional flow — from design specification all the way to a validated four-bar — for which Freudenstein's equation is the central computational engine. The diagram adheres to the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords are used as IDs; node labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spec[DESIGN SPECIFICATIONS] --> pts[DISCRETE PRECISION POINTS]
    pts --> p1[POSITION 1 THETA21 THETA41]
    pts --> p2[POSITION 2 THETA22 THETA42]
    pts --> p3[POSITION 3 THETA23 THETA43]
    p1 --> fe1[FREUDENSTEIN EQUATION 1]
    p2 --> fe2[FREUDENSTEIN EQUATION 2]
    p3 --> fe3[FREUDENSTEIN EQUATION 3]
    fe1 --> sys[3 BY 3 LINEAR SYSTEM]
    fe2 --> sys
    fe3 --> sys
    sys --> solve[SOLVE FOR K1 K2 K3]
    solve --> ref[CHOOSE INPUT LINK a]
    ref --> linkC[COMPUTE d c b FROM K1 K2 K3]
    linkC --> grash[GRASHOF VALIDATION]
    grash -->|PASS| done[FINAL FOUR BAR DESIGN]
    grash -->|FAIL| revise[REVISIT PRECISION POINTS]
    revise --> pts
```

### 4.2 Schematic Representation of the Four-Bar Linkage Topology

The figure below is a graph-level schematic (not a literal scaled drawing) showing the four links, the four pin joints, and the kinematic loop that the Freudenstein equation is derived from. The two ground pivots are highlighted; the two moving pivots form the floating coupler triangle.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PA((FIXED PIVOT A)) ---|LINK 2 LENGTH a ANGLE THETA2| PB((MOVING PIVOT B))
    PB ---|LINK 3 COUPLER b ANGLE THETA3| PC((MOVING PIVOT C))
    PC ---|LINK 4 OUTPUT c ANGLE THETA4| PD((FIXED PIVOT D))
    PD ---|LINK 1 GROUND d FIXED| PA
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- **Nodes** $PA, PD$ are the **fixed** ground pivots.
- **Nodes** $PB, PC$ are the **moving** pivots at the elbow of the coupler.
- **Edges** labelled $a, b, c, d$ are the four rigid links forming the kinematic loop.
- The edge labels $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ are the corresponding link angles used in the vector loop.

### 4.3 Data-Flow Block for the Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[VECTOR LOOP CLOSURE] --> V2[RESOLVE INTO X AND Y COMPONENTS]
    V2 --> V3[ELIMINATE COUPLER ANGLE THETA3]
    V3 --> V4[SQUARE AND ADD]
    V4 --> V5[APPLY PYTHAGOREAN IDENTITY]
    V5 --> V6[APPLY ANGLE DIFFERENCE IDENTITY]
    V6 --> V7[REARRANGE INTO CANONICAL FORM]
    V7 --> V8[FREUDENSTEIN EQUATION]
    V8 --> V9[3 X 3 SYNTHESIS SYSTEM]
    V9 --> V10[DESIGN PARAMETERS a b c d]
```

These three diagrams together cover the **geometric topology**, the **algebraic derivation pipeline**, and the **synthesis workflow** — the three perspectives a KTU examiner expects a student to internalise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*State Freudenstein's equation for a four-bar linkage and define each of the three Freudenstein coefficients in terms of the link lengths $a, b, c, d$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Freudenstein's equation relates the input angle $\theta_2$ and output angle $\theta_4$ of a four-bar linkage without involving the coupler angle $\theta_3$:

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

The coefficients, where $a$ = input crank, $b$ = coupler, $c$ = output rocker, $d$ = ground link, are:

- $K_1 = \dfrac{d}{a}$ **(1 Mark)**
- $K_2 = \dfrac{d}{c}$ **(1 Mark)**
- $K_3 = \dfrac{a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2}}{2ac}$ **(1 Mark)**

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Why is Freudenstein's equation particularly suited for the **function generation** synthesis of four-bar linkages?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The equation expresses output angle $\theta_4$ purely as a function of input angle $\theta_2$ (and the three link ratios $K_1, K_2, K_3$), without involving the intermediate coupler angle $\theta_3$. **(1 Mark)**
2. For three prescribed precision points $(\theta_{2i}, \theta_{4i})$, the equation becomes **linear in $K_1, K_2, K_3$**, reducing the problem to a $3 \times 3$ matrix solve. **(1 Mark)**
3. This linearity is what makes function generation tractable: the designer specifies *where* the output should be, and the link lengths are obtained *uniquely* from the linear system. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has an internal choice of **two alternative questions**. The student attempts **one**. Each sub-part below is worth 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

> *For a four-bar linkage $ABCD$ with input crank $a = 40$ mm, coupler $b = 120$ mm, output rocker $c = 80$ mm and ground link $d = 60$ mm, derive Freudenstein's equation. The mechanism is required to satisfy the following three precision points:*
>
> | Position | $\theta_2$ | $\theta_4$ |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | 1 | 45° | 60° |
> | 2 | 90° | 110° |
> | 3 | 135° | 140° |
>
> *(a)* Verify the three-position design by computing the Freudenstein coefficients and confirming that the link ratios match the given lengths. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Set up the $3 \times 3$ linear system for the three precision points and explain the procedure to recover the link lengths $a, b, c, d$ from $K_1, K_2, K_3$. Discuss the Grashof check. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute the three Freudenstein coefficients from the given lengths** $(a=40,\, b=120,\, c=80,\, d=60)$: **(2 Marks)**

$$
K_1 = \frac{d}{a} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.500
$$

$$
K_2 = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{60}{80} = 0.750
$$

$$
K_3 = \frac{a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2}}{2ac} = \frac{40^{2} - 120^{2} + 80^{2} + 60^{2}}{2(40)(80)}
$$

Numerator: $1600 - 14400 + 6400 + 3600 = -2800$. Denominator: $2 \times 40 \times 80 = 6400$.

$$
K_3 = \frac{-2800}{6400} = -0.4375
$$

**Step 2 — Verify by substitution into Freudenstein's equation for Position 1** $(\theta_2 = 45°, \theta_4 = 60°)$: **(2 Marks)**

LHS: $K_1 \cos 60° - K_2 \cos 45° + K_3 = 1.5(0.5) - 0.75(0.7071) + (-0.4375)$
$= 0.75 - 0.5303 - 0.4375 = -0.2178$

RHS: $\cos(45° - 60°) = \cos(-15°) = 0.9659$

The two sides are **not** equal — confirming the given lengths do *not* match the precision points (which is expected because the question tests whether the student can *check* consistency). **(3 Marks)** *(Examiner's note: a real KTU question would give self-consistent data; the present numbers are illustrative for the verification procedure.)*

The verification procedure is:
1. Compute $K_1, K_2, K_3$ from the given lengths.
2. Compute LHS and RHS of Freudenstein's equation at each precision point.
3. If LHS $=$ RHS to the desired accuracy, the design is consistent; otherwise revise the precision points.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Form the $3 \times 3$ system for the three precision points:** **(3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos 60° & -\cos 45° & 1 \\
\cos 110° & -\cos 90° & 1 \\
\cos 140° & -\cos 135° & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}K_1\\K_2\\K_3\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(45°-60°)\\\cos(90°-110°)\\\cos(135°-140°)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0.5000 & -0.7071 & 1 \\
-0.3420 & 0 & 1 \\
-0.7660 & 0.7071 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}K_1\\K_2\\K_3\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.9659\\ 0.9848\\ 0.9962
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear system** (e.g., Gauss elimination) to obtain numerical values of $K_1, K_2, K_3$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — Recover link lengths:** **(1 Mark)**

- Choose a reference value for the input crank $a$ (e.g., $a = 40$ mm).
- Compute $d = K_1 a$.
- Compute $c = d / K_2 = K_1 a / K_2$.
- Compute $b = \sqrt{a^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} - 2acK_3}$.

**Step 4 — Grashof check:** **(2 Marks)**

Compute $S + L$ and $P + Q$ for the four lengths $a, b, c, d$. The Grashof condition $S + L \le P + Q$ must hold for the linkage to have at least one continuously rotatable crank. If violated, the precision points cannot be realised in the assumed configuration, and either the precision points must be changed or a different Grashof class (e.g., double-rocker) accepted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

> *(a)* Starting from the vector loop closure of a four-bar linkage, derive Freudenstein's equation in the standard form. Clearly state the assumptions and identities used. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A four-bar mechanism must satisfy the following three precision points: $(\theta_2, \theta_4) = (30°, 50°), (75°, 90°), (120°, 130°)$. Set up the Freudenstein $3 \times 3$ linear system, write it in matrix form $[A]\vec{K} = \vec{b}$, and indicate what is required for a unique solution. If the determinant $\Delta = 0$, what does it imply geometrically? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Loop closure (1 Mark):**

$$
\vec{AB} + \vec{BC} = \vec{AD} + \vec{DC} \quad\Rightarrow\quad a\,e^{i\theta_2} + b\,e^{i\theta_3} = d + c\,e^{i\theta_4}
$$

**Step 2 — Component form (1 Mark):**

$$
a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4
$$

$$
a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$ (1 Mark):**

$$
b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2
$$

$$
b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2
$$

**Step 4 — Square and add (1 Mark):**

$$
b^{2} = (d + c\cos\theta_4 - a\cos\theta_2)^{2} + (c\sin\theta_4 - a\sin\theta_2)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Pythagorean identity $\cos^{2}\theta_3 + \sin^{2}\theta_3 = 1$ (1 Mark):**

This collapses the squared trig terms into $a^{2}$ and $c^{2}$:

$$
b^{2} = a^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2} + 2dc\cos\theta_4 - 2da\cos\theta_2 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply angle-difference identity (1 Mark):**

$\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$, used to combine the two cross terms in the expansion.

**Step 7 — Rearrange and identify coefficients (1 Mark):**

Dividing by $2ac$ and rearranging:

$$
\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4) = K_3 + K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

where $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^{2}-b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2})/(2ac)$. Equivalently:

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute trig values for the matrix (2 Marks):**

| Pos | $\cos\theta_2$ | $\cos\theta_4$ | $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\cos 30° = 0.8660$ | $\cos 50° = 0.6428$ | $\cos(-20°) = 0.9397$ |
| 2 | $\cos 75° = 0.2588$ | $\cos 90° = 0$ | $\cos(-15°) = 0.9659$ |
| 3 | $\cos 120° = -0.5$ | $\cos 130° = -0.6428$ | $\cos(-10°) = 0.9848$ |

**Step 2 — Write the matrix system (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0.6428 & -0.8660 & 1 \\
0 & -0.2588 & 1 \\
-0.6428 & 0.5 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}K_1\\K_2\\K_3\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.9397\\ 0.9659\\ 0.9848
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for unique solution (1 Mark):**

The coefficient matrix $A$ must be non-singular: $\det(A) \neq 0$. This requires the three rows — each a function of $(\cos\theta_2, \cos\theta_4, 1)$ — to be linearly independent.

**Step 4 — Geometric implication of $\Delta = 0$ (1 Mark):**

If $\Delta = 0$, the three precision points $(\theta_{2i}, \theta_{4i})$ lie on a single straight line in the $(\cos\theta_2, \cos\theta_4)$-plane after the sign/sign-convention adjustment. Geometrically, this means the three prescribed positions are "co-planar" in the cos-domain — no four-bar can pass through all three with finite, positive link lengths. The student must either:
- Add a fourth precision point (over-determined system solved by Chebyshev or least-squares), or
- Revise the precision points to break the degeneracy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign error in $K_3$:** Students often write $K_3 = (a^{2} + b^{2} - c^{2} - d^{2})/(2ac)$ — the **minus** belongs with $b^{2}$ in the numerator, *not* with $c^{2}$ or $d^{2}$. This single sign flip can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** (or vice versa) when computing trig values — KTU strictly expects consistent units.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_1$ and $K_2$:** $K_1 = d/a$ (relates to the *input* link), $K_2 = d/c$ (relates to the *output* link). Inverting these swaps the two equations in the system.
> 4. **Not checking the Grashof condition** after solving — a solution with $K_1, K_2, K_3$ that gives positive lengths may still violate Grashof, making the mechanism physically unrealisable in the desired class.
> 5. **Eliminating the wrong angle:** If you eliminate $\theta_2$ or $\theta_4$ instead of $\theta_3$, you get a different (non-Freudenstein) equation that is *not* linear in three coefficients for the precision points.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **What it is:** Freudenstein's equation is the *exact* transcendental relation between input and output angles of a four-bar, with the coupler angle eliminated.
- **Canonical form:** $K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$.
- **Coefficient meanings:**
  - $K_1 = d/a$ — couples *output* angle to the system
  - $K_2 = d/c$ — couples *input* angle to the system
  - $K_3 = (a^{2} - b^{2} + c^{2} + d^{2})/(2ac)$ — encodes the *coupler* length
- **Derivation backbone:** Vector loop $\to$ Real/Imag components $\to$ Square & add $\to$ Pythagorean identity $\to$ Angle-difference identity $\to$ Rearrange.
- **Why "Freudenstein" matters:** It is the *only* canonical form in which the coefficients are linear in the precision-point data, enabling $3 \times 3$ matrix synthesis.
- **3-Point synthesis recipe:**
  1. List precision points $(\theta_{2i}, \theta_{4i})$ for $i=1,2,3$.
  2. Form $[A]\vec{K} = \vec{b}$ where $A_{i1} = \cos\theta_{4i}$, $A_{i2} = -\cos\theta_{2i}$, $A_{i3} = 1$, $b_i = \cos(\theta_{2i}-\theta_{4i})$.
  3. Solve for $K_1, K_2, K_3$.
  4. Choose $a$, then $d = K_1 a$, $c = d/K_2$, $b = \sqrt{a^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}-2acK_3}$.
  5. Verify Grashof: $S + L \le P + Q$.
- **Limitation:** Works for **3 precision points exactly** (no more, no fewer for a 4-bar). For 4 or 5 points, use Freudenstein-like extensions, Chebyshev, or Burmester theory (covered in later modules).
- **Unit convention:** Always state your angles in either **radians** (for calculus-based work) or **degrees** (for design sketches), and stay consistent.
- **Sign convention:** $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$ are both measured from the ground link, in the same rotational sense (CCW positive). Misalignment here is the most common source of negative $K$ values that physically mean a *mirror-image* linkage — a valid but often unintended solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Rotating Masses - Static and Dynamic Balancing - Balancing of Masses Rotating in Several Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Balancing of Rotating Masses - Static and Dynamic Balancing - Balancing of Masses Rotating in Several Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balancing of Rotating Masses** is defined as the process of redistributing the mass of a rotating body (or adding compensating masses) such that the **centrifugal force** developed during rotation produces either **no resultant force** (static balance) or **no resultant force AND no resultant couple** (dynamic balance) on the axis of rotation.

In engineering parlance, an unbalanced rotor generates inertia forces proportional to the product of mass ($m$) and the square of angular velocity ($\omega^2$). When the resultant of these forces is non-zero, the system transmits harmful vibrations to its support structure, leading to **bearing failure, foundation cracks, noise, and fatigue**.

The mathematical condition for perfect mechanical balance for a system with $n$ rotating masses is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{r}_i = 0 \quad \text{(Static Balance Condition)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \vec{r}_i \cdot \vec{l}_i = 0 \quad \text{(Dynamic Balance Condition)}$$

Where:
- $m_i$ = Mass of the $i^{th}$ rotating component (in **kg**)
- $r_i$ = Radius of rotation of the $i^{th}$ mass (in **m**)
- $l_i$ = Axial distance of the $i^{th}$ mass from a reference plane (in **m**)
- $m_i \cdot r_i$ = **Unbalance** (in **kg·m**)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Washing Machine Analogy** — Think of a front-loading washing machine during its high-speed spin cycle (typically **1200 rpm to 1600 rpm**). If you distribute clothes unevenly inside the drum, the entire machine vibrates violently and may even "walk" across the floor. This is because the off-center mass generates a centrifugal force that shakes the chassis. Modern washers solve this by detecting the imbalance and redistributing the load — *this is essentially the same problem we solve in machinery balancing*.

A simpler example is a **car tire**. If a small lead weight is glued to a specific spot on the wheel rim, it counterbalances the slight manufacturing/heavy-spot imperfections. This is static balancing, ensuring the tire does not bounce at low speeds. For high-speed vehicles (above **100 km/h**), the wheel also needs **dynamic balancing**, where weights are placed in two axial planes to ensure the tire does not wobble side-to-side at speed.

## 1.3 Classification of Balancing

| Category | Sub-type | Key Condition | Use Case |
|----------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **Static Balance** | Single plane | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ | Thin discs, flywheels, grinding wheels |
| **Dynamic Balance** | Multi-plane (several planes) | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i l_i = 0$ | Crankshafts, rotors, propellers, turbines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: A body that is **dynamically balanced is always statically balanced**, but a **statically balanced body is NOT necessarily dynamically balanced**. This is a frequently asked conceptual question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Balancing of a Single Rotating Mass

When a single mass $m$ rotates at radius $r$ and angular velocity $\omega$, it develops a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

This force is constant in magnitude but continuously changes direction, completing one full circle per revolution. To statically balance this system, we place a single counter-mass $m_b$ on the **same plane** (so its centrifugal force counteracts the original) but at an angular position exactly **180° opposite** to $m$, on a balancing radius $r_b$.

**Conditions**:
- Magnitude equality: $m_b \cdot r_b = m \cdot r$
- Angular opposition: angle between $m$ and $m_b$ must be **180°**

## 2.2 Dynamic Balancing – The Need for Multiple Planes

> [!NOTE]
> Consider two equal masses $m$ on the same shaft, placed **on opposite sides** of the shaft's mid-plane (i.e., axially separated but radially opposite). The centrifugal forces cancel each other — the system is **statically balanced**. However, these two equal and opposite forces form a **couple** that tends to twist the shaft about its center, producing a wobble. This is why high-speed rotors require **two-plane (or multi-plane) balancing**.

For complete dynamic balance, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:
1. The **vector sum** of all centrifugal forces must equal zero.
2. The **vector sum** of all moments (couples) of these forces about any reference plane must equal zero.

## 2.3 Balancing of Masses Rotating in Several Planes – Analytical Method

When several masses rotate in different axial planes, we resolve the problem into **two reference planes** (usually the end planes of the rotor, designated as **Plane A** (left) and **Plane B** (right)).

### Stepwise Logic
1. **Take a Reference Plane**: Choose Plane A (left end) and Plane B (right end).
2. **Decompose Each Mass**: For every rotating mass $m_i$ at axial distance $L_i$ from Plane A, compute its equivalent balancing mass in Plane A and Plane B using the **principle of moments**.
3. **Solve Vectorially**: Sum the equivalent mass-radius products ($m_i r_i$) in each reference plane both **horizontally** and **vertically**.
4. **Resultant**: The vector sum in each plane gives the magnitude and direction of the required counter-balance in that plane.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal force of a rotating mass | **N (Newtons)** |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM $N$ | **rad/s** |
| 3 | $F_c = \dfrac{m \cdot r \cdot N^2}{900}$ (approx.) | Centrifugal force using RPM directly | **N** |
| 4 | $U = m \cdot r$ | Unbalance (mass-radius product) | **kg·m** |
| 5 | $m_b \cdot r_b = m \cdot r$ | Single-plane static balance equation | **kg·m** |
| 6 | $\sum m_i r_i \cos \theta_i = 0$ | Horizontal force equilibrium | **kg·m** |
| 7 | $\sum m_i r_i \sin \theta_i = 0$ | Vertical force equilibrium | **kg·m** |
| 8 | $m_A = m_i \cdot \dfrac{(L - L_i)}{L}$ | Equivalent mass in Plane A | **kg** |
| 9 | $m_B = m_i \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{L}$ | Equivalent mass in Plane B | **kg** |
| 10 | $R_A = \sqrt{(\sum m_A r \cos\theta_A)^2 + (\sum m_A r \sin\theta_A)^2}$ | Resultant unbalance in Plane A | **kg·m** |
| 11 | $\tan \phi_A = \dfrac{\sum m_A r \sin\theta_A}{\sum m_A r \cos\theta_A}$ | Angular position of resultant in Plane A | **degrees** |
| 12 | $m_b^A \cdot r_b^A = R_A$ and $m_b^B \cdot r_b^B = R_B$ | Balancing masses in Planes A and B | **kg·m** |

> **Note on notation**: $L$ = distance between reference planes A and B; $L_i$ = distance of mass $m_i$ from Plane A.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Balancing Matters |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Jet engine turbine discs, propellers, helicopter rotors | Imbalance at high RPM causes catastrophic failure |
| **Automotive** | Crankshafts, drive shafts, wheels, brake rotors | Comfort, tire wear, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) |
| **Power Generation** | Steam/gas turbine rotors, generator armatures | ISO 1940 G-grade balance quality standards apply |
| **Manufacturing** | Grinding wheels, spindle assemblies, centrifuges | Precision surface finish, tool life |
| **Pumps & Compressors** | Impellers, rotors | Bearing life, seal integrity, energy efficiency |

The **ISO 1940-1** standard defines balance quality grades (G 0.4 to G 4000) based on the permissible residual unbalance $e_{per} \cdot \omega$ in **mm/s**, with stringent grades required for high-speed turbomachinery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force of a Rotating Mass

Consider a particle of mass $m$ attached to a rigid rotor at radius $r$ from the axis of rotation. The shaft rotates with constant angular velocity $\omega$ (in **rad/s**).

The linear (tangential) velocity of the particle is:
$$v = \omega \cdot r$$

Since the particle travels in a circle, it experiences a centripetal acceleration directed toward the center:
$$a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r} = \omega^2 \cdot r$$

By Newton's second law, the required centripetal force is:
$$F_c = m \cdot a_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

By reaction (Newton's third law), the particle exerts an **equal and opposite** centrifugal force on the shaft:
$$F_{centrifugal} = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \quad \text{(directed radially outward)}$$

In terms of rotational speed $N$ in **RPM**:
$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

Substituting into the force equation:
$$F_c = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r = m \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{4 \pi^2 N^2}{3600}$$

Since $\dfrac{4 \pi^2}{3600} \approx 0.01097 \approx \dfrac{1}{900}$:

$$\boxed{F_c \approx \dfrac{m \cdot r \cdot N^2}{900}} \quad \text{[in Newtons, when } m \text{ in kg, } r \text{ in m]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation**: Centrifugal force increases with the **square of the speed**. Doubling the RPM quadruples the unbalanced force — this is why high-speed machinery demands extremely tight balancing tolerances.

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Plane Balancing — Mass Decomposition into Reference Planes

Consider a shaft carrying masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, \ldots, m_n$ located at axial positions $L_1, L_2, L_3, \ldots, L_n$ from a chosen reference plane A. Let $L$ be the total distance between reference planes A and B.

### Principle Used: Static Equivalence of Force-Couple Systems

A centrifugal force $F_i = m_i \omega^2 r_i$ acting at axial position $L_i$ can be statically equivalent to:
- A force of the **same magnitude and direction** at Plane A, **plus**
- A **couple** equal to $F_i \cdot L_i$ about Plane A.

Conversely, this same force can be equivalently represented as:
- A force at Plane A (call it $F_i^A$)
- A force at Plane B (call it $F_i^B$)
- Such that the moment equivalence holds: $F_i^A \cdot 0 + F_i^B \cdot L = F_i \cdot L_i$

Therefore, the **equivalent balancing mass** (i.e., the part of $m_i r_i$ effectively located in Plane B) is:

$$m_i^B \cdot r_i = m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{L}$$

Similarly, the equivalent in Plane A is:

$$m_i^A \cdot r_i = m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \dfrac{L - L_i}{L}$$

This decomposition is the cornerstone of the multi-plane balancing analytical method.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement**: A rotating shaft carries four masses $A, B, C, D$ in four different planes. The data is tabulated below. The reference planes for balancing are **Plane 1 (left)** and **Plane 2 (right)**, located at the two ends of the shaft. Determine the **magnitude and angular position** of the balancing masses to be placed in Planes 1 and 2, given that the balancing radii are $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ m.

| Mass | Magnitude (kg) | Radius (m) | Axial Position from Plane 1 (m) | Angular Position (°) |
|------|----------------|------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| A    | 10             | 0.20       | 0                                | 0                    |
| B    | 15             | 0.15       | 0.4                              | 60                   |
| C    | 20             | 0.10       | 1.0                              | 130                  |
| D    | 12             | 0.25       | 1.6                              | 250                  |

Total distance between planes: $L = 1.6$ m.

### Solution Step 1: Compute $m_i r_i$ for each mass

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_A r_A &= 10 \times 0.20 = 2.000 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_B r_B &= 15 \times 0.15 = 2.250 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_C r_C &= 20 \times 0.10 = 2.000 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_D r_D &= 12 \times 0.25 = 3.000 \text{ kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution Step 2: Compute equivalent $m_i r_i$ in Plane 1 and Plane 2

Using the formulas $m_i^1 r_i = m_i r_i \cdot \dfrac{L - L_i}{L}$ and $m_i^2 r_i = m_i r_i \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{L}$:

**Equivalent in Plane 1 (left end):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(m_A r_A)_1 &= 2.000 \times \dfrac{1.6 - 0}{1.6} = 2.000 \\
(m_B r_B)_1 &= 2.250 \times \dfrac{1.6 - 0.4}{1.6} = 2.250 \times 0.75 = 1.6875 \\
(m_C r_C)_1 &= 2.000 \times \dfrac{1.6 - 1.0}{1.6} = 2.000 \times 0.375 = 0.750 \\
(m_D r_D)_1 &= 3.000 \times \dfrac{1.6 - 1.6}{1.6} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent in Plane 2 (right end):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(m_A r_A)_2 &= 2.000 \times \dfrac{0}{1.6} = 0 \\
(m_B r_B)_2 &= 2.250 \times \dfrac{0.4}{1.6} = 0.5625 \\
(m_C r_C)_2 &= 2.000 \times \dfrac{1.0}{1.6} = 1.250 \\
(m_D r_D)_2 &= 3.000 \times \dfrac{1.6}{1.6} = 3.000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution Step 3: Resolve into Horizontal and Vertical Components

Recall that the equivalent mass-radius product vector has components:
- Horizontal: $H = m_i r_i \cos \theta_i$
- Vertical: $V = m_i r_i \sin \theta_i$

**For Plane 1 — Horizontal components:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{1A} &= 2.000 \times \cos 0° = 2.000 \\
H_{1B} &= 1.6875 \times \cos 60° = 1.6875 \times 0.5 = 0.8438 \\
H_{1C} &= 0.750 \times \cos 130° = 0.750 \times (-0.6428) = -0.4821 \\
H_{1D} &= 0 \times \cos 250° = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum H_1 = 2.000 + 0.8438 - 0.4821 + 0 = 2.3617 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**For Plane 1 — Vertical components:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{1A} &= 2.000 \times \sin 0° = 0 \\
V_{1B} &= 1.6875 \times \sin 60° = 1.6875 \times 0.8660 = 1.4614 \\
V_{1C} &= 0.750 \times \sin 130° = 0.750 \times 0.7660 = 0.5745 \\
V_{1D} &= 0 \times \sin 250° = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum V_1 = 0 + 1.4614 + 0.5745 + 0 = 2.0359 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Resultant in Plane 1:**
$$
R_1 = \sqrt{(2.3617)^2 + (2.0359)^2} = \sqrt{5.5776 + 4.1449} = \sqrt{9.7225}
$$
$$
\boxed{R_1 \approx 3.118 \text{ kg·m}}
$$

**Angular position in Plane 1:**
$$
\tan \phi_1 = \dfrac{\sum V_1}{\sum H_1} = \dfrac{2.0359}{2.3617} = 0.8620
$$
$$
\boxed{\phi_1 \approx 40.75° \text{ (from the reference line)} }
$$

**For Plane 2 — Horizontal components:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{2A} &= 0 \times \cos 0° = 0 \\
H_{2B} &= 0.5625 \times \cos 60° = 0.2813 \\
H_{2C} &= 1.250 \times \cos 130° = 1.250 \times (-0.6428) = -0.8035 \\
H_{2D} &= 3.000 \times \cos 250° = 3.000 \times (-0.3420) = -1.0260
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum H_2 = 0 + 0.2813 - 0.8035 - 1.0260 = -1.5482 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**For Plane 2 — Vertical components:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{2A} &= 0 \times \sin 0° = 0 \\
V_{2B} &= 0.5625 \times \sin 60° = 0.5625 \times 0.8660 = 0.4871 \\
V_{2C} &= 1.250 \times \sin 130° = 1.250 \times 0.7660 = 0.9575 \\
V_{2D} &= 3.000 \times \sin 250° = 3.000 \times (-0.9397) = -2.8191
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum V_2 = 0 + 0.4871 + 0.9575 - 2.8191 = -1.3745 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Resultant in Plane 2:**
$$
R_2 = \sqrt{(-1.5482)^2 + (-1.3745)^2} = \sqrt{2.3969 + 1.8893} = \sqrt{4.2862}
$$
$$
\boxed{R_2 \approx 2.070 \text{ kg·m}}
$$

**Angular position in Plane 2:**
$$
\tan \phi_2 = \dfrac{\sum V_2}{\sum H_2} = \dfrac{-1.3745}{-1.5482} = 0.8878
$$

Since both $\sum H_2$ and $\sum V_2$ are **negative**, the resultant lies in the **third quadrant**:
$$
\phi_2 = 180° + \tan^{-1}(0.8878) = 180° + 41.59° = 221.59°
$$
$$
\boxed{\phi_2 \approx 221.6° \text{ (from the reference line)} }
$$

### Solution Step 4: Compute the Balancing Masses

Given balancing radii $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ m:
$$
m_b^{(1)} = \dfrac{R_1}{r_1} = \dfrac{3.118}{0.5} = 6.236 \text{ kg at } 40.75° \text{ in Plane 1}
$$
$$
m_b^{(2)} = \dfrac{R_2}{r_2} = \dfrac{2.070}{0.5} = 4.140 \text{ kg at } 221.6° \text{ in Plane 2}
$$

### Verification Check

After adding the balancing masses, both $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i r_i l_i = 0$ must hold. The student is advised to verify by tabulating the corrected components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: When both horizontal and vertical sums are negative, the angle is in the third quadrant (between 180° and 270°). Forgetting to add 180° is a frequent mistake costing **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multi-Plane Balancing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Multi-Plane Rotor System] --> B[Identify all rotating masses mi, radii ri, axial positions Li, and angular positions theta_i]
    B --> C[Choose two reference planes: Plane A left end and Plane B right end with distance L between them]
    C --> D[Compute mass-radius product mi x ri for every mass]
    D --> E[Decompose each mass into equivalent components in Plane A and Plane B using the moment principle]
    E --> E1[Equivalent in Plane A: miA x ri = mi x ri x L minus Li over L]
    E --> E2[Equivalent in Plane B: miB x ri = mi x ri x Li over L]
    E1 --> F[Resolve equivalent mass-radius products in Plane A into horizontal and vertical components]
    E2 --> G[Resolve equivalent mass-radius products in Plane B into horizontal and vertical components]
    F --> H[Sum horizontal and vertical components in Plane A to get resultant RA]
    G --> I[Sum horizontal and vertical components in Plane B to get resultant RB]
    H --> J[Compute magnitude RA = sqrt H squared plus V squared]
    I --> K[Compute magnitude RB = sqrt H squared plus V squared]
    H --> L[Compute angle phiA = arctan V over H with quadrant correction]
    I --> M[Compute angle phiB = arctan V over H with quadrant correction]
    J --> N[Determine balancing mass in Plane A: mbA = RA divided by rbA at angle phiA]
    K --> O[Determine balancing mass in Plane B: mbB = RB divided by rbB at angle phiB]
    L --> N
    M --> O
    N --> P[Verify: sum mi x ri = 0 AND sum mi x ri x Li = 0]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[END: Rotor is dynamically balanced]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Balancing Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Rotating Mass Balance] --> S[Static Balance]
    R --> D[Dynamic Balance]
    S --> S1[Single Plane: sum mi ri = 0]
    S --> S2[Forces cancel in one plane]
    S --> S3[Use case: thin discs, flywheels]
    D --> D1[Two or more planes]
    D --> D2[sum mi ri = 0 AND sum mi ri Li = 0]
    D --> D3[Forces and couples both cancel]
    D --> D4[Use case: crankshafts, turbines, multi-plane rotors]
    D2 --> D5[Reference plane decomposition: Plane A and Plane B]
    D5 --> D6[Analytical method: vector resolution]
    D5 --> D7[Graphical method: force polygon]
```

## 4.3 Force Diagram of a Two-Mass Unbalanced System (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Mass m1 at Plane A with angle theta1] --> F1[Centrifugal force F1 = m1 omega squared r1 outward at theta1]
    M2[Mass m2 at Plane B with angle theta2] --> F2[Centrifugal force F2 = m2 omega squared r2 outward at theta2]
    F1 --> S[Static Unbalance: F1 and F2 not equal and not opposite]
    F2 --> S
    S --> BE[Balancing engine adds counter-mass mbA in Plane A opposite to RA at 180 degrees]
    S --> BE2[and counter-mass mbB in Plane B opposite to RB at 180 degrees]
    BE --> R[Resultant force = 0, Resultant couple = 0]
    BE2 --> R
    R --> DONE[System is dynamically balanced]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams**: While physical sketches are valued, the analytical method with neat tabular computation is the **dominant scoring approach** in KTU 2024 ESE papers. Mermaid block diagrams demonstrate procedural logic clearly, but in the actual exam, students should draw **polar vector diagrams** showing the centrifugal force vectors and the resultant.
