# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between static and dynamic balancing of rotating masses.**

**Model Answer**:
- **Static balancing** refers to the condition where the **resultant centrifugal force** of all rotating masses is zero, i.e., $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i = 0$. It is sufficient for a single-plane rotor (e.g., a thin disc). It is checked by placing the rotor on **frictionless knife edges** and observing whether it remains at rest in any angular position.
- **Dynamic balancing** refers to the condition where both the **resultant centrifugal force AND the resultant couple** of all rotating masses are zero. The conditions are $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i \times \vec{L}_i = 0$. It is required for rotors with masses distributed over several axial planes (e.g., crankshafts, turbine rotors). It is checked on a **dynamic balancing machine**.
- A dynamically balanced rotor is always statically balanced, but a statically balanced rotor is **not** necessarily dynamically balanced.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is the significance of the term "mass-radius product" in balancing of rotating masses? Why is balancing radius chosen based on practical considerations?**

**Model Answer**:
- The **mass-radius product** ($m \cdot r$) is a measure of the **unbalance** of a rotating mass and represents the magnitude of the centrifugal effect produced. The centrifugal force is $F_c = (m \cdot r) \cdot \omega^2$, so $m \cdot r$ is the controlling parameter for the inertia force.
- The **balancing radius** is chosen based on practical and safety considerations. A **larger radius** allows a **smaller balancing mass** to produce the same $m \cdot r$ product, but the radius is limited by the **mechanical clearance**, **rotor geometry**, and **safety margin** to prevent the balancing mass from rubbing against the casing. Typically, the balancing radius is kept slightly less than the rotor radius.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Question with Internal Choice]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A shaft carries four masses $A, B, C, D$ in four different planes. The relevant data is given in the table below. Find the **magnitude and angular position** of the balancing masses in planes **L (left)** and **R (right)**, which are at the ends of the shaft, assuming the balancing radii are **0.2 m** in plane L and **0.25 m** in plane R. **[7 Marks]**

| Mass | Mass (kg) | Radius (m) | Distance from L (m) | Angular Position (°) |
|------|-----------|------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| A    | 12        | 0.18       | 0                    | 30                   |
| B    | 18        | 0.12       | 0.3                  | 90                   |
| C    | 16        | 0.15       | 0.7                  | 180                  |
| D    | 14        | 0.20       | 1.2                  | 270                  |

**Total shaft length** $L = 1.2$ m.

**(b)** Explain with neat diagrams the **graphical method** of balancing masses rotating in several planes. Mention its limitations compared to the analytical method. **[7 Marks]**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute $m_i r_i$ (mass-radius product):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_A r_A &= 12 \times 0.18 = 2.16 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_B r_B &= 18 \times 0.12 = 2.16 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_C r_C &= 16 \times 0.15 = 2.40 \text{ kg·m} \\
m_D r_D &= 14 \times 0.20 = 2.80 \text{ kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Decompose into Plane L and Plane R using $L = 1.2$ m:**

Using $m_i^L r_i = m_i r_i \cdot \dfrac{L - L_i}{L}$ and $m_i^R r_i = m_i r_i \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{L}$:

**Plane L (left end):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(m_A r_A)_L &= 2.16 \times \dfrac{1.2 - 0}{1.2} = 2.1600 \\
(m_B r_B)_L &= 2.16 \times \dfrac{1.2 - 0.3}{1.2} = 2.16 \times 0.75 = 1.6200 \\
(m_C r_C)_L &= 2.40 \times \dfrac{1.2 - 0.7}{1.2} = 2.40 \times 0.4167 = 1.0000 \\
(m_D r_D)_L &= 2.80 \times \dfrac{1.2 - 1.2}{1.2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plane R (right end):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(m_A r_A)_R &= 2.16 \times \dfrac{0}{1.2} = 0 \\
(m_B r_B)_R &= 2.16 \times \dfrac{0.3}{1.2} = 0.5400 \\
(m_C r_C)_R &= 2.40 \times \dfrac{0.7}{1.2} = 1.4000 \\
(m_D r_D)_R &= 2.80 \times \dfrac{1.2}{1.2} = 2.8000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Resolve Plane L into horizontal and vertical components:**

| Mass | $m_i^L r_i$ | $\theta$ (°) | $\cos\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $H$ | $V$ |
|------|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|------|------|
| A    | 2.1600       | 30            | 0.8660       | 0.5000        | 1.8706 | 1.0800 |
| B    | 1.6200       | 90            | 0.0000       | 1.0000        | 0.0000 | 1.6200 |
| C    | 1.0000       | 180           | -1.0000      | 0.0000        | -1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| D    | 0            | 270           | 0.0000       | -1.0000       | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | — | **0.8706** | **2.7000** |

**Resultant in Plane L:**
$$
R_L = \sqrt{(0.8706)^2 + (2.7000)^2} = \sqrt{0.7579 + 7.2900} = \sqrt{8.0479}
$$
$$
R_L \approx 2.837 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Angle in Plane L (first quadrant since both positive):**
$$
\tan \phi_L = \dfrac{2.7000}{0.8706} = 3.1013 \implies \phi_L \approx 72.14°
$$

**Step 4: Resolve Plane R into horizontal and vertical components:**

| Mass | $m_i^R r_i$ | $\theta$ (°) | $\cos\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $H$ | $V$ |
|------|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|------|------|
| A    | 0            | 30            | 0.8660       | 0.5000        | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| B    | 0.5400       | 90            | 0.0000       | 1.0000        | 0.0000 | 0.5400 |
| C    | 1.4000       | 180           | -1.0000      | 0.0000        | -1.4000 | 0.0000 |
| D    | 2.8000       | 270           | 0.0000       | -1.0000       | 0.0000 | -2.8000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | — | **-1.4000** | **-2.2600** |

**Resultant in Plane R:**
$$
R_R = \sqrt{(-1.4000)^2 + (-2.2600)^2} = \sqrt{1.9600 + 5.1076} = \sqrt{7.0676}
$$
$$
R_R \approx 2.659 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Angle in Plane R (third quadrant since both negative):**
$$
\tan \phi_R = \dfrac{-2.2600}{-1.4000} = 1.6143 \implies \tan^{-1}(1.6143) \approx 58.21°
$$
$$
\phi_R = 180° + 58.21° = 238.21°
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Balancing Masses:**

Given balancing radii $r_L = 0.2$ m and $r_R = 0.25$ m:
$$
m_b^L = \dfrac{R_L}{r_L} = \dfrac{2.837}{0.2} = 14.185 \text{ kg} \quad \text{at angle } \phi_L = 72.14° \text{ in Plane L}
$$
$$
m_b^R = \dfrac{R_R}{r_R} = \dfrac{2.659}{0.25} = 10.636 \text{ kg} \quad \text{at angle } \phi_R = 238.21° \text{ in Plane R}
$$

**Note**: The balancing mass must be placed **at the balancing radius and at the computed angular position**. The balance condition means the balancing mass $m_b$ is placed such that its $m_b r_b$ vector is **equal and opposite** to the resultant $R$. The angles computed above already represent the location where the balancing mass must be added (not the location of $R$ itself).

#### Part (b) — Solution: Graphical Method

**Procedure**:
1. **Setup**: Construct a horizontal line representing the **shaft axis**. Mark the positions of all masses along the shaft.
2. **Draw Force Vectors**: From a common point O, draw vectors representing the centrifugal forces of each mass (i.e., $m_i r_i$ vectors) with their proper angular positions. This gives a **force polygon** (or polar diagram).
3. **Equilibrant in Reference Plane L**: Close the force polygon by drawing the equilibrant $R_L$ (which is the negative of the resultant of all the $m_i r_i$ vectors as seen from Plane L).
4. **Construct the Couple Polygon**: Using the principle of moments, draw a **couple polygon** (or funicular polygon) to determine the position of the equivalent masses in Plane R.
5. **Result**: The intersection of the equilibrant in the couple polygon gives the magnitude of $R_R$, and its angle with the reference gives the position.

**Limitations of the Graphical Method**:
- **Cumulative errors** due to ruler and protractor inaccuracies (typically $\pm 2°$ to $\pm 5°$).
- **Tedious for more than 4 masses** — the polygon construction becomes unwieldy.
- **Difficult to verify** the final result.
- The **analytical method** is preferred for high-speed, high-precision applications (e.g., aerospace, turbomachinery) because it uses exact trigonometric computations with no graphical measurement error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting to add 180°** for quadrants II, III, and IV when computing the angular position of the balancing mass — this is the **#1 most common error**, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $L_i$ and $L - L_i$** in the decomposition formula.
> 3. **Not specifying the balancing radius** in the final answer — examiners specifically look for $m_b$ and $r_b$ separately.
> 4. **Rounding off intermediate values too aggressively** (use at least 3–4 decimal places for $\sin$ and $\cos$).
> 5. **Failing to state units** (kg, m, kg·m) — minor but recurring deduction.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** A rotating shaft carries three masses in three different planes. The data is given below. Determine the **magnitude and angular position** of the balancing masses in planes **P (left)** and **Q (right)** at the ends of the shaft, with balancing radii **0.18 m** in P and **0.22 m** in Q. **[7 Marks]**

| Mass | Mass (kg) | Radius (m) | Distance from P (m) | Angular Position (°) |
|------|-----------|------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1    | 8         | 0.25       | 0                    | 0                    |
| 2    | 10        | 0.20       | 0.5                  | 90                   |
| 3    | 12        | 0.15       | 1.0                  | 200                  |

Total length $L = 1.0$ m.

**(b)** State and explain the conditions for **complete dynamic balance** of a rotor. Why is dynamic balancing essential for high-speed machinery? Cite two engineering examples. **[7 Marks]**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION OUTLINE FOR QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Solution Outline

**Step 1**: $m_1 r_1 = 2.000$, $m_2 r_2 = 2.000$, $m_3 r_3 = 1.800$ kg·m.

**Step 2**: Decompose using $L = 1.0$ m:
- **Plane P**: $2.000$ (Mass 1), $2.000 \times 0.5 = 1.000$ (Mass 2), $1.800 \times 0 = 0$ (Mass 3).
- **Plane Q**: $0$, $2.000 \times 0.5 = 1.000$, $1.800 \times 1.0 = 1.800$.

**Step 3**: Sum horizontal and vertical components in each plane.

**Plane P:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_P &= 2.000 \cos 0° + 1.000 \cos 90° + 0 = 2.000 \\
V_P &= 2.000 \sin 0° + 1.000 \sin 90° + 0 = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
R_P = \sqrt{2.000^2 + 1.000^2} = \sqrt{5.000} = 2.236 \text{ kg·m}
$$
$$
\phi_P = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.000}{2.000}\right) = 26.57°
$$
$$
m_b^P = \dfrac{2.236}{0.18} = 12.42 \text{ kg at } 26.57° \text{ in Plane P}
$$

**Plane Q:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_Q &= 0 + 1.000 \cos 90° + 1.800 \cos 200° = 0 + 0 + 1.800 \times (-0.9397) = -1.691 \\
V_Q &= 0 + 1.000 \sin 90° + 1.800 \sin 200° = 0 + 1.000 + 1.800 \times (-0.3420) = 0.384
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
R_Q = \sqrt{(-1.691)^2 + (0.384)^2} = \sqrt{2.860 + 0.147} = \sqrt{3.007} = 1.734 \text{ kg·m}
$$
$$
\tan \phi_Q = \dfrac{0.384}{-1.691} = -0.2271 \implies \phi_Q = 180° - 12.80° = 167.20° \text{ (second quadrant, since H<0, V>0)}
$$
$$
m_b^Q = \dfrac{1.734}{0.22} = 7.88 \text{ kg at } 167.2° \text{ in Plane Q}
$$

#### Part (b) — Solution Outline

**Conditions for Complete Dynamic Balance**:
- **Condition 1**: $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (translational balance — no resultant centrifugal force).
- **Condition 2**: $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$ (rotational balance — no resultant couple).

**Why essential for high-speed machinery**:
- Centrifugal force $\propto \omega^2$, so unbalance forces grow quadratically with speed.
- Unbalance causes bearing wear, vibration, noise, fatigue failure, and loss of precision.
- High-speed rotors in turbines, machine tool spindles, etc., can self-destruct if not dynamically balanced.

**Engineering examples**:
1. **Aircraft jet engine turbine disc** — operates at 10,000+ RPM; imbalance causes blade failure and catastrophic engine damage.
2. **Lathe spindle / grinding wheel** — imbalance causes surface finish defects, tool chatter, and reduced bearing life.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Balancing**

- ✅ **Static balance** = single-plane; condition: $\sum m_i r_i = 0$. Suitable for thin, disc-like rotors.
- ✅ **Dynamic balance** = multi-plane; conditions: $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$.
- ✅ **Dynamic balance ⇒ static balance**, but **NOT vice versa**.
- ✅ **Centrifugal force**: $F_c = m \omega^2 r = \dfrac{m r N^2}{900}$ (with $N$ in RPM, $m$ in kg, $r$ in m, $F_c$ in N).
- ✅ **Reference plane decomposition**: Equivalent mass in Plane A = $m_i \cdot \dfrac{L - L_i}{L}$; in Plane B = $m_i \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{L}$.
- ✅ **Resultant magnitude**: $R = \sqrt{(\sum H)^2 + (\sum V)^2}$.
- ✅ **Angle quadrant correction**:
  - $H > 0, V > 0$ → **1st quadrant** (0° to 90°)
  - $H < 0, V > 0$ → **2nd quadrant** (90° to 180°)
  - $H < 0, V < 0$ → **3rd quadrant** (180° to 270°)
  - $H > 0, V < 0$ → **4th quadrant** (270° to 360°)
- ✅ **Balancing mass** is placed at the **same angle as the resultant** (so its $m_b r_b$ vector cancels the resultant unbalance).
- ✅ **Practical balancing radius** is chosen to allow a reasonable mass placement while avoiding interference with the casing.
- ✅ **Units consistency**: All $m_i r_i$ products must be in **kg·m** for the resultant to be dimensionally consistent.
- ✅ **Verification** of dynamic balance: After adding balancing masses, both $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector) AND $\sum m_i r_i L_i = 0$ (vector) must be checked.
- ✅ **Common mistakes**: Wrong quadrant for angle, swapped $(L - L_i)$ and $L_i$, missing units, premature rounding.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 ESE focus areas**: Multi-plane analytical problems (10–14 mark questions), short notes on static vs. dynamic balance, derivation of $F_c = m \omega^2 r$.

> **End of Topic Notes — Balancing of Rotating Masses**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Reciprocating Masses - Balancing of Multicylinder In-Line Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Balancing of Reciprocating Masses - Balancing of Multicylinder In-Line Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme - Mechanics of Machinery**, the balancing of reciprocating masses in multicylinder in-line engines refers to the systematic process of arranging the reciprocating masses, crank angles, and crank lengths of several cylinders mounted in a single line (with all crank axes parallel and the cylinders aligned along a common axis) such that the net inertia forces and inertia couples acting on the engine frame (and hence the bearings) are reduced to zero, or to a small prescribed minimum, throughout one complete revolution of the crankshaft.

The **primary objective** of this analysis is to determine the magnitudes and directions of the resultant **primary inertia force**, **secondary inertia force**, **primary inertia couple**, and **secondary inertia couple** that act on the engine structure, and to choose the crank arrangement (crank sequence) and the reciprocating mass per cylinder so that these vector sums and couples vanish or remain within acceptable vibration limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a multi-cylinder in-line engine with *n* cylinders, the conditions of *complete* primary and secondary balancing require the satisfaction of **eight simultaneous equations** in two dimensions (four for primary effects and four for secondary effects). Real engines therefore only achieve *partial* balance, with a typical multi-cylinder in-line engine being primary-balanced only, while secondary forces are merely minimised, not eliminated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you and three of your friends sitting on a see-saw (a 4-person version), each holding a heavy bag. If everyone sits at random positions, the see-saw will jerk, wobble and produce a violent shaking motion. However, if all of you redistribute yourselves carefully — choosing who sits where, and placing the bags at calculated distances from the pivot — the see-saw can be made to remain perfectly still even while the bags are being moved up and down. 

**The analogy in engine balancing:**
- The **see-saw** is the **engine frame / crankshaft bearings**.
- The **four friends** are the **four pistons** of a 4-cylinder engine.
- The **heavy bags being moved up and down** are the **reciprocating masses** (`m_R`) inside each cylinder.
- The **distance from the pivot** is analogous to the **crank radius** (`R`).
- The **angular position** at which each person sits corresponds to the **crank angle** (`θ`) of that cylinder.

Just as the see-saw only stays still when the *vector sum* of all upward-downward impulses equals zero, the engine frame only stays vibration-free when the **vector sum of all primary inertia forces equals zero** (primary balance) **and** the **vector sum of all secondary inertia forces equals zero** (secondary balance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Multicylinder Engine Needs Balancing:** A single-cylinder engine has an inherent shaking force that cannot be eliminated — it must be absorbed by a balance mass. In a multicylinder engine, we exploit the fact that the shaking forces from different cylinders occur at *different crank angles* and can be arranged to *cancel each other* by careful choice of the crank configuration.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| `m_R` | Reciprocating mass per cylinder | **kg** |
| `R` | Crank radius | **m** |
| `L` | Connecting rod length | **m** |
| `n` = L/R | Ratio of connecting rod to crank radius | dimensionless (**typically 3.5–4.5**) |
| `r` | Distance of crank centre from reference line (for couple analysis) | **m** |
| `θ` | Crank angle measured from line of stroke (cylinder axis) | **radians / degrees** |
| `ω` | Angular velocity of crankshaft | **rad/s** |
| `F_P` | Primary inertia force per cylinder | **N** |
| `F_S` | Secondary inertia force per cylinder | **N** |
| `C_P` | Primary inertia couple per cylinder | **N·m** |
| `C_S` | Secondary inertia couple per cylinder | **N·m** |

The standard primary and secondary force expressions (per cylinder) that govern all multi-cylinder analysis are:

$$F_{P1} = m_R \, R \, \omega^2 \, \cos\theta$$

$$F_{S1} = m_R \, R \, \omega^2 \, \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}$$

where `n` = L/R is the connecting-rod-to-crank ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> The secondary force is much smaller than the primary force because it carries the factor `1/n` (≈ 1/4). This is why primary balancing is considered the **first priority** in engine design and is more easily achieved than secondary balancing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundations: The Forces Generated by a Single Cylinder

Before we tackle multi-cylinder in-line engines, the **force generated by one reciprocating mass** must be revisited. Considering the simple harmonic approximation of the slider-crank mechanism, the inertia of the reciprocating mass `m_R` produces two distinct force components along the line of stroke:

1. **Primary (1st-order) Inertia Force** — alternates once per revolution of the crank, acting along the cylinder axis.
2. **Secondary (2nd-order) Inertia Force** — alternates twice per revolution, also along the cylinder axis.

Resolving along the line of stroke (taken as the X-axis) and perpendicular to it (the Y-axis):

**Along line of stroke (X):**

$$F_{Px} = m_R \, R \, \omega^2 \, \cos\theta + \frac{m_R \, R \, \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta$$

**Perpendicular to line of stroke (Y):**

$$F_{Py} = m_R \, R \, \omega^2 \, \sin\theta + \frac{m_R \, R \, \omega^2}{n} \sin 2\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first term in each expression is the **primary** component; the second is the **secondary** component. The Y-components of these forces, when multiplied by the perpendicular distance `r` from the cylinder centre line to the crankshaft axis, give the corresponding **inertia couples** (primary couple and secondary couple) about the crankshaft.

### 2.2 Multi-Cylinder Extension — The Vector-Sum Principle

For a multi-cylinder in-line engine with `k` cylinders, the **net force on the engine frame** is the vector sum of the inertia forces produced by each cylinder, and the **net couple** is the vector sum of the inertia couples.

Because all cylinders in an in-line engine have their **crank axes parallel** and their **lines of stroke either parallel or coincident** (for inline engines with the same line of stroke, the X-axes of all cylinders coincide), the primary and secondary forces from each cylinder act along the **same line of stroke** (taken as the common X-axis of the engine). However, the **phase angles** of the crank rotations are different for each cylinder.

Let the crank angle of cylinder *i* with respect to a reference crank be `θᵢ`. Then the primary and secondary force components for cylinder *i* are:

$$F_{Pxi} = m_{Ri} \, R_i \, \omega^2 \, \cos(\theta + \theta_i)$$

$$F_{Sxi} = \frac{m_{Ri} \, R_i \, \omega^2}{n_i} \cos 2(\theta + \theta_i)$$

Similarly, the perpendicular components (which produce couples when multiplied by the cylinder offset `rᵢ`):

$$F_{Pyi} = m_{Ri} \, R_i \, \omega^2 \, \sin(\theta + \theta_i)$$

$$F_{Syi} = \frac{m_{Ri} \, R_i \, \omega^2}{n_i} \sin 2(\theta + \theta_i)$$

### 2.3 The Eight Conditions for Complete Balancing (Vector Form)

To completely eliminate both the inertia forces **and** the inertia couples in *both* primary and *both* secondary orders, the following **eight equations** must be simultaneously satisfied (assuming the engine is a closed system of masses):

**Primary Force Balance (along the line of stroke and perpendicular to it):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(along X)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(along Y)}$$

**Primary Couple Balance:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(about X)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(about Y)}$$

**Secondary Force Balance:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i \cos 2\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i \sin 2\theta_i = 0$$

**Secondary Couple Balance:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i r_i \cos 2\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{Ri} R_i r_i \sin 2\theta_i = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Catch:** Since `m_Rᵢ`, `Rᵢ`, and `rᵢ` are always **positive** magnitudes, the only way to satisfy the above eight equations is to use both **positive and negative crank angles** (i.e., cranks pointing in *opposite directions*). This is the reason multicylinder in-line engines use **crank pairs at 180°** — the negative sine and cosine of 180° are what balance out the positive contributions of the partner crank.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary force (per cylinder) | $F_P = m_R R \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | Along line of stroke |
| 2 | Secondary force (per cylinder) | $F_S = \dfrac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta$ | Along line of stroke |
| 3 | Primary couple (per cylinder) | $C_P = m_R R r \, \omega^2 \sin\theta$ | About crankshaft axis |
| 4 | Secondary couple (per cylinder) | $C_S = \dfrac{m_R R r \, \omega^2}{n} \sin 2\theta$ | About crankshaft axis |
| 5 | Primary force polygon (algebraic) | $\sum m_R R \cos\theta = 0$ | X-axis balance |
| 6 | Primary force polygon (algebraic) | $\sum m_R R \sin\theta = 0$ | Y-axis balance |
| 7 | Primary couple polygon | $\sum m_R R r \sin\theta = 0$ | Y-axis couple |
| 8 | Primary couple polygon | $\sum m_R R r \cos\theta = 0$ | X-axis couple |
| 9 | Secondary force polygon (algebraic) | $\sum m_R R \cos 2\theta = 0$ | X-axis secondary |
| 10 | Secondary force polygon (algebraic) | $\sum m_R R \sin 2\theta = 0$ | Y-axis secondary |
| 11 | Secondary couple polygon | $\sum m_R R r \sin 2\theta = 0$ | Y-axis couple |
| 12 | Secondary couple polygon | $\sum m_R R r \cos 2\theta = 0$ | X-axis couple |
| 13 | Two-cylinder secondary balance | $m_1 R_1 = 2 \, m_2 R_2$ (in some order) | Requires unequal reciprocating masses |
| 14 | Three-cylinder (120°) | Primary & primary couple auto-zero | Secondary couple ≠ 0 |
| 15 | Four-cylinder (180°-cranks) | Primary force, primary couple, secondary force = 0 | Secondary couple ≠ 0 |
| 16 | Six-cylinder (120°-crank groups) | All primary = 0, secondary force = 0 | Secondary couple ≠ 0 (and is small) |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical pipe `|` symbol inside the table above has been **deliberately replaced** with `\vert` or written descriptively (e.g., "X-axis balance") to avoid breaking the KTU Markdown rendering. **KTU note-renderers frequently crash on stray `|` characters in tables — do not use them.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The balancing analysis of multi-cylinder in-line engines is the **design cornerstone** of:
- **Automotive petrol/diesel engines** (3, 4, 5, 6 cylinder configurations).
- **Aerospace piston engines** (Lycoming, Rotax, Continental — typically horizontally opposed, but the inline analysis is directly applicable for the radial equivalent).
- **Industrial compressors and pumps** with multiple reciprocating stages.
- **Locomotive diesel engines** (Wärtsilä, MAN B&W inline multi-cylinder configurations).

A correctly balanced engine experiences:
- **Reduced bearing wear** (longer service life).
- **Lower NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** → quieter cabins.
- **Higher power density** (no energy wasted in shaking the frame).
- **Compliance with emission and driver comfort norms** mandated by the Indian Automotive Research Association (ARAI) and Euro NCAP.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Primary and Secondary Force Expressions for One Cylinder

Consider a single-cylinder slider-crank mechanism as shown conceptually. Let:
- Crank OA = R, rotating at angular velocity ω.
- Connecting rod AB = L.
- Slider B = piston, reciprocating mass = m_R.
- θ = angle of crank OA measured from the line of stroke (cylinder centre line).

**Step 1 — Displacement of the piston from the top dead centre (TDC):**

The position of the piston along the X-axis (line of stroke) is:

$$x_B = R \cos\theta + L \cos\phi$$

where `φ` is the angle the connecting rod makes with the line of stroke.

**Step 2 — Expressing cos φ in terms of θ:**

From the geometry of the triangle OAB:

$$L \sin\phi = R \sin\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sin\phi = \frac{R}{L} \sin\theta = \frac{1}{n} \sin\theta$$

Using the binomial expansion for cosine of a small angle (since `R/L` is small):

$$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\phi} = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sin\theta\right)^2 + \ldots$$

Keeping terms up to the first order in `1/n` (a standard KTU/UG textbook approximation):

$$\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{\sin^2\theta}{2n^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting back into x_B:**

$$x_B = R\cos\theta + L\left(1 - \frac{\sin^2\theta}{2n^2}\right)$$

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$x_B = R\cos\theta + L - \frac{L}{2n^2}\left(\frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}\right) = R\cos\theta + L - \frac{L}{4n^2} + \frac{L}{4n^2}\cos 2\theta$$

**Step 4 — Differentiating twice to obtain acceleration:**

$$\dot{x}_B = -R\omega \sin\theta - \frac{L\omega}{2n^2}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\ddot{x}_B = -R\omega^2 \cos\theta - \frac{L\omega^2}{n^2}\cos 2\theta$$

**Step 5 — Applying Newton's second law to obtain the inertia force:**

The force on the piston (inertia force) is `F = m_R · |a|`. Since the second term has `L/n² = R/n`, we get:

$$F = m_R R \omega^2 \cos\theta + \frac{m_R R \omega^2}{n}\cos 2\theta$$

> **Conversion Logic Recap (text row):** We started from geometry, expressed the cos φ using binomial expansion, used the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$ to convert the squared term to a double-frequency term, differentiated twice with respect to time (each differentiation pulls down a factor of ω), and applied Newton's second law. This is the canonical KTU derivation — it must be reproduced **verbatim in the order above** in the answer script.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Primary and Secondary Effect Comparison

| Aspect | Primary Effect (1st order) | Secondary Effect (2nd order) |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | Same as crank speed (ω) | Twice crank speed (2ω) |
| **Mathematical form** | $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$ | $\cos 2\theta$, $\sin 2\theta$ |
| **Magnitude factor** | $m_R R \omega^2$ | $\dfrac{m_R R \omega^2}{n}$ |
| **Relative size** | **Larger** (≈ 1.0 unit) | **Smaller** (≈ 0.25 unit for n = 4) |
| **Couple magnitude factor** | $m_R R r \, \omega^2$ | $\dfrac{m_R R r \, \omega^2}{n}$ |
| **Vector polygon use** | 1st-order polygon (uses crank angles θᵢ) | 2nd-order polygon (uses double angles 2θᵢ) |
| **Ease of balancing** | Relatively easy (uses 180° crank pairs) | Very difficult (needs 3-cylinder or more, with mass differences) |

### 3.3 Analytical Result — Standard Multi-Cylinder Crank Configurations

The KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly requires** the analysis of the following in-line engine configurations. The standard results are tabulated below for **uniform crank radii and uniform reciprocating masses per cylinder**, which is the most common assumption in KTU exam problems.

| Engine Type | Crank Configuration | Primary Force | Primary Couple | Secondary Force | Secondary Couple |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **2-cylinder inline** (cranks at 180°) | 1 at 0°, 2 at 180° | ≠ 0 (only zero if mR same) | 0 (if r same) | ≠ 0 | 0 |
| **3-cylinder inline** (cranks at 120°) | 1 at 0°, 2 at 120°, 3 at 240° | **0** | **0** | 0 (if r same) | ≠ 0 (residual exists) |
| **4-cylinder inline** (cranks at 180° pairs) | 1,4 at 0°; 2,3 at 180° | **0** | **0** | **0** | ≠ 0 (small) |
| **6-cylinder inline** (120°-group) | 1,6 at 0°; 2,5 at 120°; 3,4 at 240° | **0** | **0** | **0** | ≠ 0 (very small) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 - Most Probable Numerical:** The 4-cylinder inline engine with cranks at 0°-180°-180°-0° (i.e., crank 1 and 4 at 0°, crank 2 and 3 at 180°) is **the most frequently asked numerical**. Students must memorise this configuration and be able to verify the zero conditions in *under 5 minutes* during the exam.

### 3.4 Detailed Verification for a 4-Cylinder Inline Engine (Crank Angles 0°–180°–180°–0°)

Let `m_R` and `R` be the same for all four cylinders, with `rᵢ` = `r` for all. Crank angles: θ₁ = 0°, θ₂ = 180°, θ₃ = 180°, θ₄ = 0°.

**Primary force check (X-axis):**

$$\sum m_R R \cos\theta = m_R R (1 - 1 - 1 + 1) = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Primary force check (Y-axis):**

$$\sum m_R R \sin\theta = m_R R (0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Primary couple check (X-axis):**

$$\sum m_R R r \cos\theta = m_R R r (1 - 1 - 1 + 1) = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Primary couple check (Y-axis):**

$$\sum m_R R r \sin\theta = m_R R r (0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Secondary force check (X-axis, using 2θᵢ):**

$$\sum m_R R \cos 2\theta = m_R R (1 + 1 + 1 + 1) = 4 m_R R \;\;\text{(not zero, equal masses)}$$

Wait — for a 4-cylinder engine with cranks at 0°–180°–180°–0°, the secondary force does **not** vanish when all `m_R R` are equal. The correct configuration for secondary force balance in a 4-cylinder requires a **180° crank throw with appropriate mass distribution** OR a different crank sequence. Let us re-examine the standard textbook (Khurmi) result:

In the **standard 4-cylinder inline engine (cranks at 0°–180°–180°–0°, also written as 0°–180°–360°–180°)**:
- All **primary** effects = 0.
- **Secondary force** = 0.
- **Secondary couple** ≠ 0 (and is the only residual vibration source).

The student must remember that the **double-angle** trick is what cancels secondary forces here. Rechecking:

$$\sum \cos 2\theta_i = \cos 0 + \cos 360 + \cos 360 + \cos 0 = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \;\;\text{(NOT zero)}$$

This indicates the engine has equal secondary forces all in the same direction — so secondary force is **NOT balanced** in a uniform 4-cylinder inline engine. The standard textbook (Khurmi, Theory of Machines, Chapter on Balancing of Reciprocating Masses) **explicitly states** that in a 4-cylinder inline engine with cranks at 0°–180°–180°–0°:
- Primary force and primary couple are zero.
- Secondary force is zero.
- Secondary couple is non-zero (it is the only residual).

The correct verification using the **complete secondary force expression** $F_S = m_R R \omega^2 \cos 2\theta / n$ is by treating each cylinder as producing a force vector at angle `2θᵢ` and forming a closed polygon. The 2θ angles for cranks at 0°, 180°, 180°, 0° are 0°, 360°, 360°, 0° — all four vectors point in the same direction. So the **net secondary force** is `4 m_R R / n`, which is **not zero**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — High Loss-of-Marks Area:** Students frequently write "secondary force = 0" for the 4-cylinder inline engine without doing the polygon calculation. **In a 4-cylinder inline engine with equal reciprocating masses, the secondary force is NOT zero; it is a pure translation along the line of stroke.** The student must be explicit about which effects vanish and which do not. The standard 4-cylinder inline has **zero primary, zero primary couple, NON-ZERO secondary force, and NON-ZERO secondary couple**. This is a frequently asked KTU viva question.

### 3.5 Resultant Primary Couple (When Not Balanced)

In cases where complete balance is not achieved, the **resultant primary couple** is found by vector addition of the four primary couple vectors. The magnitude of the resultant primary couple for cylinder *i* is `C_Pᵢ = m_{Rᵢ} Rᵢ rᵢ ω² sin θᵢ`, and the direction is perpendicular to the line of stroke. The resultant is:

$$C_{P,\text{net}} = \omega^2 \sqrt{ \left(\sum m_{R_i} R_i r_i \cos\theta_i \right)^2 + \left(\sum m_{R_i} R_i r_i \sin\theta_i \right)^2 }$$

The angle of the resultant couple vector (measured from the X-axis) is:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\sum m_{R_i} R_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_{R_i} R_i r_i \cos\theta_i}$$

> **Conversion Logic Recap:** This is the standard 2D vector magnitude and angle formula, applied to the primary couple vector. The same form is used for **secondary couples**, except the angles are doubled: use `2θᵢ` and `m_{Rᵢ}Rᵢrᵢ` replaced by `m_{Rᵢ}Rᵢrᵢ/nᵢ`.

### 3.6 Worked Example Setup (KTU 2024 Style)

A 3-cylinder inline engine has its cranks at 60°, 180°, and 300° respectively. Each cylinder has `m_R = 1.0 kg` and `R = 0.05 m`. The connecting-rod length is `L = 0.20 m`. The cylinders are spaced `r₁ = 0 m`, `r₂ = 0.15 m`, `r₃ = 0.30 m` from a reference plane. The crank runs at `3000 rpm`. Find the resultant primary couple and secondary couple.

This type of problem tests whether the student can correctly apply the vector-sum formulas from Section 3.5 to a specific configuration. The full solution template is:

1. Compute ω = 2πN/60 = 2π(3000)/60 = 100π rad/s.
2. List crank angles θ₁ = 60°, θ₂ = 180°, θ₃ = 300°.
3. Compute 2θ₁ = 120°, 2θ₂ = 360°, 2θ₃ = 600° (≡ 240°).
4. Substitute into the primary and secondary couple formulas.
5. Combine X and Y components using Pythagoras.
6. State final magnitude with units (N·m).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Crank Configuration of a 4-Cylinder Inline Engine

The following **block-level functional diagram** shows the crank arrangement of a 4-cylinder inline engine viewed along the crankshaft axis. The arrows indicate the crank pin position (and hence the direction of the primary force) for cylinder 1 at the instant shown.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Cylinder 1<br/>Crank at 0 deg<br/>Piston moving down"] --> B["Cylinder 2<br/>Crank at 180 deg<br/>Piston moving up"]
    B --> C["Cylinder 3<br/>Crank at 180 deg<br/>Piston moving up"]
    C --> D["Cylinder 4<br/>Crank at 0 deg<br/>Piston moving down"]
    D --> A

    A -.Primary Force.-> F1["F_P1 along +X"]
    B -.Primary Force.-> F2["F_P2 along -X"]
    C -.Primary Force.-> F3["F_P3 along -X"]
    D -.Primary Force.-> F4["F_P4 along +X"]

    F1 --> SUM["Vector Sum<br/>Net Primary Force = 0"]
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    F4 --> SUM

    SUM --> OUT["Engine Frame<br/>Vibration-Free<br/>along X axis"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Cylinders 1 and 4 generate equal primary forces along +X; cylinders 2 and 3 generate equal primary forces along -X. Because the magnitudes are equal, the net primary force along X is **zero**. Similarly, all four Y-components are zero because the crank angles are 0° and 180° (sin 0° = sin 180° = 0).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Decomposition and Balancing Pipeline

The following block diagram describes the **functional architecture** of the multi-cylinder balancing analysis pipeline, from the input parameters to the final output vibration level.

```mermaid
graph LR
    INP["Input Parameters<br/>m_R per cylinder<br/>R per cylinder<br/>L per cylinder<br/>r offsets<br/>crank angles theta_i<br/>engine speed omega"] --> S1["Step 1: Compute<br/>Primary Force F_P_i<br/>= m_R R omega^2 cos theta_i"]
    INP --> S2["Step 1: Compute<br/>Secondary Force F_S_i<br/>= m_R R omega^2 cos 2 theta_i / n"]

    S1 --> S3["Step 2: Form<br/>Primary Force Polygon<br/>X = sum m_R R cos theta_i<br/>Y = sum m_R R sin theta_i"]
    S2 --> S4["Step 2: Form<br/>Secondary Force Polygon<br/>X2 = sum m_R R cos 2 theta_i<br/>Y2 = sum m_R R sin 2 theta_i"]

    S3 --> S5["Step 3: Check<br/>Primary Balance<br/>X = 0 AND Y = 0?"]
    S4 --> S6["Step 3: Check<br/>Secondary Balance<br/>X2 = 0 AND Y2 = 0?"]

    S5 --> S7["Step 4: Compute<br/>Residual Primary Couple<br/>C_P_net using<br/>C_P_i = m_R R r omega^2 sin theta_i"]
    S6 --> S8["Step 4: Compute<br/>Residual Secondary Couple<br/>C_S_net using<br/>C_S_i = m_R R r omega^2 sin 2 theta_i / n"]

    S7 --> OUT["Output: Engine Frame<br/>Loads and Vibration<br/>Characteristics"]
    S8 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Vector Polygon for 3-Cylinder Engine (120° Crank Spacing)

The 3-cylinder inline engine with cranks at 0°, 120°, 240° produces a closed equilateral triangle in both the primary and secondary force polygons, demonstrating perfect balance of forces (but not necessarily of couples).

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["F_P1<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2<br/>Angle 0 deg"] --> P2["F_P2<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2<br/>Angle 120 deg"]
    P2 --> P3["F_P3<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2<br/>Angle 240 deg"]
    P3 --> CLOSE["Polygon Closes<br/>Net F_P = 0<br/>3-Cylinder Primary Balance"]
    CLOSE --> P1

    Q1["F_S1<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2 / n<br/>Angle 0 deg"] --> Q2["F_S2<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2 / n<br/>Angle 240 deg"]
    Q2 --> Q3["F_S3<br/>Magnitude m_R R omega^2 / n<br/>Angle 480 deg = 120 deg"]
    Q3 --> QCLOSE["Polygon Closes<br/>Net F_S = 0<br/>3-Cylinder Secondary Balance<br/>(force only, not couple)"]
    QCLOSE --> Q1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The primary vectors (P1, P2, P3) form a closed equilateral triangle of side `m_R R ω²`, confirming that the vector sum is zero. The secondary vectors (Q1, Q2, Q3) form a closed triangle as well, confirming secondary force balance. However, the **couples** in this configuration are not balanced — they produce a residual couple that rotates with the engine.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Engine Frame Loading

```mermaid
graph TD
    CRANK["Crankshaft<br/>Rotating at omega"] --> P1["Piston 1<br/>F_P1 = m_R R omega^2 cos theta"]
    CRANK --> P2["Piston 2<br/>F_P2 = m_R R omega^2 cos theta 2"]
    CRANK --> P3["Piston 3<br/>F_P3 = m_R R omega^2 cos theta 3"]
    CRANK --> P4["Piston 4<br/>F_P4 = m_R R omega^2 cos theta 4"]

    P1 --> F1["F1 along Cyl 1 axis"]
    P2 --> F2["F2 along Cyl 2 axis<br/>Offset r2 from crank centre"]
    P3 --> F3["F3 along Cyl 3 axis<br/>Offset r3 from crank centre"]
    P4 --> F4["F4 along Cyl 4 axis<br/>Offset r4 from crank centre"]

    F1 --> BEAR["Crankshaft Bearings<br/>Net Force = F1+F2+F3+F4<br/>Net Couple = r1 x F1 + r2 x F2 + r3 x F3 + r4 x F4"]
    F2 --> BEAR
    F3 --> BEAR
    F4 --> BEAR

    BEAR --> FRAME["Engine Frame / Crankcase<br/>Vibration Level"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each piston pushes on its cylinder (reaction force = inertia force, equal and opposite). The crankshaft bearings must transmit the vector sum of all piston forces to the engine frame, and the crankshaft itself experiences the sum of all the moments `rᵢ × Fᵢ`. The **engine frame is the ultimate recipient** of any unbalanced forces and couples, and its vibration is the final performance metric.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define primary and secondary balancing forces in a multi-cylinder engine. Why is primary balancing given more importance than secondary balancing in engine design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **primary balancing force** in a multi-cylinder engine is the **first-order harmonic component** of the reciprocating inertia force, given by $F_P = m_R R \omega^2 \cos\theta$, which alternates once per revolution of the crankshaft. The **secondary balancing force** is the **second-order harmonic component**, given by $F_S = \dfrac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \cos 2\theta$, which alternates twice per revolution.

**Primary balancing is given more importance** for the following reasons:

1. The primary force has a **larger magnitude** (factor `m_R R ω²` versus `m_R R ω² / n`, with `n` ≈ 3.5–4.5).
2. The primary force occurs at a **lower frequency** (ω instead of 2ω), and human perception of vibration is greater at lower frequencies, making it more noticeable to the driver.
3. The primary force is **easier to balance** using simple 180° crank pairs, whereas secondary force balance requires more elaborate crank arrangements or unequal reciprocating masses.
4. The primary force has a **larger destructive effect on bearings** because it acts for a longer duration per cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Definition of primary = 1 mark; definition of secondary = 1 mark; justification of priority = 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the eight conditions for the complete balancing of a multi-cylinder in-line engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **eight conditions for complete balancing** of a multi-cylinder in-line engine, written in their vector-algebraic form, are:

**Primary force and primary couple balance (4 conditions):**

1. $\sum m_R R \cos\theta = 0$
2. $\sum m_R R \sin\theta = 0$
3. $\sum m_R R r \cos\theta = 0$
4. $\sum m_R R r \sin\theta = 0$

**Secondary force and secondary couple balance (4 conditions):**

5. $\sum m_R R \cos 2\theta = 0$
6. $\sum m_R R \sin 2\theta = 0$
7. $\sum m_R R r \cos 2\theta = 0$
8. $\sum m_R R r \sin 2\theta = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Stating all 8 conditions correctly = 3 marks. The student must clearly separate them into "primary" and "secondary" groups to obtain full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **two alternatives (Question B.A and Question B.B)** represent the **Internal Choice** typical of KTU 2024 ESE Part B. The student is expected to answer **only ONE** of the two. Both are kept to the same mark distribution (7 + 7) as per the official KTU template.

---

#### **Question B.A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted] — 14 Marks

A 3-cylinder, 90° V-engine has the following data:

| Cylinder | Reciprocating mass m_R (kg) | Crank radius R (m) | Connecting rod L (m) | Crank angle θ | Offset r (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.2 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 0° | 0.00 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 90° | 0.10 |
| 3 | 1.0 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 270° | 0.20 |

The engine runs at 2400 rpm. Determine:
**(a)** The magnitude and direction of the resultant primary force. (7 marks)
**(b)** The magnitude and direction of the resultant primary couple. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (B.A):**

**Part (a) — Resultant Primary Force**

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 80\pi \text{ rad/s} = 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

The primary force for each cylinder is $F_{Pi} = m_{Ri} R_i \omega^2 \cos\theta_i$ (along X) and $F_{PiY} = m_{Ri} R_i \omega^2 \sin\theta_i$ (along Y). To find the **resultant primary force vector**, we sum the **m_R R** products with cos and sin weights.

Compute the X-component:

$$F_{PX} = \sum m_{Ri} R_i \omega^2 \cos\theta_i = \omega^2 \left[ (1.2)(0.05)\cos 0° + (1.0)(0.05)\cos 90° + (1.0)(0.05)\cos 270° \right]$$

$$F_{PX} = (251.33)^2 \left[ 0.06 + 0 + 0 \right] = 63166.16 \times 0.06 = 3790.0 \text{ N}$$

Compute the Y-component:

$$F_{PY} = \sum m_{Ri} R_i \omega^2 \sin\theta_i = \omega^2 \left[ (1.2)(0.05)\sin 0° + (1.0)(0.05)\sin 90° + (1.0)(0.05)\sin 270° \right]$$

$$F_{PY} = 63166.16 \left[ 0 + 0.05 - 0.05 \right] = 63166.16 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

Resultant primary force magnitude:

$$F_P = \sqrt{F_{PX}^2 + F_{PY}^2} = \sqrt{3790^2 + 0^2} = 3790 \text{ N}$$

Direction: along the +X axis (line of stroke of cylinder 1). Angle = tan⁻¹(0/3790) = **0°**.

**Part (b) — Resultant Primary Couple**

Primary couple for each cylinder: $C_{Pi} = m_{Ri} R_i r_i \omega^2 \sin\theta_i$ (about Y-axis) and $m_{Ri} R_i r_i \omega^2 \cos\theta_i$ (about X-axis).

Compute the couple about X-axis:

$$C_{PX} = \sum m_{Ri} R_i r_i \omega^2 \cos\theta_i = 63166.16 \left[ (1.2)(0.05)(0)\cos 0° + (1.0)(0.05)(0.10)\cos 90° + (1.0)(0.05)(0.20)\cos 270° \right]$$

$$C_{PX} = 63166.16 \left[ 0 + 0 + 0 \right] = 0 \text{ N·m}$$

Compute the couple about Y-axis:

$$C_{PY} = \sum m_{Ri} R_i r_i \omega^2 \sin\theta_i = 63166.16 \left[ (1.2)(0.05)(0)\sin 0° + (1.0)(0.05)(0.10)\sin 90° + (1.0)(0.05)(0.20)\sin 270° \right]$$

$$C_{PY} = 63166.16 \left[ 0 + 0.005 + (-0.010) \right] = 63166.16 \times (-0.005) = -315.83 \text{ N·m}$$

Resultant primary couple magnitude:

$$C_P = \sqrt{C_{PX}^2 + C_{PY}^2} = \sqrt{0 + 315.83^2} = 315.83 \text{ N·m}$$

Direction: along the -Y axis (the negative sign indicates it is in the -Y direction).

**Final Answer:** Resultant primary force = **3790 N** along the +X axis. Resultant primary couple = **315.83 N·m** about the -Y axis.

---

#### **Question B.B** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted] — 14 Marks

A 4-cylinder inline engine has the following data. The cranks are arranged at 0°, 180°, 180°, 0° respectively. Each cylinder has `m_R = 1.5 kg`, `R = 0.04 m`, `L = 0.16 m`, and the cylinder offset distances are `r₁ = 0`, `r₂ = 0.10 m`, `r₃ = 0.20 m`, `r₄ = 0.30 m`. The engine speed is `N = 3000 rpm`. Determine:
**(a)** The resultant primary force and primary couple. (7 marks)
**(b)** The resultant secondary force and secondary couple. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (B.B):**

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \text{ rad/s} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (a) — Primary Effects**

X-component of primary force:

$$F_{PX} = m_R R \omega^2 \sum \cos\theta_i = (1.5)(0.04)(314.16)^2 \left[ \cos 0° + \cos 180° + \cos 180° + \cos 0° \right]$$

$$F_{PX} = 5921.76 \times \left[ 1 - 1 - 1 + 1 \right] = 5921.76 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

Y-component of primary force:

$$F_{PY} = m_R R \omega^2 \sum \sin\theta_i = 5921.76 \times \left[ \sin 0° + \sin 180° + \sin 180° + \sin 0° \right]$$

$$F_{PY} = 5921.76 \times \left[ 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \right] = 0 \text{ N}$$

Primary force: **0 N** (engine is primary-force balanced).

Primary couple about X-axis:

$$C_{PX} = m_R R \omega^2 \sum r_i \cos\theta_i = 5921.76 \left[ (0)(1) + (0.10)(-1) + (0.20)(-1) + (0.30)(1) \right]$$

$$C_{PX} = 5921.76 \left[ 0 - 0.10 - 0.20 + 0.30 \right] = 5921.76 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N·m}$$

Primary couple about Y-axis:

$$C_{PY} = m_R R \omega^2 \sum r_i \sin\theta_i = 5921.76 \left[ 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \right] = 0 \text{ N·m}$$

Primary couple: **0 N·m** (engine is primary-couple balanced).

**Part (b) — Secondary Effects**

Connecting-rod ratio: `n = L/R = 0.16 / 0.04 = 4`.

The double-angles 2θᵢ for cranks at 0°, 180°, 180°, 0° are: **0°, 360°, 360°, 0°** (which is equivalent to 0°, 0°, 0°, 0°).

X-component of secondary force:

$$F_{SX} = \frac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \sum \cos 2\theta_i = \frac{5921.76}{4} \left[ \cos 0° + \cos 360° + \cos 360° + \cos 0° \right]$$

$$F_{SX} = 1480.44 \times \left[ 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \right] = 1480.44 \times 4 = 5921.76 \text{ N}$$

Y-component of secondary force:

$$F_{SY} = \frac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \sum \sin 2\theta_i = 1480.44 \times \left[ 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \right] = 0 \text{ N}$$

Resultant secondary force: **5921.76 N** along the +X axis (all in the same direction).

Secondary couple about X-axis:

$$C_{SX} = \frac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \sum r_i \cos 2\theta_i = 1480.44 \left[ (0)(1) + (0.10)(1) + (0.20)(1) + (0.30)(1) \right]$$

$$C_{SX} = 1480.44 \times 0.60 = 888.27 \text{ N·m}$$

Secondary couple about Y-axis:

$$C_{SY} = \frac{m_R R \omega^2}{n} \sum r_i \sin 2\theta_i = 1480.44 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N·m}$$

Resultant secondary couple magnitude:

$$C_S = \sqrt{C_{SX}^2 + C_{SY}^2} = 888.27 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answer:** Primary force = **0 N**; Primary couple = **0 N·m**; Secondary force = **5921.76 N** (along +X); Secondary couple = **888.27 N·m** (about X-axis).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1/n** in secondary force calculations is the most common error — students frequently write `m_R R ω² cos 2θ` instead of `m_R R ω² cos 2θ / n`, leading to answers that are 4× too large.
> 2. **Confusing 2θ angles:** The double-angle is computed from the crank angle, not the cylinder index. For a crank at 180°, 2θ = 360° = 0° (i.e., the same as crank at 0°). Students often mistakenly compute 2θ for the *couple* using `r_i` and forget to convert.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "primary couple = 0" as "engine is balanced":** The engine is *primary* balanced, but the **secondary force and secondary couple are NOT zero** in a uniform 4-cylinder inline engine. The answer must explicitly state which effects are zero and which are not.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Negative Y-component of secondary couple means it is in the -Y direction. The student should explicitly state the direction (axis) of the resultant couple, not just its magnitude.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Re-read 5 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- ✅ **Two types of inertia forces** per cylinder: **Primary** ($m_R R \omega^2 \cos\theta$) and **Secondary** ($m_R R \omega^2 \cos 2\theta / n$).
- ✅ **Two types of inertia couples** per cylinder: **Primary** ($m_R R r \omega^2 \sin\theta$) and **Secondary** ($m_R R r \omega^2 \sin 2\theta / n$).
- ✅ The **secondary effect is smaller** by a factor of `1/n` (≈ 0.25) and **faster** by a factor of 2.
- ✅ The **eight conditions** for complete balancing of an in-line engine are split into **4 primary** and **4 secondary** equations.
- ✅ **2-cylinder inline (180° cranks):** primary force ≠ 0, primary couple = 0, secondary force ≠ 0, secondary couple = 0 — *if* `m_R R` and `r` are the same. To balance secondary force in a 2-cylinder, you need **m₁R₁ = 2 m₂R₂** (unequal reciprocating masses).
- ✅ **3-cylinder inline (120° cranks):** primary force = 0, primary couple = 0, secondary force = 0, secondary couple ≠ 0 (residual).
- ✅ **4-cylinder inline (0°–180°–180°–0° cranks):** primary force = 0, primary couple = 0, **secondary force ≠ 0** (all cylinders contribute in the same direction), **secondary couple ≠ 0**.
- ✅ **6-cylinder inline (120°-grouped cranks):** all primary and secondary forces = 0, secondary couple ≠ 0 (and is the smallest among the common configurations).
- ✅ **Vector polygon method:** draw all $m_R R$ vectors at their respective angles, head-to-tail; closed polygon means balanced.
- ✅ **Algebraic method:** for cosine terms (X-axis), sum $\sum m_R R \cos\theta_i = 0$; for sine terms (Y-axis), sum $\sum m_R R \sin\theta_i = 0$.
- ✅ For secondary effects, **replace θ by 2θ** in all formulas, and divide the magnitude by `n`.
- ✅ For couples, **multiply by r_i** and use the perpendicular direction (Y-axis uses sin, X-axis uses cos).
- ✅ Standard KTU 2024 numericals: 3-cylinder (120° spacing) and 4-cylinder (180°-paired) are the most common.
- ✅ The **n = L/R ratio** is typically 3.5–4.5 in real engines; in KTU problems it is usually 4.
- ✅ The **engine speed** enters through $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s; do not forget to convert rpm to rad/s.
- ✅ **Resultant magnitude** of any vector = $\sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}$; **direction** = $\tan^{-1}(Y/X)$.
- ✅ Always **state the direction** of the resultant force and couple explicitly, in terms of the X and Y axes.
- ✅ **"Balance"** does not mean "no vibration" — it means "minimum vibration that is unavoidable for the chosen crank configuration." A 4-cylinder engine is the most common choice because the primary vibration is zero, even though secondary effects persist.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gyroscope - Effects on the Stability of Sea Vessels and Aircraft (Problem Analysis), Concepts of Gyroscopic Effects on Two Wheelers and Four Wheelers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT504 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gyroscope - Effects on the Stability of Sea Vessels and Aircraft (Problem Analysis), Concepts of Gyroscopic Effects on Two Wheelers and Four Wheelers

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Gyroscopic Equation Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (Recap from Module 4 – Part 1):** Whenever a spinning rotor (mass moment of inertia $I$, spin velocity $\omega_s$) is subjected to an **active gyroscopic couple** $C$, its spin axis precesses (rotates) about an axis perpendicular to the couple axis at an **angular velocity of precession** $\omega_p$, where:
> $$C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p \quad \text{[N·m]}$$
> This is the **active gyroscopic effect** – applied couple causes precession.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactive Gyroscopic Couple (KTU High-Yield):** By Newton's third law, the rotor itself exerts a **reactive couple** of equal magnitude but opposite sense on the frame/vehicle that tries to disturb it. This is the **reactive gyroscopic couple**, and it is this reactive effect that governs the **stability of moving vehicles**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spinning Top & The Turning Bus"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Spinning Top):** When a top spins fast, it stays upright because tilting the support (applying a couple) doesn't tip it over; instead, it precesses – its tip traces a circle. The faster the spin or precession, the harder it is to knock the top down. That resistance is what we call **gyroscopic stability**.
>
> **The Bus Analogy:** Imagine holding a spinning bicycle wheel in a bus. Try to tilt it left – your hands feel a strong push to the right. That push is the **reactive gyroscopic couple**. Now replace your hands with the chassis of a sea vessel or aircraft – and the same physics decides whether the vehicle stays stable or capsizes.

## 1.3 Definition – Stability of Sea Vessels & Aircraft

**Gyroscopic Effect on a Sea Vessel:**
A ship/sea vessel is a gyroscopic body because its massive **rotating turbine / propeller / rotor** possesses a large angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\omega_s$ along its spin axis (which is usually the longitudinal axis of the ship). Whenever the ship undergoes **steering, pitching, or rolling**, the reactive gyroscopic couple tries to either stabilize or destabilize the ship's motion.

**Gyroscopic Effect on an Aircraft:**
Aircraft have *three* independent gyroscopes of practical interest – the **propeller** (with the engine), the **jet-engine rotor**, and the **gyroscopic instruments**. The reactive couples produced during pitch-up, turn, and roll directly influence the **longitudinal (pitching) stability**, **directional (yawing) stability**, and **lateral (rolling) stability** of the aircraft.

## 1.4 Effect of Gyroscope on Stability of a Two-Wheeler

A two-wheeler (motorcycle/bicycle) in motion has a **gyroscopic effect due to its rotating wheels**. The two key dynamic situations are:

1. **Gyroscopic effect while taking a turn (leaning)**
2. **Gyroscopic effect during application of brakes (front or rear)**

The magnitude of the reactive gyroscopic couple is $C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$, where $\omega_p$ is the rate of change of steering angle.

## 1.5 Effect of Gyroscope on Stability of a Four-Wheeler

In a four-wheeler, the **rear wheels** (driving wheels) act as gyroscopes. When the vehicle negotiates a curve, the **steering mechanism** rotates the front axle to a turning angle – producing a finite precession rate $\omega_p$. The reactive gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega_s \omega_p$ from the rear wheels tends to **overturn the vehicle outward** (centrifugal overturning couple) unless countered by road design (banking) and the weight distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Fundamental Motions of a Sea Vessel (and their Gyroscopic Effects)

A sea vessel can execute **three independent rotations** about three mutually perpendicular axes, each intersecting at the **center of gravity (G)**:

| Motion | Description | Axis of Rotation | Plane of Couple Vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steering (Turning / Yawing)** | Ship turns to port or starboard | Vertical (yaw) axis | Horizontal plane |
| **Pitching** | Ship rises or plunges into waves | Transverse (pitch) axis | Vertical plane |
| **Rolling** | Ship tilts sideways | Longitudinal (roll) axis | Transverse plane |

### 2.1.1 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on a Sea Vessel during STEERING (Most Important for KTU)

**Setup:**
- Propeller spin axis = **Longitudinal axis (X-axis)** of the ship
- Spin angular velocity = $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
- Mass moment of inertia of rotor + about its spin axis = $I$ kg·m²
- The ship is turned to **port (left)** or **starboard (right)** at angular velocity $\omega_p$ rad/s about the **vertical axis (Y-axis)**

**Direction of Reactive Gyroscopic Couple (Active vs Reactive):**
- **Active couple** $C$ is applied by the rudder/water on the ship → causes precession of the spin axis (the propeller axis tilts)
- **Reactive couple** $C_r$ is exerted by the rotor on the ship → **opposes the active couple**
- Magnitude: $C_r = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$ N·m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Rule:** If the ship turns to **port (left)**, the reactive gyroscopic couple causes the ship to **pitch downward at the bow (nose dives)**. If the ship turns to **starboard (right)**, the bow **pitches upward (nose lifts)**.
>
> **Mnemonic:** "**Port = Plunge**" – turning to port makes the bow plunge.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple during PITCHING

- During pitching, the ship's longitudinal axis rotates up/down about a **transverse axis** with angular velocity $\omega_p$ rad/s.
- The spin vector $\omega_s$ of the propeller is along the longitudinal axis, and the pitching angular velocity $\omega_p$ is along the transverse axis.
- The active gyroscopic couple now acts in the **horizontal plane** and causes the ship to **turn (yaw)** to port or starboard.
- **Reactive effect:** If the bow pitches **down (plunges)**, the ship tends to turn to **port (left)**. If the bow pitches **up (rises)**, the ship tends to turn to **starboard (right)**.
- **Magnitude:** $C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$ (same form)

### 2.1.3 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple during ROLLING

- Rolling is rotation about the **longitudinal axis** itself.
- Since both $\omega_s$ and $\omega_p$ are about the **same axis**, the reactive couple is **zero** (cross product of parallel vectors is zero).
- **Conclusion:** Rolling has **no gyroscopic effect** on a sea vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is rolling gyroscopically inactive?** Because the spin axis of the rotor and the axis of roll are *coincident* (both longitudinal), their vector cross product yields zero angular momentum change. This is a very common KTU multiple-choice question.

## 2.2 Three Rotational Motions of an Aircraft and their Gyroscopic Effects

| Motion of Aircraft | Axis | Couple Vector Direction | Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitching** (nose up/down) | Transverse axis (Y) | Longitudinal (X) | Causes **yawing** deviation |
| **Yawing / Turning** (nose left/right) | Vertical axis (Z) | Transverse (Y) | Causes **rolling** tendency |
| **Rolling** (banking) | Longitudinal axis (X) | Vertical (Z) | Causes **yawing** deviation |

### 2.2.1 Gyroscopic Effect on an Aircraft during PITCHING (Nose-Up / Nose-Down)

- Aircraft rotates about a **transverse axis (Y)** with angular velocity $\omega_p$
- Propeller/jet axis is along the **longitudinal axis (X)**, spin velocity $\omega_s$
- **Active gyroscopic couple** $C$ acts in the **horizontal plane** (about the vertical axis Z)
- **Reactive couple** on the aircraft makes it **yaw** (turn about the vertical axis)
- **Magnitude:** $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** When the aircraft **pitches up (nose elevated)** with precession $\omega_p$ about the transverse axis, the reactive gyroscopic couple causes the aircraft to **turn to the right (starboard)**. Conversely, **nose-down** pitch causes a **turn to the left (port)**.

### 2.2.2 Gyroscopic Effect on an Aircraft during YAWING (TURNING)

- Aircraft yaws about the **vertical axis (Z)** with $\omega_p$
- **Reactive couple** acts in the **vertical plane** about the **transverse axis (Y)**
- This causes the aircraft to **roll** (bank)
- **Magnitude:** $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Aircraft:** "Pitch causes Yaw, Yaw causes Roll, Roll causes Yaw." (These are the **cross-coupling** effects.)

### 2.2.3 Gyroscopic Effect on an Aircraft during ROLLING

- Rolling is about the longitudinal axis, parallel to the spin axis → **No gyroscopic effect** (analogous to sea vessels).

## 2.3 Gyroscopic Effect on a Two-Wheeler

### 2.3.1 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple when the Two-Wheeler Takes a Turn

**Setup:**
- Both wheels of a two-wheeler spin about a **transverse axis (Y)** at $\omega_s$
- When the vehicle turns (negotiates a curve), the steering axis (i.e., the spin axis of the wheel) **precesses** about the **vertical axis (Z)** at angular velocity $\omega_p$
- Mass moment of inertia of each wheel about its spin axis = $I$
- The precession $\omega_p$ is the rate of change of the **steering angle** $\theta$ as the rider negotiates the turn

The **reactive gyroscopic couple** acts about a **longitudinal axis (X)** and tends to **overturn the vehicle** inwards or outwards depending on the direction of turn.

**Magnitude (per wheel):**
$$C_{wheel} = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \frac{v}{R} \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

Where:
- $v$ = velocity of the two-wheeler
- $R$ = radius of the curve
- $\theta$ = angle of banking / lean

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Couple Direction (Two-Wheeler Left Turn):** When the rider turns the front wheel to the **left**, the wheel's spin axis precesses about the vertical axis. The reactive gyroscopic couple acts about the **longitudinal axis** such that it tends to **tilt (bank) the vehicle to the left** (i.e., **inward** to the turn) – which is **stabilizing**. Without this gyroscopic effect, the bike would fall outward.

### 2.3.2 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple during Application of Brakes

- When **front brake** is applied: the front wheel decelerates (spin rate changes from $\omega_s$ to lower value), introducing $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega_s}{dt}$ – a **gyroscopic torque** $T = I \cdot \alpha$ acts on the steering axis
- This produces an **extra reactive couple** that tends to flip the vehicle forward (a known cause of "**stoppies**" and front-brake-induced accidents on motorcycles)

## 2.4 Gyroscopic Effect on a Four-Wheeler

In an automobile taking a turn:
- The **driving (rear) wheels** act as gyroscopes
- The front wheels are steered through an angle $\theta$ at angular velocity $\omega_p$ (rate of change of steering angle as the vehicle negotiates a curve)
- The reactive gyroscopic couple is $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$
- This couple tends to **overturn** the vehicle **outward** at low speeds and **inward** at high speeds (theoretically), though the **net overturning effect** is dominated by the **centrifugal couple** $W \cdot v^2 / (gR)$ where $W$ = weight of vehicle

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** The gyroscopic effect of automobile wheels is usually **small** because $I$ for a wheel is small and $\omega_p$ (rate of steering) is low. So in vehicle design, the gyroscopic effect of wheels is often **neglected** compared to the centrifugal overturning couple. However, in problems, students are expected to calculate both and add/subtract them.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Reactive Gyroscopic Couple | Direction of Couple |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ship – Steering (turning) | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ | Vertical (causes pitching) |
| 2 | Ship – Pitching | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ | Horizontal plane (causes turning) |
| 3 | Ship – Rolling | $C = 0$ | – (parallel axes) |
| 4 | Aircraft – Pitching | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ | Vertical (causes yawing) |
| 5 | Aircraft – Yawing/Turning | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ | Transverse (causes rolling) |
| 6 | Aircraft – Rolling | $C = 0$ | – (parallel axes) |
| 7 | Two-Wheeler – Turning | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ per wheel | Longitudinal (tilts vehicle) |
| 8 | Two-Wheeler – Braking | $T_{gyro} = I \alpha$ | About steering axis |
| 9 | Four-Wheeler – Turning | $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ | About longitudinal axis |

**Standard Forms of $\omega_p$:**

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi N_p}{60} = \frac{v}{R} = \dot{\theta}$$

**Standard Form of Spin Velocity:**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Mass Moment of Inertia for a Solid Disc / Wheel:**

$$I = \frac{M R^2}{2} \quad \text{(disc about center, perpendicular to face)}$$

$$I = \frac{M R^2}{2} + \frac{M k^2}{4} \quad \text{(about transverse axis, where k = thickness)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic Box:**
> - **Sea vessel (propeller) → spin along longitudinal axis**
> - **Ship Steering → Couple causes Pitching**
> - **Ship Pitching → Couple causes Steering**
> - **Ship Rolling → No gyroscopic effect**
> - **Aircraft Pitching → Couple causes Yawing**
> - **Aircraft Yawing → Couple causes Rolling**
> - **Aircraft Rolling → No gyroscopic effect**
